topics.title: Introduction to Chemical Industries: Classification of Chemical Industries, Manufacture of Sulphuric Acid - DCDA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Chemical Industries: Classification of Chemical Industries, Manufacture of Sulphuric Acid - DCDA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Chemical Industry?

A **chemical industry** is a sector of the manufacturing economy that transforms raw materials (feedstocks) — derived from natural sources such as crude oil, natural gas, minerals, air, water, biomass, and metals — into a vast spectrum of finished and intermediate chemical products through physical, thermal, and chemical unit operations and unit processes. The KTU 2024 syllabus positions the chemical industry as the **backbone of every modern economy**, because virtually every other manufacturing stream (textiles, agriculture, automobiles, pharmaceuticals, construction, electronics) depends on at least one chemical input.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Box (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *The chemical industry comprises all enterprises engaged in the production of industrial chemicals, petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals, polymers, fertilizers, dyes, paints, and allied products by converting feedstocks using physical, chemical, and biological transformations.*

## 1.2 Classification of Chemical Industries

The chemical industry can be classified in **five** conventional ways, all of which are testable in KTU module-end exams.

### A. Classification Based on Raw Material Used
- **Inorganic chemical industry** – processes minerals, ores, salts, air, and water (e.g., sulphuric acid, caustic soda, ammonia).
- **Organic chemical industry** – processes carbon-based feedstocks such as petroleum, natural gas, coal, and biomass (e.g., petrochemicals, polymers, dyes).
- **Biochemical industry** – uses enzymes, microbes, or plant/animal matter (e.g., ethanol fermentation, antibiotics, biopolymers).

### B. Classification Based on Type of Product
- **Heavy (Bulk) chemicals** – produced in very large tonnages at low unit cost (e.g., H₂SO₄, NaOH, NH₃, Cl₂, O₂).
- **Fine chemicals** – produced in moderate volumes at high purity (e.g., reagents, catalysts, APIs).
- **Specialty chemicals** – low-volume, high-value, function-specific (e.g., adhesives, coatings, surfactants, additives).
- **Pharmaceuticals** – active ingredients and formulations.
- **Agrochemicals** – fertilizers, pesticides, herbicides.
- **Consumer chemicals** – soaps, detergents, cosmetics.

### C. Classification Based on End-Use
- **Industrial chemicals** – sold as raw materials to other industries.
- **Consumer chemicals** – sold directly to the public (detergents, cosmetics, toiletries).

### D. Classification Based on Scale of Production
- **Continuous process industry** – operates 24 × 7 with steady-state operation (refineries, mega-fertilizer plants).
- **Batch process industry** – operates in discrete lots (fine chemicals, pharmaceuticals).
- **Semi-batch** – hybrid of both.

### E. Classification Based on Source of Raw Material
- **Petrochemical industry** – derived from crude oil & natural gas.
- **Coal-based industry** – derived from coal & coke.
- **Mineral/inorganic industry** – derived from ores, salts, and atmospheric gases.
- **Agro-based industry** – derived from agricultural produce.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Chemical Industry is a Kitchen on a Mega-Scale"**
> 
> Imagine a giant industrial kitchen. The **raw materials** (vegetables, spices, grains) are like feedstocks (crude oil, sulphur, ores, air). The **utensils** (pressure cookers, ovens, grinders) are the *unit operations* (distillation, filtration, heat exchange). The **recipes** (curry, bread, cake) are the *unit processes* (oxidation, polymerization, nitration). The **chef's efficiency, repetition, and scale** correspond to continuous vs. batch production. Just as a chef combines steps to produce varied dishes, a chemical plant combines unit operations and unit processes to produce thousands of products from a handful of basic feedstocks.

## 1.4 The Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄) Industry — Why It Matters

Sulphuric acid is called the **"King of Chemicals"** because:
- It has the **largest tonnage production** of any industrial chemical in the world (over **260 million tonnes/year** globally).
- It directly or indirectly touches nearly every manufacturing chain: fertilizers (single superphosphate, ammonium sulphate), petroleum refining, metallurgy (ore leaching), textiles, dyes, explosives, batteries, and water treatment.
- It is a **key indicator of a nation's industrial strength** — economists use per-capita H₂SO₄ consumption as a gauge of industrial development.

## 1.5 The Contact Process and its DCDA Variant

Sulphuric acid is manufactured predominantly by the **Contact Process**, in which SO₂ is catalytically oxidized to SO₃ over a V₂O₅ catalyst, and the SO₃ is then absorbed in concentrated H₂SO₄ to give **oleum (H₂S₂O₇)**, which is diluted to the required strength.

The **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)** process is a *technological upgrade* of the classical single-contact process. It inserts an **inter-pass absorption tower** between the converter passes, removing the SO₃ produced in the first converter passes and **shifting the equilibrium** of the remaining SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ SO₃ reaction to the right (Le Chatelier's Principle). The result is an overall conversion of **> 99.5 %**, compared to ~ 97 % in the single-contact process, with significantly **lower stack emissions of unconverted SO₂**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:**
> For university exam answers, always write the *full form* of DCDA at first mention — *"Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA) process"* — then abbreviate freely thereafter. Marks are awarded for this precision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a DCDA Sulphuric Acid Plant

A DCDA plant is conceptually divided into **five blocks**:

1. **Sulphur Furnace (or Roaster)** — Generates SO₂.
2. **Gas Purification & Drying Section** — Removes dust, arsenic, halides, and moisture.
3. **Converter System (Catalytic Oxidation)** — Multi-bed catalytic converter with inter-stage heat exchangers.
4. **Absorption System (Inter-pass + Final)** — Two absorption towers, both using 98–99 % H₂SO₄.
5. **Acid Cooling & Storage** — Coolers, storage tanks, dilution section.

## 2.2 The Five Sub-Systems Explained

### 2.2.1 Sulphur Furnace
- Molten sulphur (99.5 % pure) is atomized through a **spinner burner** and burnt with dry air in a refractory-lined furnace.
- **Reaction:** S(l) + O₂(g) → SO₂(g)        ΔH = –297 kJ/mol
- Furnace exit gas typically contains **10–11 % SO₂ by volume** and is at **900–1100 °C**.

### 2.2.2 Waste Heat Boiler (WHB)
- The hot gases pass through a **waste heat boiler** that generates high-pressure steam (40–60 bar), recovering ~ 60–70 % of the reaction heat.
- Gas temperature drops to ~ **430 °C** before entering the converter.

### 2.2.3 Gas Purification & Drying Tower
- The gas is cooled further (to ~ 80–100 °C) and passed through **packed gas-cooling and drying towers** where concentrated H₂SO₄ (93–98 %) absorbs the moisture.
- **Why dry?** Because SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ in the gas phase creates a corrosive **acid mist** that destroys the catalyst and corrodes downstream equipment.

### 2.2.4 Catalytic Converter (4-pass, 5-catalyst-bed design)
- The classical DCDA converter has **five catalyst beds** (passes) with **inter-stage cooling** using external heat exchangers (cold gas from drying tower) and an **inter-pass absorption tower** between pass III and pass IV.
- **Main reaction (reversible, exothermic):**
  2 SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2 SO₃(g)        ΔH = –98.5 kJ/mol (at 25 °C)
- **Catalyst:** V₂O₅ (vanadium pentoxide) promoted with K₂O on a SiO₂/K₂SO₄ carrier, in the form of pellets or rings.
- **Optimal temperature range:** 400–600 °C. Higher temperatures speed up the reaction but shift equilibrium *backwards*; hence each successive bed operates at a lower temperature than the previous one.

### 2.2.5 Absorption Towers
- **Inter-pass absorption tower (between Pass III and Pass IV):** Absorbs SO₃ from the gas using 98.5 % H₂SO₄ at ~ 70–80 °C. This removes the product SO₃ and forces the remaining unreacted SO₂ (still ~ 4–5 %) towards further conversion in Pass IV & V, raising overall conversion to **> 99.5 %**.
- **Final absorption tower (after Pass V):** Absorbs the remaining SO₃ in fresh 98.5 % H₂SO₄.
- **Product:** Oleum (H₂S₂O₇ = SO₃ dissolved in H₂SO₄), which is diluted with water or weak acid to give commercial grades (98 %, 93 %, or 78 % H₂SO₄).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 98 % H₂SO₄ and not water for absorption?**
> Direct absorption of SO₃ in water releases enormous heat and creates a fine, corrosive H₂SO₄ mist that escapes absorption. Using 98–99 % H₂SO₄ keeps the partial pressure of water very low, so SO₃ dissolves as oleum without forming a mist. The 98 % acid is the **azeotrope of maximum boiling point (337 °C)**, ensuring minimum vapour pressure of both H₂O and SO₃.

## 2.3 Reaction Engineering of the Oxidation Step

The reaction 2 SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2 SO₃ is:
- **Exothermic** (favours low temperature for high equilibrium conversion).
- **Decrease in moles** (3 moles → 2 moles, favours high pressure for high conversion).
- **Slow kinetics** at low temperature (favours high temperature to overcome activation energy).

Hence a **compromise** is needed: a **moderate temperature (400–600 °C)** with a **highly active V₂O₅ catalyst** and **slight overpressure (1.1–1.5 atm)**.

The **equilibrium constant** depends strongly on temperature:

$$K_p = \frac{(p_{SO_3})^2}{(p_{SO_2})^2 \, p_{O_2}}$$

The integrated **van't Hoff relation** gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Because ΔH° is negative, **Kₚ decreases as T increases**, confirming that low temperature favours higher conversion.

## 2.4 Conversion Per Pass and Overall Conversion

In an ideal DCDA converter (4-5 passes with inter-pass absorption), the typical pass-wise equilibrium conversion distribution is:

| Pass | Catalyst Bed | Inlet Temperature (°C) | Outlet Temperature (°C) | Equilibrium Conversion in Pass (%) | Cumulative Conversion (%) |
|:----:|:------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------------:|:----------------------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| 1    | I            | 415–430               | 590–610                | 60–65                              | 60–65                      |
| 2    | II           | 430–450               | 490–510                | 22–25                              | 85–88                      |
| 3    | III          | 430–450               | 460–470                | 6–7                                | ~ 92                       |
| **—** | **Inter-pass absorption removes SO₃** | — | — | — | **Reset; remaining SO₂ ~ 4–5 %** |
| 4    | IV           | 420–430               | 440–450                | 80–85 *of remaining*              | ~ 98.5                     |
| 5    | V            | 410–420               | 420–430                | 60–70 *of remaining*              | **> 99.5**                 |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | KTU Use |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Stoichiometric air for S burning | $O_{2,st} = \dfrac{32}{32} = 1$ kg O₂/kg S; $V_{air,st} = \dfrac{1}{0.232} \times \dfrac{22.4}{32} = 3.02$ Nm³/kg S | Nm³/kg | Furnace design |
| 2 | Actual air for 11 % SO₂ feed | $V_{air} = \dfrac{21}{11} \times 3.02 = 5.77$ Nm³/kg S | Nm³/kg | Converter feed |
| 3 | Fractional conversion in one pass | $X = \dfrac{F_{SO_2,in} - F_{SO_2,out}}{F_{SO_2,in}}$ | dimensionless | Pass efficiency |
| 4 | Overall DCDA conversion | $X_{overall} = 1 - \prod (1 - X_i)$ for each pass | dimensionless | Plant yield |
| 5 | Acid produced per ton S (100 % conversion) | $m_{H_2SO_4} = \dfrac{98}{32} = 3.0625$ t/t S | t/t | Production target |
| 6 | Oleum production | $H_2SO_4 + SO_3 \rightarrow H_2S_2O_7$ | — | Absorption |
| 7 | van't Hoff relation | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | — | Equilibrium shift |
| 8 | Heat of reaction per kg S | $Q = \dfrac{-297\,\text{kJ/mol}}{32\,\text{g/mol}} = -9.28 \times 10^3$ kJ/kg S | kJ/kg | WHB design |
| 9 | Heat of reaction per kg H₂SO₄ | $Q = \dfrac{-98.5\,\text{kJ/mol}}{2\,\text{mol SO}_2} \times \dfrac{32}{98} = -161$ kJ/kg H₂SO₄ | kJ/kg | Converter cooling |
| 10 | Equilibrium constant (typical) | $K_p \approx 31$ at 700 K; $\approx 3.6$ at 800 K | bar⁻¹/atm⁻¹ | Reactor design |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **3.0625 t H₂SO₄ per tonne of S** number. It is the single most-tested stoichiometric ratio for this topic. Always show the molecular-mass ratio in the answer: $\dfrac{M_{H_2SO_4}}{M_S} = \dfrac{98}{32} = 3.0625$.

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Fertilizer sector (60–65 % of H₂SO₄):** Produces superphosphate, ammonium sulphate, and phosphoric acid.
- **Petroleum refining:** Alkylation catalyst regeneration, spent caustic neutralization.
- **Metallurgy:** Leaching of copper, uranium, and zinc ores; pickling of steel.
- **Chemical synthesis:** Manufacture of HCl, HNO₃, dyes, explosives (TNT, nitroglycerine), and detergents (LABSA).
- **Environmental:** pH control in effluent treatment, flue-gas scrubbing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Sulphur Combustion

**Problem (KTU 2018 type):** A DCDA plant burns 1 kg of pure sulphur. Find (a) the stoichiometric volume of air, (b) the actual air needed for a feed containing **11 % SO₂ by volume**, and (c) the moles of product gas per mole of S burned. Take air composition as **21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume**.

### Step 1 — Combustion Reaction
$$S_{(l)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow SO_{2(g)} \qquad \Delta H = -297\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical (Stoichiometric) O₂
Moles of S in 1 kg = $\dfrac{1000}{32} = 31.25$ mol.

The reaction is 1:1 in S:O₂, so theoretical O₂ = **31.25 mol**.

Volume of O₂ at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm): $31.25 \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.700\ \text{Nm}^3$.

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric Air
$$V_{air,st} = \frac{V_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{0.700}{0.21} = 3.333\ \text{Nm}^3\ \text{air per kg S}$$

### Step 4 — Actual Air for 11 % SO₂ Feed
The feed gas composition is 11 % SO₂, 10 % O₂ (excess), 79 % N₂.
For every 100 mol of feed gas, 11 mol of SO₂ is present ⇒ 11 mol S burned ⇒ 11 mol O₂ used.
Remaining O₂ = 10 mol ⇒ total O₂ supplied = 11 + 10 = 21 mol.
Total air supplied = 21 / 0.21 = 100 mol feed gas per 11 mol S.
Per mol S: $\dfrac{100}{11} = 9.09$ mol air ⇒ $9.09 \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2037\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{mol S}$.
Per kg S: $31.25 \times 0.2037 = 6.366\ \text{Nm}^3\ \text{air/kg S}$.

### Step 5 — Cross-check
**Rule of thumb:** $V_{air,actual} = \dfrac{21\%}{11\%} \times V_{air,st} = \dfrac{21}{11} \times 3.333 = 6.36\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{kg S}$. ✔ Matches.

## 3.2 Theoretical Production of H₂SO₄ per Tonne of Sulphur

### Step 1 — Net reaction
$$S + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$$

### Step 2 — Mass balance
- 1 tonne (1000 kg) S = 1000 / 32 = 31.25 kmol S.
- 1 kmol S → 1 kmol H₂SO₄ = 98 kg.
- So 1 kmol S → 98 kg H₂SO₄.
- Per tonne S: $31.25 \times 98 = 3062.5\ \text{kg} = 3.0625\ \text{tonnes H}_2\text{SO}_4$.

$$\boxed{\text{Theoretical yield} = \frac{M_{H_2SO_4}}{M_S} = \frac{98}{32} = 3.0625\ \text{t H}_2\text{SO}_4/\text{t S}}$$

### Step 3 — Actual yield at 99.5 % overall conversion
$$m_{H_2SO_4,actual} = 3.0625 \times 0.995 = 3.047\ \text{t/t S}$$

## 3.3 Material Balance Around a Single Catalyst Pass

**Problem:** A feed of **100 kmol** enters a catalyst bed with composition **7 % SO₂, 11 % O₂, 82 % N₂**. The pass achieves **60 % conversion of SO₂**. Calculate (a) the moles of SO₂, SO₃, O₂ leaving, and (b) the exit gas composition.

### Step 1 — Initial moles
- $n_{SO_2,in} = 7$ kmol
- $n_{O_2,in} = 11$ kmol
- $n_{N_2,in} = 82$ kmol
- $n_{SO_3,in} = 0$ kmol
- Total = 100 kmol.

### Step 2 — Converted moles
SO₂ reacted = $0.60 \times 7 = 4.2$ kmol.

### Step 3 — Use stoichiometry
$2\,SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2\,SO_3$.

For 4.2 kmol SO₂ reacted:
- O₂ consumed = $4.2 / 2 = 2.1$ kmol.
- SO₃ formed = 4.2 kmol.

### Step 4 — Exit moles
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{SO_2,out} &= 7 - 4.2 = 2.8\ \text{kmol} \\
n_{O_2,out} &= 11 - 2.1 = 8.9\ \text{kmol} \\
n_{SO_3,out} &= 0 + 4.2 = 4.2\ \text{kmol} \\
n_{N_2,out} &= 82.0\ \text{kmol} \\
n_{total,out} &= 2.8 + 8.9 + 4.2 + 82.0 = 97.9\ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Exit composition (% by volume)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,SO_2 &= \frac{2.8}{97.9} \times 100 = 2.86\% \\
\%\,O_2 &= \frac{8.9}{97.9} \times 100 = 9.09\% \\
\%\,SO_3 &= \frac{4.2}{97.9} \times 100 = 4.29\% \\
\%\,N_2 &= \frac{82.0}{97.9} \times 100 = 83.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Equilibrium check
The single-pass conversion of 60 % matches the textbook value for the *first* bed of a 4-pass DCDA converter at the corresponding inlet temperature (~ 415 °C).

## 3.4 Heat Released in the Converter and Steam Generation

**Problem:** Calculate the heat released in the converter per tonne of sulphur oxidized at 99.5 % overall conversion.

### Step 1 — Moles of SO₂ oxidized
Per tonne S at 100 % conversion: 31.25 kmol SO₂ produced. At 99.5 % conversion: $31.25 \times 0.995 = 31.094$ kmol SO₂ oxidized to SO₃.

### Step 2 — Heat of reaction
Per 2 mol SO₂ oxidized → 98.5 kJ released.
Per mol SO₂ → $98.5 / 2 = 49.25$ kJ.

### Step 3 — Total heat
$$
Q = 31\,094\ \text{mol} \times 49.25\ \text{kJ/mol} = 1.531 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ per tonne S}
$$

### Step 4 — Steam equivalent
With latent heat of steam = 2200 kJ/kg (at ~ 25 bar):
$$m_{steam} = \frac{1.531 \times 10^6}{2200} = 696\ \text{kg steam per tonne S}$$

This is why every modern H₂SO₄ plant is a net **steam exporter** to the grid.

## 3.5 Why Inter-pass Absorption Lifts Conversion Above 99 % — Quantitative Proof

**Problem:** After 3 catalyst passes, the gas contains **2.86 % SO₂, 9.09 % O₂, 83.76 % N₂, 4.29 % SO₃** (from §3.3 cumulative). If we now *absorb all the SO₃* in 98 % H₂SO₄ and pass the remaining SO₂ + O₂ + N₂ through passes IV and V, find the *new* fractional conversion required to reach 99.5 % overall.

### Step 1 — Re-baseline after absorption
After inter-pass absorption, SO₃ = 0; SO₂, O₂, N₂ remain at 2.8, 8.9, 82.0 kmol.

### Step 2 — Target for 99.5 % overall
Total SO₂ ever produced = 7 kmol.
Target residual SO₂ = $7 \times (1 - 0.995) = 0.035$ kmol.
Required conversion in passes IV + V = $\dfrac{2.8 - 0.035}{2.8} = 0.9875 = 98.75\%$.

### Step 3 — Compare with single-contact process
In single-contact, the same 2.8 kmol SO₂ at the same temperature would convert only **~ 70 %** in a single bed, leaving 0.84 kmol unconverted ⇒ overall conversion only **88 %**.

This **factor-of-four reduction in tail-gas SO₂** is the entire economic and environmental justification for DCDA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Booster:** Whenever you write about DCDA in an exam, always quote the **> 99.5 % conversion** number and contrast it with the **~ 97 %** of single-contact plants. Examiners specifically look for this numerical comparison.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Chemical Industries — Hierarchical Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CHEMICAL INDUSTRY"]:::root
    A --> B["By Raw Material"]
    A --> C["By Product Type"]
    A --> D["By Process Mode"]
    A --> E["By End Use"]
    A --> F["By Source"]

    B --> B1["Inorganic"]
    B --> B2["Organic"]
    B --> B3["Biochemical"]

    C --> C1["Heavy / Bulk"]
    C --> C2["Fine"]
    C --> C3["Specialty"]
    C --> C4["Pharma"]
    C --> C5["Agro"]
    C --> C6["Consumer"]

    D --> D1["Continuous"]
    D --> D2["Batch"]
    D --> D3["Semi-Batch"]

    E --> E1["Industrial"]
    E --> E2["Consumer"]

    F --> F1["Petrochemical"]
    F --> F2["Coal Based"]
    F --> F3["Mineral / Inorganic"]
    F --> F4["Agro Based"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,F1,F2,F3,F4 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DCDA Sulphuric Acid Plant

```mermaid
graph LR
    FEED["Molten Sulphur + Dry Air"]:::feed --> FURN["S1 Sulphur Furnace"]
    FURN -->|"SO2 10-11 percent 900-1100 C"| WHB["S2 Waste Heat Boiler"]
    WHB -->|"430 C Steam generated"| COOL["S3 Gas Cooling Tower"]
    COOL --> DRY["S4 Drying Tower 98 percent H2SO4"]
    DRY -->|"Dry SO2 gas 80 C"| CONV1["Pass I Catalyst V2O5"]
    CONV1 --> HX1["Heat Exchanger 1"]
    HX1 --> CONV2["Pass II Catalyst"]
    CONV2 --> HX2["Heat Exchanger 2"]
    HX2 --> CONV3["Pass III Catalyst"]
    CONV3 --> IPA["S5 Inter-Pass Absorption Tower 98.5 percent H2SO4"]
    IPA --> HX3["Heat Exchanger 3"]
    HX3 --> CONV4["Pass IV Catalyst"]
    CONV4 --> HX4["Heat Exchanger 4"]
    HX4 --> CONV5["Pass V Catalyst"]
    CONV5 --> FA["S6 Final Absorption Tower 98.5 percent H2SO4"]
    FA --> OLEUM["Product Oleum H2S2O7"]
    OLEUM --> DIL["Dilution Section"]
    DIL --> PROD["Commercial H2SO4 98 percent or 93 percent"]
    WHB --> STEAM["High Pressure Steam to Grid"]
    FA -->|"Tail gas SO2 less than 50 ppm"| STACK["S7 Stack Emission"]

    classDef feed fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class FURN,WHB,COOL,DRY,CONV1,CONV2,CONV3,CONV4,CONV5,HX1,HX2,HX3,HX4,IPA,FA,DIL fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    class STEAM,STACK fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class OLEUM,PROD fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Five-Bed Converter with Inter-Pass Absorption — Logical Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    G1["Dry SO2 7 percent O2 11 percent N2 80 C"]:::in --> B1["Bed I V2O5 415 C inlet 600 C outlet 60 percent conv"]
    B1 --> HE1["External Heat Exchanger cool to 430 C"]
    HE1 --> B2["Bed II 450 C 500 C 22 percent conv cumulative 85 percent"]
    B2 --> HE2["Heat Exchanger cool to 430 C"]
    HE2 --> B3["Bed III 430 C 460 C 6 percent conv cumulative 92 percent"]
    B3 --> AB1["Inter-Pass Absorption Tower SO3 removed"]
    AB1 --> HE3["Heat Exchanger cool to 420 C"]
    HE3 --> B4["Bed IV 420 C 445 C 80 percent of remaining conv cumulative 98.5 percent"]
    B4 --> HE4["Heat Exchanger cool to 415 C"]
    HE4 --> B5["Bed V 415 C 425 C 60 percent of remaining conv cumulative 99.5 percent plus"]
    B5 --> AB2["Final Absorption Tower SO3 removed as oleum"]
    AB2 --> ST["Tail Gas to Stack SO2 less than 50 ppm"]

    classDef in fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bed fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef ab fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,HE1,HE2,HE3,HE4 fill:#bedbff,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    class AB1,AB2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class ST fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Role of Inter-Pass Absorption — Why DCDA Works (Cause–Effect)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X1["Reversible Reaction 2SO2 plus O2 rightleftarrows 2SO3"]:::cause
    X2["Le Chatelier Principle removing product drives reaction right"]:::cause
    X3["Inter-pass absorption removes SO3 in 98 percent H2SO4"]:::cause
    X4["Remaining SO2 and O2 continue to react in pass IV and V"]:::cause
    X5["Overall conversion jumps from 97 percent to greater than 99.5 percent"]:::result
    X6["Tail gas SO2 falls from 2000 ppm to less than 50 ppm"]:::result
    X7["Plant becomes environment compliant and acid yield rises by 2.5 percent"]:::result

    X1 --> X2 --> X3 --> X4 --> X5
    X4 --> X6
    X5 --> X7

    classDef cause fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define chemical industry. Classify chemical industries based on raw materials with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
A chemical industry is a manufacturing sector that converts raw materials (feedstocks) such as minerals, crude oil, natural gas, air, water, and biomass into a wide range of chemical products through physical, chemical, and biological transformations.

Classification based on raw material:
1. **Inorganic** – uses minerals, ores, salts, air, water. *Example:* Sulphuric acid, caustic soda.
2. **Organic** – uses petroleum, natural gas, coal, biomass. *Example:* Petrochemicals, polymers.
3. **Biochemical** – uses enzymes, microbes, or biomass. *Example:* Ethanol, antibiotics.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for classification with examples.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Why is sulphuric acid called the "King of Chemicals"? List any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) is called the *King of Chemicals* because it has the largest annual production of any industrial chemical (~ 260 million tonnes worldwide) and because nearly every other manufacturing industry depends on it directly or indirectly.

Two industrial applications:
1. **Fertilizer industry:** Manufacture of superphosphate, ammonium sulphate, and phosphoric acid (consumes ~ 60 % of total H₂SO₄ produced).
2. **Petroleum refining:** Used in alkylation catalysis, spent caustic neutralization, and product treating.

*(3 marks: 1 for naming convention + 2 for applications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a) Classify chemical industries in detail with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Chemical industries can be classified into the following categories:

**(i) Based on Raw Material**
- *Inorganic chemical industry* — Uses ores, salts, air, water. Examples: H₂SO₄, NaOH, Cl₂, NH₃.
- *Organic chemical industry* — Uses petroleum, coal, biomass. Examples: Petrochemicals, dyes, polymers.
- *Biochemical industry* — Uses biomass, enzymes, microbes. Examples: Alcohol, antibiotics, biopolymers.

**(ii) Based on Type of Product**
- *Heavy / Bulk chemicals* — Large tonnage, low unit cost. Examples: H₂SO₄, O₂, NH₃.
- *Fine chemicals* — Moderate volume, high purity. Examples: Analytical reagents, catalysts, APIs.
- *Specialty chemicals* — Low volume, function-specific. Examples: Adhesives, coatings, surfactants.
- *Pharmaceuticals* — Drug substances and formulations.
- *Agrochemicals* — Fertilizers, pesticides, herbicides.
- *Consumer chemicals* — Soaps, detergents, cosmetics.

**(iii) Based on Process Mode**
- *Continuous* — Steady-state, 24 × 7. Examples: Refineries, fertilizer megaplants.
- *Batch* — Discrete lots, used for small volumes. Examples: Pharma, dyes.
- *Semi-batch* — Hybrid of the two.

**(iv) Based on End-Use**
- *Industrial chemicals* — Sold to other industries (raw materials).
- *Consumer chemicals* — Sold directly to public.

**(v) Based on Source of Feedstock**
- *Petrochemical* — From crude oil / natural gas.
- *Coal-based* — From coal and coke.
- *Mineral / Inorganic* — From ores, salts, atmospheric gases.
- *Agro-based* — From agricultural produce.

*(7 marks: 1 for each sub-classification with one example pair.)*

---

**(b) Explain the manufacture of sulphuric acid by the DCDA process with a neat flow diagram and main reactions.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
The Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA) process is a modern variant of the Contact Process for manufacturing H₂SO₄. The inter-pass absorption tower inserted between the 3rd and 4th catalyst passes raises the overall conversion of SO₂ to H₂SO₄ to **> 99.5 %**, as compared to ~ 97 % in the single-contact process.

**Main Reactions:**
1. **Sulphur burning (Furnace):** S + O₂ → SO₂; ΔH = –297 kJ/mol.
2. **Catalytic oxidation (Converter):** 2 SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2 SO₃; ΔH = –98.5 kJ/mol.
3. **Absorption (in 98 % H₂SO₄):** SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂SO₄·SO₃ (oleum, H₂S₂O₇).
4. **Dilution:** H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2 H₂SO₄.

**Plant Description (in flow sequence):**
1. **Sulphur Furnace:** Molten sulphur is atomized and burnt with dry air, producing 10–11 % SO₂ at 900–1100 °C.
2. **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB):** Recovers heat as 40–60 bar steam; gas cools to ~ 430 °C.
3. **Gas Cooling & Drying Tower:** Gas is cooled and dried over 93–98 % H₂SO₄ to remove all moisture (essential to prevent acid mist).
4. **Catalytic Converter (5 beds, 4 passes):** Dry SO₂ + O₂ passes through 5 V₂O₅ catalyst beds with inter-stage heat exchangers. After bed III, gas enters the inter-pass absorption tower.
5. **Inter-pass Absorption Tower:** SO₃ from first three beds is absorbed in 98.5 % H₂SO₄, giving oleum.
6. **Pass IV & V:** Remaining SO₂ + O₂ react further; conversion rises to > 99.5 %.
7. **Final Absorption Tower:** Remaining SO₃ absorbed in 98.5 % H₂SO₄.
8. **Dilution & Cooling:** Oleum diluted to 98 % or 93 % H₂SO₄, cooled, and stored.

**Process Conditions:**
- Catalyst: V₂O₅ with K₂O promoter on SiO₂ support.
- Inlet temperature to each bed: 415–450 °C.
- Operating pressure: 1.1–1.5 atm.
- Acid concentration for absorption: 98.5 % H₂SO₄.
- Overall conversion: **> 99.5 %**.

**Flow Diagram:** (See the Mermaid block diagram in §4.2 above for the architecture.)

**Advantages of DCDA over Single Contact:**
1. Higher conversion (> 99.5 % vs. ~ 97 %).
2. Tail gas SO₂ < 50 ppm (environmental compliance).
3. Higher acid yield per tonne of sulphur.
4. Self-sustaining through waste-heat steam generation.

*(7 marks: 1 for introduction + 1 for reactions + 3 for plant description + 1 for process conditions + 1 for advantages.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the manufacture of sulphuric acid by the DCDA process.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:** *(See block diagram in §4.2 and the full process description in Question A part (b) above.)*

For brevity, the key blocks are:
1. Sulphur furnace
2. Waste heat boiler
3. Gas cooling & drying tower
4. Catalytic converter (5 beds, 4 passes)
5. Inter-pass absorption tower (between passes III and IV)
6. Final absorption tower
7. Acid cooling & storage

Each block performs a specific unit operation or unit process; together they constitute the *functional architecture* of a DCDA H₂SO₄ plant. *(7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Write the main reactions, operating conditions, and material balance for producing 1 tonne of H₂SO₄ by the DCDA process from sulphur.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Main Reactions:**
- S + O₂ → SO₂  (furnace)
- 2 SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2 SO₃  (catalytic converter, V₂O₅ catalyst)
- SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇  (absorption)
- H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2 H₂SO₄  (dilution)

**Operating Conditions:**
- Catalyst: V₂O₅ on K₂O/SiO₂ support.
- Inlet temperature: 410–450 °C per bed.
- Operating pressure: 1.1–1.5 atm.
- Acid concentration for absorption: 98.5 % H₂SO₄.
- Overall conversion: > 99.5 %.

**Material Balance (theoretical, 100 % conversion):**

Basis: 1 tonne (1000 kg) of sulphur.
- Moles of S = 1000 / 32 = 31.25 kmol.
- Moles of H₂SO₄ produced = 31.25 kmol = 31.25 × 98 = 3062.5 kg = **3.0625 tonnes H₂SO₄**.

**Stoichiometric air required:**
- O₂ needed = 31.25 kmol = 0.70 Nm³.
- Stoichiometric air = 0.70 / 0.21 = **3.33 Nm³/kg S**.

**Actual air for 11 % SO₂ feed:**
- Air = (21 / 11) × 3.33 = **6.36 Nm³/kg S**.

**Steam generated (from converter exotherm):**
- Heat released per tonne S at 99.5 % conversion = $1.531 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ}$.
- Steam generated (latent heat 2200 kJ/kg) = $1.531 \times 10^6 / 2200 = 696\ \text{kg steam per tonne S}$.

**Acid produced at 99.5 % overall conversion:**
$$m_{H_2SO_4} = 3.0625 \times 0.995 = 3.047\ \text{tonnes per tonne S}$$

**Tail gas SO₂ concentration:**
- SO₂ in feed ≈ 7 % by volume; after 99.5 % conversion, residual ≈ 0.035 % = **350 ppm** in raw exit gas; with inter-pass absorption the final tail gas SO₂ is typically **< 50 ppm**, well within environmental limits.

*(7 marks: 2 for reactions + 1 for conditions + 4 for material balance.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (where students lose 2–4 marks):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the full form of DCDA on first mention.** Always write *"Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA) process"* in the opening line.
> 2. **Writing SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ as the absorption step.** This is *industrially wrong* — it forms acid mist. The correct industrial step is **SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇ (oleum)**, then dilution.
> 3. **Confusing single-contact (~ 97 %) with DCDA (> 99.5 %) conversion numbers.** Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 4. **Omitting the catalyst name and form.** Always specify *V₂O₅ with K₂O promoter on SiO₂ support* (pellets/rings).
> 5. **Not stating *why* 98 % H₂SO₄ is used for absorption.** Quote the *maximum-boiling azeotrope* and *low vapour pressure* rationale.
> 6. **Skipping the role of the waste-heat boiler.** It is the *economic backbone* of the plant (steam export), not just a cooler.
> 7. **In material balance problems, forgetting to convert kmol to Nm³ and to account for the 99.5 % (not 100 %) plant conversion.**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Chemical Industry Definition:** Sector that transforms feedstocks (crude oil, minerals, air, water, biomass) into chemical products via unit operations + unit processes.
- **Five classifications of chemical industries:** by raw material, by product type, by process mode, by end use, by feedstock source.
- **Sulphuric Acid = "King of Chemicals"** — highest annual tonnage (~ 260 MT globally).
- **Major applications:** Fertilizers (~ 60 %), petroleum refining, metallurgy, dyes, explosives, water treatment.
- **DCDA = Double Contact Double Absorption** — the modern industrial standard for H₂SO₄ manufacture.
- **Plant sections (5):** Sulphur Furnace → WHB → Drying Tower → Converter (5 beds) with **Inter-pass Absorption** between beds III & IV → Final Absorption → Acid Cooling.
- **Main reactions:** S + O₂ → SO₂; 2 SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2 SO₃; SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇; H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2 H₂SO₄.
- **Catalyst:** V₂O₅ + K₂O on SiO₂ support.
- **Operating conditions:** 410–600 °C, 1.1–1.5 atm, 98.5 % H₂SO₄ for absorption.
- **Overall conversion:** > 99.5 % (vs. ~ 97 % in single-contact).
- **Theoretical yield:** **3.0625 t H₂SO₄ / t S** (= 98/32).
- **Stoichiometric air:** 3.33 Nm³/kg S; **Actual air for 11 % SO₂:** 6.36 Nm³/kg S.
- **Steam export:** ~ 696 kg steam / t S (latent heat 2200 kJ/kg).
- **Inter-pass absorption rationale:** Removing SO₃ shifts equilibrium right → raises conversion > 99.5 % → tail gas SO₂ < 50 ppm.
- **Why 98 % H₂SO₄ for absorption?** It is the maximum-boiling azeotrope (337 °C) with the lowest vapour pressure, preventing acid-mist formation.
- **Why dry the gas before the converter?** Trace moisture would form H₂SO₄ mist in the catalyst bed, deactivating the V₂O₅ catalyst and corroding equipment.
- **Le Chatelier's Principle** is the key to understanding the DCDA advantage.
- **KTU Mantra:** *DCDA = Equilibrium engineering + Pollution control + Higher yield in one stroke.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphoric Acid - Wet Processes and Electric Furnace Process, Chlor-Alkali Industries - Soda Ash
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phosphoric Acid - Wet Processes and Electric Furnace Process, Chlor-Alkali Industries - Soda Ash

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Phosphoric Acid (H₃PO₄) — The Master Key Chemical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> Phosphoric acid (orthophosphoric acid, H₃PO₄) is a medium-strength mineral acid with molecular weight **97.995 g/mol**, density **1.88 g/cm³** at 100% concentration, and pKa values of **2.15, 7.20, and 12.35**. It is manufactured commercially by two principal routes: the **Wet Process** (acid digestion of phosphate rock) and the **Electric Furnace Process** (thermal reduction route).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Lemon of Industry":**
Think of phosphoric acid as the industrial equivalent of lemon juice (which is naturally citric acid). Just as lemon juice is used to:
- Make soft drinks tangy (cola industry),
- Clean metal surfaces (rust removal),
- Bind minerals in food (pH buffer),

...similarly, H₃PO₄ produced by the **Wet Process** (cheaper, impure — like a rustic homemade lemonade) is used in fertilizers, while H₃PO₄ made by the **Electric Furnace Process** (expensive, ultra-pure — like a pharmaceutical-grade extract) is used in food-grade and electronic chemicals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Production Capacity Reality:**
> Globally, **≈ 90% of phosphoric acid** is produced by the Wet Process, and only **≈ 10%** by the Electric Furnace Process. However, the furnace grade commands a **5 to 10 times higher market price** due to its purity.

## 1.2 The Wet Process — Conceptual Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wet Process Definition:**
> The Wet Process involves the digestion of finely ground phosphate rock (primarily fluorapatite, Ca₅(PO₄)₃F or Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂) with a strong mineral acid (predominantly sulfuric acid, H₂SO₄) at moderate temperatures (**75 – 85 °C**), yielding phosphoric acid and calcium sulfate (gypsum) as the by-product.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Digesting Bones":**
Imagine crushing a chicken bone (rich in calcium phosphate) and soaking it in vinegar (a weak acid). Over hours, the bone softens as the calcium dissolves out. The Wet Process does exactly this — but with strong sulfuric acid and rock-hard mineral ore — releasing phosphorus into solution as H₃PO₄.

## 1.3 The Electric Furnace Process — Conceptual Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Furnace Process Definition:**
> The Electric Furnace (Thermal) Process is a high-temperature (**1400 – 1500 °C**) carbothermic reduction of phosphate rock in the presence of silica (SiO₂) and coke (C) inside an electric arc furnace, producing elemental phosphorus (P₂/P₄) which is subsequently oxidized to P₂O₅ and hydrated to yield high-purity H₃PO₄.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Smelter's Forge":**
Think of the Electric Furnace Process like a blacksmith's forge, where intense heat (from electricity instead of coal) is used to "tease apart" a stubborn mineral ore into its elemental building blocks. The phosphate rock is "smelted" to release pure phosphorus vapor, which is then "recombined" with water to form pristine acid.

## 1.4 Chlor-Alkali Industry & Soda Ash (Na₂CO₃) — Conceptual Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soda Ash Definition:**
> Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃), commonly called **soda ash**, is a white, water-soluble, alkaline salt with molecular weight **105.99 g/mol**, melting point **851 °C**, and density **2.54 g/cm³**. It is one of the largest tonnage industrial chemicals globally, with annual production exceeding **60 million metric tonnes**. The principal modern manufacturing route is the **Solvay Process** (ammonia-soda process).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Washing Soda in Your Cupboard":**
Soda ash is the "industrial cousin" of baking soda (NaHCO₃). Just as baking soda makes cakes rise and absorbs fridge odors, soda ash is the heavy-duty version used to:
- Soften hard water in laundries,
- Manufacture glass (it's the source of Na₂O in glass),
- Regulate pH in swimming pools and chemical plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context for KTU Exams:**
> Before the Solvay Process, the **LeBlanc Process** (1791) was dominant. Nicolas Leblanc patented a route using NaCl + H₂SO₄ → Na₂SO₄, followed by reduction with coal and limestone. The Solvay Process (1861) replaced it due to higher purity and lower cost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Wet Process — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Raw Materials & Their Roles

| Component | Chemical Formula | Role in Wet Process |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Phosphate Rock | Ca₅(PO₄)₃F (Fluorapatite) | Primary source of P₂O₅ |
| Sulfuric Acid (98%) | H₂SO₄ | Digestion acid, source of H⁺ ions |
| Recycled Acid | H₃PO₄ (weak, 30–35%) | Pre-mixing to control reaction kinetics |
| Water | H₂O | Wash liquor for gypsum filtration |

### 2.1.2 Core Chemical Reactions

**Primary Reaction (Dihydrate Process):**
$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{HF}$$

**Simplified Single-Unit Reaction:**
$$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{CaSO}_4$$

**Side Reaction (Critical for KTU):**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Dihydrate vs Hemihydrate?**
> - **Dihydrate Process** (Nissan, Prayon): Operates at **75 – 85 °C**, produces gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O). Simpler, but acid concentration is limited to **28 – 32% P₂O₅**.
> - **Hemihydrate Process** (Fison, OE/Norsk Hydro): Operates at **90 – 110 °C**, produces CaSO₄·½H₂O. Higher acid strength (**40 – 50% P₂O₅**), reduced evaporation cost.

### 2.1.3 Process Flow Logic (Bullet Architecture)

- **Step 1 — Rock Preparation:** Phosphate ore is ground to **< 100 mesh** (≈ 150 μm) to maximize surface area.
- **Step 2 — Acidulation / Digestion:** Ground rock is mixed with **recirculated weak acid (30% P₂O₅)** in a single or multi-tank reactor (typically **4–6 stirred reactors in series**).
- **Step 3 — Filtration:** The reaction slurry is filtered on a **horizontal pan filter or belt filter** to separate gypsum crystals from the product acid.
- **Step 4 — Concentration:** Weak acid (28–32% P₂O₅) is evaporated under vacuum to **merchant grade (54% P₂O₅)** or **superphosphoric acid (68–72% P₂O₅)**.
- **Step 5 — Gypsum Disposal:** By-product gypsum (≈ 5 tonnes per tonne of P₂O₅) is either stockpiled, used in cement, or converted to Plaster of Paris (CaSO₄·½H₂O).

### 2.1.4 Key Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Dihydrate Range | Hemihydrate Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Temperature | 75 – 85 °C | 90 – 110 °C | Higher T favors hemihydrate crystallization |
| H₂SO₄ Concentration | 93 – 98% | 93 – 98% | Determines reaction vigor |
| Free SO₄²⁻ in slurry | 1.5 – 3.0% | 2.0 – 3.5% | Controls co-crystallization |
| Retention Time | 4 – 8 hours | 1.5 – 3 hours | Hemihydrate is faster |
| Filtration Rate | 0.5 – 1.0 t P₂O₅/m²·h | 0.8 – 1.5 t P₂O₅/m²·h | Hemihydrate gives better filterability |

---

## 2.2 The Electric Furnace Process — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Raw Materials & Their Roles

| Component | Function | Molar Ratio (per P₂O₅) |
|-----------|----------|-------------------------|
| Phosphate Rock (Ca₃(PO₄)₂) | Source of P | 1.0 |
| Silica (SiO₂) | Flux to form CaSiO₃ slag | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Coke (C) | Reducing agent | 1.4 – 1.6 |
| Electric Power | Heat source | ≈ 12 – 14 MWh / tonne P₄ |

### 2.2.2 Core Chemical Reactions

**Main Reduction Reaction (in Furnace, > 1500 °C):**
$$2\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 6\text{SiO}_2 + 10\text{C} \rightarrow 6\text{CaSiO}_3 + 10\text{CO} + \text{P}_4$$

**Simplified Form (per mole P₄):**
$$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 3\text{SiO}_2 + 5\text{C} \rightarrow 3\text{CaSiO}_3 + 5\text{CO} + \text{P}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-mechanism (Why SiO₂ is Essential):**
> Without silica, the reaction requires **> 2000 °C** because CaO (formed by direct reduction) is highly stable. Silica "mops up" the CaO by forming CaSiO₃ slag, lowering the reduction temperature to a practical **1500 °C**.

**Oxidation Step:**
$$\text{P}_4 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{P}_4\text{O}_{10} \quad (\text{or } \, 2\text{P}_2\text{O}_5)$$

**Hydration Step:**
$$\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$$

### 2.2.3 Process Flow Logic

- **Step 1 — Furnace Charge Preparation:** Phosphate rock, silica, and coke are weighed and fed as a "furnace burden" from the top.
- **Step 2 — Electric Arc Reduction:** A **3-phase AC electric arc** (typically 30 – 60 MW load) provides energy. The burden melts and forms a layered bath:
  - **Bottom:** Molten phosphorus (P₄, bp = 280 °C).
  - **Middle:** CaSiO₃ slag (mp ≈ 1500 °C).
  - **Top:** Charge burden (unreacted solids).
- **Step 3 — Phosphorus Collection:** Phosphorus vapor exits with CO gas, is condensed to liquid P₄ in water-cooled condensers.
- **Step 4 — Phosphorus Oxidation:** Liquid P₄ is sprayed into a stainless-steel combustion chamber with excess air, producing P₄O₁₀ mist.
- **Step 5 — Hydration:** P₄O₁₀ is absorbed in dilute H₃PO₄ in a hydrator tower, yielding **85–95% H₃PO₄**.

### 2.2.4 Critical Engineering Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Furnace Temperature | 1400 – 1500 °C | Drives carbothermic reduction |
| Electrode Current | 30 – 60 kA | Three-phase graphite electrodes |
| Specific Energy | 12 – 14 MWh/t P₄ | Major operating cost |
| Phosphorus Yield | ≈ 88 – 92% | Rest lost in slag and flue dust |
| Acid Purity | 85 – 99% H₃PO₄ | Food / pharma / electronics grade |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:**
> Students often write the oxidation product as P₂O₅. The actual combustion product is **P₄O₁₀ (phosphorus pentoxide)**, which dimerizes. For mass-balance calculations, always use **P₄O₁₀ = 284 g/mol**.

---

## 2.3 The Chlor-Alkali Industry — Soda Ash (Solvay Process)

### 2.3.1 Raw Materials

| Material | Formula | Function |
|----------|---------|----------|
| Brine (NaCl solution) | NaCl (≈ 25% aq.) | Source of Na⁺ ions |
| Limestone | CaCO₃ | Source of CO₂ |
| Ammonia | NH₃ | Recyclable alkaline medium |
| Coke | C | Fuel for kiln |

### 2.3.2 Core Reactions

**Limestone Calcination:**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (\approx 1000\,°\text{C})} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$

**Ammonia Recovery (from mother liquor with Ca(OH)₂):**
$$\text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(OH)}_2$$
$$2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Solvay Tower Reactions:**
$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{OH}$$
$$\text{NaCl} + \text{NH}_4\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NaHCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

**Overall Solvay Reaction:**
$$\text{NaCl} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

**Soda Ash Calcination:**
$$2\text{NaHCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (200\,°\text{C})} \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

### 2.3.3 Solvay Process — Flow Logic

- **Step 1 — Brine Purification:** Crude brine is treated with **Ca(OH)₂ + Na₂CO₃** to remove Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ impurities.
- **Step 2 — Ammoniation:** Purified brine is saturated with NH₃ in an **absorber tower**.
- **Step 3 — Carbonation:** NH₃-laden brine is passed into a **Solvay tower** where CO₂ is bubbled upward. NaHCO₃ precipitates (least soluble among the four salts in solution).
- **Step 4 — Filtration:** NaHCO₃ slurry is filtered on rotary vacuum filters.
- **Step 5 — Calcination:** Wet NaHCO₃ is calcined at **200 – 300 °C** in a rotary kiln to yield anhydrous Na₂CO₃ (soda ash) — "light ash" with bulk density **0.5 – 0.6 g/cm³**. Further hydration produces "dense ash" with bulk density **0.9 – 1.1 g/cm³**.
- **Step 6 — Ammonia Recovery:** The filtrate (containing NH₄Cl) is treated with Ca(OH)₂ in a **distillation column** to recover NH₃, which is recycled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Why NaHCO₃ precipitates preferentially?**
> Among NaCl, NH₄Cl, NaHCO₃, and NH₄HCO₃, **NaHCO₃ has the lowest solubility** at the carbonation temperature (≈ 30 °C). Therefore, by Le Chatelier's principle, it crystallizes first as the reaction shifts toward product formation.

### 2.3.4 Material Balance Snapshot (Per Tonne of Soda Ash)

| Input | Quantity | Output | Quantity |
|-------|----------|--------|----------|
| NaCl (as 100%) | ≈ 1.5 t | Na₂CO₃ | 1.00 t |
| CaCO₃ (as 100%) | ≈ 1.2 t | CaCl₂ (by-product) | ≈ 1.05 t |
| NH₃ (recycled, makeup) | ≈ 2 – 5 kg | CO₂ (recycled) | — |
| Coke (fuel) | ≈ 0.15 t | Steam | Recovered |
| Water | Process water | Effluent | Treated |

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| H₃PO₄ Molar Mass | $M = 3(1) + 31 + 4(16) = 97.995$ | g/mol | Stoichiometric yield |
| P₂O₅ Equivalent | $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \, \% = \frac{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \, \% \times 0.7243}{1}$ | wt% | Wet process product spec |
| Acid Strength Conversion | $\%\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 = \%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \times 1.381$ | wt% | Engineering conversion |
| Sulfuric Acid Stoichiometry | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 : \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = 2.33 : 1$ | mol/mol | Wet process acid requirement |
| Furnace Energy | $E_{\text{furnace}} \approx 12$ – $14$ | MWh/t P₄ | Electric furnace process |
| Solvay NaCl Stoichiometry | $\text{NaCl} : \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 = 2 : 1$ | mol/mol | Theoretical raw material |
| Solvay Actual NaCl Consumption | ≈ $1.5$ tonnes NaCl per tonne Na₂CO₃ | t/t | Real-world efficiency |
| Solvay Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\text{actual yield}}{\text{theoretical yield}} \times 100 \approx 70$ – $75$ | % | Process efficiency |
| Le Chatelier on NaHCO₃ | Solubility: NaHCO₃ (9.6 g/100g) < NaCl (35.7) < NH₄Cl (39.5) | g/100g H₂O at 30 °C | Selectivity basis |
| CaSO₄·2H₂O Production (Wet) | ≈ $4.5$ – $5.5$ t gypsum per t P₂O₅ | t/t | By-product handling |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:**
> For any "calculate the amount of H₂SO₄ required" question, always start with the **simplified stoichiometric equation** Ca₃(PO₄)₂ + 3H₂SO₄ → 2H₃PO₄ + 3CaSO₄, then **account for the actual fluorapatite formula** Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂ which gives a slightly different ratio (7 H₂SO₄ per fluorapatite unit).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Sulfuric Acid Requirement for Wet Process

> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU Question):**
> A wet-process phosphoric acid plant processes **100 tonnes/day of phosphate rock** containing **32% P₂O₅** and **4% CaCO₃** impurity. Calculate:
> (a) The mass of **98% H₂SO₄** required per day (assuming 5% excess).
> (b) The mass of **gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** produced per day.

### Step 1 — Identify the Active Phosphate Component

The phosphate in rock is present as **fluorapatite: Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂**.

The P₂O₅ content of fluorapatite:
$$\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ in fluorapatite} = \frac{M(\text{P}_2\text{O}_5) \times 3}{M(\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2)}$$

$$= \frac{141.94 \times 3}{1008.6} = \frac{425.82}{1008.6} = 0.4222 \approx 42.22\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Pure fluorapatite contains **42.22% P₂O₅** by mass.

### Step 2 — Compute Mass of P₂O₅ in Rock Feed

$$\text{Mass of P}_2\text{O}_5 = 100 \, \text{t} \times 0.32 = 32 \, \text{t/day}$$

### Step 3 — Convert P₂O₅ to Fluorapatite Equivalent

$$\text{Mass of fluorapatite} = \frac{32}{0.4222} = 75.79 \, \text{t/day}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Primary Digestion Reaction

The stoichiometric equation (per fluorapatite unit):
$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{HF}$$

**Molar masses used:**
- $M(\text{fluorapatite}) = 1008.6$ g/mol
- $M(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4) = 98.08$ g/mol
- $M(\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}) = 172.17$ g/mol

**Moles of fluorapatite per day:**
$$n_{\text{fap}} = \frac{75.79 \times 10^6 \, \text{g}}{1008.6 \, \text{g/mol}} = 75{,}150 \, \text{mol/day} \approx 7.515 \times 10^4 \, \text{mol/day}$$

### Step 5 — Stoichiometric H₂SO₄ Required

From equation: 1 mol fluorapatite requires 7 mol H₂SO₄.

$$n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{stoich}} = 7 \times 7.515 \times 10^4 = 5.2605 \times 10^5 \, \text{mol/day}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{stoich}} = 5.2605 \times 10^5 \times 98.08 \, \text{g} = 51{,}595 \, \text{kg/day} \approx 51.60 \, \text{t/day}$$

### Step 6 — Account for CaCO₃ Side Reaction

CaCO₃ impurity also consumes H₂SO₄:
$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{Mass of CaCO}_3 = 100 \times 0.04 = 4.0 \, \text{t/day}$$

$$n_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^6}{100.09} = 3.996 \times 10^4 \, \text{mol/day}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{CaCO}_3} = 3.996 \times 10^4 \times 98.08 = 3.92 \, \text{t/day}$$

### Step 7 — Total Stoichiometric H₂SO₄ (Pure Basis)

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{total, stoich}} = 51.60 + 3.92 = 55.52 \, \text{t/day}$$

### Step 8 — Apply 5% Excess and Account for Acid Purity

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{actual}} = 55.52 \times 1.05 = 58.30 \, \text{t/day} \, (\text{pure basis})$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{commercial}} = \frac{58.30}{0.98} = 59.49 \, \text{t/day}}$$

### Step 9 — Gypsum Produced

From main reaction: 1 mol fluorapatite → 7 mol CaSO₄·2H₂O.
Plus from CaCO₃ reaction: 1 mol CaCO₃ → 1 mol CaSO₄ (anhydrous, which hydrates to gypsum on washing).

$$n_{\text{gypsum, main}} = 7 \times 7.515 \times 10^4 = 5.2605 \times 10^5 \, \text{mol/day}$$

$$m_{\text{gypsum, main}} = 5.2605 \times 10^5 \times 172.17 = 90{,}569 \, \text{kg/day} = 90.57 \, \text{t/day}$$

$$n_{\text{gypsum, side}} = 3.996 \times 10^4 \, \text{mol/day}$$

$$m_{\text{gypsum, side}} = 3.996 \times 10^4 \times 172.17 = 6.88 \, \text{t/day}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{gypsum, total}} = 90.57 + 6.88 = 97.45 \, \text{t/day}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy & Mass Balance for Electric Furnace Process

### Step 1 — Theoretical P₄ from Phosphate Rock

Assume feed contains **100 kg of Ca₃(PO₄)₂** (molar mass = 310.18 g/mol).

$$n_{\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2} = \frac{100}{310.18} = 0.3224 \, \text{kmol}$$

From reduction reaction:
$$2\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 6\text{SiO}_2 + 10\text{C} \rightarrow 6\text{CaSiO}_3 + 10\text{CO} + \text{P}_4$$

**2 kmol Ca₃(PO₄)₂ produces 1 kmol P₄**, so:
$$n_{\text{P}_4}^{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{0.3224}{2} = 0.1612 \, \text{kmol}$$

$$m_{\text{P}_4}^{\text{theoretical}} = 0.1612 \times 123.88 = 19.97 \, \text{kg}$$

### Step 2 — Actual P₄ Yield (Accounting for 90% Furnace Efficiency)

$$m_{\text{P}_4}^{\text{actual}} = 19.97 \times 0.90 = 17.97 \, \text{kg}$$

### Step 3 — Convert P₄ to P₄O₁₀ (Oxidation)

$$\text{P}_4 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}$$

$$n_{\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}} = 0.1612 \times 0.90 = 0.1451 \, \text{kmol}$$

$$m_{\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}} = 0.1451 \times 283.89 = 41.18 \, \text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Hydration to H₃PO₄

$$\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$$

$$n_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4} = 4 \times 0.1451 = 0.5804 \, \text{kmol}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4} = 0.5804 \times 97.995 = 56.88 \, \text{kg (pure basis)}$$

### Step 5 — Stoichiometric Inputs

| Input | Molar Ratio | Required (kmol) | Required (kg) |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|
| SiO₂ | 3 per kmol Ca₃(PO₄)₂ | 0.9672 | 58.03 |
| C (coke) | 5 per kmol Ca₃(PO₄)₂ | 1.612 | 19.34 |

> **Key Insight:** For every **100 kg of Ca₃(PO₄)₂**, the furnace consumes **≈ 58 kg SiO₂**, **≈ 19 kg C**, and **≈ 13.5 kWh of electric energy** (assuming 13 MWh/t P₄ and ≈ 18 kg P₄ per 100 kg feed).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Solvay Process Material Balance (Per Tonne Na₂CO₃)

### Step 1 — Theoretical Stoichiometry

$$2\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2$$

$$2 \times 58.44 = 116.88 \, \text{g NaCl} \rightarrow 105.99 \, \text{g Na}_2\text{CO}_3$$

**Theoretical NaCl per tonne Na₂CO₃:**
$$m_{\text{NaCl}}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{1000 \times 116.88}{105.99} = 1102.7 \, \text{kg} = 1.103 \, \text{tonnes}$$

### Step 2 — Actual NaCl Consumption

Accounting for **side reactions** (CO₂ loss, NH₃ recycle inefficiencies, brine impurities), the actual industrial consumption is **≈ 1.5 t NaCl per t Na₂CO₃**.

### Step 3 — Limestone Required

From overall reaction: 1 mol CaCO₃ produces 1 mol Na₂CO₃.

$$m_{\text{CaCO}_3}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{1000 \times 100.09}{105.99} = 944.3 \, \text{kg} \approx 0.944 \, \text{tonnes}$$

**Actual:** **≈ 1.2 t CaCO₃ per t Na₂CO₃** (allowing for kiln losses and CO₂ escape).

### Step 4 — CO₂ Loop Quantification

- Limestone calcination provides **≈ 44 kg CO₂ per 100 kg CaCO₃**.
- Of this, **≈ 50%** is absorbed in the Solvay tower (rest lost with stack gas).
- Recycle ratio: ~**1.5–2.0 tonnes of CO₂ circulated per tonne of Na₂CO₃**.

### Step 5 — By-Product CaCl₂

$$\text{Mass of CaCl}_2 = \frac{1000 \times 110.98}{105.99} = 1047.0 \, \text{kg} \approx 1.05 \, \text{tonnes per tonne Na}_2\text{CO}_3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** The Solvay process generates **≈ 1 tonne of CaCl₂ waste liquor per tonne of Na₂CO₃**. This is a major environmental challenge, leading to gradual replacement by mineral deposits (trona) and the ammonium chloride-modified process.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Wet vs Electric Furnace vs Solvay)

| Criterion | Wet Process (H₃PO₄) | Electric Furnace (H₃PO₄) | Solvay Process (Na₂CO₃) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| Capital Cost | Low | Very High | High |
| Operating Cost | Moderate | High (electricity) | Moderate |
| Product Purity | 28 – 54% P₂O₅, contains impurities | 85 – 99% H₃PO₄, ultra-pure | 99+ % Na₂CO₃ |
| By-products | Gypsum (5 t/t P₂O₅) | CaSiO₃ slag, CO | CaCl₂ (1 t/t Na₂CO₃) |
| Energy Source | Chemical (acid) | Electrical (12–14 MWh/t) | Thermal + chemical |
| Environmental Burden | Gypsum stacks, HF emission | CO₂, P₄ toxicity | CaCl₂ liquor, brine |
| Best Application | Fertilizers | Food, pharma, electronics | Glass, soaps, detergents |
