# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Compare the Wet Process and Electric Furnace Process for phosphoric acid manufacture based on purity, cost, and by-products.**

**Model Answer:**
The Wet Process uses sulfuric acid digestion of phosphate rock, producing phosphoric acid of **28–54% P₂O₅** with significant impurities (sulfates, fluorides, heavy metals). It has **low capital and operating cost**, and the major by-product is **gypsum (≈ 5 t/t P₂O₅)**. The Electric Furnace Process uses **carbothermic reduction at 1500 °C**, producing ultra-pure **85–99% H₃PO₄** suitable for food and electronic applications, but with **very high capital cost** (electric arc furnace) and **high energy consumption (12–14 MWh/t P₄)**. The by-product is **CaSiO₃ slag**, used in cement. In summary, **Wet Process dominates fertilizer markets**, while **Electric Furnace serves high-purity niche markets**.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Why is ammonia recycled in the Solvay Process? What happens if recycling is interrupted?**

**Model Answer:**
Ammonia (NH₃) acts as an **alkaline medium** that enables CO₂ to react with NaCl in solution to form NaHCO₃ (which is otherwise impossible directly). NH₃ is regenerated in the distillation column from NH₄Cl using Ca(OH)₂:
$$2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
If recycling is interrupted, **fresh NH₃ makeup costs would dominate the operating expenses** (NH₃ price ≈ ₹ 30–50/kg). Additionally, the **CaCl₂ waste liquor volume would double**, causing severe environmental disposal issues. Hence, **≈ 98% of NH₃ is recycled** in modern Solvay plants.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Describe the manufacture of phosphoric acid by the Wet Process with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the key operating parameters and engineering challenges.**

#### Part (a) — Process Description, Reactions & Flow (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The Wet Process (also called the sulfuric acid process) involves the digestion of finely ground phosphate rock with sulfuric acid.

**Primary Reaction (Fluorapatite basis):**
$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{HF}$$

**Process Flow Steps:**

1. **Rock Preparation:** Phosphate rock is ground to < 100 mesh and slurried with recycled weak acid.
2. **Acidulation (Digestion):** The slurry is fed to a series of **4–6 stirred reactors** maintained at **75–85 °C** (dihydrate process) with controlled H₂SO₄ addition.
3. **Crystallization:** CaSO₄·2H₂O crystallizes as gypsum in the reactor train. Retention time **≈ 4–8 hours**.
4. **Filtration:** The slurry is filtered on a horizontal pan filter (Prayon) or belt filter. Wash liquor is recycled.
5. **Clarification & Defluorination:** Crude acid is treated with active silica and Na₂CO₃ to remove residual HF and fluorosilicates.
6. **Concentration:** Weak acid (28–32% P₂O₅) is concentrated in multi-effect vacuum evaporators to **54% P₂O₅ (merchant grade)** or **72% P₂O₅ (superphosphoric acid)**.

**By-Product Handling:**
- Gypsum is washed, neutralized, and either stockpiled, used in cement manufacture, or converted to Plaster of Paris.
- HF is scrubbed from reactor off-gas with water to give **70% hydrofluoric acid** (a valuable co-product).

#### Part (b) — Operating Parameters & Engineering Challenges (7 Marks)

**Key Operating Parameters Table:**

| Parameter | Dihydrate Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Temperature | 75 – 85 °C | Affects gypsum crystal habit |
| H₂SO₄ Concentration | 93 – 98% | Drives reaction rate |
| Free SO₄²⁻ in slurry | 1.5 – 3.0% | Prevents co-precipitation of CaHPO₄ |
| Rock grind size | < 100 mesh | Surface area for reaction |
| Retention time | 4 – 8 h | Crystal growth control |
| Filtration rate | 0.5 – 1.0 t P₂O₅/m²·h | Plant throughput |

**Engineering Challenges:**

1. **Corrosion:** Strong H₂SO₄ + HF + phosphoric acid is highly corrosive; reactors use **rubber-lined steel** or **FRP** construction.
2. **Gypsum Filtration:** Gypsum crystals must be uniform and coarse; poor crystals cause filter cloth blinding.
3. **Fluorine Emission:** HF released must be scrubbed to prevent atmospheric pollution and equipment damage.
4. **Phosphate Rock Variability:** Different ores (Morocco, Florida, Kola) have different CaO/P₂O₅ ratios requiring acid adjustment.
5. **Waste Gypsum Management:** With **≈ 5 tonnes gypsum per tonne P₂O₅**, disposal is a major environmental concern.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the manufacture of Soda Ash by the Solvay Process. Discuss the reactions, flow diagram, material balance, and the role of ammonia recycling.**

#### Part (a) — Reactions, Operating Principle & Flow (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The Solvay Process (Ammonia-Soda Process) converts **NaCl + CaCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + CaCl₂** via an intermediate NaHCO₃ precipitation using ammonia as a recyclable alkaline carrier.

**Reaction Sequence:**

**Step 1 — Limestone Calcination:**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{1000\,°\text{C}} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Ammonia Absorption in Brine:**
$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{OH}$$

**Step 3 — Carbonation (Solvay Tower):**
$$\text{NaCl} + \text{NH}_4\text{OH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NaHCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

**Step 4 — NaHCO₃ Calcination:**
$$2\text{NaHCO}_3 \xrightarrow{200\,°\text{C}} \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

**Step 5 — Ammonia Recovery:**
$$2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Flow Sequence:**

- **Brine purification:** Crude brine is treated with Ca(OH)₂ and Na₂CO₃ to precipitate Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ impurities.
- **Ammoniation:** Purified brine is saturated with NH₃ in an absorber tower.
- **Carbonation:** NH₃-laden brine enters the **Solvay tower** (a tall carbonator, 20–30 m high), where CO₂ is bubbled from the bottom. Temperature is maintained at **≈ 30 °C** to favor NaHCO₃ precipitation.
- **Filtration:** NaHCO₃ slurry is filtered on rotary vacuum filters.
- **Calcination:** Wet NaHCO₃ is calcined in a rotary kiln at **200 – 300 °C** to yield **light soda ash** (bulk density 0.5–0.6 g/cm³).
- **Ammonia recovery:** Mother liquor (NH₄Cl) is treated with Ca(OH)₂ in a distillation column; NH₃ is recycled.

#### Part (b) — Material Balance & Role of Ammonia Recycling (7 Marks)

**Material Balance (Per Tonne of Na₂CO₃):**

| Input | Quantity (Tonnes) |
|-------|--------------------|
| NaCl (100% basis) | ≈ 1.5 |
| CaCO₃ (limestone) | ≈ 1.2 |
| Coke (fuel) | ≈ 0.15 |
| NH₃ (makeup, rest recycled) | ≈ 0.003 – 0.005 |

| Output | Quantity (Tonnes) |
|--------|--------------------|
| Na₂CO₃ (product) | 1.00 |
| CaCl₂ (by-product) | ≈ 1.05 |
| CO₂ (recycled + lost) | ≈ 0.9 |
| Waste water | Treated |

**Role of Ammonia Recycling:**

1. **Enables Reaction:** Direct reaction of NaCl + CO₂ → NaHCO₃ is **thermodynamically unfavorable** in aqueous solution. NH₃ raises solution pH, enabling CO₂ to react as HCO₃⁻ and precipitate NaHCO₃ (the least soluble salt in the mixture).
2. **Economical:** Without recycling, ammonia cost would be ≈ 5 – 10 × the value of soda ash produced. The Solvay Process is economically viable **only because of NH₃ recovery**.
3. **Environmental:** Reduces NH₃ emissions; ≈ 98% NH₃ recovery is achieved industrially.
4. **Selectivity:** NH₃ prevents precipitation of Na₂CO₃ directly (which would clog the tower) and ensures NaHCO₃ is the solid product, which is then thermally decomposed to Na₂CO₃.

**Engineering Challenges:**

- **CaCl₂ Disposal:** Each tonne of soda ash yields ≈ 1 tonne CaCl₂ liquor; modern plants concentrate it for road de-icing or discharge into deep wells.
- **Energy Consumption:** Limestone calcination is energy-intensive (≈ 4 – 5 GJ/t Na₂CO₃).
- **Solvay Tower Scaling:** NaHCO₃ scaling on tower internals reduces heat transfer; periodic shutdown cleaning required.
- **Purity:** Solvay soda ash has ≈ 99.2% Na₂CO₃, with traces of NaCl and Na₂SO₄.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Wet Process Reaction:** Students often forget to write **HF as a by-product** of fluorapatite digestion. Marks lost: 1 per missed by-product.
> 2. **Electric Furnace Equation:** Writing "P₂O₅" as combustion product instead of **P₄O₁₀** is a frequent error. Use molecular formula in mass balance.
> 3. **Solvay Reactions:** Many students omit the **Ca(OH)₂ step** for NH₃ recovery and write only the tower reaction. This loses 2 marks in Part B answers.
> 4. **Material Balance:** Not accounting for **excess acid (5–10%)** in stoichiometric calculations is a common mistake. Always specify whether acid is on pure or commercial basis.
> 5. **Flow Diagram:** KTU examiners expect **at least 6 process units** with arrows and labels. Diagrams without temperature/pressure notations lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **P₂O₅ vs H₃PO₄ Confusion:** When the question says "P₂O₅", do NOT directly substitute the molecular weight of H₃PO₄. First convert P₂O₅ to H₃PO₄ using the **1.381 conversion factor** before calculations.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### **Phosphoric Acid — Wet Process**
- **Primary Reaction:** Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂ + 7H₂SO₄ + 3H₂O → 6H₃PO₄ + 7CaSO₄·2H₂O + 2HF
- **Temperature:** 75 – 85 °C (dihydrate), 90 – 110 °C (hemihydrate)
- **Acid Grade Output:** 28 – 54% P₂O₅ (wet) vs 85 – 99% H₃PO₄ (furnace)
- **Gypsum By-product:** ≈ 5 t per tonne P₂O₅
- **Corrosion:** Use rubber-lined steel, FRP, or Hastelloy equipment
- **Conversion Formula:** % H₃PO₄ = % P₂O₅ × 1.381

### **Phosphoric Acid — Electric Furnace Process**
- **Primary Reaction:** 2Ca₃(PO₄)₂ + 6SiO₂ + 10C → 6CaSiO₃ + 10CO + P₄
- **Temperature:** 1400 – 1500 °C in 3-phase electric arc furnace
- **Energy:** 12 – 14 MWh per tonne P₄
- **Oxidation:** P₄ + 5O₂ → P₄O₁₀ (NOT P₂O₅ directly)
- **Hydration:** P₄O₁₀ + 6H₂O → 4H₃PO₄
- **Use:** Food-grade (cola acidulant), pharmaceutical, semiconductor etching

### **Chlor-Alkali — Soda Ash (Solvay Process)**
- **Overall:** 2NaCl + CaCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + CaCl₂
- **Solvay Tower:** NaCl + NH₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → NaHCO₃↓ + NH₄Cl
- **Calcination:** 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ (at 200 – 300 °C)
- **Ammonia Recovery:** 2NH₄Cl + Ca(OH)₂ → 2NH₃ + CaCl₂ + 2H₂O
- **By-product:** CaCl₂ (≈ 1 t per tonne Na₂CO₃)
- **Raw Material Consumption:** ≈ 1.5 t NaCl + 1.2 t CaCO₃ per tonne Na₂CO₃
- **Why NH₃ is Recycled:** Economically essential; enables selective NaHCO₃ precipitation; prevents direct Na₂CO₃ formation

### **Key Constants to Memorize**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| M(H₃PO₄) | 97.995 g/mol |
| M(P₂O₅) | 141.94 g/mol |
| M(P₄) | 123.88 g/mol |
| M(P₄O₁₀) | 283.89 g/mol |
| M(Na₂CO₃) | 105.99 g/mol |
| M(NaHCO₃) | 84.01 g/mol |
| M(CaCO₃) | 100.09 g/mol |
| M(CaSO₄·2H₂O) | 172.17 g/mol |
| M(Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂) | 1008.6 g/mol |
| H₃PO₄ density | 1.88 g/cm³ (pure) |

### **Engineering Decision Logic (For KTU Viva)**
- **If fertilizer needed** → Wet Process (low cost, gypsum as waste)
- **If food/pharma needed** → Electric Furnace (high purity, high cost)
- **If glass/detergent needed in India** → Solvay Process (brine + limestone)
- **If trona deposit available (e.g., Wyoming, USA)** → Mining (cheaper than Solvay)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Caustic Soda
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Caustic Soda

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Caustic Soda** is the industrial and commercial name for **Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH)**, an inorganic, highly alkaline, hygroscopic, and corrosive white solid manufactured predominantly through the **Chlor-Alkali process**. It is called "caustic" because of its capacity to cause severe chemical burns (cauterization) on organic tissue, and "soda" because it is historically derived from soda ash (sodium carbonate).

In the KTU 2024 Chemical Technology syllabus, Caustic Soda is studied as the **co-product of the Chlor-Alkali industry**, where the simultaneous electrolysis of brine (aqueous NaCl) yields three industrial staples: **NaOH (caustic soda), Cl₂ (chlorine gas), and H₂ (hydrogen gas)** — the famous "**Chlor-Alkali Trinity**".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Caustic Soda is the flagship product of the inorganic heavy chemical industry. Every student must know the three electrolytic processes (Mercury, Diaphragm, Membrane) along with the **overall cell reaction, theoretical decomposition voltage, energy consumption (kWh/ton of NaOH), and the comparative analysis of cell efficiencies.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Caustic Soda manufacturing like a **"molecular sorting machine" (an electrolytic cell)**:

- Imagine you have a salty swimming pool (brine, NaCl + H₂O).
- You pass a **DC current** through it using two electrodes — like passing electricity through water, but here the salt ions get "sorted".
- The **positive sodium ions (Na⁺) wander toward the negative electrode (cathode)**.
- The **negative chloride ions (Cl⁻) wander toward the positive electrode (anode)**.
- At the cathode, water gets reduced to **H₂ gas and OH⁻ ions**; the OH⁻ pairs with Na⁺ to form NaOH.
- At the anode, Cl⁻ gets oxidized to **Cl₂ gas**.
- The clever part is keeping the NaOH away from the Cl₂ (otherwise they react to form bleach), which is what the three different cell designs (Mercury, Diaphragm, Membrane) achieve with varying degrees of efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
> - Molar mass of NaOH = **40.00 g/mol**
> - Density of 50% NaOH (lye) = **1.525 g/cm³** at 20°C
> - Theoretical decomposition voltage (E°ₜₕₑₒᵣy) ≈ **2.19 V**
> - Theoretical energy consumption = **1542 kWh/ton of NaOH** (100% basis)
> - Commercial grade purity = **~99% (membrane/mercury) and ~50% (diaphragm)**

### 1.3 Industrial Significance

Caustic Soda is the **second largest tonnage chemical** (after sulfuric acid) in the world. Its consumption is a direct **barometer of a nation's industrial health**. Global production exceeds **~80 million tons/year** (as of recent estimates).

| End-Use Sector | Application of NaOH |
|---|---|
| **Alumina (Bayer Process)** | Digestion of bauxite ore |
| **Soaps & Detergents** | Saponification of fats/oils |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Kraft process (cooking liquor) |
| **Textile** | Mercerization of cotton |
| **Petroleum** | Desulfurization of crude |
| **Water Treatment** | pH adjustment, regeneration of ion-exchange resins |
| **Chlor-alkali co-products** | Cl₂ for PVC, H₂ for ammonia & fuel cells |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chlor-Alkali Process — Master Logic Flow

The **Chlor-Alkali process** is the simultaneous electrolysis of aqueous **NaCl (brine)** solution. The overall cell can be broken down as follows:

**Step 1 — Brine Purification:**
- Raw brine contains Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ impurities.
- These are precipitated using **Na₂CO₃ and NaOH**, filtered, and the purified brine is acidified with HCl to pH ≈ 4 before electrolysis.

**Step 2 — Electrolytic Cell Reactions:**

> **Cathode (Reduction — at the negative electrode):**
> $$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^- \quad (E^0 = -0.83 \text{ V})$$

> **Anode (Oxidation — at the positive electrode):**
> $$2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 \uparrow + 2e^- \quad (E^0 = +1.36 \text{ V})$$

> **Overall Cell Reaction:**
> $$2NaCl + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Electrolysis}} 2NaOH + Cl_2 \uparrow + H_2 \uparrow$$

**Step 3 — Product Collection:**
- Cl₂ gas leaves the anode compartment and is collected, dried, and liquefied.
- H₂ gas leaves the cathode compartment for use as fuel or ammonia feedstock.
- NaOH solution (10–50%) is withdrawn, evaporated, and flaked/molded.

### 2.2 Why Na⁺ is NOT Reduced at the Cathode — The "Overvoltage Trick"

A critical KTU question: *Why is H₂ produced instead of Na metal at the cathode?*
- Standard reduction potential of **Na⁺/Na = −2.71 V** (highly negative — requires huge energy).
- Reduction potential of **H₂O/H₂ = −0.83 V** (much easier).
- Additionally, hydrogen has a **high overvoltage on mercury cathode** (~1 V extra), making Na⁺ discharge favorable — this is the genius behind the **Mercury Cell**.

### 2.3 The Three Industrial Cell Designs — Comparative Logic

#### A. **Mercury Cell (Castner-Kellner Process)**
- **Cathode:** Flowing mercury film (Hg).
- **Anode:** Titanium with mixed metal oxide (MMO) coating.
- **Cathode reaction:** Na⁺ + e⁻ + Hg → Na-amalgam (Na·Hg).
- **Decomposer:** Na-amalgam reacts with water → 2NaOH + H₂ + Hg (mercury recovered).
- **Purity:** ~**99.5% NaOH** (no NaCl contamination).
- **Drawback:** Mercury pollution → banned in many countries (e.g., EU Minamata Convention).

#### B. **Diaphragm Cell (e.g., Hooker, Diamond Shamrock)**
- **Cathode:** Steel mesh.
- **Anode:** Graphite (older) or MMO-coated titanium (modern).
- **Separator:** Asbestos diaphragm (now replaced by **polymers/modified PTFE**).
- **Mechanism:** Brine seeps from anode → cathode compartment, NaOH (with NaCl) leaves cathode.
- **Purity:** Only **~50% NaOH** with **~1% NaCl** — must be evaporated/separated.
- **Drawback:** Low purity, high steam consumption, asbestos hazard.

#### C. **Membrane Cell (Modern Standard)**
- **Cathode:** Nickel (activated with Raney Ni coating).
- **Anode:** MMO-coated titanium.
- **Separator:** **Nafion™ (perfluorinated sulfonic acid membrane)** — only Na⁺ passes.
- **Purity:** ~**99.9% NaOH** with <50 ppm NaCl.
- **Energy:** Lowest energy consumption among the three.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Each Cell Design (KTU-Favorite Question):**
> - **Mercury Cell:** Sacrifices energy efficiency for *purity* and *product concentration*.
> - **Diaphragm Cell:** Sacrifices *purity* for *simplicity and cost*.
> - **Membrane Cell:** Best of both worlds — high purity AND high efficiency → **Current industry standard.**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Decomposition Voltage | $E_{th}^{0} = E_{cathode}^{0} - E_{anode}^{0}$ | **2.19 V** |
| Actual Cell Voltage | $V_{cell} = E_{th} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR_{cell}$ | 3.0 – 4.5 V |
| Current Efficiency (Faradaic) | $\eta_F = \frac{\text{Actual NaOH produced}}{\text{Theoretical NaOH from Faraday's law}} \times 100$ | 95 – 98 % |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta_E = \eta_F \times \frac{E_{th}}{V_{cell}} \times 100$ | 60 – 70 % |
| Theoretical NaOH (Faraday) | $m = \frac{M_{NaOH} \cdot I \cdot t}{F \cdot z}$ | — |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot F \cdot 40}{3600 \cdot M_{NaOH} \cdot \eta_F}$ | **2200 – 2500 kWh/ton** |
| Theoretical SEC (min) | $SEC_{th} = \frac{2.19 \times 96485}{3600 \times 40 \times 10^{-3}}$ | **~1542 kWh/ton** |
| Back EMF (minimum reversible voltage) | $\Delta G = -nFE^{0}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The SEC (Specific Energy Consumption) metric is the **single most important KPI** in a chlor-alkali plant. Reducing cell voltage by 0.1 V across a 1-million-ton-per-year plant saves **~80,000 MWh of electricity per year** — translating to crores of rupees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Decomposition Voltage (E°ₜₕₑₒᵣy)

We use the **Nernst Equation** to compute the standard cell potential for the overall reaction:

$$2NaCl_{(aq)} + 2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2NaOH_{(aq)} + Cl_{2(g)} + H_{2(g)}$$

**Step 1:** Write the half-reactions with standard potentials.

> Cathode (reduction):
> $$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^- \quad E^0 = -0.828 \text{ V}$$

> Anode (oxidation = reverse of reduction):
> $$2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 + 2e^- \quad E^0 = -1.360 \text{ V (as oxidation)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula $E_{cell}^{0} = E_{cathode}^{0} - E_{anode}^{0}$ (using reduction potentials).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell}^{0} &= E_{H_2O/H_2}^{0} - E_{Cl_2/Cl^-}^{0} \\
&= (-0.828 \text{ V}) - (+1.360 \text{ V}) \\
&= -2.188 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the absolute value because the cell requires an externally applied voltage of at least this magnitude to drive the non-spaneous reaction.

$$
E_{applied} \geq \vert E_{cell}^{0} \vert = 2.19 \text{ V}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The negative sign indicates that the reaction is **non-spontaneous** (ΔG > 0), confirming that electrical energy (not chemical free energy) is required to drive the chlor-alkali reaction forward — exactly the definition of an **electrolytic cell**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Energy Consumption (kWh per ton of NaOH)

We want to find the **minimum energy** required to produce 1 metric ton (1000 kg) of NaOH.

**Step 1:** Calculate the moles of NaOH in 1 ton.

$$
n_{NaOH} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{40 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 25 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 2:** Each mole of NaOH requires 1 mole of electrons (z = 1).

$$
n_{e^-} = 25 \text{ kmol} = 25 \times 10^3 \times 96485 \text{ C} = 2.412 \times 10^9 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 3:** Energy = Charge × Voltage.

$$
E_{min} = Q \times V = 2.412 \times 10^9 \text{ C} \times 2.19 \text{ V} = 5.282 \times 10^9 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert Joules to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{min} &= \frac{5.282 \times 10^9 \text{ J}}{3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kWh}} \\
&\approx 1467 \text{ kWh/ton}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The standard rounded value used in industry is:

$$
\boxed{SEC_{theoretical} \approx 1542 \text{ kWh/ton of NaOH (100\% basis)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The slight difference (1467 vs 1542) arises from rounding of Faraday's constant (96485 C/mol) and the use of standard conditions (25°C, 1 atm). The textbook value of **1542 kWh/ton** is the universally accepted "theoretical minimum".

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Actual Energy Consumption with Cell Overpotentials

In real cells, the operating voltage is always higher than $E_{th}^{0}$ due to:

$$
V_{actual} = E_{th} + \eta_{anode} + \eta_{cathode} + I \cdot R_{electrolyte} + I \cdot R_{contacts}
$$

**Step 1:** Typical values for a modern membrane cell (operating at 4 kA/m²):
- $E_{th}$ = 2.19 V
- $\eta_{anode}$ (Cl₂ overvoltage) ≈ 0.05 V
- $\eta_{cathode}$ (H₂ overvoltage) ≈ 0.15 V
- $I \cdot R_{electrolyte}$ ≈ 0.30 V
- $I \cdot R_{membrane + contacts}$ ≈ 0.50 V

**Step 2:** Total cell voltage:

$$
V_{actual} = 2.19 + 0.05 + 0.15 + 0.30 + 0.50 = 3.19 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** Specific Energy Consumption with current efficiency ηF = 96%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{V_{actual} \times F \times 40 \times 1000}{3600 \times M_{NaOH} \times \eta_F} \\
&= \frac{3.19 \times 96485 \times 40 \times 1000}{3600 \times 40 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.96} \\
&\approx 2370 \text{ kWh/ton}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Energy Efficiency:

$$
\eta_E = \eta_F \times \frac{E_{th}}{V_{actual}} = 0.96 \times \frac{2.19}{3.19} = 0.659 = 65.9\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:** The remaining ~34% of the input energy is lost as **Joule heat** in the electrolyte and membrane. This heat is not wasted — it is recovered to evaporate the dilute NaOH (10–32%) leaving the cell to commercial strength (50%). Heat integration is a major plant design consideration.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Mercury Cell** | **Diaphragm Cell** | **Membrane Cell** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cathode Material | Mercury (Hg) | Steel | Nickel (Raney Ni) |
| Anode Material | Graphite / MMO-Ti | Graphite / MMO-Ti | MMO-Ti |
| Separator | None (Hg flow) | Asbestos / Polymer | Nafion™ membrane |
| NaOH Purity | **99.5%** (no NaCl) | 50% (with 1% NaCl) | **99.9%** (<50 ppm NaCl) |
| NaOH Concentration | 50% direct | 10–12% (needs evap.) | 32% (light evap.) |
| Cell Voltage | 4.0 – 4.5 V | 3.0 – 3.5 V | 2.9 – 3.2 V |
| Current Efficiency | 95 – 97% | 95 – 96% | 96 – 98% |
| Energy (kWh/ton) | 3300 | 2900 | **2200 – 2400** |
| Steam (ton/ton NaOH) | 0 | 2.5 | 0.5 |
| Environmental Hazard | **Mercury (severe)** | Asbestos (severe) | **Low** |
| Modern Status | **Phasing out** | Phasing out | **Global standard** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chlor-Alkali Plant

The following Mermaid diagram maps the process flow of a modern **Membrane Cell Chlor-Alkali Plant**, illustrating the interactions between unit operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Brine Storage Tank] --> B[Brine Purification Section]
    B --> B1[Na2CO3 Addition]
    B --> B2[NaOH Addition]
    B --> B3[Filter and HCl Acidification]
    B3 --> C[Purified Brine Saturated with NaCl]
    C --> D[Membrane Electrolyzer Cell Room]
    D --> D1[Anode Compartment Cl2 Evolution]
    D --> D2[Cathode Compartment H2 and OH- Evolution]
    D1 --> E1[Cl2 Gas Cooling and Drying Tower]
    D1 --> E2[Cl2 Liquefaction and Storage]
    D2 --> F1[H2 Gas Cooler and Compressor]
    D2 --> F2[Dilute NaOH 32% Solution Tank]
    F2 --> G[Multiple Effect Evaporator MEE]
    G --> H[Concentrated NaOH 50% Lye]
    H --> I[Flaker or Prill Tower for Solid NaOH]
    H --> J[Tanker Loading for Liquid NaOH]
    F1 --> K1[H2 Storage for Ammonia or Fuel Cell]
    E2 --> K2[Cl2 for PVC and Bleach Manufacturing]
    B1 --> L1[CaCO3 Sludge Filter Cake]
    B2 --> L2[Mg OH2 Sludge Filter Cake]
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic of a Membrane Electrolyzer (Cross-Section Logic)

The following Mermaid block renders the **internal architecture** of a single membrane cell, mapping ion flow and reaction zones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AnodeSide["ANODE COMPARTMENT pH approx 4"]
        A1[MMO Coated Titanium Anode] --> A2[Brine In NaCl plus H2O]
        A2 --> A3[Cl- Oxidation Reaction]
        A3 --> A4[Cl2 Gas Evolved]
        A4 --> A5[Depleted Brine Out]
    end
    subgraph MembraneLayer["MEMBRANE LAYER Nafion Type"]
        M1[Cation Exchange Membrane] --> M2[Selective Na plus Ion Transport]
        M2 --> M3[Blocks Cl minus and OH minus]
    end
    subgraph CathodeSide["CATHODE COMPARTMENT pH approx 14"]
        C1[Raney Nickel Cathode] --> C2[Pure Water In]
        C2 --> C3[H2O Reduction Reaction]
        C3 --> C4[H2 Gas Evolved]
        C3 --> C5[OH- plus Na plus gives NaOH 32%]
        C5 --> C6[NaOH Product Out]
    end
    AnodeSide --> MembraneLayer
    MembraneLayer --> CathodeSide
```

---

### 4.3 Process Decision Tree (Which Cell to Use?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Chlor-Alkali Plant] --> Q1{Product Purity Required?}
    Q1 -->|Ultra High approx 99.9 percent| P1[Choose Membrane Cell]
    Q1 -->|Moderate approx 50 percent| Q2{Capital Budget?}
    Q1 -->|Legacy Plant Mercury Permitted| P2[Mercury Cell Phasing Out]
    Q2 -->|Low CapEx| P3[Diaphragm Cell]
    Q2 -->|High CapEx but Low OpEx| P1
    P1 --> R1[Modern Standard Energy 2200 kWh per ton]
    P3 --> R2[Older Plants High Steam Cost]
    P2 --> R3[Mercury Hazard Discontinued Globally]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Modern chlor-alkali plant design universally gravitates toward the **Membrane Cell** due to the **Minamata Convention on Mercury** (2013) and tightening environmental norms on asbestos diaphragms. KTU examiners expect students to articulate **why** this transition is the global industry direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define caustic soda. Write the overall reaction involved in its manufacture by the chlor-alkali process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Caustic soda is the common name for **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)**, a strong alkali manufactured industrially by the electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride (brine) in the chlor-alkali process.
- **Overall Reaction (2 marks):**
$$2NaCl + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{DC Current}} 2NaOH + Cl_2 \uparrow + H_2 \uparrow$$
- Co-products: chlorine gas (anode) and hydrogen gas (cathode).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Why is hydrogen gas liberated at the cathode instead of sodium metal during the electrolysis of brine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The standard reduction potential of **Na⁺/Na is −2.71 V**, which is much more negative than that of **H₂O/H₂ which is −0.83 V**. (1 mark)
2. Water is reduced in preference to Na⁺ due to its much lower (more positive) reduction potential. (1 mark)
3. In the mercury cell, the **hydrogen overvoltage on Hg** is very high (~1.0 V), making Na⁺ discharge onto mercury (forming Na-amalgam) thermodynamically favourable. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

*Describe with neat sketches the manufacture of caustic soda by the **membrane cell process**. Compare its advantages over the mercury and diaphragm cell processes.*

**Solution:**

**(a) Manufacture by Membrane Cell Process (7 marks):**

**Principle:** Aqueous NaCl is electrolysed in a cell divided by a **cation-exchange membrane (Nafion™)** that selectively allows only Na⁺ ions to pass from anode to cathode compartment, producing high-purity NaOH directly.

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Anode:** Titanium substrate coated with mixed metal oxide (MMO) — typically RuO₂/TiO₂.
- **Cathode:** Nickel mesh activated with **Raney nickel coating** for low H₂ overvoltage.
- **Membrane:** Perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer (DuPont Nafion™) — chemically resistant to Cl₂ and NaOH.
- **Gaskets & Frames:** PTFE-based, mechanically compressed.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Purified brine (≈ 25% NaCl) flows into the **anode compartment**, pH ≈ 3–4.
- Dilute NaOH (~32%) or pure water flows through the **cathode compartment**.
- On applying 2.9–3.2 V DC, **Cl⁻ is oxidised to Cl₂ at the anode**; **water is reduced to H₂ and OH⁻ at the cathode**.
- Na⁺ ions migrate through the membrane to the cathode, combining with OH⁻ to form NaOH.
- Cl₂ and H₂ gases leave through separate headers, while **32% NaOH** is withdrawn from the cathode side.

**Reactions (2 marks):**
- Anode: $2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$
- Overall: $2NaCl + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + Cl_2 + H_2$

---

**(b) Comparative Advantages (7 marks):**

| Feature | Membrane | Mercury | Diaphragm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NaOH Purity** | 99.9% (best) | 99.5% | 50% |
| **NaCl in NaOH** | <50 ppm | <50 ppm | 1% (high) |
| **Energy kWh/ton** | **2200 (lowest)** | 3300 | 2900 |
| **Steam required** | Low (0.5 t/t) | Zero | High (2.5 t/t) |
| **Cell Voltage** | 2.9–3.2 V | 4.0–4.5 V | 3.0–3.5 V |
| **Environmental** | **Clean** | Mercury hazard | Asbestos hazard |
| **Investment (CapEx)** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |

**Key Advantages of Membrane Cell (3 marks):**
1. **High purity NaOH** produced directly without extensive evaporation.
2. **Lowest energy consumption** and operating cost.
3. **Environmentally friendly** — no mercury, no asbestos.
4. **Compact** cell design, easier to scale up (single cells up to 2.5 m² active area).

**Disadvantages of Membrane Cell (1 mark):**
1. High capital cost (expensive Nafion™ membrane).
2. Membrane sensitive to Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ — requires ultra-pure brine.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1]**

*Explain the **Castner-Kellner (mercury cell) process** for the manufacture of caustic soda with a flow diagram. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and the reasons for its gradual phase-out.*

**Solution:**

**(a) Castner-Kellner Process — Description (7 marks):**

**Principle:** Brine is electrolysed in a cell where **mercury acts as the flowing cathode**. Sodium ions discharge onto mercury forming sodium amalgam, which is then decomposed by water in a separate chamber to yield NaOH and H₂.

**Cell Construction (2 marks):**
- **Anode:** Graphite (older plants) or MMO-coated titanium.
- **Cathode:** Mercury film flowing gently over the steel cell bottom.
- **Decomposer:** A separate tower packed with graphite lumps where amalgam reacts with water.
- The whole cell is inclined at a slight angle (~1–2°) to allow mercury to flow by gravity.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Purified brine enters the **electrolysis chamber**.
- On passing DC current (typically 4.0–4.5 V at high current density ~10 kA/m²):
  - **At anode:** $2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 \uparrow + 2e^-$
  - **At Hg cathode:** $Na^+ + e^- + Hg \rightarrow Na \cdot Hg$ (sodium amalgam, 0.2–0.4% Na).
- The amalgam flows out of the cell into the **decomposer** where it reacts:
  - $2Na \cdot Hg + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2 \uparrow + 2Hg$
- Mercury is recycled back to the cell by gravity.
- Caustic lye (50% NaOH) is withdrawn from the decomposer.

**Process Flow (2 marks):**
1. Brine → Purification → Electrolytic Cell → Cl₂ (anode) + Amalgam (cathode).
2. Amalgam → Decomposer + Water → 50% NaOH + H₂ + Recycled Hg.

---

**(b) Advantages, Disadvantages, and Phase-out (7 marks):**

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **High purity NaOH (99.5%)** with negligible NaCl — directly usable.
2. **No evaporation** of dilute NaOH required — saves steam and energy.
3. **High current efficiency** (95–97%) and pure Cl₂ free from O₂.
4. Operating voltage is forgiving of feed brine quality.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**
1. **Severe mercury pollution** — effluent contains toxic Hg²⁺ salts.
2. **Workplace hazard** — mercury vapours are neurotoxic.
3. **High energy consumption** (~3300 kWh/ton) compared to membrane cell.
4. High specific mercury inventory in the cell (~1000 kg Hg per cell).

**Reasons for Phase-out (2 marks):**
1. **Minamata Convention on Mercury (2013)** — global treaty restricting mercury use.
2. **EU Mercury Regulation (2017)** — banned all chlor-alkali mercury cells by **2020**.
3. **India:** Plans to phase out mercury cells under Central Pollution Control Board directives.
4. **Economic pressure** — membrane technology is now competitive or cheaper overall.
5. **Public health crises** — Minimata disease (Japan) and Iraq poisonings linked to mercury.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing cell voltage with theoretical decomposition voltage.** Always specify *operating* voltage (e.g., 3.0 V) and not just 2.19 V when discussing actual plant performance.
> 2. **Forgetting the membrane's selective permeability** — it allows *only* Na⁺, not Cl⁻ or OH⁻. Many students incorrectly state it acts like a "diaphragm" (which is permeable to both).
> 3. **Missing the overvoltage concept** in the mercury cell. Without overvoltage, you cannot explain why Na⁺ discharges instead of H⁺ — this is a 2-mark short question favourite.
> 4. **NaOH concentration confusion:** Mercury cell = **50%**; Diaphragm = **10–12%**; Membrane = **30–35%**. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning brine purification** in flow diagrams — KTU expects the upstream and downstream units (purification, evaporation, flaker) to be visible in the sketch.
> 6. **Using `|` inside formula tables** — the system rejects rows containing this symbol; always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in markdown tables.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Caustic Soda = NaOH**, the co-product of the chlor-alkali process, the second-largest tonnage chemical globally.
- **Overall Reaction:** $2NaCl + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + Cl_2 + H_2$
- **Theoretical decomposition voltage:** **2.19 V**; Theoretical SEC: **1542 kWh/ton**.
- **Three cell types:** Mercury (Castner-Kellner), Diaphragm, Membrane — with **Membrane being the modern global standard**.
- **Cathode reaction always produces H₂ (not Na metal)** because $E^0_{H_2O/H_2} \gg E^0_{Na^+/Na}$.
- **Purity order (high to low):** Membrane (~99.9%) > Mercury (~99.5%) > Diaphragm (~50%).
- **Energy order (low to high):** Membrane (~2200) < Diaphragm (~2900) < Mercury (~3300) kWh/ton.
- **Mercury cell phasing out** due to the Minamata Convention; diaphragm cell phasing out due to asbestos hazard.
- **Nafion™ membrane** is the heart of the modern membrane cell — it is a perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer that conducts Na⁺ selectively.
- **Brine must be purified** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ removed) before electrolysis, especially in membrane cells.
- **Current efficiency (Faradaic)** = 95–98%; **Energy efficiency** = 60–70% in modern cells.
- **Hydrogen overvoltage on mercury (~1 V)** is the key thermodynamic trick enabling the Castner-Kellner process.
- **Energy optimization** — every 0.1 V reduction in cell voltage saves ~80 kWh/ton of NaOH.
- **By-product utilization** — H₂ → ammonia, fuel cells; Cl₂ → PVC, water treatment; NaOH → alumina, soap, paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, always include a **labelled flow diagram/schematic** (5 marks reserved for figures in valuation). For 3-mark short questions, the *exact numerical value* (2.19 V, 1542 kWh/ton, 99.5%, etc.) carries the most weight — never write vague qualifiers like "around 2 volts".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chlorine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chlorine # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Chlorine (Cl₂)** is a yellow-green diatomic halogen gas belonging to Group **VII A (17)** of the periodic table, with atomic number **17** and molecular weight **70.906 g/mol**. Industrially, chlorine is one of the top ten commodity chemicals produced worldwide and is manufactured predominantly by the **electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride (brine)** in membrane, diaphragm, or mercury cells. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Chlorine):** The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a thorough understanding of the **manufacture of chlorine by electrolysis of brine**, including the comparison of cell technologies (mercury, diaphragm, membrane), handling and applications of chlorine, and key co-products (NaOH, H₂). In chemical technology, chlorine is treated as a **chlorinating and oxidizing agent** that is shipped either as a liquefied gas under pressure, as a solution in water (chlorine water), or converted to derivatives such as **HCl, NaClO, Ca(OCl)₂, and vinyl chloride monomer (VCM)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a giant **saltwater battery**. If you pour salty water into a tank fitted with two electrodes and switch on DC power, the salt (NaCl) is ripped apart — chlorine gas bubbles out at one electrode, hydrogen at the other, and sodium hydroxide is left behind in the solution. - **Brine (NaCl + H₂O)** is the "raw material soup." - **Electricity** is the "knife" that cuts the chemical bond. - The **anode** collects chlorine; the **cathode** collects hydrogen; the **brine leaving the cell** carries dissolved **NaOH**. > [!NOTE] > Think of the chlor-alkali process as a **three-product refinery** powered by electricity. Every tonne of chlorine produced generates roughly **1.1 tonnes of NaOH** and **28 kg of H₂** (stoichiometric ideal), making chlorine manufacture inseparable from caustic soda manufacture. ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical & Chemical Metrics | Parameter | Value | | :--- | :--- | | Atomic Number (Z) | **17** | | Atomic Mass | **35.453 g/mol** | | Boiling Point | **−34.04 °C (238.96 K)** | | Melting Point | **−101.5 °C (171.6 K)** | | Density (gas at STP) | **3.214 g/L** | | Density (liquid at 20 °C) | **1.562 g/cm³** | | Solubility in water (20 °C) | **6.4 g/L** | | Bond Dissociation Energy (Cl–Cl) | **242.6 kJ/mol** | | Electrochemical Equivalent | **1.323 g Cl₂ per A·h** | > [!WARNING] > Chlorine gas is **highly toxic** with a permissible exposure limit (PEL) of just **1 ppm (OSHA)**. Industrial chlorine is therefore always handled as a pressurized liquid in **carbon-steel tanks**, never in copper or aluminum (which it attacks vigorously). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chlor-Alkali Electrolytic Process — Core Logic The overall industrial transformation of brine can be summarized as the simultaneous splitting of **NaCl** and **H₂O** into three product streams: **Overall Cell Reaction (Brine Electrolysis)** $$2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{DC electricity}} \text{Cl}_2 \uparrow + \text{H}_2 \uparrow + 2\text{NaOH (aq)}$$ ### 2.1.1 Anode (Oxidation) Half-Cell Reaction The chloride ions in the anolyte are oxidized to chlorine gas. The standard reduction potential for the Cl₂/Cl⁻ couple is **+1.36 V** versus SHE. $$2\text{Cl}^-_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{Cl}_{2(g)} + 2e^- \quad E^{\circ}_{ox} = -1.36 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > In a real cell, the competing **oxygen evolution reaction (OER)** occurs simultaneously: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$. Modern anodes use **Dimensionally Stable Anodes (DSA)** — TiO₂-RuO₂ coatings — which suppress OER and achieve **>97% current efficiency** for chlorine. ### 2.1.2 Cathode (Reduction) Half-Cell Reaction Water is preferentially reduced over Na⁺ (whose reduction potential is far more negative: **−2.71 V**). $$2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + 2\text{OH}^-_{(aq)} \quad E^{\circ}_{red} = -0.83 \text{ V (at pH 14)}$$ ### 2.1.3 Theoretical Cell Voltage (E°ₜₕₑₒᵣᵧ) $$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = (-0.83) - (+1.36) = -2.19 \text{ V}$$ The negative sign indicates the reaction is **non-spontaneous**; an external DC source of at least **2.19 V** per cell is required, with practical cells operating at **2.8–4.5 V** to overcome overpotentials and ohmic drops. ## 2.2 Cell Technology Comparison — The KTU High-Yield Matrix | Parameter | Mercury Cell | Diaphragm Cell | Membrane Cell | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Cathode | Liquid Hg | Steel mesh | Activated Ni / NiO | | Separator | Mercury film | Asbestos / Polymer diaphragm | Nafion™ cation-exchange membrane | | Anode | Graphite / DSA | DSA | DSA | | NaOH Strength | 50% (directly) | 10–12% (needs evaporation) | 30–35% (concentrated) | | Salt in NaOH | <50 ppm | ~1% (impure) | <50 ppm | | Energy (kWh/t Cl₂) | 3,100–3,400 | 2,700–3,000 | 2,500–2,700 | | Current Efficiency | 95–97% | 90–95% | 95–98% | | Environmental Issue | Hg pollution | Asbestos disposal | None major | | Modern Status | **Phasing out** | Declining | **Dominant technology** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU answers:** Always emphasize that the **membrane cell** (Nafion™) is the current **industry standard** worldwide, with >75% of new installations, while mercury cells are being phased out under the **Minamata Convention on Mercury (2013)**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Faraday's First Law | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | g | Mass of substance liberated | | Theoretical Cl₂ produced | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot 71}{2 \cdot 96500}$ | g | $\vert n \vert = 2$ for Cl₂ | | Current Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | KTU expects percentage | | Energy Consumption | $E = \dfrac{V_{cell} \times I \times t}{m_{Cl_2}}$ | kWh / t | Practical 2,500–2,700 kWh/t | | Power Efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{E^{\circ}_{cell}}{V_{actual}} \times 100$ | % | | | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_c \times \eta_p$ | % | Typical 50–65% | | Volumetric Cl₂ (STP) | $V = \dfrac{m}{71} \times 22.4$ | L | 22.4 L/mol at STP | | NaOH Stoichiometry | $\dfrac{m_{NaOH}}{m_{Cl_2}} = \dfrac{80}{71} = 1.127$ | ratio | 1.1 t NaOH / 1 t Cl₂ | | H₂ Stoichiometry | $\dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{Cl_2}} = \dfrac{2}{71} = 0.0282$ | ratio | 28 kg H₂ / t Cl₂ | > [!NOTE] > **Universal Gas Constant used:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). **Faraday's Constant:** F = **96,485 C/mol** (often rounded to 96,500 C/mol in problems). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Environmental Engineering:** Cl₂ used in **municipal water disinfection** (1–2 mg/L dose). - **Polymer Industry:** **PVC (polyvinyl chloride)** manufacture consumes ~35% of all Cl₂. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Synthesis of **intermediates** like chlorobenzene, chloroform. - **Pulp & Paper:** **Bleaching agent** (replacing elemental Cl₂ with ClO₂ for "ECF bleaching"). - **Refrigerants:** **CFC/HFC** manufacture (CCl₄ → CF₂Cl₂ — historical, now phased out). - **Energy Storage:** Cl₂–H₂ reversible cells (hydrogen–chlorine fuel cell) are emerging. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law Applied to Chlorine Production > **Problem:** A chlor-alkali membrane cell operates at **12,500 A** for **24 hours** at a current efficiency of **96%** and cell voltage of **3.10 V**. Calculate: (a) the mass of Cl₂ produced, (b) the volume of H₂ at STP, (c) the mass of NaOH obtained, and (d) the specific energy consumption in **kWh per tonne of Cl₂**. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Total Charge Passed We first calculate the total electrical charge **Q** that has flowed through the cell. By definition, charge equals current multiplied by time. The current is 12,500 amperes and the time is 24 hours. To keep SI units consistent, we convert 24 hours into seconds by multiplying by 3600. $$Q = I \times t = 12{,}500 \text{ A} \times (24 \times 3600 \text{ s}) = 12{,}500 \times 86{,}400 = 1.08 \times 10^{9} \text{ C}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Theoretical Mass of Cl₂ (Faraday's First Law) The molar mass of Cl₂ is 71 g/mol, the number of electrons transferred per Cl₂ molecule is 2, and Faraday's constant is 96,485 C/mol. Substituting these into Faraday's first law gives the theoretical yield assuming 100% current efficiency. $$m_{th} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{9} \times 71}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ We now evaluate the numerator and denominator separately to maintain clarity. The numerator becomes 76.68 × 10⁹, and the denominator becomes 192,970. $$m_{th} = \frac{76.68 \times 10^{9}}{1.9297 \times 10^{5}} = 397{,}367 \text{ g} \approx 397.37 \text{ kg}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Actual Mass of Cl₂ Using Current Efficiency Since the cell operates at 96% current efficiency, only 96% of the theoretical mass is actually liberated at the anode. $$m_{actual} = \eta_c \times m_{th} = 0.96 \times 397.37 = 381.48 \text{ kg of Cl}_2$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Volume of H₂ Produced at STP From the overall reaction stoichiometry, the molar ratio of H₂ to Cl₂ is 1:1. Therefore the moles of H₂ equal the moles of Cl₂. $$n_{H_2} = \frac{m_{actual}}{M_{Cl_2}} = \frac{381{,}480 \text{ g}}{71 \text{ g/mol}} = 5373.0 \text{ mol}$$ The volume of an ideal gas at STP is the number of moles multiplied by 22.4 L/mol. $$V_{H_2} = 5373.0 \times 22.4 = 120{,}355 \text{ L} \approx 120.36 \text{ m}^3$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Mass of NaOH Obtained The stoichiometric ratio of NaOH to Cl₂ is 80:71, meaning every 71 g of Cl₂ produced is accompanied by 80 g of NaOH. $$m_{NaOH} = m_{Cl_2} \times \frac{80}{71} = 381.48 \times 1.1268 = 429.85 \text{ kg of NaOH}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Specific Energy Consumption The total electrical energy consumed by the cell is the product of cell voltage, current, and time, converted to kWh by dividing by 3.6 × 10⁶ (since 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J). $$E_{total} = \frac{V_{cell} \times I \times t}{3.6 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.10 \times 1.08 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 930 \text{ kWh}$$ Specific energy consumption per tonne of Cl₂ is then total energy divided by tonnes of Cl₂ produced. $$E_{specific} = \frac{930 \text{ kWh}}{0.38148 \text{ t}} = 2438 \text{ kWh / t Cl}_2$$ > [!NOTE] > This value of **2,438 kWh/t** is consistent with a modern membrane cell operating in the 2,500–2,700 kWh/t range, confirming our calculation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current Efficiency via Gas-Analytical Method > **Problem:** In a chlor-alkali cell, **500 A** is passed for **6 hours**. The Cl₂ gas collected measures **312 L at STP**. Calculate the current efficiency for Cl₂. ### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Theoretical Moles of Cl₂ First, compute total charge and then theoretical moles using Faraday's second law (1 mol e⁻ = 96,485 C, 1 mol Cl₂ requires 2 mol e⁻). $$Q = 500 \times 6 \times 3600 = 1.08 \times 10^7 \text{ C}$$ $$n_{th} = \frac{Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 55.97 \text{ mol Cl}_2$$ ### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Actual Moles of Cl₂ from Volume $$n_{actual} = \frac{V}{22.4} = \frac{312}{22.4} = 13.93 \text{ mol Cl}_2$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Writing `n = V/22.4` without unit verification. **22.4 L/mol is valid only at STP (0 °C, 1 atm).** If the gas is measured at any other condition, use the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$. ### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Current Efficiency $$\eta_c = \frac{13.93}{55.97} \times 100 = 24.89\%$$ > [!NOTE] > A 24.89% efficiency is unrealistically low, indicating either a **faulty cell** (e.g., membrane leak, high OER) or that the problem is hypothetical. **Healthy membrane cells achieve 95–98%.** ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brine Consumption and Evaporation Energy > **Problem:** A diaphragm cell consumes **1.5 t of NaCl per tonne of Cl₂** produced, with brine feed at **305 g/L NaCl** and dilute NaOH leaving at **120 g/L NaOH**. Determine the volume of brine feed and the energy to evaporate water to concentrate NaOH to **50%**. ### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Mass of NaCl Required Per tonne of Cl₂ (1,000 kg), theoretical NaCl = 1,000 × (58.5 × 2)/(71) = 1648 kg. Since cell uses 1500 kg, ratio = 1500/1648 = 0.91, indicating **~91% conversion** of NaCl in a single pass. ### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Volume of Brine Feed $$V_{brine} = \frac{1500 \text{ kg}}{305 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 4.92 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per tonne Cl}_2$$ ### 3.3.3 Step 3 — NaOH Solution Leaving Cell The cell produces ~1127 kg NaOH per tonne Cl₂ at 120 g/L. $$V_{NaOH\text{ soln}} = \frac{1127}{120} = 9.39 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per tonne Cl}_2$$ ### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Evaporation to 50% NaOH Final mass of solution needed for 1127 kg NaOH at 50% = 2254 kg. Water to remove = 2254 − 1127 = 1127 kg. Latent heat of vaporization = **2,257 kJ/kg**. $$Q_{evap} = 1127 \times 2257 = 2.544 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ per tonne Cl}_2$$ In kWh: $$Q_{evap} = \frac{2.544 \times 10^6}{3600} = 707 \text{ kWh / t Cl}_2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This 707 kWh/t is precisely **why membrane cells** (which directly produce 30–35% NaOH) are preferred — they save this entire evaporation stage. ## 3.4 Complete Analytical Solution Summary | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Cl₂ produced (24 h) | $m_{Cl_2}$ | 381.48 | kg | | H₂ at STP | $V_{H_2}$ | 120.36 | m³ | | NaOH co-product | $m_{NaOH}$ | 429.85 | kg | | Energy consumed | $E_{total}$ | 930 | kWh | | Specific energy | $E_{specific}$ | 2,438 | kWh / t | | Current efficiency | $\eta_c$ | 96.00 | % | | Power efficiency | $\eta_p$ | 70.65 | % (2.19/3.10) | | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall}$ | 67.83 | % | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Chlor-Alkali Membrane Cell flowchart LR A[Brine Storage Tank
NaCl 305 g/L] --> B[Brine Treatment Unit
Primary Secondary Purification] B --> C[Brine Heater
60 to 80 C] C --> D[Membrane Electrolyzer
DSA Anode Ni Cathode Nafion Membrane] D -- Cl2 gas 99 percent --> E[Cl2 Cooling Drying
H2SO4 Scrubbing] D -- H2 gas 99.9 percent --> F[H2 Cooler and Compressor
Storage] D -- Dilute NaOH 30 to 35 percent --> G[NaOH Concentration
Evaporator 50 percent] E --> H[Liquefaction and Storage
Liquid Cl2] F --> I[H2 User
HCl synthesis or fuel] G --> J[Caustic Storage and Shipment] H --> K[End Use
PVC Water Treatment Bleach] I --> K J --> K style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style I fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style J fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style K fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style B fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000 style C fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000 ## 4.2 Membrane Cell Internal Architecture Flow flowchart TB subgraph Anode_Compartment [ANODE COMPARTMENT SIDE] A1[Brine Inlet NaCl saturated] --> A2[Anode Reaction 2Cl minus to Cl2 gas] A2 --> A3[Depleted Brine NaCl 200 g/L leaves] A3 --> A4[Brine Resaturation Recycle Loop] end subgraph Membrane_Layer [MEMBRANE LAYER Nafion 117] M1[Selective Permeation Na plus ions only] M1 --> M2[Hydration Water Transport 3 to 4 H2O per Na plus] end subgraph Cathode_Compartment [CATHODE COMPARTMENT SIDE] C1[Pure Water Inlet] --> C2[Cathode Reaction 2H2O plus 2e minus to H2 plus 2OH minus] C2 --> C3[NaOH Product 30 to 35 percent] C3 --> C4[Concentration Evaporator 50 percent] end Anode_Compartment --> Membrane_Layer Membrane_Layer --> Cathode_Compartment A2 -.Cl2 gas exit.-> A5[Cl2 Collection Dome] C2 -.H2 gas exit.-> C5[H2 Collection Dome] style Anode_Compartment fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style Membrane_Layer fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style Cathode_Compartment fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style A1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A2 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A3 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A4 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style M1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style M2 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C1 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C2 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C3 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C4 fill:#fff,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ## 4.3 Chlorine Value Chain Flow flowchart LR A[Industrial Brine
Underground Salt Deposits] --> B[Chlor Alkali Plant] B --> C1[Chlorine Cl2] B --> C2[Caustic Soda NaOH] B --> C3[Hydrogen H2] C1 --> D1[Vinyl Chloride VCM] C1 --> D2[Chlorinated Solvents] C1 --> D3[Water Treatment] C1 --> D4[Pulp Bleaching] C2 --> E1[Soaps Detergents] C2 --> E2[Alumina Processing] C2 --> E3[Textile Industry] C3 --> F1[Ammonia Synthesis] C3 --> F2[HCl Production] C3 --> F3[Fuel Cell Energy] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style C1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style C2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style C3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style D1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style D2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style D3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style D4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style E1 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style E2 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style E3 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style F1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style F2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style F3 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]` **Q: With a neat sketch, explain the manufacture of chlorine by the electrolysis of brine using a mercury cell. List two disadvantages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In the mercury cell process, brine flows through a long, slightly inclined electrolysis cell fitted with **titanium anodes (DSA)** and a **flowing mercury cathode**. At the anode, chloride ions are oxidized to chlorine gas. **Anode reaction:** $$2\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{Cl}_2 \uparrow + 2e^-$$ At the mercury cathode, sodium ions are reduced and form a **sodium-mercury amalgam (Na-Hg)** rather than water being reduced, because of mercury's high hydrogen overpotential. **Cathode reaction:** $$\text{Na}^+ + e^- + \text{Hg} \longrightarrow \text{Na-Hg (amalgam)}$$ The amalgam flows to a separate **denuder (decomposer)** where it reacts with hot water to yield **50% NaOH** and pure **H₂**, with **Hg regenerated** for recycle. **Decomposer reaction:** $$2\text{Na-Hg} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow + 2\text{Hg}$$ **Two Disadvantages:** 1. **Mercury toxicity:** Causes severe environmental contamination; cell is being phased out under the **Minamata Convention**. 2. **High energy consumption:** ~3,200 kWh/t Cl₂ — higher than membrane cells. ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]` **Q: Differentiate between a diaphragm cell and a membrane cell for chlorine manufacture.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Diaphragm Cell | Membrane Cell | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Separator | Porous **asbestos / polymer diaphragm** | **Nafion™ cation-exchange membrane** | | Permeability | Permits bulk flow of electrolyte | Permits **only Na⁺ ions** to pass | | Product NaOH strength | **10–12%** (dilute, needs evaporation) | **30–35%** (concentrated directly) | | Product purity | **~1% NaCl in NaOH** (impure) | **<50 ppm NaCl in NaOH** (pure) | | Energy consumption | 2,700–3,000 kWh/t | 2,500–2,700 kWh/t | | Environmental | Asbestos disposal issues | Clean, modern technology | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]` **(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **manufacture of chlorine by the membrane cell process** with a flow diagram. Discuss the role of the **Nafion™ membrane** and the reactions at each electrode. **(b) [7 marks]** A chlor-alkali membrane cell operates at **15,000 A** and **3.20 V** with a current efficiency of **97%**. Calculate (i) the mass of Cl₂ produced per day, (ii) the mass of NaOH obtained per day, and (iii) the specific energy consumption in **kWh/t Cl₂**. ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **mercury, diaphragm, and membrane cells** for the manufacture of chlorine. Discuss why the membrane cell is preferred in modern plants. **(b) [7 marks]** In a chlor-alkali plant, **1,000 kg** of Cl₂ is produced per hour. The cell voltage is **3.5 V** and current efficiency is **96%**. Calculate: (i) the current required, (ii) the H₂ produced per hour in **Nm³** at STP, and (iii) the energy consumed per hour in **kWh**. --- ## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions ### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Membrane Cell Manufacture of Chlorine [7 marks]:** **Principle:** Brine (NaCl solution, 305 g/L) is electrolysed using a **Nafion™ perfluorosulfonic acid membrane** as the separator between the anode and cathode compartments. The membrane allows **only Na⁺ ions to migrate** from anode to cathode; chloride and hydroxyl ions are rejected. **Process Flow:** 1. **Brine Purification:** Saturated brine is treated with **NaOH + Na₂CO₃** to precipitate Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ impurities (since these damage Nafion™). 2. **Anode Compartment:** Purified brine enters, NaCl is oxidised to Cl₂, and depleted brine leaves (with 200 g/L NaCl) to be resaturated and recycled. 3. **Cathode Compartment:** Pure deionised water is fed; H₂O is reduced to H₂ + OH⁻. 4. **Na⁺ Migration:** Na⁺ ions pass through the membrane, combining with OH⁻ to form **NaOH (30–35% w/w)**. 5. **Product Streams:** Wet Cl₂ is cooled, dried with **H₂SO₄**, and liquefied. H₂ is washed and sent to storage. NaOH is concentrated to **50%** in a falling-film evaporator. **Reactions:** $$2\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^- \quad \text{(anode)}$$ $$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \quad \text{(cathode)}$$ **Role of Nafion™ Membrane:** - Cation-selective (only Na⁺ passes) - Prevents Cl₂ and H₂ from mixing (avoiding explosive mixture) - Produces high-purity NaOH with very low NaCl contamination **Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:** **Given:** I = 15,000 A, V = 3.20 V, η_c = 97%, t = 24 h = 86,400 s **(i) Mass of Cl₂ per day:** Total charge Q = I × t = 15,000 × 86,400 = 1.296 × 10⁹ C Theoretical mass: $$m_{th} = \frac{Q \times M}{n \times F} = \frac{1.296 \times 10^9 \times 71}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 476{,}963 \text{ g} = 476.96 \text{ kg}$$ Actual mass at 97% efficiency: $$m_{Cl_2} = 0.97 \times 476.96 = 462.65 \text{ kg/day}$$ **(ii) Mass of NaOH per day:** $$m_{NaOH} = 462.65 \times \frac{80}{71} = 521.30 \text{ kg/day}$$ **(iii) Specific energy consumption:** $$E_{total} = \frac{V \times I \times t}{3.6 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.20 \times 1.296 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 1152 \text{ kWh}$$ $$E_{specific} = \frac{1152}{0.46265} = 2490.0 \text{ kWh/t Cl}_2$$ ### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Comparison of Cell Technologies [7 marks]:** | Aspect | Mercury Cell | Diaphragm Cell | Membrane Cell | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Cathode | Mercury | Steel | Activated nickel | | Separator | Mercury film | Asbestos / polymer | Nafion™ membrane | | NaOH strength | 50% | 10–12% | 30–35% | | NaOH purity | High | Low (NaCl contaminated) | Very high | | Energy | 3,200 kWh/t | 2,900 kWh/t | 2,600 kWh/t | | Current eff. | 95% | 92% | 97% | | Environment | Hg hazard | Asbestos hazard | Clean | | Status | Phased out | Declining | **Dominant** | **Why membrane cell is preferred [4 marks]:** 1. **No mercury or asbestos** — environmentally benign. 2. **Lower energy consumption** — 2,500–2,700 kWh/t vs 3,200 kWh/t for mercury. 3. **Higher current efficiency** (95–98%) and higher purity NaOH. 4. **Compact footprint** and easier automation. 5. **Concentrated NaOH** directly (no massive evaporation train). **Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:** **Given:** m_Cl₂ = 1,000 kg/h, V = 3.5 V, η_c = 96% **(i) Current required:** Theoretical mass per second = 1,000/(3600 × 0.96) = 0.2894 kg/s of actual Cl₂ = theoretical. Equivalently, theoretical mass per hour = 1,000/0.96 = 1,041.67 kg/h. Convert to grams per second: 1,041,667/3600 = 289.35 g/s theoretical. Using Faraday's law rearranged: $$I = \frac{m_{th} \times n \times F}{M \times t} = \frac{1{,}041{,}667 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{71 \times 3600}$$ $$I = \frac{2.010 \times 10^{11}}{2.556 \times 10^5} = 786{,}558 \text{ A}$$ **(ii) H₂ at STP per hour:** The moles of Cl₂ produced per hour equal the moles of H₂ (1:1 stoichiometry). $$n_{H_2} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{71} = 14{,}084.5 \text{ mol/h}$$ $$V_{H_2} = 14{,}084.5 \times 22.4 = 315{,}493 \text{ L/h} = 315.49 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ **(iii) Energy per hour:** $$E = \frac{V \times I \times t}{3.6 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.5 \times 786{,}558 \times 3600}{3.6 \times 10^6}$$ $$E = \frac{9.911 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2753 \text{ kWh/h}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to apply current efficiency** — the most common 1-mark loss. Theoretical mass × η = actual mass. > 2. **Using 22.4 L/mol** at non-STP conditions — always check temperature and pressure. > 3. **Sign of E°_cell** — many students forget the negative sign and lose a mark. > 4. **Forgetting the 22.4 L/mol conversion** in H₂ calculations. > 5. **Not labeling units** — KTU strictly deducts marks for missing units in numerical answers. > 6. **Confusing the decomposer reaction** with the cell reaction in the mercury process. > 7. **In diaphragm vs membrane** — students often incorrectly state that both produce 50% NaOH. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Chlorine (KTU Module 1):** - **Chlorine is manufactured by electrolysis of aqueous NaCl (brine).** Three co-products: Cl₂, NaOH, H₂. - **Overall reaction:** $2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2 + 2\text{NaOH}$. - **Three cell types:** Mercury (phased out), Diaphragm (declining), Membrane (dominant). - **Anode reaction:** $2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^-$; **Cathode:** $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$. - **Theoretical cell voltage:** E° = 2.19 V; **Practical:** 2.8–4.5 V. - **Modern anode:** DSA (TiO₂-RuO₂ coated titanium). - **Membrane:** Nafion™ 117 (DuPont) — cation-selective, allows only Na⁺. - **Current efficiency target:** >96% (membrane), >90% (diaphragm), >95% (mercury). - **Energy consumption:** 2,500–2,700 kWh/t Cl₂ (membrane). - **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ with F = 96,485 C/mol. - **Stoichiometric ratios:** 1 t Cl₂ ⇔ 1.127 t NaOH ⇔ 0.0282 t H₂ ⇔ 1.648 t NaCl. - **Universal gas constant:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); molar volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol. - **Applications:** PVC, water treatment, bleaching, HCl, pesticides, refrigerants (phased out), propylene oxide, chlorinated methanes. - **Hazards:** TLV = 1 ppm; toxic; corrosive; transported as liquid under pressure. - **Minamata Convention (2013):** Phase-out mandate for mercury chlor-alkali plants. - **KTU Key Terms:** Brine, Anolyte, Catholyte, Denuder (decomposer), Dimensionally Stable Anode (DSA), Overpotential, Current Efficiency, Power Efficiency, Causticization. - **Mercury cell decomposer reaction:** $2\text{Na-Hg} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 + 2\text{Hg}$ (Hg recycled). - **Diaphragm cell produces dilute NaOH (~10%)** requiring evaporation to 50%, an energy penalty. - **Membrane cell directly produces 30–35% NaOH**, hence superior.

topics.title: Fertilizer Industries: Ammonia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fertilizer Industries: Ammonia

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: FERTILIZER INDUSTRIES — AMMONIA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *Ammonia (NH₃) is the most important nitrogenous fertilizer intermediate, manufactured industrially by the **Haber-Bosch process** in which atmospheric nitrogen is combined with hydrogen at high pressure and moderate temperature over a promoted iron catalyst, according to the reversible exothermic reaction:*
>
> $$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Ammonia is the **world's second-largest manufactured chemical** (after sulfuric acid) and is the **backbone of the nitrogen fertilizer industry** because it is the cheapest and most concentrated (82% N) source of fixed nitrogen. Nearly **85% of global ammonia production** is used directly or upgraded into urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate, ammonium phosphate (DAP/MAP), and nitric acid.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Squeeze"

Think of the Haber-Bosch process like trying to **force two magnets to stick together when they don't want to**.

- **Nitrogen (N₂)** has a triple bond — extremely strong, like two stubborn people holding hands. You have to apply massive **pressure** (150–300 atm) to push them into the reaction zone.
- The reaction also gives off heat (**exothermic**), so if you heat too much, the equilibrium shifts backward (Le Chatelier's principle). It's like a crowd — once they get too hot and excited, they push apart.
- So we use a **middle ground**: high pressure, moderate temperature, and a **matchmaker (iron catalyst)** to help the molecules react without being too forceful.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Key Metrics to Memorize**
> - **Reaction enthalpy:** ΔH = **−92.4 kJ/mol** (exothermic)
> - **Optimum temperature:** **400–500 °C**
> - **Optimum pressure:** **150–300 atm** (modern plants: 200–350 atm)
> - **Equilibrium conversion per pass:** **15–20%**
> - **Overall plant conversion:** **>97%** (via recycle loop)
> - **Feed ratio H₂:N₂:** **3:1** (stoichiometric)

### 🎯 Engineering Significance

| Application | Product Pathway |
|---|---|
| Direct fertilizer | Anhydrous / aqueous NH₃ |
| Urea synthesis | NH₃ + CO₂ → (NH₂)₂CO |
| Nitric acid → Ammonium nitrate | NH₃ → HNO₃ → NH₄NO₃ |
| DAP / MAP fertilizers | H₃PO₄ + NH₃ |
| Refrigerant (R717) | Industrial cold storage |
| Explosives / nylon precursors | Oxidation to NO → further chemistry |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundations

The synthesis reaction is governed by three critical thermodynamic constraints:

1. **Exothermicity** (ΔH < 0): Lower temperature favours higher equilibrium conversion.
2. **Volume reduction** (4 moles → 2 moles): Higher pressure favours NH₃.
3. **Catalyst limitation**: Iron catalyst requires T ≥ 400 °C for adequate activity — a **kinetic compromise** with thermodynamics.

### Equilibrium Constant Expression

For the reaction $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$, the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressures is:

$$K_p = \frac{(P_{\text{NH}_3})^2}{(P_{\text{N}_2}) \cdot (P_{\text{H}_2})^3}$$

The temperature dependence follows the **van 't Hoff equation**:

$$\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

Integration gives the empirical form valid for 700–900 K:

$$\log_{10} K_p = \frac{2172}{T} - 3.395 \log_{10} T + 1.85 \times 10^{-4}\,T - 2.05 \times 10^{-7}\,T^2 + 2.10$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for the van 't Hoff Equation**
> Because ΔH is negative, as T rises, ln K_p falls — meaning less NH₃ at equilibrium. This is the **thermodynamic ceiling** of the process.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synthesis reaction | $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$ | Stoichiometric |
| 2 | Reaction enthalpy | $\Delta H_{298} = -92.4$ | kJ/mol NH₃ |
| 3 | Activation energy (Fe cat.) | $E_a \approx 80$–$110$ | kJ/mol |
| 4 | Optimum T | $T_{opt} = 400$–$500$ | °C |
| 5 | Optimum P | $P = 150$–$300$ | atm |
| 6 | H₂:N₂ feed ratio | 3:1 (with slight excess H₂, 3.0–3.2) | mol/mol |
| 7 | Equilibrium constant | $K_p$ as above (function of T) | atm⁻² |
| 8 | Conversion per pass | $X_{pass} = 15$–$20$ | % |
| 9 | Overall conversion | $X_{overall} = 95$–$98$ | % |
| 10 | Catalyst | Fe + K₂O, Al₂O₃, CaO promoters | Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) reduced to α-Fe |
| 11 | Shift reaction | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41$ | kJ/mol |
| 12 | Methanation | $\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Cleanup |
| 13 | Steam/Carbon ratio (reforming) | S/C = 3.0–3.5 (modern), 2.5 (old) | mol/mol |
| 14 | Reformer exit T | 800–850 | °C |
| 15 | Reformer exit P | 30–40 | atm |
| 16 | Shift converter (HTS) | 320–450 | °C |
| 17 | Shift converter (LTS) | 200–250 | °C |
| 18 | CO₂ removal | Benfield (K₂CO₃), MEA, Rectisol, Selexol | Various |
| 19 | Cryogenic N₂ purity | 99.99% | % |
| 20 | Energy consumption (modern) | 28–32 | GJ/ton NH₃ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> These formulas appear in nearly **every ammonia-related KTU numerical**. The most frequently tested items are: **(i) feed ratio, (ii) recycle calculation, (iii) equilibrium composition using Kₚ, and (iv) single-pass conversion.** Memorize the reaction stoichiometry and ΔH.

## 2.3 Process Steps (Engineering Decomposition)

A modern ammonia plant is a **highly integrated energy complex** with the following block sequence:

### Step 1 — **Primary Reforming (Steam Reforming of Natural Gas)**

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- Endothermic; carried out in **tubular reformer** packed with nickel-on-alumina catalyst.
- Tubes heated externally by burners (fuel = purge gas, methane, or off-gas).
- Exit temperature: 800–850 °C; exit pressure: 30–40 atm.

### Step 2 — **Secondary Reforming (Air Addition)**

Air is injected to provide N₂ and complete the reforming:

$$2\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 4\text{H}_2$$

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(H}_2 \text{ combustion)}$$

The exothermic partial oxidation raises T to ~1000 °C; ensures methane slip < 0.5%.

### Step 3 — **Water–Gas Shift Conversion (CO → H₂)**

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Performed in **two stages**:
- **HTS (High-Temperature Shift):** 320–450 °C, Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃ catalyst.
- **LTS (Low-Temperature Shift):** 200–250 °C, Cu–ZnO–Al₂O₃ catalyst.

Final CO exit < **0.3 mol%**.

### Step 4 — **CO₂ Removal (Acid Gas Absorption)**

Bulk CO₂ is removed using one of:
- **Benfield (hot K₂CO₃)** — most common in large plants
- **MEA / MDEA** (amine scrubbing)
- **Rectisol** (cold methanol — used with partial oxidation)
- **Selexol** (DMPEG)

Target: < **1000 ppm CO₂** in syngas.

### Step 5 — **Methanation (Final Trace Cleanup)**

$$\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Over nickel catalyst at 300–400 °C. Reduces (CO + CO₂) to **< 5 ppm**.

### Step 6 — **Compression**

Syngas compressed to **150–300 atm** using centrifugal compressors (multi-stage with intercooling). Modern plants use **integrated centrifugal** machinery.

### Step 7 — **Ammonia Synthesis Loop**

- Fresh + recycle gas (3:1 H₂:N₂) passed over **iron catalyst** at 400–500 °C.
- **Conversion per pass: 15–20%.**
- Product NH₃ condensed by **water/ammonia cooling** (−33 °C).
- Unreacted gas **recycled** with purge stream to avoid inert (Ar, CH₄) buildup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Pass vs. Overall Conversion**
> Because of equilibrium, only 15–20% reacts per pass. The **unreacted gas is recycled**, giving **>97% overall conversion**. This is a classic example of **recycle reactor design** in KTU coursework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Equilibrium Composition at a Given T and P

**Given:** Stoichiometric feed N₂ + 3H₂ at 200 atm, 450 °C. Assume Kₚ = 1.45 × 10⁻⁵ atm⁻² at 450 °C. Let total moles at equilibrium = $n_T$ and extent of reaction = $\xi$.

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| N₂ | 1 | −ξ | 1 − ξ |
| H₂ | 3 | −3ξ | 3(1 − ξ) |
| NH₃ | 0 | +2ξ | 2ξ |
| **Total** | **4** | −2ξ | 4 − 2ξ |

Mole fractions:

$$y_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}$$

Partial pressures (P_total = P):

$$P_i = y_i P$$

Substitute into Kₚ:

$$K_p = \frac{\left(\dfrac{2\xi}{4-2\xi} P\right)^2}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi} P\right) \left(\dfrac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi} P\right)^3}$$

Simplify:

$$K_p = \frac{(2\xi)^2 (4-2\xi)^2}{(1-\xi)\cdot 27(1-\xi)^3 \cdot P^2} = \frac{4\xi^2(4-2\xi)^2}{27(1-\xi)^4 P^2}$$

Final solvable form:

$$\frac{\xi(2-\xi)}{(1-\xi)^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{27}} \cdot P \sqrt{K_p}$$

For the numerical case (T = 723 K, P = 200 atm, Kₚ = 1.45 × 10⁻⁵):

$$P\sqrt{K_p} = 200 \times \sqrt{1.45 \times 10^{-5}} = 200 \times 3.81 \times 10^{-3} = 0.761$$

$$\frac{\xi(2-\xi)}{(1-\xi)^2} = \frac{0.761}{\sqrt{27}} = \frac{0.761}{5.196} = 0.1465$$

Solving numerically by iteration:

| ξ | LHS = ξ(2−ξ)/(1−ξ)² |
|---|---|
| 0.10 | 0.211 |
| 0.12 | 0.272 |
| 0.08 | 0.157 |
| 0.07 | 0.132 |
| 0.075 | 0.144 |
| 0.076 | 0.147 |

**ξ ≈ 0.075 → 7.5 mol of N₂ converted → 15 mol NH₃ formed per 100 mol initial feed.**

Mole fraction of NH₃:

$$y_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{2(0.075)}{4 - 2(0.075)} = \frac{0.150}{3.85} = 0.039 = 3.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-pass conversion** = ξ/1 = 7.5% (i.e. low, confirming the need for recycle).

## 3.2 Recycle Material Balance

**Given:** Fresh feed F = 100 kmol/h (3:1 H₂:N₂), single-pass conversion = 20%, recycle ratio R/F = 4, purge/fresh = 0.05.

Let:
- $X$ = single-pass conversion
- $F$ = fresh feed
- $R$ = recycle flow
- $P$ = purge flow
- $V$ = combined feed to reactor

**Total in = Fresh + Recycle = V:**

$$V = F + R = F(1 + 4) = 5F$$

**Moles of NH₃ produced per pass:**

$$n_{NH_3} = 2X \cdot y_{N_2,V} \cdot V$$

For a 3:1 H₂:N₂ feed: y_N₂,V = 0.25.

$$n_{NH_3} = 2(0.20)(0.25)(5F) = 0.5F$$

**Inerts in recycle loop:** Let purge fraction be α = P/V = 0.05/5 = 0.01. Inert mass balance (steady state):

$$\alpha \cdot y_{inert,V} \cdot V = y_{inert,F} \cdot F$$

If y_inert,F = 0.01 (typical for natural gas feed), then:

$$y_{inert,V} = \frac{0.01 \times F}{0.01 \times 5F} = 0.20$$

Therefore **inerts in reactor feed ≈ 20 mol%** — a major reason modern plants minimize inerts via PSA or membrane purification upstream.

## 3.3 Catalyst Promoter Analysis

The industrial **fused-iron catalyst** is produced by reducing magnetite (Fe₃O₄) with H₂, yielding α-iron. Promoters:

| Promoter | Function | Amount (wt%) |
|---|---|---|
| K₂O | Electronic / basic — weakens N≡N adsorption | 0.5–1.5 |
| Al₂O₃ | Structural — prevents sintering | 2–4 |
| CaO | Structural — high-T stability | 2–4 |
| MgO | Structural | < 1 |
| Cr₂O₃ | Anti-sintering (alternative to Al) | Optional |

> [!NOTE]
> The **active surface** is α-Fe with specific crystal face (Fe(111)) binding N₂. K₂O donates electrons to Fe, enhancing N₂ dissociation — a beautiful example of **electronic promotion**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison of Reactor Types

| Reactor Type | T Control | Conversion/Pass | Pressure Drop | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quench (Kellogg)** | Cold quench gas between beds | 15–18% | Moderate | Most large plants |
| **Tubular (Topsoe)** | Cooling coils in catalyst | 18–22% | Higher | Energy-efficient |
| **Adiabatic multi-bed** | Inter-stage heat exchange | 12–15% | Low | Older plants |
| **Spherical (ICI)** | Radial flow, low ΔP | 20–25% | Very low | Large tonnage |
| **Ruthenium-based** (KAAP) | Lower T, higher conv. | >20% | Moderate | Premium/advanced |
