# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✏️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Haber-Bosch process. State the operating conditions used for ammonia synthesis.**

**Model Answer:**

The Haber-Bosch process is the **catalytic synthesis of ammonia from atmospheric nitrogen and hydrogen** according to:

$$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3 \quad \Delta H = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Operating conditions:**
- **Temperature:** 400–500 °C (compromise between kinetics and equilibrium)
- **Pressure:** 150–300 atm
- **Catalyst:** Promoted iron (α-Fe + K₂O/Al₂O₃/CaO)
- **H₂:N₂ ratio:** 3:1 (stoichiometric, sometimes slight H₂ excess)

The reaction is **reversible and exothermic**, so the conditions are optimized to balance **rate (high T)** with **equilibrium yield (low T)**, and **favour forward shift (high P)**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark reaction, 1 mark conditions, 1 mark catalyst explanation)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of promoters in the iron catalyst used for ammonia synthesis.**

**Model Answer:**

Pure α-iron is active but mechanically weak and rapidly deactivates. **Promoters** are added to enhance performance:

- **Structural promoters** (Al₂O₃, CaO, MgO) — form a high-surface-area skeletal framework that **prevents iron crystallite sintering** at synthesis temperatures (450 °C).
- **Electronic promoter** (K₂O, KOH) — donates electron density to Fe, **weakening the strong N≡N triple bond** by enhancing back-donation into N₂ π* orbitals, thus accelerating dissociation.

Together, they keep the iron in a high-dispersion, electronically activated state, raising the rate of NH₃ formation by a factor of **~10×** over unpromoted Fe.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of ammonia from natural gas** by the **Haber-Bosch process**, highlighting the function of each unit.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The manufacture proceeds through the following integrated steps:

1. **Feed preparation — Desulfurization:**
   Natural gas (CH₄) is first passed through **cobalt-molybdenum (Co-Mo) hydrogenation** and **zinc oxide (ZnO) adsorption** beds to remove sulfur compounds to < **0.1 ppm**, because sulfur is a **permanent poison** for the nickel reformer and iron ammonia catalysts.

2. **Primary reforming:**
   Desulfurized CH₄ is mixed with **process steam (S/C = 3.0–3.5)** and passed over **nickel-on-α-Al₂O₃ catalyst** in a fired tubular reformer at 800–850 °C and 30–40 atm:
   
   $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$$

3. **Secondary reforming:**
   Preheated **air** is added to introduce N₂ and complete conversion of residual CH₄ by partial oxidation at ~1000 °C, giving an H₂:N₂ ratio of ~3:1.

4. **Shift conversion (HTS + LTS):**
   CO is converted to CO₂ with steam, releasing more H₂:
   
   $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$
   
   Two stages: HTS at 400 °C (Fe₂O₃-Cr₂O₃) and LTS at 220 °C (Cu-ZnO-Al₂O₃), driving CO below 0.3%.

5. **CO₂ removal:**
   Done by **hot potassium carbonate (Benfield)** or amine (MEA/MDEA) absorption, reducing CO₂ to < 1000 ppm.

6. **Methanation (final cleanup):**
   Residual CO and CO₂ are hydrogenated to CH₄ over nickel at 300–400 °C, achieving < 5 ppm total oxides.

7. **Compression:**
   Purified syngas is compressed to **200–300 atm** in centrifugal compressors.

8. **Synthesis loop:**
   Compressed gas enters a multi-bed **iron catalyst converter** at 400–500 °C. NH₃ formed (~15–20% per pass) is condensed; unreacted gas is recycled.

9. **Storage:**
   Liquid NH₃ stored in **spherical or bullet tanks at −33 °C / atmospheric pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *(Allocate ~1 mark per major unit, plus 1 mark for stoichiometric correctness and 1 for flow-diagram clarity.)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **thermodynamic and kinetic considerations** that govern the choice of operating conditions (T, P, catalyst) for ammonia synthesis.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The reaction $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3 \quad \Delta H = -92.4$ kJ/mol is **exothermic** with **mole reduction (4 → 2)**.

**Thermodynamic considerations:**

(i) **Effect of temperature** — Applying **Le Chatelier's principle** and the **van 't Hoff equation**, the equilibrium constant $K_p$ decreases as T increases (since ΔH < 0). Thus low T favours higher NH₃ yield, e.g.:

$$K_p(700\,\text{K}) = 4.34 \times 10^{-5} \gg K_p(900\,\text{K}) = 1.45 \times 10^{-7}$$

(ii) **Effect of pressure** — Since moles of gas decrease, high P shifts the equilibrium to the right, increasing NH₃ at equilibrium. Increasing P from 100 → 300 atm nearly **doubles the equilibrium NH₃ content**.

**Kinetic considerations:**

(i) The uncatalyzed N≡N bond dissociation energy is **945 kJ/mol**, making the reaction immeasurably slow at low T. Iron catalyst reduces the activation energy to **~80 kJ/mol**, but only above ~400 °C.

(ii) This creates a **conflict**:
   - Thermodynamics → low T (≤ 200 °C ideal)
   - Kinetics → high T (≥ 400 °C)

**Resolution (Compromise):**
The chosen temperature of **400–500 °C** is a kinetic compromise, justified by:
- Using a **highly active promoted iron catalyst**.
- Operating at the **highest possible pressure (200–300 atm)** to push equilibrium forward despite the temperature compromise.
- **Recycling unreacted gases** to compensate for low per-pass conversion.

(iii) **Catalyst role:** Promoted iron with K₂O (electronic) and Al₂O₃ (structural) achieves practical rates at 450 °C; ruthenium-based catalysts (KAAP) allow operation at 350–400 °C, increasing equilibrium yield.

> [!NOTE]
> *(Marks split: ~3 marks thermodynamics, ~3 marks kinetics, 1 mark for the compromise explanation and catalyst justification.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **recycle loop in ammonia synthesis** with a flow diagram. Why is purge necessary, and how is purge gas handled?

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Because the per-pass conversion is only 15–20% (thermodynamic equilibrium limit), the unreacted H₂ + N₂ must be **recycled** to achieve 95–98% overall conversion.

**Loop operation:**
1. Make-up syngas (3:1 H₂:N₂) mixes with recycle gas in a **mixer**.
2. The combined stream is heated in a **feed–effluent heat exchanger** using hot reactor exit gas.
3. The pre-heated gas enters the **converter** (3–4 catalyst beds) at 400–500 °C.
4. Exit gas (containing ~16% NH₃) is cooled, and NH₃ is condensed in a **chiller (−33 °C)** and separated in an **HP separator**.
5. The uncondensed gas (mostly H₂ + N₂ + inerts) is **recompressed** and returned to the converter.

**Recycle ratio R/F = 4–5** is typical.

**Why purge is necessary:**
Inerts (Ar, CH₄ from methanation, He) entering with make-up gas accumulate in the recycle loop. If unremoved, they:
- **Dilute the reactants**, reducing partial pressures and shifting equilibrium left.
- **Lower the reaction rate** by lowering the partial pressures of H₂ and N₂.

**Purge handling:**
- A small stream (~3–5% of loop flow) is continuously vented as **purge gas**.
- Modern plants route this purge to a **purge-gas recovery unit (hydrogen recovery)** using **PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption)** or **membrane separation (Prism)** to recover H₂, returning ~70–90% of the H₂ content back to the loop.
- Residual gas is used as **reformer fuel**.

> [!TIP]
> *(3 marks for flow explanation, 2 marks for purge necessity, 2 marks for recovery techniques.)*

---

**(b)** A stoichiometric mixture of N₂ and H₂ is fed to an ammonia synthesis reactor at **200 atm and 450 °C**, where $K_p = 1.45 \times 10^{-5}$ atm⁻². Calculate the **equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃** and the **single-pass conversion of N₂**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let initial moles: 1 mol N₂ + 3 mol H₂. Let ξ = moles of N₂ reacted at equilibrium.

| Species | Initial | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|
| N₂ | 1 | 1 − ξ |
| H₂ | 3 | 3(1 − ξ) |
| NH₃ | 0 | 2ξ |
| **Total** | **4** | **4 − 2ξ** |

Mole fractions:

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{H_2} = \frac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{NH_3} = \frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}$$

Substituting into:

$$K_p = \frac{y_{NH_3}^2 \cdot P^2}{y_{N_2} \cdot y_{H_2}^3 \cdot P^4} = \frac{y_{NH_3}^2}{y_{N_2} \cdot y_{H_2}^3} \cdot P^{-2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{y_{NH_3}^2}{y_{N_2} \cdot y_{H_2}^3} = K_p \cdot P^2$$

Compute $K_p \cdot P^2$:

$$K_p \cdot P^2 = 1.45 \times 10^{-5} \times (200)^2 = 1.45 \times 10^{-5} \times 4 \times 10^4 = 0.58$$

Substituting mole fractions:

$$\frac{\left(\dfrac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)^2}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)\left(\dfrac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}\right)^3} = 0.58$$

$$\frac{4\xi^2 (4-2\xi)^2}{27(1-\xi)^4} = 0.58$$

$$\frac{\xi^2(2-\xi)^2}{(1-\xi)^4} = \frac{0.58 \times 27}{4} = 3.915$$

Taking square root:

$$\frac{\xi(2-\xi)}{(1-\xi)^2} = 1.979$$

**Iterative solution:**

| ξ | LHS |
|---|---|
| 0.30 | 1.530 |
| 0.40 | 2.667 |
| 0.36 | 2.166 |
| 0.34 | 1.984 |
| 0.339 | 1.971 |

$$\boxed{\xi \approx 0.34}$$

**Single-pass conversion of N₂:**

$$X_{N_2} = \xi = 34\%$$

**Mole fraction of NH₃:**

$$y_{NH_3} = \frac{2(0.34)}{4 - 2(0.34)} = \frac{0.68}{3.32} = 0.205$$

$$\boxed{y_{NH_3} \approx 20.5\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> *(Mark split: 2 marks for setting up stoichiometric table, 2 marks for Kₚ expression, 2 marks for algebraic simplification, 1 mark for the numerical answer with units.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Confusing pass conversion (15–20%) with overall conversion (>97%).** Examiners specifically look for the recycle loop explanation.
> 2. **Forgetting the heat-exchanger network** in the flow diagram. A KTU flow sheet MUST show: **Feed–Effluent HX, Waste Heat Boiler, and Steam system** — these carry **2 marks** on their own.
> 3. **Writing ΔH with wrong sign** or using "ΔH = +92.4 kJ/mol" — reaction is exothermic, so the sign is **negative**.
> 4. **Skipping the role of promoters** — K₂O (electronic) and Al₂O₃ (structural) are **separate** concepts; examiners award **1 mark each**.
> 5. **Not mentioning purge gas handling** in Part B answers about recycle — this is a **favourite follow-up** question.
> 6. **In equilibrium problems, forgetting to convert total pressure** when using $K_p$. Always substitute P in **atm**, not bar or kPa, unless stated.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap — Quick Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Points to Remember Before Exam**
> - ✅ Haber-Bosch reaction: $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$, $\Delta H = -92.4$ kJ/mol
> - ✅ Operating window: **400–500 °C, 150–300 atm**
> - ✅ Catalyst: **Fe (α) + K₂O + Al₂O₃ + CaO**
> - ✅ Feed ratio: **3:1 (H₂:N₂)**, sometimes 3.1–3.2 with H₂ excess
> - ✅ Per-pass conversion: **15–20%**; overall: **>97%** (recycle)
> - ✅ HTS at 400 °C, LTS at 220 °C; final CO < 0.3%
> - ✅ CO₂ removal: **Benfield (K₂CO₃)** is most common
> - ✅ Methanation: **<5 ppm** residual oxides
> - ✅ Modern energy consumption: **~28–32 GJ/ton NH₃**
> - ✅ Process flow: **Desulf → Primary Reformer → Secondary Reformer → HTS → LTS → CO₂ Removal → Methanation → Compression → Synthesis Loop → Refrigeration → Storage**
> - ✅ Synthesis loop must include: **reactor, chiller, separator, recycle compressor, purge stream**
> - ✅ KTU favourite: equilibrium mole fraction calculation (van 't Hoff + Kₚ)
> - ✅ KTU favourite: reasons for **purge + recycle**
> - ✅ Promoters — K₂O = **electronic**, Al₂O₃ = **structural**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Urea

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Urea**, also known as **carbamide**, is the diamide of carbonic acid with the chemical formula **(NH₂)₂CO** and IUPAC name **carbonyl diamide**. It is the first organic compound synthesised from inorganic raw materials (Friedrich Wöhler, 1828), a landmark event that overturned the doctrine of *vitalism* in chemistry.

**Molecular formula:** $(NH_2)_2CO$

**Molecular weight:** $M_w = 60.06 \text{ g/mol}$

**Structural formula:** $H_2N{-}C({=}O){-}NH_2$

**CAS Number:** 57-13-6

Urea is a white, odourless, crystalline solid at standard conditions with a melting point of **133 °C** and density of **1.32 g/cm³**. It is highly soluble in water (1080 g/L at 20 °C) and slightly soluble in ethanol. Industrially, urea is overwhelmingly produced by the **Bosch–Meiser process** (also called the *total-recycle process*), in which liquid ammonia and gaseous carbon dioxide react at high pressure (140–200 bar) and temperature (170–200 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Nomenclature Tip (KTU Board Favourite):**
> The reaction proceeds in **two reversible steps** through an ammonium carbamate intermediate:
> $$2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons NH_2COONH_4 \quad \text{(carbamate formation, exothermic)}$$
> $$NH_2COONH_4 \rightleftharpoons (NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \quad \text{(dehydration, mildly endothermic)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **"marriage broker" molecule**. Carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) is a "bachelor" molecule that wants to react. Ammonia ($NH_3$) is the "matchmaker." When two ammonia molecules meet one carbon dioxide molecule under high pressure and heat (like a crowded, energetic party), they first form an unstable intermediate called **ammonium carbamate** — this is the *"engagement"*. The carbamate is then dehydrated (loses a water molecule, the *"wedding ceremony"*), giving us the stable, useful product **urea** — the *"marriage"*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> Urea is the world's most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, accounting for roughly **50% of global nitrogen fertiliser consumption**. The nitrogen content of urea is **46% by weight**, the highest among solid nitrogenous fertilisers. So when we talk about "feeding the world," we are largely talking about urea.

A second, simpler analogy: think of urea production like a **sandwich shop**:
1. **Bread + filling = carbamate** (intermediate)
2. **Toasted/squeezed sandwich = urea** (final product)
3. **Customers who don't like the sandwich = unreacted ammonia and CO₂** → sent back to the counter (recycle).

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Properties (High-Yield)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Appearance | White crystalline solid (prills or granules) |
| Molecular weight | **60.06 g/mol** |
| Melting point | **133 °C** (decomposes before boiling) |
| Density (solid) | **1.32 g/cm³** |
| Nitrogen content | **46.6 % (highest among solid fertilisers)** |
| Solubility in water (20 °C) | **1080 g/L** |
| Heat of combustion | -632 kJ/mol |
| pH (10 % aqueous solution) | ~9.0 (slightly alkaline) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biuret formation:** At temperatures above **135 °C**, two urea molecules can condense to form **biuret** $(H_2N{-}CO{-}NH{-}CO{-}NH_2)$, which is **toxic to plants** when concentration in fertiliser exceeds **1.5 %**. KTU questions frequently test this as a quality-control parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bosch–Meiser Process — Conceptual Breakdown

The industrial Bosch–Meiser process is the **dominant route** (> 95 % of global production) and operates on the principle of **total recycle** of unreacted ammonia and CO₂. The complete reaction sequence is analysed below in structured logical steps.

### Step 1: Carbamate Formation (Condenser / High-Pressure Reactor)
$$2NH_{3(g)} + CO_{2(g)} \xrightarrow{\text{150-200 bar, 160-180°C}} NH_2COONH_{4(l)}$$
- **Highly exothermic** ($\Delta H_1 \approx -117 \text{ kJ/mol}$).
- The reaction is **fast** (equilibrium-controlled) and almost **quantitative** in the liquid phase.
- Excess ammonia (NH₃ : CO₂ molar ratio typically **2.0–4.0 : 1**) shifts equilibrium to the right, suppressing carbamate decomposition in subsequent stages.

### Step 2: Carbamate Dehydration (Urea Reactor)
$$NH_2COONH_{4(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{140-200 bar, 180-200°C}} (NH_2)_2CO_{(l)} + H_2O_{(l)}$$
- **Mildly endothermic** ($\Delta H_2 \approx +15.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$).
- Conversion in a single urea reactor is only **50–60 %** because the reverse reaction (hydrolysis of urea back to carbamate) is favoured at high water partial pressures.
- Residence time: **45–60 minutes**.

### Step 3: Overall Reaction
$$2NH_{3(g)} + CO_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons (NH_2)_2CO_{(l)} + H_{2}O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H_{overall} \approx -101.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
- The negative overall enthalpy confirms the reaction is **exothermic** (heat must be removed).
- Equilibrium is **volume-reducing** on the left side (3 mol gas → 0 mol gas + 2 mol liquid), so **high pressure strongly favours urea formation** (Le Chatelier's principle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why recycle?**
> The single-pass conversion of CO₂ to urea is only **35–45 %**. Hence, unreacted carbamate in the urea reactor effluent must be decomposed back to NH₃ + CO₂ in **decomposition stages** (low-pressure separators) and recycled to the carbamate condenser. This is what makes the process economically viable — without recycle, raw material costs would be prohibitive.

## 2.2 Process Flow Description (Logical Sequence)

The modern **Snamprogetti or Stamicarbon stripping process** performs the following stages:

1. **Liquid NH₃** (from recycle + fresh) and gaseous CO₂ are compressed to **140–200 bar**.
2. Mixed in the **carbamate condenser** (a heat exchanger; exothermic heat is recovered as steam).
3. Liquid carbamate enters the **urea reactor** (vertical, baffled tower; ~ 180 °C).
4. Reactor effluent flows to the **stripper** (CO₂ stripping at synthesis pressure) — most unreacted carbamate is decomposed and stripped back to the condenser.
5. Stripped solution goes to **low-pressure decomposition (LPD)** at **3–5 bar**.
6. Then to **vacuum decomposition** at **0.3–0.5 bar**.
7. Finally to **evaporation/crystallisation/prilling** to obtain solid urea prills.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving numericals on urea technology. Memorise all entries.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight urea $M_{urea}$ | $60.06$ | g/mol |
| Molecular weight NH₃ $M_{NH_3}$ | $17.03$ | g/mol |
| Molecular weight CO₂ $M_{CO_2}$ | $44.01$ | g/mol |
| Molecular weight carbamate $M_{carb}$ | $78.07$ | g/mol |
| Stoichiometric NH₃ per kg urea (theoretical) | $\frac{2 \times 17.03}{60.06}$ = **0.567 kg** | kg NH₃ / kg urea |
| Stoichiometric CO₂ per kg urea (theoretical) | $\frac{44.01}{60.06}$ = **0.733 kg** | kg CO₂ / kg urea |
| Overall $\Delta H$ (carbamate formation) | $-117$ | kJ/mol |
| $\Delta H$ (dehydration) | $+15.5$ | kJ/mol |
| Overall $\Delta H$ | $-101.5$ | kJ/mol |
| Heat of crystallisation | $-242$ | kJ/kg urea |
| Synthesis pressure | $140 - 200$ | bar |
| Synthesis temperature | $170 - 200$ | °C |
| NH₃ : CO₂ molar ratio (feed) | $2.0 - 4.0 : 1$ | (excess NH₃) |
| H₂O : CO₂ molar ratio (feed) | $0 - 0.7 : 1$ | (zero in Stamicarbon) |
| Single-pass CO₂ conversion | $35 - 45$ | % |
| Biuret limit in fertiliser-grade | $\leq 1.5$ | wt % |
| Nitrogen content of urea | $46.6$ | wt % |

### 2.3.1 Conversion Formulas

**Overall CO₂ conversion to urea:**
$$X_{CO_2}^{overall} = \frac{\text{moles of urea produced}}{\text{moles of CO}_2 \text{ fed}} \times 100 \, \%$$

**Per-pass conversion in urea reactor (carbamate → urea):**
$$\eta_{reactor} = \frac{[NH_2COONH_4] - [NH_2COONH_4]_{out}}{[NH_2COONH_4]_{in}} \times 100 \, \%$$

**Stoichiometric (theoretical) NH₃ requirement per unit mass urea:**
$$\dot{m}_{NH_3}^{th} = \frac{2 \, M_{NH_3}}{M_{urea}} = \frac{2 \times 17.03}{60.06} = 0.567 \, \frac{\text{kg NH}_3}{\text{kg urea}}$$

**Actual NH₃ requirement with excess ratio $R$ (= actual NH₃ / stoichiometric NH₃):**
$$\dot{m}_{NH_3}^{act} = R \times 0.567 \times \dot{m}_{urea}$$

**Overall mass balance on CO₂ (with recycle):**
$$\dot{n}_{CO_2,fresh} = \dot{n}_{CO_2,consumed} + \dot{n}_{CO_2,purge}$$
where in steady state $\dot{n}_{CO_2,purge}$ is typically small (1–2 % of fresh feed).

### 2.3.2 Thermodynamic — Why Pressure and Temperature?

The reaction $2NH_3 + CO_2 \to (NH_2)_2CO + H_2O$ converts **3 mol of gas → 0 mol gas + 2 mol liquid**. By Le Chatelier's principle:

- **High pressure** ($\uparrow P$) → favours products (volume decrease).
- **High temperature** ($\uparrow T$) → favours urea over carbamate (kinetically), but thermodynamically the dehydration step is mildly endothermic, so equilibrium favours urea slightly.

Practical optimum: **170–200 °C** and **140–200 bar**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture (≈ 90 % of urea produced):** as the world's most concentrated solid nitrogen fertiliser.
- **Animal feed (urea-grade, biuret < 0.3 %):** as a non-protein nitrogen source for ruminants.
- **Resins & plastics:** urea–formaldehyde and urea–melamine–formaldehyde resins.
- **AdBlue / Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF):** 32.5 % aqueous urea solution injected into SCR catalytic converters to reduce NOx from diesel engines.
- **Cosmetics & pharmaceuticals:** skin moisturisers, hair conditioners, keratolytic agents.
- **Explosives:** urea nitrate is a cheap, low-sensitivity explosive for industrial blasting.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Raw Material Requirement

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** Calculate the stoichiometric quantities of ammonia and CO₂ required to produce **1000 kg/day** of pure urea, and the mass of water formed as by-product.

**Step 1 — Write the overall balanced reaction.**
$$2NH_3 + CO_2 \to (NH_2)_2CO + H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Identify molecular weights.**
$$M_{NH_3} = 17.03, \quad M_{CO_2} = 44.01, \quad M_{urea} = 60.06, \quad M_{H_2O} = 18.02 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute moles of urea per day.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n_{urea} &= \frac{1000 \text{ kg/day}}{60.06 \text{ kg/kmol}} \\
&= 16.65 \text{ kmol/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply stoichiometric ratios.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n_{NH_3} &= 2 \times n_{urea} = 2 \times 16.65 = 33.30 \text{ kmol/day} \\
n_{CO_2} &= 1 \times n_{urea} = 1 \times 16.65 = 16.65 \text{ kmol/day} \\
n_{H_2O} &= 1 \times n_{urea} = 16.65 \text{ kmol/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass.**
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{NH_3} &= 33.30 \times 17.03 = 567.0 \text{ kg/day} \\
m_{CO_2} &= 16.65 \times 44.01 = 732.8 \text{ kg/day} \\
m_{H_2O} &= 16.65 \times 18.02 = 300.0 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verify mass balance.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{In} &= 567.0 + 732.8 = 1299.8 \text{ kg/day} \\
\text{Out} &= 1000 + 300.0 = 1300.0 \text{ kg/day} \quad \checkmark \text{ (rounding error)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick check:** Per kg urea → **0.567 kg NH₃ + 0.733 kg CO₂ → 1.0 kg urea + 0.300 kg H₂O.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Heat Duty of Carbamate Condenser

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The carbamate condenser of a urea plant receives **10,000 kmol/day** of CO₂ feed at 50 °C. The exit carbamate stream leaves at **175 °C**. Determine the heat to be removed by the carbamate condenser. Assume complete conversion to carbamate in the condenser.

**Step 1 — Moles of carbamate formed (per stoichiometry).**
$$n_{NH_3} = 2 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}000 \text{ kmol/day}, \quad n_{carb} = 10{,}000 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 2 — Heat released by reaction.**
$$Q_{rxn} = n_{carb} \times \lvert \Delta H_1 \rvert = 10{,}000 \times 117 = 1{,}170{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat to raise liquid carbamate from 50 °C to 175 °C.**
Use $C_{p,carb} \approx 1.55 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $M_{carb} = 78.07 \text{ kg/kmol}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{sens} &= n_{carb} \times M_{carb} \times C_p \times \Delta T \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 78.07 \times 1.55 \times (175 - 50) \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 78.07 \times 1.55 \times 125 \\
&= 1.512 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat to be removed.**
$$Q_{total} = Q_{rxn} + Q_{sens} = 1.17 \times 10^{6} + 1.512 \times 10^{8} \approx 1.524 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ/day}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 1763 \text{ kW} \quad (1.524 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ/day} \div 86400 \text{ s/day})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** This is why the carbamate condenser is the **biggest heat source** in a urea plant — typically producing **2–4 MPa saturated steam** that is reused to drive downstream reboilers, dramatically improving plant energy integration.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Recycle Ratio Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a urea plant, fresh NH₃ feed = 50,000 kg/day, fresh CO₂ feed = 70,000 kg/day, single-pass CO₂ conversion to urea = 40 %. Urea product (dry basis) = 95,000 kg/day. Compute the recycle ratio of unreacted NH₃.

**Step 1 — Moles of fresh feeds per day.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n_{CO_2,fresh} &= \frac{70{,}000}{44.01} = 1590.5 \text{ kmol/day} \\
n_{NH_3,fresh} &= \frac{50{,}000}{17.03} = 2936.0 \text{ kmol/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ consumed (= kmol urea) on single pass basis.**
$$n_{CO_2,consumed} = 0.40 \times 1590.5 = 636.2 \text{ kmol/day}$$
$$n_{urea} = 636.2 \text{ kmol/day} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{urea} = 636.2 \times 60.06 = 38{,}206 \text{ kg/day}$$

*(Note: in a real total-recycle plant the *fresh* feed equals the *consumed* feed at steady state — the 40 % applies only to the *internal* carbamate reactor feed. The student is expected to recognise this convention.)*

**Step 3 — For this pedagogical problem, compute NH₃ recycled to the condenser.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total NH}_3 \text{ needed stoichiometrically} &= 2 \times 636.2 = 1272.4 \text{ kmol/day} \\
\text{Unreacted NH}_3 \text{ to be recycled} &= 2936.0 - 1272.4 = 1663.6 \text{ kmol/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Recycle ratio (RR).**
$$RR = \frac{\text{mass recycled}}{\text{mass fresh}} = \frac{1663.6 \times 17.03}{50{,}000} = \frac{28{,}331}{50{,}000} = 0.566$$

$$\boxed{RR_{NH_3} \approx 0.57 \text{ (i.e., about 57 % of fresh-feed NH₃ is recycled)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real industrial practice, recycle ratios of 3–5 are common because of the very large internal liquid recycle from the LPD stages. This is a major reason why **ejector- and stripping-based energy integration** (Stamicarbon, Snamprogetti) became dominant over the original partial-recycle designs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Industrial Urea Processes

| Feature | Conventional Partial-Recycle (Once-Through) | Total Recycle (Bosch–Meiser) | Stamicarbon CO₂-Stripping | Snamprogetti NH₃-Stripping |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Recycle of NH₃ / CO₂ | Partial | Total | Total (CO₂ as stripping agent) | Total (NH₃ as stripping agent) |
| Synthesis pressure (bar) | 200–350 | 140–200 | 130–160 | 150–200 |
| NH₃ : CO₂ molar ratio | ~ 2 : 1 | ~ 2–4 : 1 | ~ 2.5–3 : 1 | ~ 3.5 : 1 |
| H₂O : CO₂ ratio | ~ 1 : 1 | ~ 0.5–0.7 : 1 | ~ 0 : 1 | ~ 0.3 : 1 |
| Single-pass conversion | 35–50 % | 40–55 % | 50–60 % | 55–65 % |
| Energy efficiency | Poor (moderate) | Better | Best (low steam consumption) | High |
| Investment cost | Low | High | High | High |
| Operator skill required | Low | Moderate | High | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern (favourite 7-mark question):** "Compare any two commercial processes for manufacture of urea." Always frame the comparison along the four axes: (i) recycle philosophy, (ii) energy consumption, (iii) conversion achieved, and (iv) material of construction (urea is **highly corrosive to carbon steel at > 180 °C**, requiring SS 316 L or titanium-lined equipment).

```mermaid
    E --> F[Urea Synthesis Reactor 180C 150 bar]

    I --> J[Urea Solution Evaporator]
    J --> K[Prilling Tower or Granulator]

```

**Reading guide:**
- **Blue boxes** = feed storage / supply.
- **Yellow boxes** = high-pressure synthesis loop (heart of the plant).
- **Green box** = finished product.
- **Orange box** = solid handling (prilling/granulation).
- The two **recycle arrows** back to the carbamate condenser (`G → E` and `M → E`) demonstrate the **total-recycle principle** that defines Bosch–Meiser technology.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's-level expectations** (CO₂: Apply; CO₃: Analyse; CO₄: Design/Evaluate). Marks indicated in square brackets are typical KTU valuation marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] [3 Marks]
**"List any three important industrial applications of urea with one-line justifications."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Nitrogen fertiliser (agriculture):** Highest nitrogen content (**46.6 %**) among solid fertilisers; provides plants with essential nitrogen for amino-acid and chlorophyll synthesis.
2. **Diesel Exhaust Fluid / AdBlue:** 32.5 % aqueous urea solution injected into SCR catalytic converters, decomposing thermally to NH₃ which selectively reduces NOx emissions to N₂ and H₂O.
3. **Urea–formaldehyde resins:** Used as wood adhesives in particle board, plywood, and moulding powders (e.g., electrical switchgear); curing is acid-catalysed condensation of urea with formaldehyde.
*(Any three valid applications accepted.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] [3 Marks]
**"Explain the term 'biuret' and state its significance in urea manufacture."**

**Model Answer:**
Biuret ($H_2N{-}CO{-}NH{-}CO{-}NH_2$) is a **condensation by-product** formed when two urea molecules eliminate one molecule of ammonia at temperatures above **135 °C**.

**Significance:**
- Biuret is **phytotoxic** — concentrations above **1.5 wt %** in fertiliser-grade urea cause leaf burn and stunted growth.
- Therefore, evaporator and prilling-tower temperatures are deliberately kept **below 135 °C** to keep biuret < 0.9 wt % in agricultural-grade and < 0.3 wt % in feed-grade urea.

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) drawing flow sheets without labelled streams, (b) writing only the *overall* reaction and forgetting the **carbamate intermediate**, and (c) confusing **per-pass** with **overall** conversions. Read carefully before answering.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Board Style
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow sheet the **manufacture of urea by the Bosch–Meiser total-recycle process**. Discuss the reaction chemistry, operating conditions, and the role of the carbamate condenser.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A urea plant produces **1500 tonnes/day** of granular urea. The fresh feed contains **NH₃ : CO₂ molar ratio = 3.0 : 1** and the **overall CO₂ conversion** to urea is **45 %**. Calculate:
  1. The mass of fresh NH₃ and CO₂ required per day.
  2. The mass of water formed as by-product.
  3. The amount of unreacted CO₂ recycled back to the carbamate condenser.

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**1. Reaction Chemistry [2 marks]**
The Bosch–Meiser process proceeds through a **two-step reversible reaction** in the liquid phase at high pressure:

**Step 1 (carbamate formation, exothermic):**
$$2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons NH_2COONH_4 \quad \Delta H_1 = -117 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 (carbamate dehydration, mildly endothermic):**
$$NH_2COONH_4 \rightleftharpoons (NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \quad \Delta H_2 = +15.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Overall:**
$$2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons (NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \quad \Delta H_{net} \approx -101.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**2. Operating Conditions [1 mark]**
- Pressure: **140–200 bar**
- Temperature: **170–200 °C**
- NH₃ : CO₂ molar ratio: **2.5–4.0 : 1** (excess NH₃ suppresses carbamate decomposition and increases per-pass CO₂ conversion).

**3. Flow Sheet [3 marks]** — Refer to Section 4.1 flow diagram.

**Process description:**
- Fresh liquid NH₃ (from recycle + makeup) and compressed CO₂ enter the **carbamate condenser** at **140–200 bar**, where they react exothermically to form liquid ammonium carbamate. The released heat (≈ 117 kJ/mol of carbamate) generates **2.5–4 MPa LP steam** used elsewhere in the plant.
- The liquid carbamate then flows down through the **urea reactor** (typically a vertical baffled tower) at **180–195 °C**, where it dehydrates to urea + water with **per-pass conversion of 50–60 %**.
- The reactor effluent enters the **CO₂ stripper**, where fresh CO₂ strips unreacted NH₃ and carbamate out of the urea solution, sending them back to the carbamate condenser.
- Stripped urea solution goes through **LPD** (3–5 bar), **vacuum decomposer** (0.3–0.5 bar), **evaporator**, and finally the **prilling tower**.
- Unreacted gases from each stage are absorbed in **carbamate condensers** and recycled.

**4. Role of Carbamate Condenser [1 mark]**
- Performs **two functions simultaneously:** (i) acts as a reactor (completes carbamate formation), and (ii) acts as a **heat exchanger** (removes exothermic heat of reaction by generating medium-pressure steam).
- Operates on the **shell-and-tube** principle: process fluid on tube side, water/steam on shell side.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:** Production = 1500 t/day; NH₃:CO₂ molar feed ratio = 3:1; overall CO₂ conversion = 45 %.

**Step 1 — Moles of urea produced per day.**
$$n_{urea} = \frac{1500 \times 1000}{60.06} = 24{,}975 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of CO₂ consumed (= moles of urea at overall basis).**
$$n_{CO_2,consumed} = 24{,}975 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of fresh CO₂ fed.**
At 45 % overall conversion: $n_{CO_2,fresh} = \frac{24{,}975}{0.45} = 55{,}500 \text{ kmol/day}$

$$m_{CO_2,fresh} = 55{,}500 \times 44.01 = 2{,}442{,}555 \text{ kg/day} \approx 2442.6 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of fresh NH₃ fed (3 : 1 molar ratio to fresh CO₂).**
$$n_{NH_3,fresh} = 3 \times 55{,}500 = 166{,}500 \text{ kmol/day}$$
$$m_{NH_3,fresh} = 166{,}500 \times 17.03 = 2{,}835{,}495 \text{ kg/day} \approx 2835.5 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 5 — Moles of NH₃ consumed (stoichiometric).**
$$n_{NH_3,consumed} = 2 \times 24{,}975 = 49{,}950 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 6 — Moles of NH₃ recycled.**
$$n_{NH_3,recycle} = 166{,}500 - 49{,}950 = 116{,}550 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 7 — Mass of water formed.**
$$n_{H_2O} = n_{urea} = 24{,}975 \text{ kmol/day}$$
$$m_{H_2O} = 24{,}975 \times 18.02 = 450{,}049 \text{ kg/day} \approx 450 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 8 — Mass of unreacted CO₂ recycled.**
$$n_{CO_2,recycle} = 55{,}500 - 24{,}975 = 30{,}525 \text{ kmol/day}$$
$$m_{CO_2,recycle} = 30{,}525 \times 44.01 = 1{,}343{,}405 \text{ kg/day} \approx 1343.4 \text{ t/day}$$

**Summary of Answers:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Fresh NH₃ feed | **2835.5 t/day** |
| Fresh CO₂ feed | **2442.6 t/day** |
| Water formed | **≈ 450 t/day** |
| CO₂ recycled | **≈ 1343.4 t/day** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **engineering problems of materials of construction** encountered in urea plants. Why is **silver lining** used in critical areas?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Stamicarbon CO₂-stripping process** with the **Snamprogetti NH₃-stripping process** for urea manufacture, covering at least five parameters. Justify which process is more energy efficient.

#### Model Solution Outline — Part B(a)

**Key corrosion problems in urea plants [4 marks]:**

1. **Ammonium carbamate is highly corrosive** — at 180 °C it attacks carbon steel rapidly, producing iron carbamate and reducing equipment life.
2. **Urea hydrolyses** back to carbamate on cooling, releasing NH₃ and CO₂ that stress-relieve weldments, causing **stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)**.
3. **Biuret formation** above 135 °C lowers product quality and forms corrosive deposits.
4. **Oxygen in CO₂ feed** accelerates corrosion; CO₂ must be **deoxygenated to < 50 ppm** before entering the synthesis loop.

**Materials used [3 marks]:**
- **Carbon steel** — for low-pressure, low-temperature sections (utilities, storage).
- **SS 304 L** — for moderate conditions (e.g., LPD vessels).
- **SS 316 L (urea-grade)** — the workhorse alloy for the high-pressure synthesis loop; contains **2.5–3.0 % Mo** for chloride resistance.
- **Silver lining (or silver-bearing 316 L)** — used in critical high-pressure areas (reactor internals, carbamate condenser tube sheets) because silver forms a **passive protective layer** of silver oxide that resists ammonium carbamate attack; extends equipment life from ~ 8–10 years (unlined SS) to > 25 years.
- **Titanium** — used in stripper tube sheets and condenser covers in the most aggressive service; expensive but virtually immune to carbamate corrosion.
- **Zirconium** — used in some Snamprogetti designs for stripper internals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board tip:** A common error is saying "silver lining prevents corrosion" — the *correct* mechanism is that silver forms a **passive oxide film** that is self-healing under the reducing conditions of the urea loop.

#### Model Solution Outline — Part B(b)

| Comparison Parameter | Stamicarbon CO₂-Stripping | Snamprogetti NH₃-Stripping |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Stripping agent | CO₂ (cheap, readily available) | NH₃ (already in excess) |
| 2. Operating pressure | 130–160 bar (slightly lower) | 150–200 bar |
| 3. NH₃ : CO₂ molar ratio | ~ 2.5–3 : 1 | ~ 3.5 : 1 (higher excess) |
| 4. H₂O : CO₂ in feed | ~ 0 (minimal recycle water) | ~ 0.3 (some water recycle) |
| 5. Per-pass CO₂ conversion | 50–60 % (higher) | 55–65 % |
| 6. Steam consumption (GJ/t urea) | ~ 4.5–5.0 | ~ 5.0–5.5 |
| 7. Critical material of construction | SS 316 L (silver-lined), Ti | SS 316 L (silver-lined), Zr |
| 8. Recycle philosophy | One high-pressure recycle stream (simpler) | Two recycle loops (LP + HP) |
| 9. Plant capacity range (typical) | 600–3500 t/day (modular) | 1500–6000 t/day (large grassroots) |

**Verdict (justification) [2 marks]:**
The **Stamicarbon CO₂-stripping process is more energy-efficient per tonne** because:
- (i) the use of CO₂ as stripping agent exploits the **most readily available feed gas**, avoiding the energy cost of vaporising large amounts of liquid NH₃;
- (ii) the absence of recycle water improves the per-pass urea conversion (Le Chatelier — less water pushes equilibrium toward urea);
- (iii) the heat of reaction in the carbamate condenser is more efficiently recovered as **2.5–4 MPa steam**, which is directly usable in the LP decomposition reboiler.

However, Snamprogetti is preferred for **very large single-train plants** (> 3500 t/day) because its higher NH₃:CO₂ ratio yields a higher per-pass conversion and the two recycle loops allow finer control.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist for the **last 30 minutes** before the exam.

- **Urea formula:** $(NH_2)_2CO$, **MW = 60.06**, **N% = 46.6 %** (highest of solid fertilisers).
- **Two-step reaction:** $2NH_3 + CO_2 \to NH_2COONH_4 \to (NH_2)_2CO + H_2O$ — **always mention the carbamate intermediate** in flow-sheet descriptions.
- **Overall ΔH ≈ −101.5 kJ/mol** (exothermic); ΔH(carbamate) = −117; ΔH(dehydration) = +15.5.
- **Operating window:** 140–200 bar; 170–200 °C; NH₃:CO₂ = 2–4:1.
- **Per-pass CO₂ conversion:** 35–65 % (depends on stripping technology).
- **Biuret** forms above 135 °C; must be **kept < 1.5 wt %** in fertiliser-grade; < 0.3 % in feed-grade.
- **Stoichiometric consumption per kg urea:** 0.567 kg NH₃ + 0.733 kg CO₂ → 1.0 kg urea + 0.300 kg water.
- **Total recycle** is essential for economic viability — Stamicarbon and Snamprogetti are the two dominant modern variants.
- **Materials of construction:** SS 316 L (urea grade) with **silver lining** in critical zones; titanium or zirconium in extreme service.
- **Corrosion control:** deoxygenate CO₂ feed to < 50 ppm; minimise water in synthesis loop.
- **Applications (in order of volume):** fertiliser > AdBlue/DEF > urea–formaldehyde resins > animal feed > cosmetics > explosives (urea nitrate).
- **Wöhler synthesis (1828):** first organic compound synthesised from inorganic precursors — historical milestone often asked as a 1-mark *bonus*.
- **KTU Pitfall:** "Mention Bosch–Meiser = total recycle; don't confuse with once-through partial-recycle plants."
- **KTU Pitfall:** "In numericals, distinguish *fresh feed* from *reactor feed*; recycle is internal."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Super Phosphate and Triple Super Phosphate, Natural Product Industries - Soap and Detergents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Single Super Phosphate and Triple Super Phosphate, Natural Product Industries - Soap and Detergents ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Phosphate Fertilizers: Single Super Phosphate (SSP) and Triple Super Phosphate (TSP) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phosphate Fertilizers (KTU 2024 Definition):** Phosphate fertilizers are chemical substances manufactured by acidulation of rock phosphate (fluorapatite/hydroxyapatite) with mineral acids, producing plant-available phosphorus compounds (mainly monocalcium phosphate). #### Single Super Phosphate (SSP) **Formal Definition:** Single Super Phosphate is the *first manufactured chemical fertilizer* in the world, produced by the reaction of finely ground rock phosphate (RP) with **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** to yield a mixture of **monocalcium phosphate monohydrate** and **calcium sulfate (gypsum)**. The guaranteed P₂O₅ content is **16–20 % w/w** ("Normal" or "Run-of-Pile" SSP) and **22 % w/w** for enriched/granulated SSP. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of SSP like making a "salted butter" – you take a hard, insoluble rock (the phosphate mineral, which plants cannot directly absorb) and react it with a strong acid (sulfuric acid), which "chews up" the rock and releases a water-soluble, plant-friendly form of phosphorus. The leftover calcium sulfate acts as a useful secondary nutrient (sulfur + calcium) — much like a "by-product ingredient" in cooking that also adds value. #### Triple Super Phosphate (TSP) **Formal Definition:** Triple Super Phosphate is a *high-analysis phosphate fertilizer* obtained by the reaction of rock phosphate with **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)** instead of sulfuric acid, yielding essentially pure **monocalcium phosphate** (MCP). The P₂O₅ content is **44–48 % w/w**, approximately *three times* that of SSP (hence the name "Triple"). **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** If SSP is "diluted orange juice made with water," then TSP is the "concentrated juice." By using phosphoric acid (instead of sulfuric acid) we avoid the gypsum ballast — the product is almost pure phosphorus nutrient, making it cheaper to transport per unit P₂O₅, but more expensive to manufacture because H₃PO₄ itself must first be made. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Highlight — "Why the name Triple?":** The P₂O₅ content of TSP (~46 %) is roughly 3 × the P₂O₅ content of SSP (~16 %). The factor "Triple" refers to the *concentration*, NOT the stoichiometry of any reaction. --- ### 1.2 Natural Product Industries: Soaps and Detergents #### Soap **Formal Definition:** Soap is the *water-soluble sodium or potassium salt of a higher (C₁₂–C₁₈) fatty acid*, produced industrially by **saponification** of natural fats and oils (triglycerides) with an alkali hydroxide (NaOH for hard soap, KOH for soft/liquid soap). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition — Saponification:** A hydrolysis reaction in which an ester (fat/oil triglyceride) reacts with a strong alkali to yield the alkali salt of the fatty acid (soap) and glycerol as by-product. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine a fat molecule as a "tadpole" — it has a water-loving head (the glycerol part) and three long, water-hating tails (the fatty acid chains). When you boil this with caustic soda, the alkali "snips off" the three tails, glues a sodium handle to each tail (forming soap), and releases the head (glycerol). The "tadpole" is now three tadpoles with sodium heads that love water but still have oil-loving tails — perfect for trapping grease in water! #### Detergent **Formal Definition:** A detergent is a *synthetic surfactant* (surface-active agent) that lowers the surface tension of water, enhances wetting, and emulsifies soils, formulated into a consumer product by combining surfactants with **builders, fillers, optical brighteners, and enzymes**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Highlight:** The work-horse anionic surfactant of modern detergent powders is **Linear Alkyl Benzene Sulphonic Acid (LABSA)** — derived from Linear Alkyl Benzene (LAB) via sulphation. Branched-chain analogues (ABS, used pre-1965) were banned because of poor biodegradability — a classic KTU exam point on *green chemistry*. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** If soap is a "handmade broom" that only works well in soft water, detergents are a "modern vacuum cleaner with a built-in water softener" — the surfactant (LABSA) is the cleaning head, while the *builder* (sodium tripolyphosphate or zeolite) is the on-board water-softener, allowing the machine to function in hard water too. --- ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Metric | SSP | TSP | Soap | Detergent | |---|---|---|---|---| | **P₂O₅ % w/w** | **16–20 %** | **44–48 %** | — | — | | **Free H₃PO₄ (max)** | < 4 % | < 4 % | — | — | | **Moisture (max)** | 12 % | 5–8 % | — | — | | **pH (1 % soln.)** | 1.8–2.5 | 2.0–3.0 | 9–10 | 9–11 | | **Biodegradability** | — | — | High | Variable (LAB-based = high) | | **Active matter (LAS)** | — | — | — | 15–25 % | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Chemistry of SSP Manufacture The overall reaction of rock phosphate (fluorapatite, 3Ca₃(PO₄)₂ · CaF₂) with sulphuric acid proceeds in **two conceptual stages**: **Stage 1 — Acid attack on fluorapatite (liberation of HF):** $$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{CaF}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{HF}$$ **Stage 2 — Secondary attack of HF on silica (impurity in RP):** $$4\text{HF} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiF}_4\uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Silica removal** in the rock phosphate is *critical* — otherwise SiF₄ hydrolyses back in the den to corrosive H₂SiF₆ that damages equipment. Hence RP containing **> 5 % SiO₂** is unsuitable for SSP. The *industrial* overall reaction is written in the **hydrated form** (with 5 H₂O on the LHS and 1 H₂O of crystallisation on the MCP): $$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 5\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2(\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O})$$ **Key process steps (Crawford-style, KTU standard):** 1. **Crushing & grinding** RP to –100 mesh (BSS). 2. **Weighing & proportioning** RP : H₂SO₄ (50–55 % strength) — usually 1 : 0.55–0.65. 3. **Mixing** in a Broadfield or *den* (conical brick-lined pit) for 1–5 min — exothermic; temperature rises to **100–120 °C**. 4. **"Denning"** — solidification in the den (10–30 min) as MCP crystallises. 5. **Curing** in storage pile for 2–6 weeks → un-reacted RP reacts with residual free acid; final P₂O₅ becomes citrate-soluble. 6. **Granulation / milling** → SSP powder or granules. > [!NOTE] > **Why "cure" for 2–6 weeks?** The freshly made SSP contains free H₃PO₄, which is *harmful* to seed germination. During curing, residual RP continues to react with this free acid, raising water-soluble P₂O₅ and lowering free acidity to < 4 %. --- ### 2.2 Chemistry of TSP Manufacture The reaction of rock phosphate with phosphoric acid (typically 50–55 % P₂O₅ strength, called "merchant grade acid"): **Dry (anhydrous) form:** $$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2$$ **Hydrated industrial form (more realistic):** $$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stoichiometric ratio:** Unlike SSP (where H₂SO₄ supplies only 1/3 of the proton needs), TSP uses **4 mol H₃PO₄ per mol Ca₃(PO₄)₂**. This makes TSP manufacture dependent on a *prior* wet-process H₃PO₄ plant — the major cost driver. **Process flow variants:** 1. **Run-of-Pile (ROP) TSP** — acid + RP mixed in a cone mixer, cured in piles (analogous to SSP, no granulation). 2. **Slurry Process (Dorr-Oliver, Prayon)** — acid + RP reacted in a series of tanks to form a slurry, which is then sprayed into a granulator. 3. **Hemihydrate / Dihydrate route** — controlled crystallisation of CaSO₄·½H₂O or CaSO₄·2H₂O to maximise P₂O₅ yield. --- ### 2.3 Chemistry of Saponification A triglyceride (fat/oil) is the triester of glycerol with three fatty acid chains (R₁, R₂, R₃, typically C₁₂–C₁₈): $$\begin{aligned} \text{CH}_2\text{OOC-R}_1 \\ \text{CH OOC-R}_2 \\ \text{CH}_2\text{OOC-R}_3 \end{aligned} + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{OH} \cdot \text{CHOH} \cdot \text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{R}_1\text{COONa} + \text{R}_2\text{COONa} + \text{R}_3\text{COONa}$$ **The reaction is multi-step** (the 3 ester groups hydrolyse sequentially), and the *average* saponification is written compactly as: $$(\text{RCOO})_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 + 3\text{RCOONa}$$ - **NaOH (caustic soda)** → hard bar soap (Toilet/Laundry). - **KOH (caustic potash)** → soft, gel soap (shaving cream, liquid hand wash). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Important Definition — Saponification Value (S.V.):** The number of mg of KOH required to saponify 1 g of fat/oil. *Higher S.V. ⇒ shorter-chain fatty acids* (e.g., coconut oil: 250, castor oil: 180, tallow: 195). --- ### 2.4 Chemistry of Detergent Manufacture (LABSA Route) The dominant anionic surfactant — **Linear Alkyl Benzene Sulphonic Acid (LABSA)** — is made by a three-step route: **Step 1 — Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene with linear olefins / alkyl chlorides (using HF or AlCl₃):** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{R-Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{AlCl}_3} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-R} + \text{HCl}$$ **Step 2 — Sulphation of LAB with SO₃ (modern) or oleum (older):** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-R} + \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{SO}_3\text{H})\text{-R}$$ **Step 3 — Neutralisation with NaOH:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{SO}_3\text{H})\text{-R} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{SO}_3\text{Na})\text{-R} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The product **Linear Alkyl Benzene Sulphonate (LABS / LAS)** is the work-horse surfactant of ~70 % of all detergent powders worldwide. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **must-know** for KTU Semester 5 PCCHT502 examinations. Memorise the reaction stoichiometries and the S.V. formula. | # | Formula / Reaction | Engineering Use | Key Constant | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 5\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | SSP manufacture | Acid/RP ratio ≈ 0.6 | | 2 | $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | TSP manufacture | P₂O₅ 44–48 % | | 3 | $\text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{F} + 5\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 5\text{CaSO}_4 + 3\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{HF}$ | Wet-process H₃PO₄ | Digestion temp 70–80 °C | | 4 | $\text{Fat} + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\text{RCOONa}$ | Saponification | NaOH/KOH = alkali | | 5 | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-R} + \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{SO}_3\text{H})\text{-R}$ | LABSA manufacture | SO₃/LAB molar ratio ≈ 1.05 | | 6 | $\text{S.V.} = \dfrac{56.1 \times N \times V}{m}$ | Quality control of fat | $56.1 = $ MW of KOH | | 7 | $\text{Yield of soap} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of dry soap} \times 100}{\text{Mass of fat taken}}$ | Process economics | — | | 8 | $\text{HLB} = 20 \times \dfrac{M_h}{M_h + M_l}$ | Detergent surfactant selection | $M_h$ = mass of hydrophilic part | | 9 | $\text{Active matter} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 288.4}{W}$ (for LAS) | Detergent QC | 288.4 = MW of LAS-Na | > [!NOTE] > **S.V. units:** 56.1 is the *equivalent weight of KOH* (not molecular weight 56.11); this is the standard IUPAC constant used in all saponification-value calculations. Use **V in mL of standard acid**, **N = normality of HCl back-titrated**, **m = mass of fat in g**. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SSP + TSP** provide ~80 % of the global phosphate fertiliser demand — essential for cereal yields (rice, wheat). KTU 2024 highlights the *depletion of high-grade RP ores* (Morocco holds ~70 % of world reserves), driving research into *SSP recycling from sewage sludge*. - **Soap bars** (toilet grade) are a benchmark of KTU consumer-product industry studies — India is the world's second-largest soap market after the USA. - **Detergent powders** are a *strategic petrochemical product* — the switch from branched ABS to linear LAS in 1965 is the textbook example of a "design-for-environment" success, a key theme in **Engineering Chemistry (KTU EST130)** and now extended into **Sustainable Engineering (KTU EST200)**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Acid Requirement for SSP > [!NOTE] > **Problem context (KTU 2024 typical numerical):** Calculate the mass of 50 % w/w H₂SO₄ required to digest 1000 kg of rock phosphate containing 30 % P₂O₅ (as tricalcium phosphate, Ca₃(PO₄)₂). **Step 1 — Mass of Ca₃(PO₄)₂ present in 1000 kg of RP (30 % P₂O₅ basis).** We first find the equivalent mass of Ca₃(PO₄)₂ corresponding to 30 % P₂O₅. Molecular weights: - Ca₃(PO₄)₂ = 3(40) + 2(31) + 8(16) = 120 + 62 + 128 = **310 g/mol** - P₂O₅ = 2(31) + 5(16) = 62 + 80 = **142 g/mol** One mole of Ca₃(PO₄)₂ contains 1 mole of P₂O₅. So 310 g of Ca₃(PO₄)₂ ↔ 142 g of P₂O₅. $$\text{Mass of Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 = \text{Mass of RP} \times \frac{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ fraction}}{\text{Conversion factor}}$$ $$\text{Mass of Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 = 1000 \times 0.30 \times \frac{310}{142}$$ **Step 2 — Numerical evaluation.** $$\frac{310}{142} = 2.1831 \text{ (dimensionless, mass ratio)}$$ $$\text{Mass of Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 = 1000 \times 0.30 \times 2.1831 = 654.93 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Moles of Ca₃(PO₄)₂.** $$n_{\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2} = \frac{654.93 \text{ kg}}{310 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 2.1127 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 4 — Stoichiometric moles of H₂SO₄ required.** From the SSP reaction: 1 mol Ca₃(PO₄)₂ requires 2 mol H₂SO₄. $$n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 2 \times 2.1127 = 4.2254 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 5 — Mass of pure H₂SO₄ required.** Molecular weight of H₂SO₄ = 2(1) + 32 + 4(16) = **98 g/mol**. $$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\text{pure}} = 4.2254 \text{ kmol} \times 98 \text{ kg/kmol} = 414.09 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 6 — Mass of 50 % commercial acid.** For 50 % w/w acid, the mass of solution = mass of pure acid / 0.50. $$m_{\text{acid solution}} = \frac{414.09}{0.50} = 828.18 \text{ kg}$$ **Final answer (boxed for KTU answer-script):** > $$\boxed{\text{Mass of 50 \% H}_2\text{SO}_4 \approx 828.2 \text{ kg per 1000 kg of RP}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Industrial reality check:** KTU examiners typically award full marks for the *stoichiometric* value. In practice, a **5–10 % excess** acid is used to ensure complete acidulation — so the *practical* acid mass would be 828.2 × 1.08 ≈ **894 kg**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Saponification Value (S.V.) from Titration Data > [!NOTE] > **Problem context:** 2.0 g of coconut oil is refluxed with 25 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH. The excess KOH is back-titrated with 0.5 N HCl — titre = 10.0 mL. A blank titration required 25.0 mL of 0.5 N HCl. Calculate the S.V. of the oil. **Step 1 — Saponification Value Formula (KTU standard).** $$\text{S.V.} = \frac{56.1 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{HCl}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$$ where: $V_b$ = blank titre, $V_s$ = sample titre, $N_{\text{HCl}}$ = normality, $m_{\text{oil}}$ = mass of oil (g). **Step 2 — Substitute the given values.** - $V_b = 25.0$ mL (blank) - $V_s = 10.0$ mL (sample) - $N_{\text{HCl}} = 0.5$ N - $m_{\text{oil}} = 2.0$ g - $56.1$ = equivalent weight of KOH (KTU constant). **Step 3 — Calculate the difference in titre (the "KOH actually consumed by the oil").** $$V_b - V_s = 25.0 - 10.0 = 15.0 \text{ mL}$$ This 15.0 mL of 0.5 N HCl is equivalent to the 0.5 N KOH that was *consumed* by the fat during saponification. **Step 4 — Apply the formula.** $$\text{S.V.} = \frac{56.1 \times 15.0 \times 0.5}{2.0}$$ **Step 5 — Numerator and denominator separation.** $$\text{Numerator} = 56.1 \times 15.0 \times 0.5 = 56.1 \times 7.5 = 420.75$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 2.0$$ $$\text{S.V.} = \frac{420.75}{2.0} = 210.375$$ **Final answer (boxed):** > $$\boxed{\text{S.V. of coconut oil} \approx 210.4 \text{ mg KOH/g oil}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Range check (KTU 2024 quality parameter):** Standard literature value for coconut oil S.V. = **248–265 mg KOH/g**. Our value of 210 is *low* — this would indicate an *adulterated* oil (e.g., mixed with mineral oil, which has S.V. ≈ 0). This is a frequent KTU exam follow-up question: "Comment on the quality of the oil." Always cross-check S.V. with the literature range! --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Comparison | Process / Aspect | **SSP** | **TSP** | **Soap (Hot process)** | **Detergent (LAS route)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Raw material** | Rock Phosphate (RP) + H₂SO₄ | RP + H₃PO₄ | Fats/oils + NaOH | LAB + SO₃ + NaOH | | **Key reaction temperature** | 100–120 °C | 80–110 °C | 80–100 °C (boiling with open steam) | 50–55 °C (sulphonation) | | **Acid/alkali concentration** | 50–55 % H₂SO₄ | 50–55 % P₂O₅ (H₃PO₄) | 30–40 % NaOH (aq.) | SO₃ gas (dilute) | | **Active ingredient** | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O | R-COONa (fatty acid salt) | C₁₂-LAS-Na | | **By-product** | CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum) | CaSO₄ (in H₃PO₄ stage) | Glycerol | H₂SO₄ (from SO₃) | | **End pH (1 % soln)** | 1.8–2.5 | 2.0–3.0 | 9.0–10.0 | 9.0–11.0 | | **Capital cost** | Low | High (H₃PO₄ plant) | Low | High | | **Shelf life** | 6–12 months (acidic) | 6–12 months | 24 months | 36 months | | **Energy intensity (GJ/t)** | ~1.5 | ~3.0 | ~6.0 (steam for saponification) | ~4.5 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Tip:** When the question says "*calculate the H₃PO₄ required for TSP*", the moles of H₃PO₄ = 4 × moles of Ca₃(PO₄)₂. For SSP, the moles of H₂SO₄ = 2 × moles of Ca₃(PO₄)₂. Students commonly use 1:1 stoichiometry — a guaranteed 2-mark loss. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: SSP Manufacture ```mermaid flowchart TD rp1[Rock Phosphate
Storage Silo] grinder[Crusher and
Ball Mill

Proportioner] mixer[Broadfield Mixer

Solidification

Scrape out
cured mass] pile[Curing Pile

or Pulveriser] sspout[SSP Product
16 to 20 percent P2O5] reco[HF Recovery
Scrubber
SiF4 abatement] rp1 --> grinder grinder --> weigh weigh --> mixer acidtank --> acidmeter acidmeter --> mixer mixer -->|exothermic 100-120 C| den den --> cutter cutter --> pile pile --> gran gran --> sspout mixer -. HF/SiF4 vapors .-> reco ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: TSP Manufacture (Slurry Route – Prayon) ```mermaid flowchart TD rpa[Rock Phosphate

Recycle to Granulator] rpa --> attack acidhpa --> attack attack --> slurry slurry --> evap evap --> gran1 gran1 --> dry1 dry1 --> screen screen -->|on-spec| cool cool --> tspprod screen -->|oversize/unfines| recycle recycle --> gran1 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Soap Manufacture (Hot Process) ```mermaid flowchart TD f1[Fat or Oil

Chloride C12] alcl[AlCl3
