Powder] bz --> alky labcl --> alky alcl --> alky alky --> neulab neulab --> sulfo so3 --> sulfo sulfo --> labsa labsa --> neut neut --> las las --> crutch crutch --> spray spray --> powd ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Engineering Insight:** The "Spray Dryer" tower in the LAS route operates with **co-current hot air (400 °C inlet, 100 °C outlet)** to produce *hollow spherical beads* of detergent powder. This is the key reason detergent powders are *dust-free* and free-flowing — a direct exam-favourite question on *process intensification*. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between "Single Super Phosphate (SSP)" and "Triple Super Phosphate (TSP)" with respect to (i) the acid used for acidulation, (ii) P₂O₅ content, and (iii) the major by-product formed.* **Model Answer (KTU Key pattern):** | Feature | SSP | TSP | |---|---|---| | (i) Acid used | Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) | Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) | | (ii) P₂O₅ content | 16–20 % w/w | 44–48 % w/w | | (iii) Major by-product | Calcium sulphate (gypsum) — CaSO₄·2H₂O | Calcium sulphate (formed in *H₃PO₄* plant) — but TSP itself yields *no* by-product salt | > [!NOTE] > **Common student error:** Writing "CaSO₄ is formed in TSP manufacture" — this is true in the *upstream H₃PO₄ plant*, but the *TSP reaction itself* (Ca₃(PO₄)₂ + 4H₃PO₄) produces **only monocalcium phosphate** as solid product, with no gypsum ballast — this is precisely the reason TSP has 3× the P₂O₅ of SSP. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define "Saponification". Why is NaOH used for toilet soap and KOH for shaving cream?* **Model Answer:** **Saponification (KTU Definition):** The alkaline hydrolysis of an ester (triglyceride of a fat or oil) by a strong alkali (NaOH or KOH) to yield the alkali metal salt of higher fatty acids (i.e., soap) and glycerol. **Reason for alkali choice:** - **NaOH (caustic soda) → Sodium salt of fatty acid** → solid, hard bar (high melting, ~230–250 °C). Suitable for toilet/laundry bars that must retain shape in warm water. - **KOH (caustic potash) → Potassium salt of fatty acid** → soft, gel-like consistency, more water-soluble. Suitable for *shaving cream* (must lather readily and stay soft on the skin) and *liquid hand wash* soaps. > [!NOTE] > **Hardness/softness rule (KTU Memory Aid):** Na-soaps = solid, K-soaps = soft. Mnemonic — **"Na-Solid, K-Keep Soft"**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the industrial manufacture of Single Super Phosphate (SSP) starting from rock phosphate, with a neat flow diagram. State the chemistry of the process and write the key operating parameters. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Raw material preparation:** Rock phosphate (fluorapatite, 3Ca₃(PO₄)₂·CaF₂) is crushed and ground in a *ball mill* to a fineness of **–100 mesh BSS** (≈ 150 µm). This fine grinding is essential because the acidulation reaction is *heterogeneous* and the rate is limited by the surface area of the solid. **Step 2 — Acid preparation:** Commercial **50–55 % w/w H₂SO₄** is metered from a lead-lined storage tank. Concentration is critical: - Acid that is *too dilute* (e.g., 30 %) → crystallisation of MCP is slow, den is pasty. - Acid that is *too concentrated* (e.g., 70 %) → releases SO₃ fumes, corrodes equipment. **Step 3 — Mixing in the Broadfield mixer / den:** Rock phosphate and 50–55 % H₂SO₄ are mixed in the ratio **1 : 0.55–0.65** (mass of RP to mass of acid) for **1–5 minutes** in a *Broadfield mixer* (a horizontal pug-mill) or directly in the *den* (a brick-lined conical pit, 5–10 m diameter). The reaction is **highly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ –300 kJ/mol of Ca₃(PO₄)₂); the temperature of the mass rises to **100–120 °C** without external heating. **Step 4 — Chemistry of the reaction:** $$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 5\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The HF generated (from fluorapatite impurity) reacts with silica present in the rock: $$4\text{HF} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiF}_4\uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 5 — Denning / Solidification:** The semi-fluid mass is allowed to stand in the den for **10–30 min**; it solidifies as **monocalcium phosphate monohydrate (MCP) crystals** form and gypsum co-crystallises as CaSO₄·2H₂O. The gases (HF, SiF₄, water vapour) are scrubbed in a *wet scrubber* using dilute H₂SiF₆. **Step 6 — Curing (the most time-consuming step):** The den mass is cut out by a *rotary cutter* and transferred to a *curing pile* for **2–6 weeks**. During this period: - Un-reacted rock phosphate (typically 5–15 %) reacts with the free H₃PO₄ present. - The *water-soluble* P₂O₅ content (WSP) rises from ~14 % to ~16–18 %. - The *citrate-soluble* P₂O₅ (i.e., total available P₂O₅) is enriched. **Step 7 — Granulation / milling:** The cured SSP is pulverised or granulated. Granulated SSP ("GSSP") has 22 % P₂O₅ (enriched grade) and is free-flowing, suitable for mechanical sowing. **Key operating parameters (Box):** > $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \text{RP grind} &= -100 \text{ mesh BSS} \\ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ strength} &= 50{-}55 \text{ \%} \\ \text{RP : Acid mass ratio} &= 1 : 0.55{-}0.65 \\ \text{Mixing time} &= 1{-}5 \text{ min} \\ \text{Den temperature} &= 100{-}120 \text{ °C} \\ \text{Curing time} &= 2{-}6 \text{ weeks} \\ \text{Final WSP} &= 16{-}18 \text{ \% P}_2\text{O}_5 \end{aligned}}$$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Batch Kettle Process** of soap manufacture. What are the criteria for selection of a fat/oil for soap-making? **Model Solution:** **Process Steps:** 1. **Charging the kettle:** A blend of fats/oils (e.g., tallow 80 % + coconut oil 20 %) is melted in an open steam-jacketed kettle (capacity 30,000 – 100,000 kg). The blend is chosen to balance hardness (tallow) and lathering (coconut). 2. **Saponification (Pasting stage):** Caustic soda (≈ 30–40 % aqueous, slight excess of 3–5 %) is added in portions. The mixture is boiled with open steam for **2–4 hours**. The fat first emulsifies ("pasting"), then gradually thickens. 3. **Graining (Salting out):** Solid **NaCl** is added, breaking the emulsion. Soap (less dense) rises to the top; spent lye (containing glycerol, excess alkali, salt, impurities) settles at the bottom. The lye is drained and sent to **glycerol recovery** (lime treatment → crude glycerol → distillation). 4. **Washing & Strengthening:** The soap is boiled with fresh water + NaOH for 1 hour to remove last traces of glycerol and impurities. This is repeated 2–3 times. 5. **Fitting (Cooling and settling):** Steam is shut off, kettle is closed, soap cools slowly for 3–5 days. Three layers form: - **Top (Neat soap)** — 70 % fatty acid, 30 % water; the premium grade. - **Middle (Nigre)** — darker, contains iron impurities; re-boiled. - **Bottom** — water + salts. 6. **Crutching:** Neat soap is mixed with additives — perfume, colour, TiO₂ (whitener), optical brightener, glycerol (for transparency in "glycerine soap") — in a **crutcher** (mechanical mixer). 7. **Framing / Milling / Plodding:** The crutched soap is solidified into large slabs, cut into chips, dried in a tunnel, then passed through a **roller mill** (homogenises texture) and a **plodder** (extrudes continuous bar). Bars are cut, stamped, and packed. **Selection criteria for fats/oils (Boxed):** > $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \text{(i) Saponification Value (S.V.)} &\rightarrow 200{-}250 \text{ mg KOH/g} \\ \text{(ii) Iodine Value (I.V.)} &\rightarrow < 50 \text{ for hard soap} \\ \text{(iii) Titre (Titer test)} &\rightarrow 38{-}42 \text{ °C for hardness} \\ \text{(iv) Acid Value} &\rightarrow < 5 \text{ (otherwise rancid)} \\ \text{(v) Colour, Odour} &\rightarrow \text{Refined, neutral} \\ \text{(vi) Cost \& Availability} &\rightarrow \text{Local supply} \end{aligned}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Iodine Value rule of thumb (KTU 2024):** *Higher I.V. = more unsaturation = softer soap.* Coconut oil has low I.V. (10) → hard, white soap. Linseed oil has high I.V. (180) → unsuitable; produces soft, sticky soap that goes rancid. --- #### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the manufacture of **Triple Super Phosphate (TSP)** by the *slurry process*. Compare its merits over SSP. **Model Solution:** **TSP — Slurry Process (Prayon / Dorr-Oliver route):** **Step 1 — Acid and rock preparation:** Phosphoric acid of strength **50–55 % P₂O₅** ("merchant grade" acid) is charged to the *attack tank*. Rock phosphate (finely ground) is fed separately. **Step 2 — Attack (Tank 1):** The mixture is agitated at **70–80 °C** for 1–2 hours. The acid attacks the RP, converting the insoluble fluorapatite into soluble monocalcium phosphate: $$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The slurry is then pumped to the *slurry tank* (Tank 2) for further reaction and ageing. **Step 3 — Slurry concentration / Evaporation:** Water is partially evaporated (or hot flue gas is used) to raise the solids content to **55–65 %**, making the slurry pumpable but not too dilute. **Step 4 — Granulation:** The concentrated slurry is sprayed onto a rolling bed of recycled fines in a *granulator* (e.g., spherodiser, drum granulator, or pug-mill). The droplets coat the seed particles, forming granules. **Step 5 — Drying & Sizing:** The wet granules are dried in a **rotary dryer** (hot flue gas 200–300 °C), then classified in vibrating screens. **Oversize** is crushed and recycled; **undersize (fines)** is recycled to the granulator; **on-spec** granules (2–4 mm) are cooled in a *rotary cooler* and stored. **Step 6 — Final product:** TSP with **44–48 % P₂O₅** (water-soluble), < 4 % free acid, < 5 % moisture. **Merits of TSP over SSP (Boxed):** > $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \text{(i) High P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ (46 \% vs 16 \%)} &\rightarrow \text{3× less transport cost per unit P}_2\text{O}_5 \\ \text{(ii) No gypsum ballast} &\rightarrow \text{lighter, no sulphur added} \\ \text{(iii) Lower storage volume} &\rightarrow \text{smaller godown} \\ \text{(iv) Suitable for long-distance export} &\rightarrow \text{granular TSP is a globally traded commodity} \\ \text{(v) Compatible with urea and DAP} &\rightarrow \text{used as NPK complex precursor} \\ \text{(vi) Disadvantage: needs H}_3\text{PO}_4 \text{ plant} &\rightarrow \text{higher capital cost} \end{aligned}}$$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the formulation of a modern detergent powder. What are the functions of (i) surfactant, (ii) builder, and (iii) optical brightener? **Model Solution:** A modern detergent powder is a *formulated mixture* of typically 8–12 ingredients. A typical *high-foam household powder* has the following composition (mass %): > $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \text{LAS (surfactant)} &= 15{-}20 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP, builder)} &= 25{-}35 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Sodium sulphate (filler)} &= 20{-}40 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Sodium silicate (anti-corrosion)} &= 5{-}10 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Soda ash (alkaline buffer)} &= 2{-}5 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Optical brightener} &= 0.1{-}0.3 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Enzyme (protease/amylase)} &= 0.5{-}1 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Perfume} &= 0.1{-}0.3 \text{ \%} \\ \text{Water (moisture)} &= 5{-}10 \text{ \%} \end{aligned}}$$ **Functions of the three key ingredients:** **(i) Surfactant — Linear Alkyl Benzene Sulphonate (LAS, 15–20 %):** - The **primary cleaning agent** — reduces surface tension of water from 72 mN/m to ~30 mN/m. - Molecule has a **hydrophilic head** (–SO₃Na) and a **hydrophobic tail** (C₁₂ linear alkyl). - It **emulsifies** oily soil, **disperses** solid dirt, and **wets** fabric fibres. - Wetting time for cotton fabric drops from ~60 s (water alone) to ~5 s (0.1 % LAS). **(ii) Builder — Sodium Tripolyphosphate (STPP, 25–35 %):** - **Sequesters** Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions of hard water (chelation), preventing their precipitation as carbonate scale on fabric. - Maintains **alkaline pH (9–10)**, which helps saponify grease and enhances surfactant efficiency. - **Anti-redeposition** — keeps removed soil suspended so it does not re-deposit on fabric. - *Modern (eco-friendly) alternatives:* **Zeolite 4A** (Na₁₂Al₁₂Si₁₂O₄₈·27H₂O) and **sodium citrate** — used in *phosphate-free* detergents, especially in Europe and Japan (KTU 2024: eutrophication concern). **(iii) Optical Brightener — Stilbene derivative, 0.1–0.3 %:** - Also called **Fluorescent Whitening Agent (FWA)** or "OBA". - **Absorbs UV light (~350 nm)** and **re-emits blue light (~440 nm)**. - Blue emission **cancels the yellow tint** of aged fabric, making whites appear "whiter than white". - Common FWA: **DASD (Diamino-Stilbene-Disulphonic acid) disodium salt** — Tinopal CBS-X, Blankophor. - Note: FWA is *not* a cleaning agent — it is purely a *cosmetic additive*. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 "Green" Trend:** *Zeolite 4A* has replaced STPP in many countries because phosphate-rich wash-water causes **eutrophication** of lakes (algal blooms). Zeolite 4A is non-toxic, biodegradable, and equally good at water softening — but it does not provide the same anti-redeposition benefit. Modern formulations use *zeolite + sodium carbonate + polycarboxylate polymer* as a "phosphate-free builder cocktail." > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points:** > 1. **Conflating SSP and TSP:** Students frequently state "gypsum is a by-product of TSP manufacture." Strictly, gypsum is a by-product of the *wet-process H₃PO₄* plant that *feeds* TSP — the *TSP reaction itself* yields only MCP. Examiner will deduct 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the "linear" in LAS:** Stating "ABS" instead of "LABS/LAS" marks you out of the green-chemistry point. *Always* write "linear" alkyl benzene. > 3. **Saponification value calculation:** Using MW of KOH (56.11) instead of equivalent weight (56.1) — a numerical error of 0.02 % but flagged as a *conceptual error* by the KTU valuation key. > 4. **S.V. units:** Writing "S.V. = 210.4 g" instead of "210.4 mg KOH/g" — guaranteed 1-mark cut. > 5. **Detergent builder question:** Writing "STPP removes stains" instead of "sequesters Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ (water softening)" — examiner is looking for the *chelation* mechanism. > 6. **Forgetting curing step in SSP:** Skipping the *2–6 weeks curing* step in the SSP answer = 2-mark loss. Curing is the *quality-improving* step that converts free H₃PO₄ → MCP. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024, Module 2):** - ✅ **SSP** = RP + H₂SO₄ → Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O + CaSO₄·2H₂O; **16–20 % P₂O₅**; 2-week curing is the quality-determining step. - ✅ **TSP** = RP + H₃PO₄ → Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O; **44–48 % P₂O₅**; *no* gypsum ballast in the product. - ✅ **"Triple" = 3× the P₂O₅** of SSP, *not* a stoichiometric factor of 3. - ✅ **Saponification** = Fat + 3 NaOH → 3 R-COONa + glycerol; Na-soap = hard, K-soap = soft. - ✅ **S.V. = (56.1 × N × V_blank − sample) / m_oil**; standard range for coconut oil ≈ 248–265. - ✅ **LAS = Linear Alkyl Benzene Sulphonate** = benzene + C₁₂ olefin → LAB → sulphonation with SO₃ → neutralisation with NaOH. - ✅ **Builder = STPP or Zeolite 4A** (water softener, alkaline pH buffer, anti-redeposition). - ✅ **Optical brightener = FWA** (UV absorber → blue emitter) — *cosmetic*, not a cleaning agent. - ✅ **"Soda-Silicate" in detergent = anti-corrosion** of washing-machine drum (not a cleaning agent). - ✅ **KTU Green-chemistry landmark:** 1965 — switch from branched **ABS** to linear **LAS** for biodegradability. - ✅ **Detergent powder is made by spray-drying** a slurry in a *co-current* hot-air tower; produces hollow spheres, free-flowing. - ✅ **Soap ≠ detergent in hard water:** soap forms *insoluble scum* (Ca/Mg stearate); detergent does not (because Ca/Mg-LAS is soluble).

topics.title: Glycerine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Glycerine # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Glycerine ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Glycerine** (also known as **Glycerol** or **Glycerin**) is a simple **trihydric alcohol** with the molecular formula **C₃H₈O₃** and the IUPAC name **Propane-1,2,3-triol**. It is the simplest and most commercially significant triol, characterized by three hydroxyl (-OH) groups attached to a three-carbon aliphatic backbone. Glycerine is a **viscous, colorless, odorless, hygroscopic, and sweet-tasting** liquid that is completely miscible with water and many polar solvents due to extensive hydrogen bonding. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In Chemical Technology, glycerine is studied as a **major byproduct of the oleochemical industry** (saponification of fats and transesterification of oils) and as a **key synthetic intermediate** (epichlorohydrin route from propylene). Its downstream derivatives (nitroglycerin, alkyd resins, polyols) make it industrially critical. ## 1.2 Synonyms and Identification | Parameter | Value/Description | |---|---| | Common Names | Glycerine, Glycerol, Glycerin | | IUPAC Name | Propane-1,2,3-triol | | Molecular Formula | C₃H₈O₃ | | Molar Mass | **92.09 g/mol** | | CAS Number | 56-81-5 | | Functional Group | Trihydric alcohol (3 -OH groups) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of glycerine as the "molecular sponge" of the chemical world.** Just as a sponge soaks up water, glycerine has three "arms" (the three -OH groups) that grab and hold water molecules tightly through **hydrogen bonding**. This is why it feels *slippery and moist* and is used in: > - **Moisturizers** (to keep skin hydrated) > - **Toothpaste** (to prevent drying out) > - **Foods** (to retain moisture in cakes and cookies) Imagine a **three-pronged fork**, where each prong represents a hydroxyl group reaching out to grab a water molecule. Because all three prongs are attached to a small, light carbon backbone, glycerine is a small molecule that does an *enormous* job — just like a small, lightweight Swiss Army knife that performs many functions. ## 1.4 Key Physical Properties (Standard Metrics) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following are the **standard reference values** you must memorize for KTU 2024 board exams: - **Boiling Point:** **290 °C** (at 1 atm, with partial decomposition) - **Melting Point:** **17.8 °C** (exists as crystals in pure form) - **Density:** **1.261 g/cm³** at 20 °C - **Viscosity:** ~1.5 Pa·s at 20 °C (about 1500× more viscous than water) - **Refractive Index:** 1.4746 at 20 °C - **Flash Point:** 177 °C (open cup) - **Autoignition Temperature:** 400 °C - **Solubility:** Completely miscible with water, ethanol, methanol; insoluble in hydrocarbons. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **glycerine** (the commercial liquid mixture) with **glycerol** (the pure compound). In the laboratory sense, glycerol is the pure chemical; in industry, "glycerine" refers to a refined crude product (typically 99.5–99.7% pure). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sources of Glycerine Glycerine is obtained from **two broad categories** of sources: ### A. Natural Sources (Oleochemical) Glycerine is a structural component of **triglycerides** — the principal constituents of animal fats (tallow, lard) and vegetable oils (palm, soybean, coconut, rapeseed). On average, triglycerides yield **~10% glycerine by weight** upon hydrolysis or transesterification. The general triglyceride structure is: $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Lipase/H}^+} \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{Fatty Acids}$$ ### B. Synthetic Sources (Petrochemical) Glycerine is synthesized from **propylene** (a petroleum by-product) via the **Epichlorohydrin Route** (also called the **Shell process** or **Dow process**). ## 2.2 Manufacturing Routes — Step-by-Step Logic ### Route 1: From Fats and Oils (Saponification/Transesterification) **Step 1 — Saponification (Alkaline Hydrolysis):** Fats/oils are treated with **NaOH** (or KOH) at 80–100 °C to produce soap (sodium salt of fatty acids) and crude glycerine ("sweetwater"): $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{RCOONa (Soap)}$$ **Step 2 — Transesterification (Methanolysis):** For biodiesel production, oils are reacted with **methanol in the presence of NaOH/KOH catalyst** at 60–70 °C: $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 \text{ (Biodiesel)}$$ **Step 3 — Purification:** Crude glycerine is purified by: - **Acidulation** (with HCl to remove soaps as fatty acids) - **Neutralization, filtration, evaporation, distillation under vacuum** - **Decolorization** (activated carbon) - **Ion exchange** to yield **refined glycerine (USP grade)** ### Route 2: From Propylene — Epichlorohydrin Route The principal synthetic route involves **chlorination of propylene to allyl chloride**, followed by reaction with hypochlorous acid to form **dichlorohydrin**, and finally dehydrochlorination to give **epichlorohydrin**. Hydrolysis of epichlorohydrin yields glycerine. **Step-by-Step:** **Step A — Allyl Chloride Formation (Substitution Reaction):** $$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{500\,°\text{C}} \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$$ **Step B — Hypochlorination (Addition of HOCl):** $$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\!-\!\text{CHOH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \text{ (Dichlorohydrin)}$$ **Step C — Dehydrochlorination (Cyclization using Ca(OH)₂):** $$\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\!-\!\text{CHOH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} \underset{\text{Epichlorohydrin}}{\text{CH}_2\!-\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}} + \text{HCl}$$ (Here, the bracketed structure represents the epoxide ring: CH₂—CH—CH₂Cl with the oxygen bridge.) **Step D — Hydrolysis to Glycerine:** $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{OCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 \text{ (Glycerol)} + \text{HCl}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Reality:** Today, the **oleochemical route** dominates global glycerine production (~70%+) due to the **biodiesel boom**, making synthetic propylene-route glycerine economically less attractive. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Molecular Weight of Glycerol | $M = 3(12) + 8(1) + 3(16) = 92.09$ | g/mol | | 2 | Theoretical Yield from Triglyceride | 1 mol TG → 1 mol glycerol | 1:1 molar ratio | | 3 | Mass Yield (%) | $\%Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}}\times 100$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Glycerine content in fats | $\sim 10\text{–}12\%$ by mass | Empirical industrial value | | 5 | Saponification Equivalent (moles of KOH per g oil) | $S_V = \dfrac{56.1\,V}{W}$ | mg KOH/g | | 6 | Acrolein test (dehydration) | $\text{Glycerol} \xrightarrow{\text{KHSO}_4,\,200\,°\text{C}} \text{Acrolein} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Qualitative test | | 7 | Density correction | $\rho_T = \rho_{20} - 0.0007(T-20)$ | g/cm³, valid 15–30 °C | | 8 | Biodiesel glycerol byproduct | $\sim 10\%\ w/w$ of biodiesel | per 100 kg oil | | 9 | Nitroglycerin stoichiometry | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + 3\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Mixed acid (HNO₃+H₂SO₄) | ## 2.4 Industrial Applications and Real-World Utility - **Pharmaceuticals & Cosmetics:** humectant, solvent, emollient, lubricant - **Food Industry:** sweetener (sweetness ~60% of sucrose), humectant, solvent for flavors - **Tobacco:** humectant to prevent drying - **Explosives:** **Nitroglycerin** (glyceryl trinitrate) — the active component of dynamite - **Alkyd Resins:** for paints and coatings - **Polyurethane Polyols:** for foams - **Antifreeze:** non-toxic alternative to ethylene glycol - **Personal Care:** toothpastes, mouthwashes, soaps, lotions ## 2.5 Quality Specifications (USP / Pharmaceutical Grade) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Expected:** A question on the "specifications of refined glycerine" is a high-probability 3-mark item. | Property | USP Grade Requirement | |---|---| | Purity | ≥ 99.5% | | Water content | ≤ 0.5% | | Colour (APHA) | ≤ 10 | | Heavy metals (as Pb) | ≤ 5 ppm | | Ash content | ≤ 0.01% | | Chlorides (as Cl) | ≤ 10 ppm | | Density at 25 °C | ≥ 1.249 g/cm³ | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Mass of Glycerine from a Given Oil ### Problem Setup A saponification plant processes **1000 kg of coconut oil** (average molar mass of triglyceride = **800 g/mol**). Determine the **theoretical mass of glycerine** produced assuming complete hydrolysis. ### Step 1: Moles of Triglyceride The number of moles of triglyceride is calculated as: $$n_{\text{TG}} = \dfrac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Molar Mass}} = \dfrac{1000\,\text{kg}}{800\,\text{g/mol}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000\,\text{g}}{800\,\text{g/mol}}$$ $$n_{\text{TG}} = 1250\,\text{mol}$$ ### Step 2: Stoichiometric Conversion From the balanced saponification reaction: $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{RCOONa}$$ The stoichiometric ratio of **triglyceride : glycerol = 1 : 1**. Therefore: $$n_{\text{glycerol, theoretical}} = 1250\,\text{mol}$$ ### Step 3: Convert Moles to Mass $$m_{\text{glycerol}} = n \times M = 1250\,\text{mol} \times 92.09\,\text{g/mol}$$ $$m_{\text{glycerol}} = 115{,}112.5\,\text{g} \approx 115.11\,\text{kg}$$ ### Step 4: Interpretation Thus, the theoretical yield of glycerine is **~115.11 kg per 1000 kg of oil** (i.e., ~11.5% by mass), which aligns with the **empirical industrial value of 10–12%**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Glycerine Yield Percentage Calculation ### Problem Setup In an industrial batch, **500 kg of palm oil** (M = 850 g/mol) was saponified and produced **52 kg of refined glycerine**. Calculate the **percentage yield**. ### Step 1: Moles of Triglyceride $$n_{\text{TG}} = \dfrac{500{,}000\,\text{g}}{850\,\text{g/mol}} = 588.24\,\text{mol}$$ ### Step 2: Theoretical Mass of Glycerine $$m_{\text{theoretical}} = 588.24 \times 92.09 = 54{,}168.7\,\text{g} \approx 54.17\,\text{kg}$$ ### Step 3: Percentage Yield $$\%Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{52}{54.17} \times 100$$ $$\%Y = 95.99\% \approx 96\%$$ ### Conclusion This is an **excellent industrial yield** (>95% indicates a well-optimized process with minimal losses to soap-glycerine entrapment). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nitroglycerin Production Stoichiometry ### Problem Setup Calculate the mass of **nitroglycerin (C₃H₅N₃O₉)** produced from **92.09 g (1 mol)** of glycerine using the reaction: $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{HNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### Step 1: Molar Mass of Nitroglycerin $$M_{\text{NG}} = 3(12) + 5(1) + 3(14) + 9(16) = 36 + 5 + 42 + 144 = 227\,\text{g/mol}$$ ### Step 2: Stoichiometric Ratio Mole ratio: **C₃H₈O₃ : C₃H₅N₃O₉ = 1 : 1** Therefore, **1 mol of glycerine → 1 mol of nitroglycerin**. ### Step 3: Mass of Nitroglycerin $$m_{\text{NG}} = 1 \times 227 = 227\,\text{g}$$ ### Step 4: Mass Increase Ratio $$\text{Mass Gain Factor} = \dfrac{227}{92.09} = 2.465$$ **Interpretation:** Each gram of glycerine theoretically yields ~2.47 g of nitroglycerin, a powerful explosive (~1.6× more energetic than TNT by weight). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Glycerine Production Routes | Parameter | Oleochemical (Saponification) | Oleochemical (Transesterification) | Synthetic (Epichlorohydrin) | |---|---|---|---| | **Raw Material** | Natural fats/oils | Vegetable oils + methanol | Propylene + Cl₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | | **Byproduct** | Soap | Biodiesel | HCl, CaCl₂ | | **Purity of Crude** | 80–88% | 60–80% | 99%+ | | **Energy Demand** | Moderate | Moderate | High | | **Environmental Impact** | Low (renewable) | Low (renewable) | High (chlorinated waste) | | **Current Global Share** | ~25% | ~50% | ~5% (declining) | | **Cost Competitiveness** | Tied to oil prices | Tied to biodiesel economics | Tied to petro prices | | **Process Complexity** | Low–Medium | Low | High (multi-step) | ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Mass Balance Across the Glycerine Purification Train ### Problem Setup A crude glycerine stream from saponification contains **85% glycerol, 8% water, 5% NaCl, 2% fatty matter**. Calculate the **refined glycerine mass** from **10,000 kg of crude**, assuming a **97% recovery of glycerol** in the refined product, and **complete removal** of impurities. ### Step 1: Glycerol in Crude Feed $$m_{\text{glycerol, in}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.85 = 8{,}500\,\text{kg}$$ ### Step 2: Glycerol Recovered $$m_{\text{glycerol, recovered}} = 8{,}500 \times 0.97 = 8{,}245\,\text{kg}$$ ### Step 3: Refined Glycerine Mass (Purity = 99.5%) $$m_{\text{refined}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{glycerol, recovered}}}{\text{purity fraction}} = \dfrac{8{,}245}{0.995}$$ $$m_{\text{refined}} = 8{,}286.43\,\text{kg}$$ ### Step 4: Total Impurity Output $$m_{\text{impurities out}} = 10{,}000 - 8{,}286.43 = 1{,}713.57\,\text{kg}$$ (Composed of residual water, NaCl, fatty matter, and unrecovered glycerol losses.) # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Glycerine Production — Oleochemical Route (Saponification + Purification) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Fats and Oils] --> B[Saponification Reactor
NaOH 80 to 100 C] B --> C[Soap + Crude Glycerine
Sweetwater] C --> D[Gravity Separation] D --> E[Soap Outlet
To Drying] D --> F[Crude Sweetwater] F --> G[Acidulation
HCl pH 4 to 5] G --> H[Fatty Acid Removal
Skimming] H --> I[Neutralization
Alkali] I --> J[Filtration] J --> K[Vacuum Evaporation
Multi Effect] K --> L[Vacuum Distillation
180 to 200 C, 5 to 25 mbar] L --> M[Decolorization
Activated Carbon] M --> N[Ion Exchange Polish] N --> O[Refined Glycerine
USP Grade 99.5%] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Each block represents a unit operation. Arrows denote material flow. The **sweetwater** (crude aqueous glycerol, ~8–15% glycerol) is concentrated in **multi-effect evaporators** to ~80%, then vacuum-distilled to USP grade. ## 4.2 Synthetic Route — Epichlorohydrin Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Propylene from Cracker] --> B[Chlorination Reactor
500 C Substitution] B --> C[Allyl Chloride] C --> D[Hypochlorination Unit
HOCl Addition] D --> E[Dichlorohydrin] E --> F[Dehydrochlorination
Ca OH 2 Cyclization] F --> G[Epichlorohydrin] G --> H[Hydrolysis Reactor
NaOH Aqueous] H --> I[Crude Synthetic Glycerol] I --> J[Purification Train
Evap + Distill + Decolor] J --> K[Pure Glycerine
99.7% Synthetic] ``` ## 4.3 Application Tree — Downstream Derivatives of Glycerine ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Glycerine C3H8O3] --> B[Pharma and Cosmetics] A --> C[Food Industry] A --> D[Industrial] A --> E[Explosives] A --> F[Polymers] B --> B1[Toothpaste] B --> B2[Moisturizers] B --> B3[Syrups] C --> C1[Humectant in Cakes] C --> C2[Solvent for Flavors] C --> C3[Sweetener 60 percent Sucrose] D --> D1[Antifreeze] D --> D2[Plasticizer] D --> D3[Tobacco Humectant] E --> E1[Nitroglycerin] E --> E2[Dynamite] F --> F1[Alkyd Resins] F --> F2[Polyurethane Polyols] F --> F3[Epichlorohydrin for Epoxy] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Glycerine Process Flow Integration ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FeedSection[Feed Preparation] R1[Oil Storage Tank] --> R2[Heater 60 C] R2 --> R3[Mixer with Methanol] end subgraph ReactionSection[Reaction Zone] R3 --> S1[Transesterification Reactor] S1 --> S2[Separator: Glycerol Phase] S1 --> S3[Separator: Biodiesel Phase] end subgraph PurificationSection[Glycerol Refining] S2 --> P1[Neutralization HCl] P1 --> P2[Vacuum Evaporation] P2 --> P3[Distillation Column] P3 --> P4[Activated Carbon Filter] P4 --> P5[Refined Glycerine Storage] end subgraph UtilitySection[Utilities] U1[Steam Supply] -.-> P2 U2[Vacuum Pump] -.-> P3 U3[Cooling Water] -.-> P3 end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List any three physical properties of glycerine and mention the functional group responsible for its hygroscopic nature.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Viscous, colorless, odorless, sweet-tasting** liquid with a boiling point of **290 °C** and density **1.261 g/cm³**. (1 mark) 2. Completely **miscible with water and alcohol**; insoluble in hydrocarbons. (1 mark) 3. The **trihydric hydroxyl group (-OH × 3)** is responsible for its hygroscopic nature, as it forms extensive **hydrogen bonds** with water molecules. (1 mark) --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the "acrolein test"? Write the reaction involved.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **acrolein test** is a qualitative test for glycerol (and other polyols) in which the sample is heated with a **dehydrating agent** such as **KHSO₄ (potassium hydrogen sulfate)** or **P₂O₅** to ~200 °C. (1.5 marks) - A characteristic **pungent, irritating odor of acrolein** (CH₂=CH-CHO) confirms the presence of glycerol. (0.5 mark) - Reaction: (1 mark) $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{KHSO}_4,\,200\,°\text{C}} \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CHO} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]` **(a)** Describe in detail the **manufacture of glycerine from fats and oils by the saponification process**, with a neat flow diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The saponification process is the **traditional alkaline hydrolysis** of triglycerides (fats and oils) to yield soap and glycerine. The steps are: **Step 1 — Feed Preparation (1 mark):** Animal fats (tallow, lard) or vegetable oils (palm, coconut) are melted and clarified. Excess free fatty acids are removed to improve yield. **Step 2 — Saponification (2 marks):** The oil is charged into a **kettle** (open or closed) and treated with **8–12% NaOH solution** at **80–100 °C** with vigorous agitation for 4–6 hours: $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{RCOONa}$$ The reaction produces a homogeneous emulsion of **soap + crude glycerine (sweetwater)**. **Step 3 — Separation (1 mark):** The mixture is **salted out** by adding **NaCl** (common-salt process), causing soap to float and the denser glycerol-rich lye to settle at the bottom. The lower aqueous layer (containing 8–15% glycerol) is drawn off. **Step 4 — Purification of Crude Glycerine (2 marks):** - **Acidulation** with HCl converts residual soap back to free fatty acids (which are skimmed off). - **Filtration** removes suspended solids. - **Vacuum evaporation** concentrates the sweetwater to ~80% glycerol. - **Vacuum distillation** at 180–200 °C and 5–25 mbar gives technical-grade glycerol. - **Decolorization** with activated carbon and **ion exchange** yield USP-grade (99.5%+). **Step 5 — Storage and Dispatch (1 mark):** Refined glycerine is stored in **stainless-steel tanks** to avoid corrosion. **Flow Diagram (mentioned in text):** Raw oil → Kettle (NaOH) → Settling (salt) → Soap ↑ / Sweetwater ↓ → Acidulation → Evaporation → Distillation → Decolorization → Refined Glycerine. --- **(b)** Explain the **manufacture of glycerine from propylene via the epichlorohydrin route** with balanced equations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The synthetic route is a four-step sequence converting propylene to glycerine via **epichlorohydrin**. **Step 1 — Chlorination of Propylene (1.5 marks):** Propylene is chlorinated at **500–530 °C** (substitution at the allylic position): $$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$$ **Step 2 — Hypochlorination (1.5 marks):** Allyl chloride is reacted with **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** to form **1,3-dichlorohydrin**: $$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\!-\!\text{CHOH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$$ **Step 3 — Dehydrochlorination (2 marks):** Dichlorohydrin is treated with **calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂)** to cyclize to **epichlorohydrin** (an epoxide): $$\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\!-\!\text{CHOH}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Ca(OH)}_2} \underset{\text{Epichlorohydrin}}{\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{OCl}} + \text{HCl}$$ **Step 4 — Hydrolysis to Glycerine (2 marks):** Epichlorohydrin is hydrolyzed with aqueous **NaOH** at 100–150 °C: $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{OCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + \text{HCl}$$ The crude glycerol is then purified by evaporation, distillation, and ion exchange as in the oleochemical route. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]` **(a)** Discuss the **industrial applications of glycerine** in detail, with special emphasis on its use in **pharmaceuticals, explosives, and polymers**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Glycerine is one of the most versatile chemical intermediates, with applications spanning over **1,500 documented uses**. **1. Pharmaceuticals & Cosmetics (2.5 marks):** - **Humectant** in skin creams, lotions, and toothpaste (prevents moisture loss). - **Solvent** for injections, cough syrups, and herbal extracts. - **Lubricant** in ophthalmic preparations and suppositories. - **Plasticizer** in gelatin capsules. **2. Explosives (2 marks):** - **Nitroglycerin (glyceryl trinitrate)** — produced by nitration of glycerine with mixed acid (HNO₃ + H₂SO₄): $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ - Used in **dynamite** (mixed with kieselguhr) and as a **vasodilator** in heart medication (e.g., nitroglycerin tablets for angina). **3. Polymers & Resins (2.5 marks):** - **Alkyd resins** — condensation of glycerine with phthalic anhydride and fatty acids; used in **paints, varnishes, and lacquers**. - **Polyurethane polyols** — glycerine-based polyether polyols are reacted with diisocyanates to make **rigid PU foams** for insulation. - **Epoxy resins** — glycerine is converted back to **epichlorohydrin** for epoxy resin manufacture (the reverse of the synthetic route). --- **(b)** **500 kg of a vegetable oil (average molar mass 860 g/mol) is saponified. Calculate: (i) the theoretical mass of glycerine, and (ii) the percentage yield if the actual yield is 50 kg. (Molar mass of glycerine = 92.09 g/mol)** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Moles of Triglyceride (1 mark):** $$n_{\text{TG}} = \dfrac{500{,}000\,\text{g}}{860\,\text{g/mol}} = 581.40\,\text{mol}$$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometry (1 mark):** From the balanced equation, **1 mol TG → 1 mol glycerol**. $$n_{\text{glycerol, theoretical}} = 581.40\,\text{mol}$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical Mass (2 marks):** $$m_{\text{theoretical}} = 581.40 \times 92.09 = 53{,}533.13\,\text{g} = 53.53\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Percentage Yield (2 marks):** $$\%Y = \dfrac{50}{53.53} \times 100 = 93.42\%$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The yield of **~93.4%** is excellent industrially; losses (~6.6%) arise from soap-glycerol entrapment, hydrolysis inefficiency, and purification losses. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in Glycerine Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "Glycerine" with "Glycerol":** Glycerine is the commercial product (≥ 95% glycerol + water); glycerol is the pure compound. Examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for interchange. > 2. **Skipping balanced equations:** Any flow-sheet question without **balanced chemical equations** loses at least **1 mark per equation omitted**. > 3. **Forgetting the stoichiometric ratio:** In yield problems, students often use 1:3 or 3:1 ratio instead of 1:1 for TG : glycerol. Always derive from the balanced equation. > 4. **Missing the role of H₂SO₄ in nitration:** Mixed acid is essential (HNO₃ is the nitrating agent; H₂SO₄ is the dehydrating agent that drives the equilibrium). > 5. **No flow diagram in long answers:** A 14-mark answer without a process flow diagram typically loses **1–2 marks** under KTU valuation scheme. > 6. **Wrong IUPAC name:** Writing "1,2,3-propanetriol" is acceptable; writing "1,2,3-propanediol" is wrong (diol = 2 OH groups). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam — Glycerine:** - ✅ **Glycerine = Glycerol = Propane-1,2,3-triol = C₃H₈O₃**, MW = **92.09 g/mol**. - ✅ **Functional group:** Trihydric alcohol (3 -OH groups) → responsible for **hydrogen bonding** → **hygroscopic, viscous, high BP (290 °C)**. - ✅ **Two main sources:** (1) **Natural** — triglycerides in fats/oils; (2) **Synthetic** — propylene via epichlorohydrin. - ✅ **Oleochemical routes:** (a) **Saponification** with NaOH → soap + glycerine; (b) **Transesterification** with methanol → biodiesel + glycerine. - ✅ **Synthetic route (Epichlorohydrin):** Propylene → Allyl chloride → Dichlorohydrin → Epichlorohydrin → Glycerol (4 steps). - ✅ **Glycerine yield from oils:** approximately **10–12% by mass** (theoretical ~11.5% for 1:1 stoichiometry). - ✅ **Acrolein test:** KHSO₄ + heat → pungent acrolein vapor; **qualitative identification test** for glycerol. - ✅ **Nitroglycerin:** Glycerine + HNO₃ (H₂SO₄ catalyst) → glyceryl trinitrate (used in dynamite + heart medicine). - ✅ **USP-grade specs:** ≥ 99.5% glycerol, density ≥ 1.249 g/cm³, low heavy metals & ash. - ✅ **Major applications:** Pharma (humectant), food (sweetener, humectant), explosives (nitroglycerin), polymers (alkyd resins, PU foams), antifreeze. - ✅ **Biodiesel byproduct is now the dominant source** of crude glycerine globally (~50%+). - ✅ **Key equations to memorize:** Saponification, transesterification, propylene chlorination, hypochlorination, dehydrochlorination, hydrolysis, and nitration (all seven balanced equations above). - ✅ **Molar mass quick-calc:** C₃H₈O₃ = 3(12) + 8(1) + 3(16) = **92 g/mol**. - ✅ **Industrial reality:** Oleochemical glycerine is "green" and renewable; synthetic glycerine is being phased out due to chlorinated waste and cost.

topics.title: Pulp and Paper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pulp and Paper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Pulp and Paper Technology?

**Pulp and Paper Technology** is the branch of chemical engineering concerned with the conversion of lignocellulosic raw materials (mainly wood) into **pulp** (a fibrous mass) and subsequently into **paper** (a sheet of intertwined fibers used for writing, packaging, and printing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Pulp and Paper is a chemical process industry in which the **lignocellulosic biomass** (wood, bagasse, bamboo, straw) is converted into a fibrous cellulosic pulp through **mechanical**, **chemical**, or **semi-chemical** pulping methods. The pulp is then processed (beating, sizing, filling, coloring) and formed into sheets on a paper machine to produce paper of specified grammage, strength, and brightness.

### 1.2 The Two-Step Vision: From Tree to Paper

The manufacture of paper essentially involves three major operations:

1. **Pulping** – Separating cellulose fibers from lignin (the natural glue in wood).
2. **Bleaching** – Whitening the brown pulp by removing residual lignin.
3. **Papermaking** – Forming the pulp into a continuous sheet, removing water, drying, and finishing.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Spaghetti and Glue" Concept

Imagine a **bowl of cooked spaghetti** drenched in thick tomato sauce.

- The **spaghetti strands** represent **cellulose fibers** (long, strong, white, and flexible).
- The **tomato sauce** represents **lignin** (brown, brittle, surrounding and cementing the fibers together).
- **Pulping** = Carefully washing away the sauce to free the spaghetti strands.
- **Bleaching** = Further cleaning/whitening the strands to make them shine.
- **Papermaking** = Pressing the clean, wet strands flat into a thin mat that dries into a strong sheet — exactly what paper is!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Paper is essentially a **web of intertwined cellulose fibers** held together by **hydrogen bonds** formed during drying. The **strength** of paper depends on fiber length, fiber bonding, and degree of beating. The **brightness** depends on how completely lignin is removed during pulping and bleaching.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Grammage (Basis weight) | **g/m²** | Mass per unit area of paper |
| Bursting Strength | **kPa** (or kg/cm²) | Resistance to rupture |
| Tensile Strength | **kN/m** | Force per unit width to break |
| Tear Factor | dimensionless | Resistance to tearing |
| Brightness | **% ISO** (0–100 scale) | Whiteness measured at 457 nm |
| Cobb Value | **g/m²** | Water absorption (sizing test) |
| Freeness (CSF) | **mL** | Drainage property of pulp |
| Freeness (CSF) | **mL** | Drainage property of pulp |

> [!NOTE]
> The **freeness** of pulp is measured as **Canadian Standard Freeness (CSF)** in **mL**, and the standard temperature of operation in paper mills is maintained around **25–60 °C** depending on the process stage.

### 1.5 Raw Materials for Pulp & Paper

| Source | Examples | Pulp Yield | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Softwood (Coniferous)** | Pine, Spruce, Fir | 45–50% | Strong papers (kraft, sack) |
| **Hardwood (Deciduous)** | Eucalyptus, Poplar | 50–55% | Printing, tissue |
| **Non-wood** | Bagasse, Bamboo, Straw, Hemp | 45–60% | Specialty / India-specific |
| **Recycled fiber** | Old newsprint, OCC (Old Corrugated Containers) | 75–90% (after deinking) | Newsprint, board |

### 1.6 The Three Pillars of Pulping Technology

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **three main pulping routes** that every student must master:

- **Mechanical Pulping** – Uses physical force (grinding, refining). High yield (~90–95%), low strength, retains lignin.
- **Chemical Pulping** – Uses chemicals (NaOH + Na₂S in Kraft; Ca(HSO₃)₂ in Sulfite). Low yield (45–55%), high strength, lignin is dissolved.
- **Semi-Chemical / Chemi-mechanical** – Hybrid: mild chemical pretreatment + mechanical refining. Yield 65–85%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Kraft process (Sulfate process)** is the most widely used pulping method globally, accounting for **over 80% of chemical pulp production**, because of its superior strength, ability to recover chemicals, and tolerance to diverse wood species.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Wood

Wood is a **lignocellulosic composite** of three main polymers:

| Component | Formula / Nature | % in Wood | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose** | (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ, n = 1000–10000 | 40–50% | Structural fiber; provides strength |
| **Hemicellulose** | Branched polysaccharides (xylan, mannan) | 20–30% | Bonding between fibers |
| **Lignin** | Aromatic polymer (C₉H₁₀O₃ units) | 20–30% | Natural adhesive, hydrophobic |

A minor fraction (1–5%) consists of **extractives** (resins, tannins, turpentine).

> [!NOTE]
> The **lignin structure** contains phenylpropane units (guaiacyl in softwood, syringyl in hardwood) linked by ether and C–C bonds. Pulping chemistry revolves around **cleaving these ether bonds** to liberate cellulose.

## 2.2 The Kraft (Sulfate) Process — Detailed Operating Logic

The **Kraft process** is the most important process for KTU exams. It uses an aqueous solution of **NaOH + Na₂S**, called **white liquor**, at high temperature.

### 2.2.1 Operating Conditions

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Active alkali (as Na₂O) | **15–25%** on oven-dry wood |
| Sulfidity (Na₂S / (NaOH+Na₂S)) | **25–35%** |
| Liquor-to-wood ratio | **3 : 1 to 5 : 1** |
| Cooking temperature | **165–175 °C** |
| Cooking time | **1.5–3 hours** |
| Pressure | **0.7–1.0 MPa** |
| Pulp yield (kappa number 25–35) | **45–55%** |

### 2.2.2 The "Why" Behind the Kraft Chemistry

The pulping reactions are fundamentally of **two types**:

**Step 1 — Delignification (Base-catalyzed cleavage of lignin ether bonds)**

The hydroxide ion (OH⁻) attacks the α-ether linkages in lignin, breaking it into fragments that dissolve in the alkaline liquor.

$$\text{Lignin–O–R} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Lignin}^- + \text{ROH}$$

**Step 2 — Sulfide-promoted delignification**

The hydrosulfide ion (HS⁻) is the **key active species** in Kraft pulping. It selectively attacks the lignin C–C and C–O bonds while **preserving cellulose** from alkaline degradation (peeling reaction).

$$\text{Lignin–O–CH}_3 + \text{HS}^- \rightarrow \text{Lignin–SH} + \text{CH}_3\text{O}^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sulfidity matters:** High sulfidity (above 25%) accelerates delignification, gives lower kappa number, reduces rejects, and protects cellulose. This is the core reason the **Sulfate process** dominates over the obsolete **Soda process** (which uses only NaOH and degrades cellulose).

### 2.2.3 Mass Balance Logic for Kraft Cooking

Let the wood be charged at mass **M_w** (oven-dry basis) and the pulp obtained be **M_p** (oven-dry basis). Then:

$$\text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{M_p}{M_w} \times 100$$

The rejects (uncooked chips) mass **M_r** must be removed by screening, and the screened pulp yield is:

$$\text{Screened Yield (\%)} = \frac{M_p - M_r}{M_w} \times 100$$

## 2.3 The Sulfite Process (Comparison)

| Feature | Kraft Process | Sulfite Process |
|---|---|---|
| Chemicals | NaOH + Na₂S | Ca(HSO₃)₂ / Mg(HSO₃)₂ / Na₂SO₃ |
| pH | 13–14 (alkaline) | 1.5–4 (acidic) |
| Temperature | 165–175 °C | 130–145 °C |
| Pulp strength | High | Moderate |
| Lignin residue | More (kappa 25–35) | Less (kappa 15–25) |
| Bleachability | Difficult | Easy |
| Chemical recovery | Excellent | Difficult (for calcium base) |
| Wood tolerance | Wide | Limited (no resinous wood) |
| Dominant use | Sack, kraft linerboard | Newsprint, dissolving pulp |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Kraft process** is the dominant industrial process today, while the **Sulfite process** has declined due to environmental concerns (sulfur dioxide emissions) and limited chemical recovery.

## 2.4 Mechanical Pulping

There are two main variants:

### 2.4.1 Stone Groundwood (SGW)

- Logs are pressed against a **grindstone** under water spray.
- Fibers are torn from the wood by abrasion and shear.
- **Yield: 92–96%**, brightness 55–60% ISO.
- Used for **newsprint**.

### 2.4.2 Refiner Mechanical Pulp (RMP) / Thermomechanical (TMP)

- Wood chips pass through a **disc refiner** at high speed.
- **TMP** uses **preheating at 110–135 °C** to soften lignin.
- **Yield: 85–95%**, brightness 55–65% ISO.
- **CTMP** (chemi-thermomechanical) uses mild Na₂SO₃ impregnation.

## 2.5 Bleaching of Pulp

Brown (unbleached) pulp still contains ~3–6% residual lignin, giving it a dark color. **Bleaching** is a multi-stage process to whiten the pulp using oxidizing agents.

### 2.5.1 Conventional CEDED Sequence (Elemental Chlorine Free)

| Stage | Chemical | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | Cl₂ (chlorine) | Chlorinates lignin, makes it soluble |
| **E** | NaOH (extraction) | Dissolves chlorinated lignin |
| **D** | ClO₂ (chlorine dioxide) | Oxidizes remaining lignin |
| **E** | NaOH | Extracts further lignin |
| **D** | ClO₂ | Final whitening to >88% ISO |

### 2.5.2 Elemental Chlorine Free (ECF) — Modern Standard

Sequence: **D₀ – E(P) – D₁ – E(P) – D₂**

Replaces Cl₂ with **ClO₂**, reducing formation of toxic **dioxins** and **AOX (adsorbable organic halides)**.

### 2.5.3 Totally Chlorine Free (TCF)

Uses **O₂, H₂O₂, O₃** only — fully environment-friendly.

## 2.6 The Paper Machine (Fourdrinier)

The **Fourdrinier machine** is the heart of papermaking. Its sections are:

1. **Headbox** – Distributes pulp slurry evenly (consistency ~0.3–1%).
2. **Wire section** – Water drains through a moving wire mesh; wet web forms.
3. **Press section** – Removes more water by mechanical pressing (50–55% dryness).
4. **Dryer section** – Steam-heated cylinders evaporate water (up to 92–95% dryness).
5. **Calender / Sizing** – Surface finishing, gloss, water resistance.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{M_p}{M_w} \times 100$ | Pulping yield (pulp mass / wood mass) | % |
| 2 | $\text{Brightness (ISO)} = \dfrac{R_{457}}{R_{\text{MgO}}} \times 100$ | ISO brightness (reflectance at 457 nm) | % |
| 3 | $\text{Kappa Number} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KMnO}_4} \times N}{m_{\text{pulp}}}$ | Lignin content indicator | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{CSF (Freeness)} = 10\,V$ | Canadian Standard Freeness, $V$ = water volume | mL |
| 5 | $\text{Burst Index} = \dfrac{B}{G}$ | Bursting strength / grammage | kPa·m²/g |
| 6 | $\text{Tear Index} = \dfrac{T}{G}$ | Tear strength / grammage | mN·m²/g |
| 7 | $\text{Tensile Index} = \dfrac{F}{w \cdot G}$ | Tensile strength per unit width / grammage | N·m/g |
| 8 | $\text{Grammage (G)} = \dfrac{m}{A}$ | Mass per unit area | g/m² |
| 9 | $\text{Sulfidity (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Na}_2\text{S}}{\text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}} \times 100$ | Active alkali composition | % |
| 10 | $\text{Active Alkali} = \text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}$ (as Na₂O) | Total titratable alkali | % on wood |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{recovery}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{Na}_2\text{S recovered}}}{M_{\text{Na}_2\text{S charged}}} \times 100$ | Chemical recovery efficiency | % |
| 12 | $\text{Cobb}_{60} = \dfrac{m_{\text{water absorbed}}}{A} \times 100$ | Water absorption in 60 s (sizing) | g/m² |
| 13 | $\text{Opacity (\%)} = \dfrac{R_0}{R_\infty} \times 100$ | Print show-through | % |
| 14 | $\text{Drainage rate} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu L}$ | Darcy's law in wire section | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU derivations and numerical problems, the **Kappa Number** is the most important — it is inversely related to pulp brightness and directly related to residual lignin content. A **kappa number 25** corresponds approximately to **4% residual lignin** in softwood kraft pulp.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Kraft recovery boiler**: Generates steam and recovers Na₂S + Na₂CO₃ from black liquor (energy self-sufficiency).
- **ECF bleaching** dramatically reduces AOX discharge — mandated by EU regulations.
- **Closed-loop water systems** in modern mills reduce freshwater intake by 80%.
- **Biorefinery concept**: Lignin and hemicellulose side streams are now being valorized into **biofuels, vanillin, and bio-adhesives**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pulping Yield from Mass Balance

**Problem Setup:**
A continuous digester is charged with **10,000 kg (oven-dry basis) of wood chips** containing 48% cellulose, 28% hemicellulose, 23% lignin, and 1% extractives. After Kraft cooking and screening, the pulp mass obtained is **4,800 kg (oven-dry)** with **lignin content 3.5%** and rejects of **150 kg**.

**Step 1: Write the basic definition of total yield**

$$\text{Total Yield (\%)} = \frac{M_p}{M_w} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values**

$$\text{Total Yield (\%)} = \frac{4{,}800}{10{,}000} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Perform the numerical evaluation**

$$\text{Total Yield (\%)} = 0.48 \times 100 = 48.0\%$$

**Step 4: Compute the screened yield (excluding rejects)**

$$\text{Screened Yield (\%)} = \frac{M_p - M_r}{M_w} \times 100$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values**

$$\text{Screened Yield (\%)} = \frac{4{,}800 - 150}{10{,}000} \times 100$$

**Step 6: Perform the calculation**

$$\text{Screened Yield (\%)} = \frac{4{,}650}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 46.5\%$$

**Step 7: Compute the Lignin Removal Efficiency**

Lignin entering with wood = $10{,}000 \times 0.23 = 2{,}300 \text{ kg}$
Lignin remaining in pulp = $4{,}800 \times 0.035 = 168 \text{ kg}$

$$\text{Lignin Removal (\%)} = \frac{2{,}300 - 168}{2{,}300} \times 100$$

**Step 8: Evaluate**

$$\text{Lignin Removal (\%)} = \frac{2{,}132}{2{,}300} \times 100 = 92.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 92.7% lignin removal is characteristic of a well-operated Kraft cook, typically yielding a pulp with **kappa number 25–30**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kappa Number and Brightness Correlation

The **Kappa number** is defined as the volume (in mL) of **0.1 N KMnO₄** consumed by **1 g of oven-dry pulp** under standard conditions, corrected to 50% reduction.

**Standard Formula:**

$$\text{Kappa Number} = \frac{V \times N}{m}$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of KMnO₄ consumed (mL)
- $N$ = normality of KMnO₄ (0.1 N)
- $m$ = oven-dry mass of pulp (g)

**Step 1: Suppose 0.5 g of pulp consumes 12.5 mL of 0.1 N KMnO₄.**

$$\text{Kappa Number} = \frac{12.5 \times 0.1}{0.5}$$

**Step 2: Compute**

$$\text{Kappa Number} = \frac{1.25}{0.5} = 2.5$$

Wait — the **uncorrected** Kappa number is computed first, then a **correction factor** $f$ (depending on actual consumption) is applied. The corrected formula is:

$$K = \frac{V \times N}{m} \times f$$

**Step 3: With the correction factor (assume $f = 1.0$ for this case)**

$$K = 2.5 \times 1.0 = 2.5$$

**Step 4: Convert Kappa number to approximate residual lignin**

For softwood kraft pulp, **lignin (%) ≈ 0.15 × Kappa number**

$$\text{Lignin (\%)} = 0.15 \times 2.5 = 0.375\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Kappa number below 5 indicates pulp suitable for **TOTALLY CHLORINE FREE (TCF)** bleaching; below 2 indicates **dissolving-grade pulp** for rayon/film.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Active Alkali and Sulfidity Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A digester is charged with **8,000 kg (oven-dry)** of wood. The white liquor contains:
- NaOH = 100 g/L
- Na₂S = 35 g/L
- Liquor-to-wood ratio = 4 L liquor per kg wood
- Active alkali expressed as **Na₂O equivalent** (factor: Na₂O = NaOH × 0.775, Na₂S × 0.794)

**Step 1: Total volume of white liquor used**

$$V_{\text{liquor}} = 4 \times 8{,}000 = 32{,}000 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2: Mass of NaOH charged**

$$m_{\text{NaOH}} = 100 \times 32{,}000 = 3{,}200{,}000 \text{ g} = 3{,}200 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Mass of Na₂S charged**

$$m_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}} = 35 \times 32{,}000 = 1{,}120{,}000 \text{ g} = 1{,}120 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Na₂O equivalent**

$$m_{\text{Na}_2\text{O from NaOH}} = 3{,}200 \times 0.775 = 2{,}480 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{\text{Na}_2\text{O from Na}_2\text{S}} = 1{,}120 \times 0.794 = 889.3 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Total active alkali (as Na₂O)**

$$\text{AA} = 2{,}480 + 889.3 = 3{,}369.3 \text{ kg Na}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 6: Active alkali as % on wood**

$$\text{AA (\%)} = \frac{3{,}369.3}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 42.1\%$$

**Step 7: Compute sulfidity**

$$\text{Sulfidity} = \frac{\text{Na}_2\text{S (as Na}_2\text{O)}}{\text{Total AA (as Na}_2\text{O)}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Sulfidity} = \frac{889.3}{3{,}369.3} \times 100$$

**Step 8: Evaluate**

$$\text{Sulfidity} = 26.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **26.4% sulfidity** lies in the optimum industrial range of 25–35%, ensuring good delignification rate and minimum cellulose degradation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Grammage Calculation and Burst Index

**Problem Setup:**
A paper sample has dimensions **25 cm × 20 cm** and weighs **5.0 g** (oven-dry). Its bursting strength is **2.4 kPa** measured on a Mullen tester.

**Step 1: Area of the sample**

$$A = 25 \times 20 = 500 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Grammage (basis weight)**

$$G = \frac{m}{A} = \frac{5.0}{0.05} = 100 \text{ g/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Burst index**

$$\text{Burst Index} = \frac{B}{G} = \frac{2.4}{100} = 0.024 \text{ kPa·m}^2/\text{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A burst index of **0.024 kPa·m²/g** corresponds to ordinary writing/printing paper. Kraft sack paper typically has a burst index around **0.06–0.10 kPa·m²/g**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Chemical Recovery in Kraft Process

**Step 1: The recovery boiler mass balance**

Black liquor from the digester is concentrated to **65–75% solids** and burned in a **recovery boiler**. The combustion reactions are:

$$2\text{NaOH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{C} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2: Define chemical recovery efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{chem}} = \frac{m_{\text{Na}_2\text{S} + \text{NaOH recovered}}}{m_{\text{Na}_2\text{S} + \text{NaOH charged}}} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Modern recovery boilers achieve 95–98% chemical recovery**, with the heat of combustion of black liquor organics generating **3.5 GJ per ton of pulp** as high-pressure steam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kraft process is energy **self-sufficient** due to the combustion of dissolved lignin and hemicellulose — a unique advantage among chemical industries.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Pulping Methods

| Property | Mechanical (SGW) | Kraft | Sulfite | CTMP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield | 92–96% | 45–55% | 45–55% | 80–90% |
| Kappa number | 60–100 | 25–35 | 15–25 | 50–80 |
| Brightness (% ISO) | 55–60 | 20–30 | 40–50 | 55–60 |
| Tensile index (N·m/g) | 25–35 | 70–100 | 55–75 | 45–65 |
| Tear index (mN·m²/g) | 4–6 | 12–18 | 8–12 | 7–10 |
| Opacity | High | Low | Low | High |
| Bulk | High | Low | Medium | High |
| Yellowing on aging | Yes | Slight | Moderate | Yes |
| Bleachability | Poor | Moderate (multi-stage) | Easy | Moderate |
| Capital cost | Low | High | High | Medium |
| Energy use | High (1.5–2.5 MWh/t) | Medium (recovered) | Medium | Medium-High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A common KTU question asks: *"Why is mechanical pulp not used for high-strength paper?"* The answer: mechanical pulp retains **lignin**, which is brittle, hydrophobic, and causes **yellowing** upon exposure to light. Fibers are also shortened and damaged by grinding.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Integrated Kraft Pulp and Paper Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WoodYard["WOOD YARD AND CHIPPER"] --> ChipScreener["CHIP SCREENER"]
    ChipScreener --> Digester["CONTINUOUS BATCH DIGESTER"]
    Digester --> BrownStockWasher["BROWN STOCK WASHER"]
    BrownStockWasher --> ScreenRoom["SCREEN ROOM AND CLEANERS"]
    ScreenRoom --> BleachPlant["MULTI STAGE BLEACH PLANT ECF"]
    BleachPlant --> PulpChest["BLEACHED PULP CHEST"]
    PulpChest --> BeaterRefiner["BEATER AND REFINER"]
    BeaterRefiner --> Headbox["HEADBOX OF PAPER MACHINE"]
    Headbox --> WireSection["FOURDRINIER WIRE SECTION"]
    WireSection --> PressSection["PRESS SECTION"]
    PressSection --> DryerSection["STEAM HEATED DRYER CYLINDERS"]
    DryerSection --> Calendar["CALENDER AND SIZE PRESS"]
    Calendar --> Reel["REEL AND WINDER"]
    Reel --> FinishedPaper["FINISHED PAPER ROLLS"]
    Digester --> BlackLiquor["BLACK LIQUOR TO EVAPORATORS"]
    BlackLiquor --> RecoveryBoiler["KRAFT RECOVERY BOILER"]
    RecoveryBoiler --> Causticizer["CAUSTICIZING TANK"]
    Causticizer --> WhiteLiquor["REGENERATED WHITE LIQUOR"]
    WhiteLiquor --> Digester
    BleachPlant --> Effluent["EFFLUENT TREATMENT PLANT"]
    Effluent --> RecycleWater["RECYCLED WATER TO PROCESS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows returning from **Recovery Boiler → Causticizer → White Liquor → Digester** form a **closed chemical loop** — this is the heart of the Kraft process's economic and environmental sustainability.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Bleaching Sequence in an ECF Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BrownPulp["BROWN STOCK PULP KAPPA 30"] --> StageD0["D0 STAGE CHLORINE DIOXIDE"]
    StageD0 --> StageEP["EP STAGE SODIUM HYDROXIDE PEROXIDE"]
    StageEP --> StageD1["D1 STAGE CHLORINE DIOXIDE"]
    StageD1 --> StageE2["E2 STAGE EXTRACTION"]
    StageE2 --> StageD2["D2 STAGE FINAL CLORINE DIOXIDE"]
    StageD2 --> BrightPulp["BRIGHT PULP BRIGHTNESS 90 PLUS ISO"]
    StageD0 --> Filtrate1["FILTRATE 1 RECYCLE"]
    StageEP --> Filtrate2["FILTRATE 2 EFFLUENT OR RECOVERY"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each D-stage oxidizes residual lignin, and each E-stage extracts the dissolved lignin fragments using NaOH. **Filtrate recycling** between stages is critical for **water economy** and **heat recovery**.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Paper Machine Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PulpSlurry["DILUTE PULP SLURRY 0.5 PERCENT CONSISTENCY"] --> Headbox2["HEADBOX DISTRIBUTES EVENLY"]
    Headbox2 --> WireSect["WIRE SECTION WATER DRAINS"]
    WireSect --> VacuumBoxes["VACUUM BOXES SUCTION"]
    VacuumBoxes --> CouchRoll["COUCH ROLL TRANSFERS WEB"]
    CouchRoll --> PressSect["PRESS SECTION MECHANICAL WATER REMOVAL"]
    PressSect --> DryerSect["DRYER SECTION STEAM CYLINDERS"]
    DryerSect --> SizePress["SIZE PRESS SURFACE SIZING"]
    SizePress --> Calender2["CALENDER STACK GLOSS AND SMOOTHNESS"]
    Calender2 --> Reel2["REEL WINDING"]
    Reel2 --> Winder["SLITTER AND WINDER"]
    Winder --> FinalRolls["FINISHED ROLLS OR SHEETS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Consistency** is the key process variable: pulp entering the headbox is **0.3–1% solids**; after the wire section, **15–20% solids**; after pressing, **40–55% solids**; after drying, **92–96% solids**.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Kraft Chemical Recovery Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BlackLiquorEvap["BLACK LIQUOR EVAPORATOR 65 PERCENT SOLIDS"] --> RecoveryBoil2["RECOVERY BOILER COMBUSTION"]
    RecoveryBoil2 --> Smelt["GREEN LIQUOR NA2CO3 PLUS NA2S"]
    Smelt --> DissolvingTank["DISSOLVING TANK WEAK WASH WATER"]
    DissolvingTank --> GreenLiquor["GREEN LIQUOR"]
    GreenLiquor --> Causticizer2["CAUSTICIZER CA OH 2 SLURRY ADDED"]

    HighPressureSteam --> Power["ELECTRICAL POWER AND PROCESS HEAT"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The recovery boiler is **the single most important unit** in a Kraft mill. It simultaneously:
> 1. Recovers **Na₂S and Na₂CO₃** (chemicals).
> 2. Generates **high-pressure steam** (energy).
> 3. Reduces **BOD/COD load** (environmental compliance).
