## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the term "Iodine Value" of an oil. How is it used to classify oils?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Iodine Value (IV)** is defined as the **number of grams of iodine (I₂) absorbed by 100 grams of the oil/fat sample**, indicating the degree of unsaturation (the number of C=C double bonds).

**Classification:**
- **Drying oils**: IV > 150 (e.g., linseed oil, IV ≈ 180)
- **Semi-drying oils**: IV = 100–150 (e.g., sunflower oil, IV ≈ 130)
- **Non-drying oils**: IV < 100 (e.g., coconut oil, IV ≈ 8)

The higher the IV, the more unsaturated the oil, and the more suitable it is for **paint, varnish, and film-forming applications**.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between "Acid Value" and "Saponification Value".**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Acid Value (AV) | Saponification Value (SV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | mg KOH to neutralize **free** fatty acids in 1 g oil | mg KOH to **saponify** (hydrolyze) 1 g oil (both free and bound fatty acids) |
| **Target** | Only free fatty acids (FFA) | All fatty acids (free + esterified) |
| **Procedure** | Direct titration with KOH | Reflux with excess KOH, back-titrate with HCl |
| **Typical value** | Refined oil: < 1; crude oil: 5–20 | 180–260 depending on oil type |
| **Indicates** | Hydrolytic rancidity / quality | Average molecular weight of fatty acids |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Define Saponification Value, Acid Value, and Iodine Value of an oil. Explain with reactions how these are determined experimentally.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** 2.0 g of a vegetable oil sample was saponified with 50.0 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH. The excess KOH was back-titrated with 0.5 N HCl, of which 20.0 mL was required. A blank titration consumed 49.0 mL of 0.5 N HCl. The iodine value of the same oil was found to be 90. Calculate: (i) the Saponification Value, (ii) the average molecular weight, and (iii) classify the oil as drying, semi-drying, or non-drying.

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Reactions:**

*(See Section 2.1.1, 2.1.2, 2.1.3 of these notes for the board-standard definitions.)*

**Acid Value Determination (Reaction):**
$$\text{RCOOH} + \text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{RCOOK} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
Direct titration of oil dissolved in neutral alcohol with standardized KOH using phenolphthalein indicator.

**Saponification Value Determination (Reaction):**
$$\text{RCOOCH}_2 + 3\text{KOH (excess)} \rightarrow \text{RCOOK} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$
Reflux oil with known excess alcoholic KOH, then back-titrate unreacted KOH with standard HCl.

**Iodine Value Determination (Reaction, Wijs Method):**
$$\text{R-CH=CH-R'} + \text{ICl} \rightarrow \text{R-CHI-CHCl-R'}$$
$$\text{ICl} + \text{KI} \rightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$
$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Saponification Value (SV):**

$$SV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.11}{W}$$

Substituting:
- $V_b = 49.0$ mL
- $V_s = 20.0$ mL
- $N = 0.5$
- $W = 2.0$ g

$$SV = \frac{(49.0 - 20.0) \times 0.5 \times 56.11}{2.0}$$

$$SV = \frac{29.0 \times 0.5 \times 56.11}{2.0} = \frac{29.0 \times 28.055}{2.0}$$

$$SV = \frac{813.6}{2.0}$$

$$\boxed{SV = 406.8 \text{ mg KOH/g oil}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A value of 406.8 is **physically impossible** for a natural oil (real SV ranges 170–260). The problem data is internally inconsistent — possibly a typo in the original question paper. In an exam, **proceed with the calculation as given** and **add a note** that the value is "higher than typical vegetable oils, possibly indicating a low molecular weight synthetic ester or an error in sample weight."

**Step 2 — Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{avg} = \frac{3 \times 56.11 \times 1000}{SV} = \frac{168330}{406.8} \approx 413.9 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_{avg} \approx 414 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 3 — Classification Based on Iodine Value:**

Given IV = 90. By the classification rule:
- IV < 100 → **Non-drying oil**

$$\boxed{\text{Classification: Non-drying oil (e.g., similar to groundnut or olive oil)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> For a "realistic" exam problem, expect SV around 190 and IV around 90 (e.g., groundnut oil). The numbers above were chosen to test your formula-handling ability, not your memorization of typical values.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** What is Hydrogenation of oils? Explain the industrial process with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the formation of trans-fats.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** 5.0 g of butter sample was saponified, acidified, and the volatile fatty acids were steam-distilled. The distillate required 30.0 mL of 0.1 N KOH for neutralization. A blank consumed 1.5 mL of 0.1 N KOH. Calculate the Reichert Value and comment on the purity of the butter sample.

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Hydrogenation of Oils:**

**Definition:**
Hydrogenation is the **catalytic addition of molecular hydrogen (H₂) to the C=C double bonds of unsaturated oils**, converting liquid oils into semi-solid fats. This is the core process in **vanaspati (hydrogenated vegetable oil) manufacture**.

**Reaction:**

$$\text{R-CH=CH-R'} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, 150-200°C, 5-30 atm}} \text{R-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-R'}$$

**Process Description (Step-by-step):**

1. **Feed preparation:** Refined, bleached vegetable oil is pre-heated to 100–150°C.
2. **Mixing with catalyst:** Finely divided Ni catalyst (0.05–0.15% by weight of oil) is added as a slurry.
3. **Reactor:** Oil is charged into a closed converter. H₂ gas is bubbled at **5–30 atm** pressure at **150–200°C**.
4. **Reaction time:** 1–4 hours depending on target IV.
5. **Filtration:** The spent Ni catalyst is filtered out (sometimes reactivated and recycled).
6. **Deodorization:** Steam stripping at 240°C removes any residual odors and the "hydrogenation smell".
7. **Cooling and packaging:** The hardened fat is cooled, often blended with groundnut oil, and packed.

**Role of Catalyst:**
- Nickel is the most common — it provides a **surface for H₂ dissociation** and for **olefin chemisorption**.
- A **more active catalyst** (e.g., Pd) speeds up hydrogenation but at higher cost.
- **Selective hydrogenation** is achieved by **lowering temperature and using a less active catalyst** — this preferentially reduces linolenic → linoleic → oleic.

**Trans-fat Formation:**
- At high temperatures, the *cis* C=C double bond **isomerizes** to the *trans* configuration → **elaidic acid** (the trans isomer of oleic acid).
- Trans-fats increase **LDL cholesterol** and decrease **HDL cholesterol** in blood → **cardiovascular disease risk**.
- Indian regulation **FSSAI (2017)** caps trans-fats in edible oils at **5% by weight**.

**Part (b) — Reichert Value Calculation:**

**Given data:**
- Mass of butter = 5.0 g
- Volume of 0.1 N KOH for sample distillate = 30.0 mL
- Volume of 0.1 N KOH for blank = 1.5 mL

**Reichert Value formula (for 5 g sample, 0.1N KOH):**

$$RV = V_s - V_b = 30.0 - 1.5 = 28.5 \text{ mL of 0.1 N KOH}$$

$$\boxed{RV = 28.5}$$

**Comment on Purity:**
- **Pure, genuine butter** has RV in the range of **28–32**.
- Our calculated value of **28.5 falls squarely within the pure butter range**.
- If the sample had been **adulterated with margarine or vanaspati** (which lack butyric acid), RV would drop below 5.

$$\boxed{\text{Conclusion: The butter sample is GENUINE and of high purity.}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Oils and Fats" Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion for Iodine Value:** Students often forget the "per 100 g" scaling and use 1.269 instead of 12.69. **Lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Blank vs sample swap:** In saponification, the *blank* uses MORE HCl (no oil, so all KOH is free). Students sometimes write $(V_s - V_b)$ instead of $(V_b - V_s)$. **Lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up definitions:** AV measures *free* fatty acids, SV measures *total* (free + bound). Writing them interchangeably **loses 3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting the 3 in molecular weight formula:** $M = 3 \times 56.11 \times 1000 / SV$. The "3" comes from the 3 fatty acid equivalents per triglyceride. **Lose 2 marks**.
> 5. **Cetane value confusion with octane:** Octane number is for **gasoline (petrol)**; cetane number is for **diesel**. **Lose 1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this and review the night before your KTU ESE Module 2 exam:**

- ☐ **Oils and Fats** = triglycerides (esters of glycerol + 3 fatty acids).
- ☐ **Oils** are liquid at 25°C; **Fats** are solid at 25°C.
- ☐ **Acid Value (AV)** = mg KOH to neutralize **free** fatty acids in 1 g oil.
- ☐ **Saponification Value (SV)** = mg KOH to **saponify** 1 g oil; inversely related to chain length.
- ☐ **Iodine Value (IV)** = g I₂ absorbed by 100 g oil; measures unsaturation.
- ☐ **Reichert Value (RV)** = mL 0.1N KOH for volatile FA from 5 g sample; detects **butyric acid** (butter purity test).
- ☐ **Cetane Value (CN)** = ignition quality of biodiesel (FAME); 0 (α-methylnaphthalene) to 100 (n-cetane).
- ☐ **AV formula** = $(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 56.11 / W$ (direct titration).
- ☐ **SV formula** = $(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.11 / W$ (back-titration, note the swap).
- ☐ **IV formula** = $(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69 / W$ (Wijs method, thiosulphate).
- ☐ **$M_{avg}$** = $3 \times 56.11 \times 1000 / SV \approx 168330 / SV$.
- ☐ **Drying oils**: IV > 150 (linseed, tung, perilla) → **paints, varnishes**.
- ☐ **Semi-drying oils**: IV 100–150 (sunflower, soybean) → **soft soaps, cooking**.
- ☐ **Non-drying oils**: IV < 100 (coconut, olive, groundnut, castor) → **soaps, cooking, cosmetics**.
- ☐ **Coconut oil** has the **highest SV** (250–265) due to lauric and myristic acid dominance.
- ☐ **Butter has the highest RV** (28–32) due to butyric and caproic acids.
- ☐ **Linseed oil has the highest IV** (170–200) among common oils.
- ☐ **Hydrogenation** = catalytic H₂ addition to C=C bonds → vanaspati manufacture; uses **Ni catalyst**, 150–200°C, 5–30 atm.
- ☐ **Trans-fats** form during partial hydrogenation; FSSAI limits them to **5% max** in India.
- ☐ **Rancidity types**: hydrolytic (lipase + water), oxidative (O₂ + unsaturation), ketonic (fungal), metal-catalyzed.
- ☐ **Common antioxidants**: BHA, BHT, TBHQ — terminate radical chain mechanism.
- ☐ **Biodiesel (FAME)** is made by **transesterification** of oil with methanol (6:1 molar ratio) using NaOH/KOH catalyst; co-product is glycerol.
- ☐ **Indian diesel standard (IS 1460)** requires **CN ≥ 51**.
- ☐ **Equivalent weight of KOH = 56.11**; **equivalent weight of I₂ = 126.91**; **12.69 = 126.91/10** is the IV-formula constant.
- ☐ **Castor oil is unique** — contains **ricinoleic acid** (12-hydroxy oleic acid) → high acetyl value, used in cosmetics and lubricants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Starch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Starch** is a naturally occurring, biodegradable, semicrystalline **polysaccharide carbohydrate** composed of long chains of **D-glucose (dextrose) units** linked primarily by **α-glycosidic bonds**. It is the principal storage carbohydrate of green plants, the most abundant edible biopolymer on Earth, and a foundational **industrial raw material** in food, textile, paper, pharmaceutical, and fermentation industries.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **starch** as nature's **"battery pack"** for plants — just as a power bank stores electrical energy in a compact, releasable form, plants pack away glucose units into tightly coiled starch granules inside their seeds, tubers, and roots. When the seed germinates or the tuber needs fuel, enzymes called **amylases** simply "unzip" the glucose chains and release energy.

From a chemical engineering perspective, imagine starch as a **warehouse full of identical Lego bricks (D-glucose units)**:
- The bricks can be arranged in two different layouts — **straight chains (amylose)** and **branched chains (amylopectin)**.
- These bricks can later be broken apart, rearranged, or chemically modified to make **glucose syrups, ethanol, biodegradable plastics, adhesives, and pharmaceuticals**.

### 1.2 Botanical Sources & Industrial Feedstocks

| Botanical Source | Starch Content (% wet basis) | Primary Industrial Starch |
|---|---|---|
| **Maize (Corn)** | 60–72 % | **Maize Starch** (global leader) |
| **Cassava (Tapioca)** | 20–35 % | **Tapioca Starch** |
| **Potato** | 15–25 % | **Potato Starch** |
| **Wheat** | 55–75 % | **Wheat Starch** |
| **Rice** | 70–80 % | **Rice Starch** |
| **Sago Palm** | ~65 % | **Sago Starch** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant (KTU High-Yield):** The worldwide industrial production of starch exceeds **75 million metric tons/year**, with **maize starch accounting for ~73 %** of global production. Always quote these values in numerical problems.

### 1.3 Molecular Architecture — The Two Components

Starch is never a single homogeneous polymer; it is a **binary mixture** of two glucans:

**A. Amylose (15–30 % of native starch)**
- **Linear, unbranched** chains of α-1,4-linked D-glucose units.
- Molecular weight: **1 × 10⁵ to 1 × 10⁶ Da** (Degree of Polymerization ≈ 300–3000).
- Soluble in hot water; forms **deep blue complex** with iodine (λ_max ≈ **660 nm**).
- Responsible for **gel film formation** and retrogradation.

**B. Amylopectin (70–85 % of native starch)**
- **Highly branched** polymer: α-1,4 backbone with α-1,6 branch points (every 24–30 glucose units).
- Molecular weight: **1 × 10⁷ to 1 × 10⁹ Da** (DP ≈ 2000–200,000) — one of the largest natural polymers.
- Insoluble in cold water; swells to form **starch paste**.
- Gives **purple-red color** with iodine (λ_max ≈ **530–550 nm**).

> [!TIP]
> **High-Amylose Starch (HAOS):** Genetically modified cultivars contain **>50 % amylose** — used in biodegradable films and resistant starch. **Waxy Starch** contains **<5 % amylose** (mostly amylopectin) — used as a thickener.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemical Structure & Bonding

**A. The Monomer — α-D-Glucopyranose**
- Molecular formula: **C₆H₁₂O₆** (Molar mass = **180.16 g/mol**)
- Contains **five hydroxyl groups (–OH)** and one acetal oxygen in a **six-membered pyranose ring**.
- The **α-anomer** has the anomeric –OH in the **axial (downward) position** — this is critical because **β-anomers form cellulose**, not starch.

**B. The Two Glycosidic Linkages in Starch**

**Linkage 1: α-1,4-glycosidic bond (dominant, ~95 %)**
- Connects C1 of one glucose to C4 of the next.
- Produces a **helical (V-amylose) structure** with **6 glucose units per turn** and a pitch of **0.81 nm**.
- This geometry is what allows iodine to slip inside the helix and form the famous **starch–iodine inclusion complex**.

**Linkage 2: α-1,6-glycosidic bond (branch points, ~5 %)**
- Connects C1 of one glucose to C6 of another.
- Creates the **branching architecture of amylopectin**.
- Average branch length: **20–30 glucose units** between branch points.

### 2.2 Granular Morphology (Supramolecular Organization)

Starch is biosynthesized as discrete, semi-crystalline **granules** inside amyloplasts:

| Structural Level | Size / Scale | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Granule** | 1–100 µm | Native, insoluble, birefringent (Maltese cross under polarized light) |
| **Growth Ring** | 120–500 nm | Alternating semi-crystalline (dense) and amorphous (sparse) shells |
| **Blocklet** | 20–50 nm | Superstructures of crystalline and amorphous lamellae |
| **Lamellae** | ~9 nm | Alternating crystalline (DP ~14) and amorphous (DP ~6) layers |
| **Double Helix** | 0.5 nm | Amylopectin side chains in helical conformation |
| **Glucose Unit** | 0.45 nm | The fundamental monomer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Birefringence Test:** When viewed under **crossed polarizers**, native starch granules show a characteristic **"Maltese Cross"** pattern at the hilum — a definitive KTU viva question.

### 2.3 Physicochemical Properties (Must-Memorize for KTU)

| Property | Typical Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Density (granular)** | ~1.5 g/cm³ |
| **Bulk Density** | 0.6–0.9 g/cm³ |
| **Moisture Content (native)** | 10–20 % |
| **Gelatinization Temp. (Corn)** | 62–72 °C |
| **Gelatinization Temp. (Potato)** | 56–66 °C |
| **Gelatinization Temp. (Waxy maize)** | 63–72 °C |
| **Iodine affinity (Amylose)** | 18–20 % (deep blue) |
| **Iodine affinity (Amylopectin)** | <1 % (red-purple) |
| **Pasting viscosity** | Depends on amylose/amylopectin ratio |
| **Calorific value** | ~4.2 kcal/g (dry basis) |
| **Optical rotation [α]D²⁰** | +180° to +200° |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{162}$ | $M_n$ = number-average molecular weight; 162 = molar mass of anhydroglucose (C₆H₁₀O₅) |
| 2 | Hydrolysis Yield (Theoretical) | $\% \text{ Glucose} = \dfrac{180}{162} \times 100 = 111.1\,\%$ | Gain = 11.1 % mass increase on hydrolysis (water addition) |
| 3 | Iodine Binding (Blue Value) | $BV = A_{620} \times \text{dilution factor}$ | $A_{620}$ = absorbance at 620 nm (blue complex) |
| 4 | Swelling Power (SP) | $SP = \dfrac{W_s}{W_d}$ | $W_s$ = weight of swollen gel; $W_d$ = dry weight |
| 5 | Water Solubility Index (WSI) | $WSI = \dfrac{W_{ds}}{W_s} \times 100$ | $W_{ds}$ = dry solids in supernatant |
| 6 | Starch Paste Yield (mass balance) | $Y = \dfrac{M_{glucose\ produced} \times 0.90}{M_{starch\ fed}}$ | 0.90 = stoichiometric factor for C₆H₁₀O₅ → C₆H₁₂O₆ |
| 7 | Gelatinization Enthalpy (DSC) | $\Delta H_{gel}$ (J/g) | Measured by Differential Scanning Calorimetry |
| 8 | Reducing Sugar (Lane-Eynon) | $R = \dfrac{T \times f}{W} \times 100$ | T = titer; f = Fehling's factor; W = sample weight |
| 9 | Saccharification Conversion | $\% \, DE = \dfrac{\text{Reducing sugars as glucose}}{\text{Total dry solids}} \times 100$ | DE = Dextrose Equivalent |
| 10 | Bradford Protein (contaminant) | $C_p = \dfrac{A_{595} \times V}{m \times \varepsilon}$ | Contaminant protein in starch isolation |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry (≈ 60 %):** Thickener, binder, encapsulant, gelling agent (gravies, sauces, noodles, confectionery).
- **Paper & Corrugated Board (≈ 15 %):** Surface sizing adhesive — starch increases surface strength and printability.
- **Textile Industry (≈ 8 %):** Warp sizing of cotton yarns to prevent breakage during weaving.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Tablet binder, disintegrant, sustained-release matrix, plasma volume expander (HES).
- **Fermentation / Bioethanol:** Acid/enzyme hydrolysis → glucose → yeast fermentation → bioethanol (C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂).
- **Biodegradable Plastics:** Thermoplastic starch (TPS) blended with PLA/PHA for packaging films.
- **Adhesives:** Dextrin-based glues for envelopes, labels, and corrugated board.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Glucose Yield on Complete Hydrolysis

**Problem Statement:** What is the maximum mass of D-glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, M = 180 g/mol) obtainable from **1.00 kg of pure starch (C₆H₁₀O₅, M = 162 g/mol)**, and why is the yield *greater than 100 %*?

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric hydrolysis reaction.**

The repeating anhydroglucose unit C₆H₁₀O₅ combines with one molecule of water during hydrolysis:

$$
(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + n\,H_2O \longrightarrow n\,C_6H_{12}O_6
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the molecular weights.**

$$
M_{\text{anhydroglucose}} = 6(12) + 10(1) + 5(16) = 72 + 10 + 80 = 162 \ \text{g/mol}
$$

$$
M_{\text{glucose}} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 \ \text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass conversion ratio.**

Each 162 g of starch anhydroglucose unit produces 180 g of glucose after gaining 18 g of water. Hence:

$$
\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{M_{\text{glucose}}}{M_{\text{anhydroglucose}}} = \frac{180}{162} = 1.1111
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to 1.00 kg of starch.**

$$
m_{\text{glucose}} = 1.00 \ \text{kg} \times \frac{180}{162} = 1.111 \ \text{kg of D-glucose}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the >100 % yield.**

The **11.11 % mass increase** is purely due to **water of hydrolysis** being incorporated into the product. The carbon balance is unchanged; only hydrogen and oxygen from water are added.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem gives an "apparent yield > 100 %", the explanation is *always* hydrolytic mass addition. This is a guaranteed 2-mark short answer.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Degree of Polymerization from Iodine Blue Value

**Problem Statement:** A sample of maize starch has a number-average molecular weight $M_n = 1.0 \times 10^6$ Da. Calculate the **Degree of Polymerization (DP)**, the **number of glucose units**, and the **approximate length of the chain in nanometres**.

**Step 1 — Apply the DP formula.**

$$
DP = \frac{M_n}{M_{\text{anhydroglucose unit}}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{6}}{162} = 6173
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the contour length of the extended chain.**

The projected length of one glucose residue along the helix axis is **~0.45 nm**:

$$
L = DP \times 0.45 \ \text{nm} = 6173 \times 0.45 = 2778 \ \text{nm} \approx 2.78 \ \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Account for helicity.**

A V-amylose helix has 6 residues per turn of pitch 0.81 nm, so:

$$
L_{\text{helix}} = \frac{DP}{6} \times 0.81 \ \text{nm} = \frac{6173}{6} \times 0.81 = 833 \ \text{nm} \approx 0.83 \ \mu m
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.**

A linear amylose chain of DP 6173 spans nearly 3 µm in extended form — comparable to many biological macromolecules, confirming the giant molecular architecture of native starch.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Balance in a Maize Wet-Milling Plant

**Problem Statement:** A wet-milling plant processes **10,000 kg/day of maize** (14 % moisture, 70 % starch content on dry basis). After steeping, milling, and separation, **98 % of the starch is recovered** with **88 % purity in the A-starch stream** and **10 % purity in the B-starch stream**. The **protein in gluten** is **60 % pure**. Calculate the daily outputs of A-starch, B-starch, and gluten.

**Step 1 — Calculate dry mass of maize input.**

$$
m_{\text{dry maize}} = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.14) = 10{,}000 \times 0.86 = 8600 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Total starch in feed.**

$$
m_{\text{starch in}} = 8600 \times 0.70 = 6020 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total recovered starch (98 % yield).**

$$
m_{\text{starch out}} = 6020 \times 0.98 = 5899.6 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Split between A and B streams using purity relation.**

Let the masses of wet A-starch and B-starch streams be $x$ and $y$:

$$
0.88x + 0.10y = 5899.6
$$

Assuming standard plant split ratio **A : B = 5 : 1** (typical for corn wet milling):

$$
x = 5y
$$

Substituting:

$$
0.88(5y) + 0.10y = 5899.6
$$

$$
(4.40 + 0.10)y = 5899.6
$$

$$
y = \frac{5899.6}{4.50} = 1311.0 \ \text{kg/day (B-starch wet)}
$$

$$
x = 5 \times 1311.0 = 6555.0 \ \text{kg/day (A-starch wet)}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify starch recovered.**

$$
0.88(6555) + 0.10(1311) = 5768.4 + 131.1 = 5899.5 \ \text{kg/day} \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Compute gluten mass (using protein balance).**

Total protein in feed ≈ 10 % of dry maize = 860 kg/day. Recovery ~90 % in gluten:

$$
m_{\text{gluten}} = \frac{860 \times 0.90}{0.60} = 1290 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 7 — Tabulate the results.**

| Stream | Daily Output (kg) | % Purity |
|---|---|---|
| A-Starch (wet) | **6,555** | 88 % |
| B-Starch (wet) | **1,311** | 10 % |
| Corn Gluten (wet) | **1,290** | 60 % protein |
| Total Starch Recovered | 5,900 (98 % yield) | — |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gelatinization vs. Retrogradation vs. Dextrinization

| Process | Temperature Range | Phenomenon | Reversible? | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gelatinization** | 60–80 °C (with H₂O) | Granule swelling, loss of birefringence, viscosity peak | **Irreversible** | Sauce thickeners, instant puddings |
| **Pasting** | 80–95 °C | Complete granule rupture, paste formation | Irreversible | Adhesive sizing |
| **Retrogradation** | 4 °C, hours–days | Amylose re-association, gel network, syneresis | Reversible by reheating | Staling of bread, resistant starch (RS-3) |
| **Dextrinization** | 150–200 °C (dry or acid) | Partial hydrolysis, branching, color formation | Irreversible | Gum adhesives, cyclodextrins |
| **Cold-Water Swelling** | <40 °C (pre-gelatinized) | Granules already ruptured, swell in cold water | Irreversible | Instant starches, baby food |
| **Annealing** | T < gelatinization, long time | Increased crystalline order | Reversible | Modified starch with narrow gelatinization range |
| **Heat-Moisture Treatment (HMT)** | 90–120 °C, low moisture | Reorganization without gelatinization | Reversible | Slowly digestible starch |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Structure of Native Starch

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Maize Kernel Feedstock"] --> B1["Steeping in SO2 Water 50C 24 to 48 hr"]
    B1 --> C1["Coarse Grinding"]
    C1 --> D1["Germ Separation by Hydrocyclone"]
    D1 --> E1["Fine Grinding"]
    E1 --> F1["Fiber Washing"]
    F1 --> G1["Gluten Starch Separation Centrifuge"]
    G1 --> H1["A Starch Concentration Hydrocyclone"]
    G1 --> I1["Gluten Concentration"]
    H1 --> J1["Dewatering Centrifuge"]
    J1 --> K1["Drying Pneumatic Flash Dryer"]
    K1 --> L1["Pure Native Maize Starch 99 percent Purity"]
    I1 --> M1["Corn Gluten Meal Animal Feed"]
    L1 --> N1["Amylose Linear alpha 1 4 25 percent"]
    L1 --> O1["Amylopectin Branched alpha 1 4 plus alpha 1 6 75 percent"]
    N1 --> P1["Helical V Amylose 6 Residues per Turn"]
    O1 --> Q1["Cluster Model Branching Every 24 to 30 Units"]
    P1 --> R1["Starch Iodine Complex Deep Blue 660 nm"]
    Q1 --> S1["Starch Iodine Complex Red Purple 540 nm"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture: Starch Modification Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2["Native Starch Granule"] --> B2["Physical Modification"]
    A2 --> C2["Chemical Modification"]
    A2 --> D2["Enzymatic Modification"]
    A2 --> E2["Genetic Modification"]
    B2 --> B3["Pre gelatinization Drum Drying"]
    B2 --> B4["Heat Moisture Treatment 120C"]
    B2 --> B5["Annealing 50C 24 hr"]
    C2 --> C3["Acid Hydrolysis 0.1M HCl 50C"]
    C2 --> C4["Oxidation NaOCl pH 9"]
    C2 --> C5["Cross linking POCl3 STMP"]
    C2 --> C6["Esterification Acetylation"]
    C2 --> C7["Etherification Hydroxypropylation"]
    C2 --> C8["Cationization QUAT 188"]
    D2 --> D3["alpha Amylase Liquefaction 95C"]
    D2 --> D4["Glucoamylase Saccharification 60C"]
    D2 --> D5["Pullulanase Debranching"]
    D2 --> D6["Cyclodextrin Glycosyltransferase CGTase"]
    E2 --> E3["High Amylose 70 percent"]
    E2 --> E4["Waxy Maize 99 percent Amylopectin"]
    C3 --> F2["Acid Modified Starch Thin Boiling"]
    C5 --> F3["Cross Linked Starch Shear Resistant"]
    C6 --> F4["Starch Acetate E1414"]
    C7 --> F5["Hydroxypropyl Starch E1440"]
    C8 --> F6["Cationic Starch Paper Sizing"]

    D6 --> G4["Alpha Beta Gamma Cyclodextrin"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse starch with cellulose.** Both are glucose polymers, but starch has **α-1,4 bonds (digestible)** and cellulose has **β-1,4 bonds (indigestible to humans)**. A 4-mark question on linkages is lost in seconds by this slip.
> 2. **Do not write "amylose is soluble in cold water"** — it is soluble in **hot water** only. This is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 3. **The "blue value" of amylopectin is NOT zero** — it is **<1 %**, not 0 %. Be precise.
> 4. In mass-balance problems, **always include the 11.1 % hydrolytic mass gain** if the question asks for glucose yield from starch.
> 5. When asked "types of starch", write **amylose AND amylopectin** (not "corn starch, potato starch" — that is source, not type).

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **amylose and amylopectin** with respect to structure, solubility, iodine color, and molecular weight.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Amylose | Amylopectin |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Linear, unbranched; α-1,4 links | Branched; α-1,4 + α-1,6 links |
| % in native starch | 15–30 % | 70–85 % |
| Molecular weight | 1 × 10⁵ – 1 × 10⁶ Da | 1 × 10⁷ – 1 × 10⁹ Da |
| Solubility in hot water | Soluble; forms gel | Insoluble; swells to paste |
| Iodine test | **Deep blue (λ_max 660 nm)** | **Red-purple (λ_max 540 nm)** |
| Retrogradation | Strong | Weak |

**(1 mark per major distinguishing point; 3 marks total.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the **"Maltese Cross" phenomenon** observed under a polarized light microscope for native starch granules.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Native starch granules are **semi-crystalline** with radially oriented amylopectin double helices (1 mark).
- Under **crossed polarizers**, the birefringent crystalline domains rotate the plane of polarized light, producing a characteristic **"Maltese Cross" (or interference cross)** pattern centered at the **hilum** (1 mark).
- Upon **gelatinization** (heating in water), the crystalline order is destroyed, the birefringence is lost, and the Maltese Cross **disappears** — this is the standard test to confirm gelatinization (1 mark).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 2 — Choice Pattern (Standard KTU 2024)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **industrial wet-milling process** for the production of maize starch with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **sulphur dioxide (SO₂) steep water** and the separation principles used for germ, fiber, and gluten removal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Steeping (2 marks):**
Maize kernels are steeped in **0.1–0.2 % SO₂ solution at 48–52 °C for 24–48 hours** in counter-current batteries. SO₂ performs three functions:
- **Lactobacilli growth inhibition** to prevent fermentation loss.
- **Disulfide bond reduction** in the protein matrix (especially in glutelin) → protein network loosens.
- **Lactic acid formation** lowers pH to ~4.0, preventing microbial spoilage and softening cell walls.

**2. Coarse Grinding (1 mark):**
Softened kernels are passed through attrition mills to liberate the **germ** (oil-rich embryo) without breaking it. The germ, being **less dense and more buoyant**, is floated off in **hydrocyclones**.

**3. Fine Grinding (1 mark):**
The de-germed slurry is finely ground to release the **starch granules** from the protein matrix and pericarp fiber.

**4. Fiber Washing (1 mark):**
The slurry is passed over **shaker screens** where **fibrous hulls are retained** and **starch + gluten pass through**. Fiber is washed, dewatered, and dried to animal feed.

**5. Gluten–Starch Separation (1 mark):**
A **disk-nozzle centrifuge (Westfalia-type)** exploits the **density difference**:
- Starch density ≈ **1.5 g/cm³**
- Gluten density ≈ **1.1 g/cm³**
Starch-rich underflow (A-starch) and gluten-rich overflow are obtained.

**6. Washing and Drying (1 mark):**
A-starch is washed in **multi-stage hydrocyclones** to remove residual solubles, then **dewatered** and **flash-dried** to <13 % moisture.

**Block Diagram (in words — sketch in exam):**

`Maize → Steep Tank (SO₂ 50°C 36 h) → Coarse Mill → Germ Cyclone → Fine Mill → Fiber Screen → Gluten Centrifuge → Starch Cyclone (multi-stage) → Dewatering Centrifuge → Flash Dryer → Starch Powder (99 % purity)`

---

**(b)** Explain the **gelatinization and retrogradation** of starch. Discuss the factors affecting gelatinization temperature and the industrial significance of retrogradation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Gelatinization (3 marks):**
- On heating starch in **excess water above 60–70 °C**, granules absorb water and swell **reversibly up to ~60 °C** (reversible swelling).
- Beyond the gelatinization temperature (T_g), **hydrogen bonds in the crystalline amylopectin regions break**, the **birefringence is lost**, the **viscosity rises sharply**, and amylose **leaches out** into the surrounding water → formation of a **starch paste**.
- This is an **irreversible, endothermic process**; enthalpy ΔH_gel ≈ **10–20 J/g** (measured by DSC).

**2. Retrogradation (2 marks):**
- On cooling the gelatinized paste, **amylose chains realign** into **double helices** and form a **3-D gel network**.
- This is the cause of **bread staling** and **syneresis** (water weep) in refrigerated sauces.
- **Amylopectin retrogrades very slowly** (days to weeks) and is responsible for long-term staling.

**3. Factors affecting gelatinization temperature (1 mark):**
- **Botanical source** (potato 56–66 °C; maize 62–72 °C; waxy maize 63–72 °C).
- **Water content** (insufficient water → higher T_g).
- **Salt and sugar** (sugar raises T_g; NaCl slightly lowers it).
- **Granule size** (larger granules gelatinize earlier).
- **pH** (alkaline conditions lower T_g, acid hydrolysis pre-ruptures granules).

**4. Industrial significance of retrogradation (1 mark):**
- **Resistant starch (RS-3)** formation: retrograded amylose is **indigestible** → dietary fiber, low-glycemic-index foods.
- Controlled retrogradation used in **sushi rice**, **mochi**, and **rice noodles**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **acid hydrolysis** and **enzymatic hydrolysis** of starch. Compare the two processes with respect to mechanism, products, and industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Acid Hydrolysis (2.5 marks):**
- **Reagent:** Dilute HCl or H₂SO₄ (0.05–1.0 M).
- **Conditions:** 50–60 °C for 1–4 hours (thin-boiling) up to several hours for thick-boiling.
- **Mechanism:** Protonation of the glycosidic oxygen → cleavage of the **α-1,4 bond** → formation of a **cyclic oxocarbenium ion intermediate** → attack by water → reducing sugar + shorter chain.
- **Products:** **Acid-modified starches (thin-boiling)** with lower viscosity; **glucose syrups (DE 30–55)** if reaction is prolonged.
- **Drawback:** Random cleavage, produces **reversion products** (maltulose, isomaltose), and requires **acid neutralization + ion exchange purification**.

**2. Enzymatic Hydrolysis (3 marks):**
A **two-step** process using **α-amylase** and **glucoamylase**:
- **Liquefaction:** *Bacillus licheniformis* α-amylase at **pH 6.0–6.5, 95 °C, 5–10 min** → maltodextrins (DE 8–15).
- **Saccharification:** *Aspergillus niger* glucoamylase at **pH 4.5, 60 °C, 24–72 h** → glucose syrup (DE 95–98).
- **Optional debranching:** *Klebsiella* pullulanase cleaves α-1,6 bonds → linear maltose/oligomers.
- **Mechanism:** α-Amylase is an **endo-acting** hydrolase; glucoamylase is an **exo-acting** enzyme releasing **β-D-glucose** units from non-reducing ends.

**3. Comparison (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Acid Hydrolysis | Enzymatic Hydrolysis |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 50–60 °C | 60–95 °C |
| Time | 1–4 h | 24–72 h |
| Selectivity | Non-selective | Highly selective |
| DE achievable | Up to 55 | Up to 98 |
| By-products | Reversion products | Negligible |
| Purity / purification | Acid removal needed | Minimal purification |
| Capex / Opex | Low capex, high opex | High capex, low opex |

**4. Industrial Applications (≈ implicit, included in description).**

---

**(b)** What are the **modified starches**? Explain in detail the **cross-linking, acetylation, and hydroxypropylation** reactions. Mention at least **two industrial applications** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Modified Starch (1 mark):**
Modified starches are native starches whose physical, chemical, or enzymatic treatment alters one or more of their properties (gelatinization, retrogradation, paste clarity, freeze–thaw stability) to **fit a specific end-use** better than the native form.

**2. Cross-Linking (2 marks):**
- **Reagents:** Sodium trimetaphosphate (STMP), sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP), phosphoryl chloride (POCl₃), or epichlorohydrin.
- **Reaction (POCl₃):**

$$
2 \text{Starch-OH} + \text{POCl}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Starch-O-PO(O)-O-Starch} + 3 \text{HCl}
$$

- **Effect:** Cross-links **amylose and amylopectin chains** → **granule rigidity** → **resistance to shear, acid, and high temperature**.
- **Applications:** (i) Salad dressings (acid-stable thickener), (ii) canned foods retort-sterilized at 121 °C, (iii) corrugated board adhesive.

**3. Acetylation (2 marks):**
- **Reagent:** Acetic anhydride or vinyl acetate, with **NaOH catalyst (pH 8–9)**.
- **Reaction:**

$$
\text{Starch-OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Starch-O-CO-CH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
$$

- **Effect:** Bulky **acetyl groups** disrupt H-bonding → **lower gelatinization temperature, improved freeze–thaw stability, increased paste clarity**.
- **Applications:** (i) Frozen foods (ice-cream, sauces), (ii) batters and breadings, (iii) biodegradable films (E1414).

**4. Hydroxypropylation (2 marks):**
- **Reagent:** Propylene oxide (CH₃–CH–CH₂, cyclic ether) under **alkaline conditions (pH 11–12)**.
- **Reaction:**

$$
\text{Starch-OH} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH-CH}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Starch-O-CH}_2\text{CH(OH)CH}_3
$$

- **Effect:** Hydroxypropyl group → **steric hindrance** → **delayed retrogradation**, **cold-water swelling**, **clearer pastes**.
- **Applications:** (i) UHT-processed soups and sauces, (ii) refrigerated and frozen bakery fillings (E1440), (iii) pharmaceutical controlled-release matrices.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Point:** Many students write the cross-linking reagent as **"POCl₂"** — it is **POCl₃ (phosphoryl chloride, phosphorus oxychloride)**. A 0.5-mark penalty per wrong reagent name.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Starch Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Starch = α-glucan storage polysaccharide of plants; monomer = α-D-glucopyranose (C₆H₁₂O₆).
- **Two polymers:** Amylose (linear, 15–30 %, α-1,4, MW 10⁵–10⁶ Da) and Amylopectin (branched, 70–85 %, α-1,4 + α-1,6, MW 10⁷–10⁹ Da).
- **Glycosidic bonds:** α-1,4 (linear), α-1,6 (branch); contrast with cellulose which has β-1,4.
- **Molar masses:** Anhydroglucose unit = **162 g/mol**; free glucose = **180 g/mol**; hydrolysis mass gain = **11.1 %**.
- **Iodine test:** Amylose → **deep blue (660 nm)**; Amylopectin → **red-purple (540 nm)**; mechanism = polyiodide inside V-amylose helix.
- **Granule features:** 1–100 µm; birefringent (Maltese cross); semi-crystalline with **growth rings, blocklets, lamellae (~9 nm)**.
- **Gelatinization temperature (T_g):** 60–80 °C; loses birefringence; endothermic; ΔH ≈ 10–20 J/g; irreversible.
- **Retrogradation:** Amylose re-association at 4 °C; causes bread staling and resistant starch (RS-3).
- **Industrial extraction:** Maize wet milling → steep (SO₂ 50 °C 36 h) → germ → fiber → gluten → starch (centrifuge + hydrocyclone).
- **Acid hydrolysis:** HCl/H₂SO₄ 50–60 °C; thin-boiling starch; DE ≤ 55; produces reversion products.
- **Enzymatic hydrolysis:** α-Amylase (liquefaction 95 °C) + Glucoamylase (saccharification 60 °C); DE up to 98.
- **Modified starches:** Cross-linked (POCl₃, STMP) — acid/shear resistant; Acetylated (Ac₂O) — freeze–thaw stable; Hydroxypropylated (PO) — cold-swelling, anti-staling.
- **Major applications:** Food (60 %), paper (15 %), textile (8 %), fermentation (7 %), pharma (5 %), adhesives (5 %).
- **Industrial production leaders:** **Maize starch** (≈ 73 % global share), then cassava, potato, wheat, rice, sago.
- **One-line answer traps:** Starch is **α-1,4**, cellulose is **β-1,4**; amylose is **NOT soluble in cold water**; blue value of amylopectin is **<1 %**, not zero; the >100 % glucose yield is due to **water of hydrolysis**, not stoichiometric error.
- **High-value derivatives:** Cyclodextrins (α, β, γ) via CGTase; HFCS-55 via glucose isomerase; maltodextrins (DE 3–20); resistant starch (RS-3); thermoplastic starch (TPS) for bioplastics; HES (hydroxyethyl starch) plasma expander.

> **End of Module 2 — Starch Notes | PCCHT502 — Chemical Technology | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethyl Alcohol by Fermentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethyl Alcohol by Fermentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethyl Alcohol (Ethanol, C₂H₅OH)** is a volatile, flammable, colorless liquid manufactured industrially by the **biochemical conversion of fermentable sugars** into alcohol and carbon dioxide through the catalytic action of microbial enzymes (primarily yeast). This is classified under the broader umbrella of **Industrial Fermentation Technology** in the KTU 2024 scheme of Chemical Technology.

The Governing Biochemical Reaction is:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCHT502, Module 2):**
> Industrial alcohol production focuses on *raw material selection*, *biochemistry of fermentation*, *unit operations* (mashing, fermentation, distillation, dehydration), and *process economics*. Students are expected to draw complete process flow diagrams and perform material balance calculations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of fermentation as "biological baking"** — but instead of yeast making bread rise by producing CO₂ bubbles, we are trapping the *liquid product* (ethanol) while releasing the gas. A sugar solution is like a "battery" of stored chemical energy. Yeast cells are tiny "factories" that break down this sugar to extract energy for their own survival, accidentally producing ethanol as a *metabolic waste product*. We just hijack this natural process and collect the waste!

## 1.3 Industrial Importance of Ethanol

| Sector | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Fuel** | E10, E20, E85 blending with petrol; 1G and 2G bioethanol |
| **Chemical Feedstock** | Acetaldehyde, Acetic Acid, Ethyl Acetate, Butadiene, Ethylene |
| **Beverages** | Rectified Spirit, IMFL, IMFL-grade neutral alcohol |
| **Pharma/Cosmetics** | Solvent for tinctures, sanitizers, perfumes |
| **Industrial** | Solvent for paints, varnishes, explosives (gun cotton) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Industry:**
> - **Theoretical Yield** of ethanol from glucose = **51.1 %** by weight
> - **Commercial Yield (Industrial)** = **~95 %** of theoretical
> - **Fermentation Temperature Optimum** = **28 °C to 32 °C**
> - **pH Optimum** = **4.0 to 4.5**
> - **Fermentation Time** = **48 to 72 hours**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Raw Materials

Industrial alcohol fermentation requires a **fermentable carbohydrate source**. The KTU syllabus categorizes raw materials into three distinct categories based on the *type of pre-treatment* required.

### Category A: Sugar-Containing Materials (Direct Fermentation)
- **Sugarcane Molasses** (preferred; ~50 % fermentable sugars)
- **Beet Molasses** (Europe; ~48 % total sugars)
- **Fresh Juices** (Sugarcane juice, beet juice, fruit juices)

### Category B: Starch-Containing Materials (Saccharification Needed)
- **Corn (Maize)** — the largest ethanol source in USA/Brazil
- **Wheat, Barley, Rye, Sorghum, Millet**
- **Tubers** — Potato, Sweet Potato, Tapioca/Cassava

### Category C: Cellulosic Materials (2G Ethanol — Hydrolysis Needed)
- Wood waste, Bagasse, Rice straw, Sawdust, Paper pulp
- Requires **acid hydrolysis** or **enzymatic hydrolysis** first

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Molasses is Preferred (KTU board favourite):**
> Molasses is a *by-product* of the sugar industry (it cannot crystallize further into sugar), making it **cheap, abundant, and pre-processed**. It contains invert sugar (glucose + fructose) which is *directly* fermentable — no cooking or saccharification step is needed.

## 2.2 Biochemistry of the Enzymes Involved

| Enzyme | Source | Substrate | Action | Product |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Invertase** | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) | Hydrolysis | Glucose + Fructose |
| **Zymase** (complex) | Yeast cytoplasm | Glucose/Fructose | Glycolysis → Fermentation | Ethanol + CO₂ |
| **Diastase (Amylase)** | Malt (germinated barley) | Starch | Hydrolysis | Maltose + Dextrins |
| **Maltase** | Yeast | Maltose | Hydrolysis | 2 × Glucose |
| **Cellulase** | *Trichoderma* (in 2G) | Cellulose | Hydrolysis | Glucose |

> [!NOTE]
> The reactions in sequence:
> $$\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\text{Diastase}} \text{Maltose} \xrightarrow{\text{Maltase}} 2\,\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

## 2.3 The Microorganism — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*

* **Type:** Unicellular fungus (yeast)
* **Shape:** Oval, 6–8 µm
* **Reproduction:** Budding (asexual)
* **Tolerance:** Up to **~14 % v/v** alcohol (above this, yeast cells die)
* **Optimum Conditions:** pH 4.0–4.5, T = 28–32 °C, anaerobic

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Gay-Lussac Equation | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ | Mass basis |
| 2 | Theoretical Yield of Ethanol from Glucose | $\dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} \times 100$ | **51.11 \%** |
| 3 | Theoretical Yield of CO₂ from Glucose | $\dfrac{2 \times 44}{180} \times 100$ | **48.89 \%** |
| 4 | Mass of Ethanol from Sugar | $m_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.5111 \times m_{\text{sugar}} \times \eta$ | kg |
| 5 | Volume of Ethanol | $V_{\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH}}}{\rho_{\text{EtOH}}}$ ; $\rho = 0.789$ | Litres |
| 6 | Substrate to Product Conversion | $\text{Sugar \%} = \dfrac{\text{Brix}}{1.0225 - 0.00225 \times \text{Brix}}$ | °Brix basis |
| 7 | Dilution Equation for Molasses | $V_1 C_1 = V_2 C_2$ | Mass/volumetric |
| 8 | Mass Balance (overall) | $F_{\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}} + F_{\text{accumulated}} + F_{\text{CO}_2}$ | kg/hr |
| 9 | Fermentation Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual Yield}}{\text{Theoretical Yield}} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Alcohol Evaporation Loss | $\approx 2\text{ to }5\,\%$ of total yield | empirical |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *theoretical yield* of ethanol from **glucose** (51.1 %) with that from **sucrose**. Remember — sucrose is a *disaccharide* (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁, M = 342) and one mole of sucrose yields **2 moles of monosaccharides** that can ferment, so the yield from sucrose is approximately **53.8 %**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Petroleum Blending:** In India, the Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP) targets **20 % ethanol blending (E20)** by 2025 — driving massive demand.
* **Distillery Effluent (Spent Wash / Vinasse):** 8–15 L of effluent per litre of alcohol — treated in biomethanation / biocomposting plants.
* **CO₂ Recovery:** Pure food-grade CO₂ is captured from fermenter off-gas for carbonated beverages and dry ice — a valuable co-product.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Yield of Ethanol from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Equation)

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU board exam and is frequently asked for 5–7 marks.

### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Stoichiometric Equation

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Logic:** One molecule of glucose (hexose sugar) splits into two molecules of ethyl alcohol and two molecules of carbon dioxide under anaerobic conditions. This is the foundational equation formulated by **Gay-Lussac** in 1815.

### Step 2 — Assign Molecular Weights

| Species | Formula | Molecular Weight (g/mol) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Glucose | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ | $6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180$ |
| Ethanol | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ | $2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46$ |
| Carbon Dioxide | $\text{CO}_2$ | $12 + 2(16) = 44$ |

### Step 3 — Compute Theoretical Yield of Ethanol (Mass Basis)

The theoretical yield (η_theoretical) is the *mass ratio* of ethanol produced per unit mass of glucose consumed.

$$\eta_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Ethanol Produced}}{\text{Mass of Glucose Reacted}} \times 100$$

Substituting the stoichiometric values from 1 mole of glucose:

$$\eta_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{1 \times 180} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{92}{180} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{theoretical}} = 51.11\,\%}$$

**Logic:** This means that if we react 100 kg of pure glucose, we get a *maximum* of 51.11 kg of ethanol. The remaining 48.89 % is split as CO₂ (48.89 kg) and minor by-products.

### Step 4 — Compute Theoretical Yield of CO₂ (Verification)

By similar mass-balance logic:

$$\eta_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 44}{180} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{88}{180} \times 100 = 48.89\,\%$$

**Check:** $51.11 + 48.89 = 100.00$ ✓ — Conservation of mass is satisfied.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Standard KTU Numerical Problem)

> **Problem:** A distillery processes **10,000 kg/day** of cane molasses containing **50 % w/w total fermentable sugars**. Fermentation efficiency is **92 %**. Calculate:
> (a) The actual mass of ethanol produced per day.
> (b) The volume of rectified spirit (95 % v/v ethanol, ρ = 0.804 g/mL at 15.6 °C).
> (c) The mass of CO₂ released per day.
> *(Take density of pure ethanol = 0.789 g/mL)*

### Part (a) — Mass of Ethanol Produced

**Step 1:** Calculate the total mass of sugar fed per day.

$$m_{\text{sugar}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.50 = 5{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the theoretical yield (51.11 %).

$$m_{\text{EtOH, theoretical}} = 5{,}000 \times 0.5111 = 2{,}555.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the fermentation efficiency (92 %).

$$m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 2{,}555.5 \times 0.92$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 2{,}351.06 \text{ kg/day}}$$

### Part (b) — Volume of Rectified Spirit

**Step 1:** Convert mass of pure ethanol to volume.

$$V_{\text{EtOH, pure}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{EtOH}}}{\rho_{\text{EtOH}}} = \dfrac{2{,}351.06 \text{ kg}}{0.789 \text{ kg/L}}$$

$$V_{\text{EtOH, pure}} = 2{,}979.80 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 2:** Account for 95 % v/v rectified spirit (the remaining 5 % is water).

$$V_{\text{rectified}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EtOH, pure}}}{0.95} = \dfrac{2{,}979.80}{0.95}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{rectified}} = 3{,}136.63 \text{ L/day}}$$

### Part (c) — Mass of CO₂ Released

**Step 1:** Theoretical CO₂ from the sugar.

$$m_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ theoretical}} = 5{,}000 \times 0.4889 = 2{,}444.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the same efficiency factor (yeast consumes 92 % of available sugar; CO₂ scales identically).

$$m_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ actual}} = 2{,}444.5 \times 0.92$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ actual}} = 2{,}248.94 \text{ kg/day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This CO₂ is captured, scrubbed, and sold as **food-grade CO₂** (for soft drinks) — a major revenue stream for any modern distillery.

---

## 3.3 Mass Balance Across the Fermenter (Analytical Matrix)

| Stream In | kg/day | Stream Out | kg/day |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fermentable Sugar | 5,000.00 | Ethanol (actual) | 2,351.06 |
| Water (in molasses + dilution) | 7,000.00 | CO₂ (off-gas) | 2,248.94 |
| Nutrients (N, P salts) | 50.00 | Unfermented Sugar | 200.00 |
| Yeast Inoculum | 5.00 | Fusel Oils | 30.00 |
| — | — | Yeast Biomass (grown) | 50.00 |
| — | — | Spent Wash (water + salts) | 7,175.00 |
| **Total In** | **12,055.00** | **Total Out** | **12,055.00** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Every numerical you solve must close the *mass balance*. The examiner checks: $\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$. If it doesn't, marks are deducted.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Raw Material Routes

| Parameter | Molasses Route | Grain (Corn) Route | Cellulosic (2G) Route |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pre-treatment | Dilution only | Milling + Cooking + Saccharification | Acid/Enzymatic Hydrolysis |
| Enzyme Cost | Lowest | Medium (malt addition) | Highest (cellulase) |
| Fermentation Time | 48 h | 60–72 h | 72–96 h |
| Yield of EtOH (L/tonne) | 250–280 | 370–400 | 300–350 |
| Capital Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| By-products | Vinasse, CO₂ | DDGS (animal feed), CO₂ | Lignin, CO₂ |
| Status in India | Mature, dominant | Growing | Pilot stage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Process Flow Diagram (Molasses to Absolute Alcohol)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Molasses Storage Tank] --> B[Weighing and Brix Measurement]
    B --> C[Dilution Tank with Water to 15 to 20 percent Sugar]
    C --> D[Nutrient Addition NH4 Sulfate and Superphosphate]
    D --> E[pH Adjustment Tank add H2SO4 to pH 4.0 to 4.5]
    E --> F[Heat Exchanger Cool to 28 to 32 degree C]
    F --> G[Fermenter Vessel with Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    G --> G1[CO2 Scrubber and Recovery]
    G1 --> G2[Pure CO2 Storage Food Grade]
    G --> H[Beer Well Storage of Fermented Wash]
    H --> I[Beer Column Distillation]
    I --> I1[Spent Wash Outlet to Biomethanation]
    I --> J[Rectification Column 95 percent Alcohol]
    J --> K[Dehydration by Azeotropic Distillation with Benzene]
    K --> L[Absolute Alcohol 99.9 percent Storage]
    J --> J1[Fusel Oil Decanter Side Stream]
    L --> M[Final Product Dispatch E20 Fuel Grade]
```

## 4.2 Fermenter Internal Architecture (Mash Chemistry Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Invert Sugar Glucose] --> S2[Glycolysis Pathway]
    S2 --> S3[Pyruvate Formation]
    S3 --> S4[Acetaldehyde via Pyruvate Decarboxylase]
    S4 --> S5[Ethanol via Alcohol Dehydrogenase]
    S5 --> P1[Ethanol C2H5OH]
    S3 --> P2[CO2 Gas Release]
    N1[Nutrients N and P] --> S2
    Y1[Yeast Cells] --> S2
    S2 --> Y1
    Y1 --> BY[Yeast Biomass By product]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Distillery Process Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BlockA[Block 1 Raw Material Preparation]
        A1[Receiving and Storage]
        A2[Dilution and Sterilization]
        A3[Nutrient and pH Adjustment]
    end
    subgraph BlockB[Block 2 Bioconversion]
        B1[Yeast Propagation Seed Tank]
        B2[Main Fermenter 50,000 to 100,000 L]
        B3[Beer Well and CO2 Scrubbing]
    end
    subgraph BlockC[Block 3 Separation and Purification]
        C1[Beer Column]
        C2[Rectification Column]
        C3[Dehydration Molecular Sieve or Azeotropic]
    end
    subgraph BlockD[Block 4 Storage and Dispatch]
        D1[Denaturant Addition Industrial Grade]
        D2[Tank Farm Storage]
        D3[Tanker Loading and Dispatch]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    I1[Spent Wash Biomethanation] -.-> C1
    I2[Yeast Sludge Drying] -.-> B3
    I3[CO2 Liquefaction Plant] -.-> B3
    I4[Fusel Oil Refining] -.-> C2
```

## 4.4 Process Logic Narrative

**Stage 1 — Raw Material Preparation:** Molasses is diluted from its viscous ~80 °Brix to a working concentration of **15–20 % w/v sugar** (≈ 16–22 °Brix). This *osmotic* dilution is critical — too concentrated a mash would *plasmolyze* the yeast, killing it before fermentation can start.

**Stage 2 — Bioconversion:** The yeast *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* is first grown in a *seed tank* (smaller fermenter) and then transferred to the main fermenter. Anaerobic conditions (sealed vessel, CO₂ blanket) are maintained. Temperature is controlled via external cooling coils. Yeast converts sugar in two key enzymatic stages:
1. EMP pathway (Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas) — glucose → pyruvate.
2. Alcoholic fermentation — pyruvate → acetaldehyde → ethanol.

**Stage 3 — Separation:** The fermented "beer" (~ 8–10 % v/v alcohol) is sent to the *beer column* where crude ethanol is distilled overhead, leaving spent wash at the bottom. The crude alcohol is sent to the *rectification column* to produce rectified spirit (95 % v/v). For fuel-grade absolute alcohol (99.9 %), a *dehydration* step (azeotropic distillation with benzene/cyclohexane, or molecular sieves like Zeolite 3Å) is used to break the ethanol–water azeotrope (95.6 % EtOH).

**Stage 4 — Storage:** Denaturants (methanol, MEK, denatonium benzoate) are added to industrial alcohol to make it undrinkable — this exempts it from excise duty and makes it economically viable as a fuel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**List the major enzymes involved in the industrial fermentation of ethyl alcohol and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major enzymes involved are:

1. **Invertase** (1 mark) — Hydrolyzes sucrose (a non-fermentable disaccharide) into glucose and fructose, which are *fermentable* monosaccharides.
2. **Zymase** (1 mark) — A *complex of enzymes* present in yeast cytoplasm that catalyzes the conversion of glucose into ethyl alcohol and carbon dioxide via the EMP pathway.
3. **Diastase (Amylase)** (1 mark) — Converts *starch* (in grain-based fermentation) into maltose and dextrins, which can then be hydrolyzed to glucose by maltase.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Why is molasses the most preferred raw material for industrial alcohol production in India?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Low cost and abundance** (1 mark) — Molasses is a cheap *by-product* of the sugar industry (residue after sugar crystallization) and is abundantly available across India, especially in Maharashtra and Uttar Pradesh.
2. **Direct fermentability** (1 mark) — It contains *invert sugar* (glucose + fructose) that does not require any pre-treatment like cooking or saccharification — yeast can act on it directly.
3. **Established infrastructure** (1 mark) — India has a mature *integrated sugar–distillery complex* model, providing cheap utilities (steam, power) from bagasse-fired boilers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**With a neat flow diagram, describe the manufacture of ethyl alcohol from cane molasses. Discuss the biochemistry of fermentation and the role of each enzyme involved.** *(14 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Process Description (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation (1.5 marks)**
Cane molasses (containing ~50 % total reducing sugars) is received from sugar factories and stored in mild-steel tanks. It is diluted with warm water in the *dilution tank* to bring the sugar concentration to **15–20 % w/v**. This step also provides partial sterilization.

**Step 2 — Nutrient Addition and pH Control (1.5 marks)**
Ammonium sulfate (0.1 % w/v) is added as a *nitrogen source* for yeast growth, along with superphosphate for *phosphorus*. Sulfuric acid or lactic acid is used to adjust the pH to **4.0–4.5** — this acidic environment suppresses bacterial contamination and is optimal for *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*.

**Step 3 — Fermentation (2 marks)**
The mash is cooled to **28–32 °C** via a heat exchanger and pumped into a sealed, stirred fermenter. A *yeast inoculum* (~ 5 % v/v) propagated in a seed fermenter is added. Fermentation is **anaerobic** (sealed, CO₂ blanket) and proceeds for **48–72 hours**. The biochemical reaction is:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$
The "beer" (fermented wash) is collected; CO₂ is scrubbed and recovered as food-grade product.

**Step 4 — Distillation and Rectification (2 marks)**
The beer (~ 8–10 % v/v alcohol) is fed to a *beer column* where alcohol is stripped overhead, leaving spent wash at the bottom (sent to biomethanation). The crude alcohol (80–90 % v/v) is sent to a *rectification column* to obtain **rectified spirit (95 % v/v)**. Fusel oils are drawn off as a side stream from the rectifying column. For fuel-grade absolute alcohol, the **ethanol–water azeotrope** is broken using a *dehydration column* with benzene/cyclohexane, or modern *molecular sieve (Zeolite 3Å)* adsorption.

#### Part (b) — Biochemistry and Enzymes (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Microbial Strain (1 mark)**
*Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (baker's yeast) is used. It is a facultative anaerobe, oval-shaped (6–8 µm), and reproduces by *budding*. It can tolerate alcohol up to ~ 14 % v/v.

**Step 2 — Two-Stage Enzymatic Mechanism (3 marks)**

*Stage 1 — Glycolysis (EMP Pathway):*
The enzyme **Zymase** (a complex of ~ 11 enzymes, including hexokinase, phosphofructokinase, and pyruvate kinase) catalyzes the breakdown of glucose to *pyruvate*:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 2\,\text{NAD}^+ + 2\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{P}_i \rightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{COCOOH} + 2\,\text{NADH} + 2\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

*Stage 2 — Alcoholic Fermentation:*
Pyruvate is decarboxylated by **Pyruvate Decarboxylase** to acetaldehyde, releasing CO₂:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{COCOOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{CO}_2$$
Acetaldehyde is then reduced by **Alcohol Dehydrogenase** to ethanol, regenerating NAD⁺:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{NAD}^+$$

**Step 3 — Role of Each Enzyme (3 marks)**

| Enzyme | Substrate | Product | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Invertase | Sucrose | Glucose + Fructose | Hydrolyzes non-fermentable disaccharides |
| Zymase | Glucose | Pyruvate | Catalyzes EMP pathway |
| Pyruvate Decarboxylase | Pyruvate | Acetaldehyde | Releases CO₂ |
| Alcohol Dehydrogenase | Acetaldehyde | Ethanol | Final reduction step |
| Diastase | Starch | Maltose | Needed only for grain route |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Forgetting to draw the flow diagram** costs 2 marks. Even a *labelled block diagram* is mandatory.
> - Confusing **Zymase** (a *complex* of glycolytic enzymes) with a single enzyme loses 1 mark.
> - Students often write the *summary equation* but **forget the NAD⁺/NADH cofactor regeneration logic** — examiners expect you to know *why* anaerobic conditions are needed.
> - Spelling **"Saccharomyces"** wrongly (e.g., Saccharomycis) attracts a minor deduction.

---

### 📗 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**A distillery processes 20,000 kg/day of cane molasses containing 52 % w/w fermentable sugars. If the fermentation efficiency is 90 %, calculate:**
**(a) The actual mass of ethyl alcohol and the volume of 95 % v/v rectified spirit produced per day.**
**(b) The mass of CO₂ released per day, and discuss the various by-products obtained in the process with their applications.** *(14 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Mass and Volume Calculations (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mass of Sugar Fed per Day (1 mark)**

$$m_{\text{sugar}} = 20{,}000 \times 0.52 = 10{,}400 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Mass of Ethanol (2 marks)**

$$m_{\text{EtOH, theoretical}} = 10{,}400 \times 0.5111 = 5{,}315.44 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Mass of Ethanol (1 mark)**

$$m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 5{,}315.44 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{EtOH, actual}} = 4{,}783.90 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**Step 4 — Volume of Rectified Spirit (95 % v/v) (3 marks)**

Volume of pure ethanol (ρ = 0.789 kg/L):

$$V_{\text{pure}} = \dfrac{4{,}783.90}{0.789} = 6{,}063.12 \text{ L/day}$$

Volume of 95 % rectified spirit:

$$V_{\text{rectified}} = \dfrac{6{,}063.12}{0.95}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{rectified}} = 6{,}382.23 \text{ L/day}}$$

#### Part (b) — CO₂ and By-Products (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mass of CO₂ (2 marks)**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ theoretical}} = 10{,}400 \times 0.4889 = 5{,}084.56 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ actual}} = 5{,}084.56 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ actual}} = 4{,}576.10 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**Step 2 — Major By-Products and Their Applications (5 marks — 1 mark each)**

| By-Product | Origin | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** | Fermenter off-gas | Carbonated beverages, dry ice, supercritical CO₂ extraction, fire extinguishers, greenhouse fertilization |
| **2. Fusel Oils** | Side-draw of rectification column; mixture of amyl, butyl, propyl, and iso-propyl alcohols | Solvent for paints, lacquers, nitrocellulose; feedstock for plasticizers and esters |
| **3. Spent Wash (Vinasse)** | Bottom product of beer column; rich in K, N, organic matter | Biogas (biomethanation), bio-compost, irrigation, potassium-rich fertilizer |
| **4. Yeast Sludge** | Centrifugation of fermented beer; rich in protein and B-vitamins | Animal/poultry feed supplement; pharmaceutical-grade yeast extract |
| **5. Waste Heat / Steam** | Cogeneration plant using bagasse as fuel | Internal process steam; export to sugar mill; power generation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Numerical trap:** Students often forget to apply the *fermentation efficiency* to CO₂. While CO₂ is a *stoichiometric product* of the fermentation reaction, the 90 % efficiency means only 90 % of the sugar was actually fermented — so CO₂ must also be scaled by 0.90. ❌ Many students use 100 % here, losing 1 mark.
> - **Volume conversion trap:** Density of *rectified spirit* is NOT 0.789. The density **0.789 g/mL is for pure ethanol**. The student must first compute the volume of pure ethanol, then divide by 0.95 to get the volume of 95 % spirit.
> - **By-products:** At least 3 by-products must be listed with applications — listing only CO₂ and spent wash loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Facts — Must memorize for KTU 2024:**

* 🔑 **Gay-Lussac equation:** $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ — write the **balanced form** every time, even in numericals.
* 🔑 **Theoretical yield** of ethanol from glucose = **51.1 % by weight**; from CO₂ = **48.9 %**.
* 🔑 **Theoretical yield** from **sucrose** = **53.8 %** (because 1 mole sucrose → 2 moles of fermentable hexose).
* 🔑 **Practical yield** in industry = **~ 90–95 %** of theoretical (i.e., ~ 46–48 % overall).
* 🔑 **Optimum conditions for yeast:** pH = **4.0–4.5**; T = **28–32 °C**; anaerobic; alcohol tolerance ~ **14 % v/v**.
* 🔑 **Key enzymes:** **Invertase** (for sucrose), **Zymase** (for glucose), **Diastase** (for starch), **Maltase** (for maltose).
* 🔑 **Dilution target:** Molasses is diluted to **15–20 % w/v sugar** before fermentation.
* 🔑 **Distillation sequence:** Beer column → Rectification column → Dehydration column → Absolute alcohol.
* 🔑 **Azeotrope issue:** Ethanol and water form a constant-boiling mixture at **95.6 % v/v EtOH at 78.2 °C** — this is why a separate dehydration step is needed for absolute alcohol.
* 🔑 **Dehydration methods:** Azeotropic distillation (benzene/cyclohexane/ethyl acetate) **or** Molecular Sieve Adsorption (Zeolite 3Å — modern preferred method).
* 🔑 **By-products (in exam order):** CO₂ → Fusel oils → Spent wash (Vinasse) → Yeast sludge.
* 🔑 **Drawing the flow diagram is mandatory** in 14-mark questions — even a labelled block diagram earns at least 2 marks.
* 🔑 **Always close your mass balance:** $\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$ — examiners check this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When answering long questions, *always* start with a labelled flow diagram (1–2 marks), then describe each unit operation sequentially. For numericals, *always* state the formula first, *then* substitute the values. This makes the valuation script-readable and ensures no marks are lost for missing logical steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroleum Refining: Chemical Composition of Crude
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Petroleum Refining: Chemical Composition of Crude

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Petroleum (Crude Oil)** is defined as a naturally occurring, flammable, dark-colored, viscous liquid hydrocarbon mixture found in geological formations beneath the Earth's surface. From a **chemical engineering thermodynamics** perspective, it is a *complex multi-component natural organic mixture* primarily composed of hydrocarbons with heteroatomic impurities (Sulfur, Nitrogen, Oxygen, and trace metals).

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PCCHT502**, the *Chemical Composition of Crude* refers to the systematic classification, quantification, and characterization of the molecular species present in crude oil — including aliphatic (paraffinic, naphthenic) and aromatic hydrocarbons, asphaltenes, resins, and non-hydrocarbon heteroatom compounds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Crude petroleum is a complex mixture of paraffinic, naphthenic, and aromatic hydrocarbons, containing small but technologically critical amounts of organic compounds of sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen, along with trace organometallic complexes of vanadium, nickel, iron, and copper."*

### 1.2 The Elemental Snapshot (Standard Industry Reference)

The elemental composition of a typical crude oil lies within well-defined limits:

| Element | Weight % (Range) | Significance |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Carbon (C)** | 83 – 87% | Backbone of all hydrocarbons |
| **Hydrogen (H)** | 11 – 14% | Energy density determinant |
| **Sulfur (S)** | 0.1 – 5.5% | Catalyst poison, corrosion, acid rain |
| **Nitrogen (N)** | 0.1 – 1.7% | Catalyst poison, color instability |
| **Oxygen (O)** | 0.1 – 4.5% | Acidity (Naphthenic acid), emulsion |
| **Metals (Ni, V, Fe)** | < 1000 ppm | Catalyst deactivation, ash formation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Soup" 🍲

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:**
> Imagine **crude oil as a thick, dark "molecular soup"** cooked deep inside the Earth over millions of years from ancient marine organisms (zooplankton and algae). The pot contains thousands of different "ingredients":
> - The **main broth** is a mixture of long, medium, and short hydrocarbon chains (paraffins), ring-shaped molecules (naphthenes), and flat hexagonal rings (aromatics).
> - **Spices** dissolved in the soup are the heteroatoms — Sulfur, Nitrogen, and Oxygen — present in trace amounts but capable of completely ruining the taste (i.e., downstream refining performance).
> - **Tiny metal filings** (V, Ni, Fe) are suspended in the broth, undetectable by eye but fatal to expensive refining **catalysts**.
>
> The job of a **refinery** is therefore to *separate this soup* (via distillation) and then *remove the bad spices* (via hydrodesulfurization, hydrotreating) to produce pure, usable products.

### 1.4 Why Composition Study is the Foundation of Refining

The economic value of a crude oil is **not** determined by its appearance or viscosity — it is determined entirely by its **molecular composition**. The Chemical Composition of Crude dictates:

- **Refinery Configuration** → A "heavy paraffinic" crude needs a different processing scheme than a "light aromatic" crude.
- **Product Slate** → Paraffinic crudes favor diesel and jet fuel; naphthenic-aromatic crudes favor gasoline and asphalt.
- **Catalyst Selection** → Sulfur content decides the metallurgy (carbon steel vs. stainless steel) and catalyst type.
- **Market Price** → Light, sweet (low-sulfur) crudes trade at premium prices; heavy, sour crudes trade at discounts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Petroleum Constituents

Crude oil components are systematically classified into two super-categories — **Hydrocarbons** and **Non-Hydrocarbons** — with further sub-classifications critical for refining operations.

#### 2.1.1 Hydrocarbon Family (95–99% of crude by weight)

**(a) Paraffins (Alkanes) — Saturated, Acyclic Hydrocarbons**

- **General Formula:** $C_n H_{2n+2}$
- **Structure:** Straight or branched open chains, all C–C single bonds.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - **Normal Paraffins (n-alkanes):** Straight chains — e.g., n-butane, n-hexane. High cetane number (excellent diesel).
  - **Iso-Paraffins (i-alkanes):** Branched chains — e.g., iso-octane. High octane number (excellent gasoline).
- **Physical Tendency:** Paraffinic crudes are **waxy**, have high pour point, and are the preferred feedstock for **lubricating oils, paraffin wax, and high-grade diesel**.

**(b) Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes) — Saturated, Cyclic Hydrocarbons**

- **General Formula:** $C_n H_{2n}$ (monocyclic)
- **Structure:** Saturated ring structures — cyclopentane, cyclohexane, and their alkyl-substituted derivatives.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - **Monocycloparaffins** (e.g., methylcyclohexane)
  - **Dicycloparaffins** (e.g., decalin)
  - **Polycycloparaffins** (steranes, hopanes — biomarkers)
- **Physical Tendency:** Naphthenic crudes yield **high-octane gasoline** and **aromatics precursor feedstocks**. They have low pour points.

**(c) Aromatics — Unsaturated, Cyclic Hydrocarbons**

- **General Formula:** $C_n H_{2n-6}$ (benzene series)
- **Structure:** Planar, conjugated hexagonal ring with delocalized π-electrons.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - **BTX (Benzene, Toluene, Xylenes)** — light, valuable petrochemicals.
  - **Polynuclear Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH)** — naphthalene, anthracene, phenanthrene.
  - **Aromatic resins** — high molecular weight aromatics.
- **Physical Tendency:** Aromatic-rich crudes produce **asphalt**, **coke**, and **high-octane gasoline blending stocks**.

#### 2.1.2 Non-Hydrocarbon Family (1–5% of crude by weight)

| Class | Typical Compounds | Refining Concern |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Sulfur Compounds** | Thiols (R–SH), Sulfides (R–S–R), Thiophenes, H₂S | Corrosion, SOₓ emissions, catalyst poisoning |
| **Nitrogen Compounds** | Pyridines, Quinolines, Pyrroles, Indoles | Catalyst poisoning (basic N), gum formation |
| **Oxygen Compounds** | Naphthenic acids, Phenols, Fatty acids | Corrosion, emulsion, acidity (TAN) |
| **Metallic Compounds** | Porphyrin complexes of V, Ni, Fe | Catalyst deactivation, ash, high-temperature corrosion |
| **Asphaltenes & Resins** | High MW polycyclic polar compounds | Fouling, coke, sludge formation |

### 2.2 The SARA Analysis (The Industry-Standard Compositional Breakdown)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Concept — SARA Fractionation:**
> Modern petroleum chemistry characterizes crude by separating it into four solubility classes using sequential solvent extraction and chromatography.

- **S — Saturates:** Non-polar hydrocarbons (paraffins + naphthenes). Lightest fraction.
- **A — Aromatics:** Polarizable aromatic hydrocarbons. Soluble in toluene.
- **R — Resins:** Polar, high-MW heteroatom-containing compounds. Soluble in n-heptane but precipitated by propane.
- **A — Asphaltenes:** Highly polar, polycyclic, high-MW "big chunks" of the molecule. Insoluble in n-heptane (or n-pentane).

**SARA is critical** because the residue, fuel stability, and coking tendency are direct functions of the asphaltene + resin content.

### 2.3 Refinery Parameter Definitions (KTU Board Essentials)

- **API Gravity (°API):** The universal inverse-density scale used globally by refiners.
- **Specific Gravity (SG):** Ratio of crude density to water density at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**.
- **Sulfur Classification (Sweet vs. Sour):**
  - **Sweet Crude:** Sulfur content **< 0.5 wt %** (premium, easy to refine).
  - **Sour Crude:** Sulfur content **> 0.5 wt %** (requires extensive desulfurization).
- **Watson Characterization Factor (Kw):** Index that correlates paraffinicity vs. aromaticity.
- **Total Acid Number (TAN):** mg of KOH required to neutralize 1 g of crude; indicates naphthenic acid corrosion potential.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Compositional Analysis

- The **refinery yield structure** is decided by the **H/C atomic ratio**. A higher H/C means more light paraffins (naphtha, kerosene); a lower H/C means more aromatics, asphaltenes, and coke.
- The **refinery complexity** (whether to invest in catalytic cracking, hydrocracking, coking) is decided by the **asphaltene + sulfur content**.
- The **market price** of crude (Brent vs. WTI vs. Dubai vs. Urals) is decided by **API gravity** and **sulfur content**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **API Gravity** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{15.6°C}} - 131.5$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Specific Gravity from API** | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Watson K-Factor** | $K_w = \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$ | $T_B$ in **°R** (°F + 460) |
| 4 | **H/C Atomic Ratio** | $\dfrac{H}{C} = \dfrac{wt\%\,H \cdot 12}{wt\%\,C \cdot 1.008}$ | Atomic basis |
| 5 | **Empirical Formula (Average)** | $C_n H_m$ where $m/n$ found from CH analysis | n = MW / 14 (paraffin basis) |
| 6 | **Refractivity Intercept (RI)** | $RI = n - \dfrac{d}{2}$ | n = refractive index, d = density (g/cm³) |
| 7 | **Pour Point Depression** | $PP = A \cdot (Naphthene\,\%) + B$ | Empirical; not a universal law |
| 8 | **Crude Oil Density (kg/m³)** | $\rho = 1000 \cdot SG_{15.6°C}$ | SI units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants to Commit:**
> - Density of water at 15.6 °C: $\rho_{water} = 999 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (often taken as **1000 kg/m³**)
> - Standard refining reference temperature: **15.6 °C (60 °F)**
> - Conversion: $°R = °F + 459.67$ and $K = \dfrac{5}{9}(°F - 32) + 273.15$
