## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The **American Petroleum Institute (API)** defined the gravity scale to give lighter crudes (lower density) *higher* API values — a counterintuitive but commercially logical convention.

**Starting Definition:** Specific gravity is the ratio of the density of the oil to the density of water at a reference temperature.

$$SG_{15.6°C} = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$$

The API institute defined the scale such that water has an **API gravity of exactly 10°**:

$$°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

**Verification Step:** For pure water at 15.6 °C, $SG = 1.000$.

$$°API_{water} = \dfrac{141.5}{1.000} - 131.5 = 10.0 \, °API \;\;\checkmark$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A crude with SG = 0.85 (density = 850 kg/m³) gives $°API = (141.5/0.85) - 131.5 = 35.0\,°API$ — this is a **light crude**, ideally suited for high-yield gasoline production. A heavy crude with SG = 0.98 gives $°API = 12.8°API$ — needs intensive upgrading (coking, hydrocracking).

### 3.2 Derivation: Empirical Molecular Formula of Crude

Given the **ultimate (elemental) analysis** of a crude oil sample:

- Weight % of C = $C\%$
- Weight % of H = $H\%$

The **H/C atomic ratio** is:

$$\dfrac{H}{C}_{atomic} = \dfrac{(H\%/1.008)}{(C\%/12.01)} = \dfrac{H\% \cdot 12.01}{C\% \cdot 1.008}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Board-style):**

A crude oil analysis reports: **C = 85.5 wt %**, **H = 12.0 wt %**. Find the empirical formula on a CH₂-incremental basis.

**Step 1:** Compute atomic ratio.

$$\dfrac{H}{C} = \dfrac{12.0 / 1.008}{85.5 / 12.01} = \dfrac{11.905}{7.119} = 1.672$$

**Step 2:** Normalize per 1 carbon atom.

$$C_1 H_{1.672}$$

**Step 3:** Rescale to a whole-number empirical formula by dividing each subscript by the smaller one (which is 1):

$$C_1 H_{1.672}$$

For paraffinic base $C_n H_{2n+2}$, the ratio $H/C = 2 + 2/n$. Solving $1.672 = 2 + 2/n$ gives $n = -6.1$ (negative, meaning the H/C is *less* than 2 — i.e., the oil has naphthenic or aromatic character).

**Step 4:** Express as $C_n H_{2n-x}$ family:

$$H/C = 2 - x/n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1.672 = 2 - x/n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x/n = 0.328$$

For an assumed average ring number $R = 1$ (one ring), the formula approximates to a **monocycloparaffin base** $C_n H_{2n}$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Watson Characterization Factor (Kw)

The **Watson K-factor** is *not* derived from first principles — it is a **purely empirical, defined parameter** correlating boiling point with specific gravity:

$$K_w = \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$$

where $T_B$ is the **mean average boiling point** in degrees Rankine (°R = °F + 460).

**Interpretation Matrix:**

| Kw Range | Crude Type | Product Tendency |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $K_w > 12.0$ | **Paraffinic** | Premium diesel, lube oil, wax |
| $11.5 \le K_w \le 12.0$ | **Paraffinic-Naphthenic** | Balanced yield |
| $10.5 \le K_w < 11.5$ | **Naphthenic** | Gasoline, jet fuel, asphalt precursors |
| $K_w < 10.5$ | **Aromatic** | Asphalt, coke |

### 3.4 Refractive Index – Density – Molecular Weight (n-d-M) Method

The **n-d-M method** is a high-yield analytical technique to determine the **carbon distribution** (% $C_A$ aromatic, % $C_N$ naphthenic, % $C_P$ paraffinic) in a petroleum fraction.

**Required inputs (measured in lab):**
- $n$ = refractive index at 20 °C (sodium D-line, 589 nm)
- $d$ = density at 20 °C (g/cm³)
- $M$ = average molecular weight (by cryoscopy / VPO)

**Computed parameter — Refractivity Intercept (RI):**

$$RI = n_{20} - \dfrac{d_{20}}{2}$$

**Computed parameter — Refractivity Specific Dispersion (Δ):**

$$\Delta = \dfrac{n_F - n_C}{d} \times 10^3$$

where $n_F$ and $n_C$ are refractive indices at the hydrogen F-line (486 nm) and C-line (656 nm).

**Correlation Equations (ASTM D3238):**

$$\% C_A = 410 \cdot \Delta + 3660 \cdot \dfrac{RI}{M} - 8.5 \cdot RI + 5.0 \cdot \Delta \cdot RI + 0.018 \cdot M \cdot \Delta$$

$$\% C_R = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{RI}{M} - 6.0 \cdot \%C_A - 11.0$$

$$\% C_P = 100 - \%C_A - \%C_R$$

where $C_A$ = aromatic carbon, $C_R$ = naphthenic carbon, $C_P$ = paraffinic carbon, all on a **ring-free basis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** The n-d-M method is a favorite KTU question topic under "Chemical Composition of Crude." Master the refractivity intercept formula and the carbon-distribution interpretation.

### 3.5 Worked Example: From API Gravity to Composition Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A crude oil has $°API = 32.0$ and $K_w = 11.8$. Estimate its paraffinicity class and the likely predominant product slate.

**Step 1:** Convert API to Specific Gravity.

$$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{32.0 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{163.5} = 0.8655$$

**Step 2:** Compute density.

$$\rho = 1000 \times 0.8655 = 865.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Interpret Watson K-factor.

$$K_w = 11.8 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 11.5 \le K_w \le 12.0$$

This places the crude in the **Paraffinic–Naphthenic** zone, with a balanced gasoline/diesel yield and good lube-oil potential.

**Step 4:** Cross-verify with API.

$$°API = 32.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Light Crude (API > 31.1)}$$

**Step 5:** Refinery implication → simple atmospheric + vacuum distillation suffices; secondary conversion (FCC) may not be mandatory.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Watson Characterization Factor (Kw)". Explain its significance in classifying crude oils.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Watson Characterization Factor (Kw) is an **empirical parameter** that correlates the **mean average boiling point** of a petroleum fraction with its **specific gravity**, defined as:

$$K_w = \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$$

where $T_B$ is in °R and $SG$ is at 15.6 °C.

**Significance (1.5 marks):** It serves as an index of **paraffinicity vs. aromaticity**:
- $K_w > 12$ → **Paraffinic** crude → high wax, good diesel/lube yield.
- $K_w = 11.5$–$12.0$ → **Paraffinic–Naphthenic** → balanced yield.
- $K_w = 10.5$–$11.5$ → **Naphthenic** → gasoline and asphalt precursors.
- $K_w < 10.5$ → **Aromatic** → high asphaltene, coke, asphalt tendency.

**Why it matters:** Kw is a *one-number classification* that tells the refiner whether the crude is paraffin-rich (premium value) or aromatic-rich (needs severe upgrading). (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is SARA analysis? List its four fractions with their chemical nature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SARA analysis is the **standard chromatographic separation technique** used by the petroleum industry to classify crude oil (or its distillation cuts) into four solubility-based compositional groups (1 mark):

| Fraction | Solubility Property | Chemical Nature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Saturates (S)** | Elute first in n-heptane | Non-polar paraffins + naphthenes (1 mark) |
| **Aromatics (A)** | Elute in toluene | Polarizable aromatic hydrocarbons (0.5 mark) |
| **Resins (R)** | Adsorbed on alumina/clay | Polar, high-MW heteroatomic compounds (0.5 mark) |
| **Asphaltenes (A)** | Precipitate in n-heptane | Highly polar, high-MW polycyclic "molecular chunks" (0.5 mark) |

**Significance:** SARA dictates the **refining route** — high asphaltenes → coking; high paraffins → simple distillation suffices.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Describe the **chemical composition of crude petroleum** in detail. Classify the hydrocarbons and non-hydrocarbon constituents with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A crude oil sample has $°API = 28.0$. Compute its **(i)** specific gravity, **(ii)** density in kg/m³, and **(iii)** classify the crude as light/medium/heavy. Comment on the **product slate** you would expect. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Crude petroleum is a naturally occurring complex mixture of hydrocarbons with minor heteroatomic constituents. Hydrocarbons constitute 95–99% by weight, while non-hydrocarbons (S, N, O, metals) form the remaining 1–5%.

**Hydrocarbon Classification (4 marks):**

1. **Paraffins (Alkanes)** — saturated, acyclic, $C_n H_{2n+2}$.
   - *n-Paraffins:* straight chain (e.g., n-hexane, n-decane) — high cetane, good diesel.
   - *Iso-Paraffins:* branched (e.g., iso-octane) — high octane, good gasoline.
2. **Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes)** — saturated cyclic, $C_n H_{2n}$ (monocyclic).
   - Examples: cyclopentane, cyclohexane, methylcyclohexane, decalin.
   - Yield high-octane gasoline and aromatics precursors.
3. **Aromatics** — unsaturated cyclic with benzene ring, $C_n H_{2n-6}$.
   - Examples: benzene, toluene, xylene (BTX), naphthalene, phenanthrene.
   - Yield asphalt, coke, and high-octane gasoline.

**Non-Hydrocarbon Classification (2 marks):**
- **Sulfur compounds:** Mercaptans (thiols), sulfides, disulfides, thiophenes, H₂S. Cause corrosion, catalyst poisoning, SOₓ emissions.
- **Nitrogen compounds:** Pyridines, quinolines, pyrroles, indoles. Cause catalyst poisoning and color/gum instability.
- **Oxygen compounds:** Naphthenic acids, phenols, fatty acids. Cause corrosion; quantified by TAN.
- **Metallic compounds:** Porphyrin complexes of V, Ni, Fe. Cause catalyst deactivation and high-temperature corrosion.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The chemical composition of crude fundamentally governs the choice of refinery configuration and product slate.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $°API = 28.0$

**(i) Specific Gravity Calculation (2 marks):**

$$SG_{15.6°C} = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{28.0 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{159.5}$$

$$SG = 0.8872 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**(ii) Density Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\rho = 1000 \times SG = 1000 \times 0.8872 = 887.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(iii) Classification (1 mark):**

| API Range | Class |
|:---:|:---:|
| $> 31.1$ | Light |
| $22.3 \le °API \le 31.1$ | **Medium** |
| $< 22.3$ | Heavy |

Since $°API = 28.0$, the crude is a **Medium Crude**.

**Product Slate Commentary (2 marks):**
- A medium crude like this typically yields a **balanced product slate**: ~30% gasoline, ~25% diesel/jet, ~20% fuel oil, and ~25% residues.
- It is well-suited for **atmospheric + vacuum distillation** with **moderate hydrotreating**; a refiner may consider **catalytic cracking (FCC)** for additional gasoline yield.
- The exact slate depends on the **sulfur content** and **K-factor** which are not specified here.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** What is **API gravity**? Derive the relationship between API gravity and specific gravity. A crude oil has a specific gravity of 0.92. Calculate its API gravity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **SARA analysis** of crude oil with a neat block flow diagram. Explain how the asphaltene content affects refinery operations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition of API Gravity (2 marks):**
API gravity is the **American Petroleum Institute's inverse-density scale** used worldwide to express the lightness or heaviness of crude oil and petroleum products. It is defined such that water has an **API of 10°** and lighter liquids have *higher* API values.

**Derivation of API–SG Relationship (3 marks):**

Specific gravity is the ratio of oil density to water density at 15.6 °C:

$$SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$$

The API scale is defined by anchoring water at 10°API and using a hyperbolic inverse relationship:

$$°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

**Verification:** For water, $SG = 1.000 \Rightarrow °API = 141.5/1.0 - 131.5 = 10.0 \, °API$ ✓

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given $SG = 0.92$:

$$°API = \dfrac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5 = 153.804 - 131.5 = 22.30 \, °API$$

**Conclusion:** The crude has $°API \approx 22.3$, placing it at the **lower bound of "Medium Crude"** — borderline heavy, requiring moderate to intensive refining.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**SARA Analysis Explanation (3 marks):**
SARA is the **sequential chromatographic separation** of crude oil (or its cuts) into four chemically distinct fractions based on polarity and solubility:

- **Saturates (S):** Non-polar hydrocarbons — elute first with n-heptane.
- **Aromatics (A):** Polarizable aromatic hydrocarbons — elute with toluene.
- **Resins (R):** Polar, high-MW heteroatom-rich compounds.
- **Asphaltenes (A):** Highly polar, polycyclic, high-MW compounds — precipitate in n-heptane.

**Block Flow Diagram (1.5 marks) — Already shown in Section 4.2 above.**

**Impact of Asphaltene Content on Refinery Operations (2.5 marks):**

| Operational Issue | Consequence of High Asphaltenes |
|:---|:---|
| **Fouling** | Asphaltenes deposit on heat exchanger surfaces, reducing heat transfer and causing unscheduled shutdowns. |
| **Coking** | In FCC and coker units, asphaltenes crack to produce **petroleum coke** — must be removed periodically. |
| **Sludge Formation** | In storage tanks, asphaltenes + water + sediment form "BS&W" (basic sediment & water) sludge. |
| **Catalyst Deactivation** | Asphaltenes contain Ni, V, Fe porphyrins that poison FCC catalyst permanently. |
| **Emulsion Stability** | Asphaltenes act as natural surfactants, stabilizing water-in-oil emulsions in desalters. |
| **Incompatibility** | Blending two crudes with different asphaltene/resin ratios can cause **precipitation** and pipeline plugging. |

**Conclusion:** Asphaltene content is a **first-order refining concern** — a crude with > 5 wt % asphaltenes is commercially classified as a "bottom-of-the-barrel" feedstock requiring **delayed coking, solvent deasphalting, or hydrocracking**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Board Examiner Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **API Gravity Conversion Reversal (–2 marks):** Students confuse $°API = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ with $SG = 141.5/°API - 131.5$. The correct SG-from-API is $SG = 141.5/(°API + 131.5)$. Write it correctly the first time.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Reference Temperature (–1 mark):** SG of crude must be reported at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**. If you simply state "SG = 0.85" without the reference, the examiner docks a mark.
>
> 3. **Misclassifying SARA Fractions (–2 marks):** A common mistake is to put asphaltenes as "the lightest fraction" — they are actually the **heaviest, most polar, highest MW** fraction. Remember: SARA goes light → heavy: **S → A → R → A**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Empirical with Molecular Formula (–2 marks):** For crude oil, the "molecular formula" is meaningless in the strict sense (it's a mixture). The correct term is **empirical formula** or **average formula**. Examiners explicitly check for this distinction.
>
> 5. **Watson K-factor in Wrong Units (–2 marks):** $T_B$ **must be in °Rankine**, not °C or K. Always convert: $°R = °F + 460 = \frac{9}{5}K$. Forgetting this gives a numerically wrong answer.
>
> 6. **H/C vs. C/H Confusion (–1 mark):** H/C is always the ratio of lighter-to-heavier element. Atomic H/C for crude is typically 1.5–1.9; do not invert.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!NOTE]
> **🔖 High-Density Revision Checklist — Petroleum Refining: Chemical Composition of Crude**

- ✅ Crude petroleum is a **complex natural mixture** of hydrocarbons (95–99%) + heteroatom compounds (1–5%).
- ✅ Three main hydrocarbon families: **Paraffins** ($C_n H_{2n+2}$), **Naphthenes** ($C_n H_{2n}$), **Aromatics** ($C_n H_{2n-6}$).
- ✅ Non-hydrocarbon heteroatoms: **S, N, O, metals (V, Ni, Fe)**.
- ✅ **API Gravity** scale: $°API = 141.5/SG - 131.5$. Water = 10°API. Light crude > 31.1°API, Medium 22.3–31.1°API, Heavy < 22.3°API.
- ✅ **Sweet** crude: S < 0.5 wt %; **Sour** crude: S > 0.5 wt %.
- ✅ **Watson K-factor**: $K_w = (T_B)^{1/3}/SG$ in °R. $K_w > 12$ paraffinic; $K_w < 10.5$ aromatic.
- ✅ **SARA** = Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes — the four-fraction chromatographic classification.
- ✅ **n-d-M method** gives %$C_A$, %$C_N$, %$C_P$ on a ring-free basis; uses refractive index, density, and MW.
- ✅ **H/C atomic ratio** is the master indicator of crude quality — higher H/C = lighter, more paraffinic; lower H/C = heavier, more aromatic.
- ✅ **Asphaltenes** are the refinery's nightmare: fouling, coking, catalyst deactivation, emulsion stability.
- ✅ **Elemental snapshot to memorize:** C = 83–87%, H = 11–14%, S = 0.1–5.5%, N = 0.1–1.7%, O = 0.1–4.5%, Metals < 1000 ppm.
- ✅ Standard refining reference temperature is **15.6 °C (60 °F)**.
- ✅ The n-d-M method's key output is the **refractivity intercept** $RI = n - d/2$.
- ✅ Crude oil composition dictates **refinery configuration, catalyst selection, metallurgy, and product slate**.
