topics.title: Petroleum Refinery Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Petroleum Refinery Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Petroleum Refinery Products** are the fractionated, treated, and converted outputs obtained from the physical separation and chemical transformation of crude petroleum oil in a refinery. Crude oil is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons (paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics) and heteroatomic compounds (S, N, O, metals). Refining converts this crude mixture into commercially valuable, specification-grade products such as **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)**, **Naphtha**, **Motor Gasoline (MS)**, **Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF/Kerosene)**, **High-Speed Diesel (HSD)**, **Fuel Oils**, **Lubricating Oils**, **Bitumen**, and **Petroleum Coke**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 framework, refinery products are classified based on **boiling range (°C)**, **carbon number distribution**, and **end-use application**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The product slate from a modern refinery is governed by the refinery configuration — a simple *Hydroskimming Refinery* yields predominantly fuels, while a *Conversion Refinery* (with FCC, Hydrocracker, Coker) maximizes transport fuels and petrochemical feedstocks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling-Point Layer Cake"

Imagine crude oil as a **horizontally layered cocktail** in a giant test tube. When you heat the bottom, the **lightest, most volatile components evaporate first** (they have the lowest boiling points — like the alcohol in a mixed drink), and the **heaviest, stickiest tars settle at the bottom** (like molasses). A petroleum refinery is essentially a giant industrial version of this "controlled boiling" process, but with hundreds of additional chemical "tricks" (cracking, reforming, treating) to upgrade what nature gave us into high-value, market-grade products.

**The Student-Friendly Mental Model:**
- **Top of the barrel** → Gases (LPG) — *boil off first*
- **Upper-middle** → Gasoline, Naphtha — *light, volatile, ignite easily*
- **Middle** → Kerosene, ATF — *mid-weight, used for jets*
- **Lower-middle** → Diesel, Gas oil — *heavier, used for trucks*
- **Bottom** → Fuel oil, Lubricants, Bitumen, Coke — *heavy, viscous, sticky*

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **API Gravity (°API):** Inverse measure of density; higher °API = lighter crude.
$$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{\text{Specific Gravity at 15.6°C}} - 131.5$$
- **Standard reference temperature for petroleum products:** **15.6 °C (60 °F)**
- **Average crude oil composition by mass:** ~ **84–87 % Carbon**, **11–14 % Hydrogen**, **< 5 % S + N + O + metals**
- **One barrel of crude = 158.987 litres = 42 US gallons**
- **Energy benchmark:** 1 barrel of crude ≈ **5.8 × 10⁶ BTU** ≈ **6.12 GJ**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — API Gravity:** The American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity scale is used universally to classify crude oils. Light sweet crudes (e.g., Brent, WTI) have °API > 30, while heavy sour crudes (e.g., Canadian heavy) have °API < 22.

## 1.4 Classification of Refinery Products — Master Table

| Product Class | Carbon Number (n) | Boiling Range (°C) | Primary End Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refinery Gases (C1–C4) | 1–4 | < 30 | LPG, fuel gas, petrochem feedstock |
| Naphtha (Light) | C5–C7 | 30–90 | Gasoline blending, steam cracker feed |
| Naphtha (Heavy) | C7–C11 | 90–200 | Gasoline, reformer feed |
| Motor Gasoline (MS) | C5–C12 | 40–200 | Spark-ignition automobile engines |
| Kerosene / ATF | C10–C16 | 150–300 | Jet fuel, domestic lighting, illuminant |
| Diesel (HSD) | C12–C18 | 200–350 | Compression-ignition engines |
| Fuel Oil (FO) | C18–C30+ | 300–600+ | Marine boilers, industrial furnaces |
| Lubricating Oil | C20–C50 | 350–600 | Machinery, engines |
| Paraffin Wax | C20–C40 | 350–500 | Candles, packaging, cosmetics |
| Bitumen / Asphalt | C30+ | Non-distillable | Road paving, roofing, waterproofing |
| Petroleum Coke | C30+ solid | Solid residue | Electrodes, fuel, metallurgy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Product Formation — Step-by-Step

Refining follows a structured sequence from lightest to heaviest products. Let us deconstruct the "**Why**" and "**How**" of each unit operation:

### Step 1 — Atmospheric Distillation (The Skeleton of the Refinery)
- **Why:** Crude oil is a continuous mixture; fractional distillation exploits the **volatility difference** (Antoine equation dependency on vapour pressure).
- **How:** Crude is preheated (desalter → heat exchanger → furnace at ~ 350–400 °C) and flashed into the **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)**. Vapours rise, condense on trays at progressively lower temperatures, and are withdrawn as side-cuts: Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO), and Atmospheric Residue (AR) at the bottom.

### Step 2 — Vacuum Distillation (Recovering the Heavy End)
- **Why:** At atmospheric pressure, residues would crack (thermal decomposition) above 400 °C, fouling the column.
- **How:** Pressure is reduced to **10–50 mmHg** (absolute), which depresses the boiling point by ~ 200 °C. The **Vacuum Distillation Column (VDC)** recovers **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** and **Vacuum Residue (VR)**.

### Step 3 — Conversion Processes (Molecular Re-engineering)
- **Why:** Market demand (especially for gasoline and diesel) far exceeds what straight-run distillation yields. Conversion breaks heavy, long-chain molecules into lighter, higher-value products.
- **How (key processes):**
  * **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** VGO → gasoline + LPG + coke (acidic zeolite catalyst, 500 °C).
  * **Hydrocracking:** VGO/AR → high-quality diesel + jet (hydrogen + bi-functional catalyst, high pressure).
  * **Catalytic Reforming:** Heavy Naphtha (C7–C11 n-paraffins) → high-octane aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylenes) + hydrogen. Uses Pt-Re/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 500 °C.
  * **Alkylation:** C3+C4 olefins + isobutane → high-octane iso-octane (C8). Uses H₂SO₄ or HF catalyst at low temperature.
  * **Isomerization:** n-pentane, n-hexane → iso-pentane, iso-hexane (boosts octane without aromatics).
  * **Coking (Delayed/Fluid):** VR → cracked distillates + petroleum coke.

### Step 4 — Treatment / Finishing (Cleaning the Product)
- **Why:** Sulfur, nitrogen, and olefins cause corrosion, emissions, and gum formation.
- **How:**
  * **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):** R–S + H₂ → R–H + H₂S (Co-Mo / Ni-Mo on Al₂O₃, 300–400 °C, 30–80 bar).
  * **Merox (Mercaptan Oxidation):** RSH → RSSR (sweetening of LPG/naphtha).
  * **Caustic washing:** Removes H₂S and phenols.

### Step 5 — Blending (The Final Recipe)
- **Why:** No single refinery stream meets all fuel specifications. Blending optimizes octane, volatility, sulfur, cetane.
- **How:** Linear programming (LP) models select component flow rates to minimize cost while meeting spec constraints.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Property | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | $\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{15.6/15.6}} - 131.5$ | Density classification of crudes and products |
| **Watson K-Factor (Characterization Factor)** | $K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$ | Distinguishes paraffinic (K > 12) vs naphthenic (K < 11.5) feedstocks |
| **Antoine Equation (Vapour Pressure)** | $\log_{10} P = A - \frac{B}{C+T}$ | Vapour–liquid equilibrium for distillation design |
| **Octane Number (RON/MON)** | Blend of iso-octane (100) + n-heptane (0) | Resistance to knock in spark-ignition engines |
| **Cetane Number (CN)** | Blend of n-cetrane (100) + α-methylnaphthalene (0) | Ignition quality in diesel engines |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | Lowest T at which vapours ignite | Fire safety classification of fuels |
| **Pour Point (°C)** | Lowest T at which oil flows | Low-temperature operability of diesel/lubricants |
| **Cloud Point (°C)** | T at which wax crystals first appear | Cold-flow property of middle distillates |
| **Aniline Point (°C)** | Miscibility T with aniline | Aromaticity indicator; lower = more aromatic |
| **Smoke Point (mm)** | Max flame height without smoke | Kerosene / ATF quality (ASTM D1322) |
| **Sulfur Content (ppm / wt %)** | Determined by ASTM D4294 / D2622 | Emission control, corrosion |
| **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** | Vapour pressure at 37.8 °C, vapour:liquid = 4:1 | Volatility, vapour-lock tendency of gasoline |
| **Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom)** | wt % carbon after pyrolysis | Coking tendency of heavy oils |
| **Heat of Combustion (HHV, MJ/kg)** | Dulong's formula | Energy content of fuel |
| **Yield of Distillate (Volume %)** | $Y = \frac{V_{\text{distillate}}}{V_{\text{crude}}} \times 100$ | Refinery economics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** **Octane Number** is for gasoline (spark-ignition), **Cetane Number** is for diesel (compression-ignition). They are *inversely* related to ignition delay — high octane = long delay; high cetane = short delay.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Configuration Selection:** A KTU question may ask you to choose between FCC, Hydrocracking, and Coking based on feedstock and desired product slate. Hydrocracking yields cleaner diesel; FCC yields more gasoline; Coking handles the dirtiest residues.
- **Product Pricing Differential:** Gasoline and diesel command premium prices; fuel oil and coke are bottom-of-the-barrel. Refiners design configurations to *maximize high-value distillate yield*.
- **Emission Compliance:** BS-VI / Euro-VI norms force deep desulfurization (HSD sulfur < 10 ppm) and aromatic reduction — directly tied to HDS unit severity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — API Gravity from Specific Gravity

**Given:** Specific gravity (SG) at 15.6 °C / 15.6 °C.

**Step 1: Definition of Specific Gravity.**
$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$
where both densities are measured at 15.6 °C.

**Step 2: Reference water density at 15.6 °C.**
$$\rho_{\text{water}} = 0.9990 \text{ g/cm}^3 \approx 1.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Empirical API definition.**
The American Petroleum Institute defined gravity such that the *inverse* of density gives a linear scale convenient for industry.

**Step 4: Assemble the relationship.**
$$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example.**
For a crude with SG = 0.85:
$$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97 \text{ °API}$$

This corresponds to a **light crude**.

> [!NOTE]
> Note that 141.5 and 131.5 are *empirical constants* (dimensionless). The number 141.5 arises so that an oil with SG = 1.0 gives 10 °API (the "cut-off" between water and oil on this scale).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Watson Characterization Factor (K-Factor)

**Step 1: Definition.**
$$K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_{B, \text{Rankine}}}}{SG}$$

**Step 2: Converting boiling point to Rankine.**
$$T_B(\text{R}) = T_B(°F) + 459.67 = \frac{9}{5} T_B(°C) + 491.67$$

**Step 3: Interpretation rule.**
- $K > 12.0$ → **Paraffinic** crude (high wax, e.g., Assam, North Sea crudes).
- $K = 11.0 - 12.0$ → **Intermediate** (most Gulf crudes).
- $K < 11.0$ → **Naphthenic** (e.g., Coastal Louisiana, Baku).

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**
For a crude with T_B = 400 °C and SG = 0.88:
$$T_B(°F) = \frac{9}{5} \times 400 + 32 = 752 \text{ °F}$$
$$T_B(\text{R}) = 752 + 459.67 = 1211.67 \text{ R}$$
$$K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{1211.67}}{0.88} = \frac{10.66}{0.88} = 12.11$$

This indicates a **paraffinic** feedstock.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) Correction to True Vapour Pressure

**Step 1: Definition.**
RVP is measured at 37.8 °C (100 °F) with a vapour-to-liquid volume ratio of **4:1**.

**Step 2: API correlation for RVP to true vapour pressure (TVP).**
$$TVP = RVP \times \left(\frac{V/L + 1}{V/L}\right)$$

**Step 3: For RVP measurement, V/L = 4:**
$$TVP = RVP \times \left(\frac{4+1}{4}\right) = RVP \times 1.25$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**
If RVP of a gasoline blend is 9.0 psi:
$$TVP = 9.0 \times 1.25 = 11.25 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 5: Engineering implication.**
- **High RVP** (> 9 psi) → vapour lock, evaporative emissions, driveability problems in hot weather.
- **Low RVP** (< 7 psi) → hard starting in cold weather.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Refinery Products vs. Specification Tests

| Product | Key Test | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| LPG | Vapor pressure (GPA) | < 2080 kPa (gauge) | Safety, vapour–liquid balance |
| LPG | Sulfur content | < 50 mg/kg | Corrosion, emission |
| Gasoline (MS) | RON (Research Octane Number) | ≥ 91 (BS-IV) / ≥ 95 (BS-VI) | Knock resistance |
| Gasoline (MS) | RVP (psi) | 7–9 | Volatility, emissions |
| Gasoline (MS) | Sulfur | < 10 ppm (BS-VI) | Emission control |
| ATF / Kerosene | Smoke point (mm) | ≥ 25 (Jet A-1) | Combustion cleanliness |
| ATF | Freeze point (°C) | < –47 (Jet A-1) | High-altitude operation |
| Diesel (HSD) | Cetane number | ≥ 51 (BS-VI) | Ignition quality |
| Diesel | Cloud point (°C) | < 6 (winter grade) | Cold operability |
| Diesel | Pour point (°C) | < 0 (tropical) | Pumpability |
| Diesel | Sulfur | < 10 ppm (BS-VI) | Particulate reduction |
| Fuel Oil (FO) | Viscosity at 50 °C (cSt) | 180–380 | Atomization in burners |
| Fuel Oil | Flash point (°C, PMCC) | ≥ 66 | Storage safety |
| Lubricant | Viscosity Index (VI) | > 90 (multigrade) | Temp–viscosity stability |
| Bitumen | Penetration (1/10 mm at 25 °C) | 30–200 | Consistency grade |
| Bitumen | Softening point (°C, R&B) | 35–95 | Thermal behavior |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Cetane Number from Cetane Index (Estimated)

**Step 1: ASTM Cetane Index (D4737) — two-variable equation.**
$$CI = 45.2 + 0.0892 \times T_{10} + (0.131 + 0.901 \times B) \times T_{50} + (0.0523 - 0.42 \times B) \times T_{90}$$
where $B = \exp(-0.0035 \times API)$ and $T_{10}, T_{50}, T_{90}$ are the 10 %, 50 %, 90 % ASTM distillation temperatures (°C).

**Step 2: Numerical Example.**
For a diesel stream with:
- $T_{10} = 220$ °C, $T_{50} = 280$ °C, $T_{90} = 350$ °C
- API = 35 (SG ≈ 0.8498)

Compute B:
$$B = \exp(-0.0035 \times 35) = \exp(-0.1225) = 0.8847$$

Compute each term:
$$0.0892 \times 220 = 19.624$$
$$(0.131 + 0.901 \times 0.8847) \times 280 = (0.131 + 0.7971) \times 280 = 0.9281 \times 280 = 259.87$$
$$(0.0523 - 0.42 \times 0.8847) \times 350 = (0.0523 - 0.3716) \times 350 = -0.3193 \times 350 = -111.75$$

Sum:
$$CI = 45.2 + 19.624 + 259.87 - 111.75 = 212.94$$

**Step 3: Clamp to realistic range** (Cetane Index capped at 100):
$$CI_{\text{reported}} = \min(212.94, 100) = 100$$

This indicates an **excellent diesel** (saturation by upper bound).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Cetane Index is an *estimate* of Cetane Number based on distillation; it does not require an engine test. It is used for rapid QC in refineries.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Modern Refinery Product Slate (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Storage and Desalting] --> B[Atmospheric Distillation Column ADC]
    B --> C1[Refinery Off Gases C1 to C2]
    B --> C2[LPG C3 to C4]
    B --> C3[Heavy Naphtha C7 to C11]
    B --> C4[Kerosene C10 to C16]
    B --> C5[Diesel C12 to C18]
    B --> C6[Atmospheric Residue C18 plus]
    C6 --> D[Vacuum Distillation Column VDC]
    D --> E1[Vacuum Gas Oil VGO]
    D --> E2[Vacuum Residue VR]
    C1 --> F1[Gas Treating Amine Unit]
    C2 --> F2[LPG Merox Sweetening]
    C3 --> F3[Hydrodesulfurization Naphtha HCU]
    C4 --> F4[Kerosene Merox]
    C5 --> F5[Hydrodesulfurization Diesel HSD Unit]
    C3 --> G1[Catalytic Reformer CCR]
    E1 --> G2[Fluid Catalytic Cracker FCC]
    E1 --> G3[Hydrocracker HC]
    E2 --> G4[Delayed Coker DCU]
    G1 --> H1[High Octane Reformate]
    G2 --> H2[Cracked Naphtha and LPG]
    G3 --> H3[High Quality Diesel]
    G4 --> H4[Coker Distillate and Petroleum Coke]
    H1 --> I[Product Blending Pool]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    F5 --> I
    C4 --> I
    C5 --> I
    C3 --> I
    I --> J1[Motor Gasoline MS Tank]
    I --> J2[ATF Kerosene Tank]
    I --> J3[Diesel HSD Tank]
    I --> J4[Bitumen Drum]
    E2 --> J4
    H4 --> J5[Petroleum Coke Pile]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Refinery Stream Categories (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Distillate Fractions]
      R1[LPG]
      R2[Gasoline]
      R3[ATF]
      R4[Diesel]
    end
    subgraph S2[Heavy Streams]
      R5[Fuel Oil]
      R6[Lubricating Oil]
      R7[Bitumen]
      R8[Petroleum Coke]
    end
    subgraph S3[Conversion Upgrades]
      R9[Reformate]
      R10[Alkylate]
      R11[Isomerate]
      R12[Cracked Distillates]
    end
    R1 --> P1[Auto LPG and Bottling]
    R2 --> P2[Motor Spirit Pump]
    R3 --> P3[Aviation Refueling]
    R4 --> P4[Diesel Dispenser]
    R5 --> P5[Marine Bunker and Boiler Feed]
    R6 --> P6[Engine Oil Blending]
    R7 --> P7[Road Construction and Roofing]
    R8 --> P8[Anode Grade for Aluminium]
    R9 --> Q1[Premium Gasoline Pool]
    R10 --> Q1
    R11 --> Q1
    R12 --> P4
    R12 --> Q1
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Atmospheric Distillation Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crude Oil Charge Pump] --> B[Desalter]
    B --> C[Heat Exchanger Train]
    C --> D[Crude Furnace 350 to 400 C]
    D --> E[Atmospheric Distillation Column]
    E --> F1[Overhead Vapor C1 to C4]
    E --> F2[Heavy Naphtha Side Draw]
    E --> F3[Kerosene Side Draw]
    E --> F4[Diesel Side Draw]
    E --> F5[AGO and Residue Bottom]
    F1 --> G1[Overhead Receiver and Reflux]
    F1 --> G2[Gas Plant C1 C2 C3 C4]
    F5 --> H[Vacuum Distillation Feed]
    G2 --> I[LPG Product]
    F2 --> J[Naphtha Pool]
    F3 --> K[Kerosene and ATF]
    F4 --> L[Diesel Pool]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The arrows from the ADC go *outward and downward* in boiling point, because lighter fractions exit the top and heavier exit the bottom. This is the **inverse** of an extraction column.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]** — **Define Octane Number and Cetane Number. State the difference in their measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

**Octane Number (ON):** It is the measure of resistance of a gasoline (spark-ignition) fuel to *knocking* (auto-ignition) in an engine. It is determined in a **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine** by comparing the fuel's knock intensity with blends of **iso-octane (assigned ON = 100)** and **n-heptane (ON = 0)**.

- **RON (Research Octane Number):** Measured at 600 rpm, 125 % of rated speed.
- **MON (Motor Octane Number):** Measured at 900 rpm, more severe conditions.
- **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** = (RON + MON) / 2 — the number posted on fuel pumps.

**Cetane Number (CN):** It measures the *ignition delay* of diesel fuel in a compression-ignition (CI) engine. It compares the fuel's ignition quality with blends of **n-cetane (C₁₆H₃₄, CN = 100)** and **α-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0)**.

**Key Difference:**
| Parameter | Octane Number | Cetane Number |
|---|---|---|
| Engine | SI (gasoline) | CI (diesel) |
| Quality measured | Resistance to knock | Ease of ignition |
| High value means | Smooth combustion | Quick ignition |
| High-quality stream | Reformate, alkylate | Paraffinic straight-run diesel |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Similar)]** — **List any six petroleum refinery products with their carbon number range and primary application.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Product | Carbon Number (n) | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) | C3 – C4 | Domestic and auto fuel |
| 2 | Naphtha (Heavy) | C7 – C11 | Gasoline blending, reformer feed, steam cracker feed |
| 3 | Motor Gasoline (MS) | C5 – C12 | Spark-ignition automobile engines |
| 4 | Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF) | C10 – C16 | Jet aircraft engines |
| 5 | High-Speed Diesel (HSD) | C12 – C18 | Trucks, locomotives, diesel cars |
| 6 | Lubricating Oil | C20 – C50 | Engine and machinery lubrication |
| 7 | Bitumen / Asphalt | C30+ (non-distillable) | Road paving, roofing, waterproofing |
| 8 | Petroleum Coke | C30+ (solid) | Anodes, metallurgical fuel |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern:** KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice (Module-specific). Below are two parallel questions — *either/or*. A student answers ONE.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q.7(a) – Modified]** — Petroleum Refinery Products.

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation** of crude oil. List the main products obtained and their boiling ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Catalytic Reforming** in detail. Discuss its feed, products, operating conditions, and significance in improving gasoline octane number. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Atmospheric Distillation (ADC):**

Crude oil is first desalted (electrical desalting to remove salts, water, and sediments) to prevent downstream fouling. It is then preheated in a heat exchanger train (where it exchanges heat with hot product streams) to about 250 °C, then sent to a **crude furnace** where it is heated to **350–400 °C** — this temperature is deliberately kept below the cracking threshold to prevent thermal decomposition.

The hot crude enters the ADC (a tall, 30–50 tray fractionating column) as a flash mixture. Inside the column:
- **Vapours rise** through bubble-cap or valve trays.
- **Liquid (reflux) flows downward** counter-current.
- The temperature profile decreases from bottom (~350 °C) to top (~120 °C).
- Each tray establishes a **vapour–liquid equilibrium**; the lightest component (refinery gas) exits the top, the heaviest (atmospheric residue) exits the bottom.

**Side Draws (in order of increasing boiling point):**
1. **Heavy Naphtha** (~90–200 °C) — gasoline blending
2. **Kerosene / ATF** (~150–250 °C) — jet fuel
3. **Diesel / AGO** (~250–350 °C) — diesel pool
4. **Atmospheric Residue (AR)** (~350 °C+) — vacuum unit feed, fuel oil, bitumen

**Vacuum Distillation (VDC):**
The atmospheric residue contains valuable heavy hydrocarbons, but heating it above 400 °C at atmospheric pressure causes **thermal cracking** (C–C bond breaking → coke, gas). To avoid this, the residue is fed to a vacuum column operating at **10–50 mmHg absolute** (achieved by steam ejectors or mechanical vacuum pumps). The pressure reduction lowers the boiling point by ~200 °C, allowing distillation up to **550–600 °C equivalent boiling point at 1 atm**.

**Vacuum Distillation Products:**
- **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** — feed to FCC or Hydrocracker.
- **Vacuum Residue (VR)** — feed to coker, deasphalter, or bitumen.

| Product | Boiling Range (°C) |
|---|---|
| Refinery gas (C1–C2) | < 30 |
| LPG (C3–C4) | –42 to 0 |
| Heavy Naphtha | 90 – 200 |
| Kerosene | 150 – 250 |
| Diesel | 200 – 350 |
| Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) | 350 – 380 |
| Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO) | 380 – 550 (at 1 atm equivalent) |
| Vacuum Residue (VR) | > 550 |

#### Model Solution (b)

**Catalytic Reforming:**

**Objective:** Convert low-octane, n-paraffinic heavy naphtha (RON ~ 40) into high-octane, aromatic-rich reformate (RON ~ 95–100), and produce byproduct hydrogen for use in **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** and **Hydrocracking (HC)** units.

**Feed:** Heavy Naphtha — typically C7–C11 hydrocarbons, low in sulfur and nitrogen (pre-treated in a Naphtha Hydrotreater to protect the platinum catalyst from poisoning).

**Chemistry (Reactions):**
1. **Dehydrogenation of naphthenes to aromatics** (dominant, endothermic):
$$\text{Cyclohexane} \rightarrow \text{Benzene} + 3H_2$$
2. **Isomerization of n-paraffins to iso-paraffins**:
$$\text{n-Hexane} \rightarrow \text{2-Methylpentane} + \text{3-Methylpentane}$$
3. **Dehydrocyclization of paraffins to aromatics**:
$$\text{n-Heptane} \rightarrow \text{Toluene} + 4H_2$$
4. **Hydrocracking** (minor, gives LPG) — minimized to maintain liquid yield.

**Catalyst:** **Platinum (Pt) on chlorided alumina** (monometallic) or **Pt–Rhenium (Pt-Re)** on chlorided alumina (bi-metallic, more stable, lower coke). Acidic sites (from chloride) and metallic sites (from Pt) work synergistically. Promoters: Re, Ge, Sn, Ir.

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Temperature:** 480–525 °C
- **Pressure:** 0.35–3.5 MPa (3.5–35 bar); modern units use **low-pressure (0.35 MPa)** to maximize aromatics.
- **H₂/Hydrocarbon molar ratio:** 3–6
- **Space Velocity (LHSV):** 1–2 h⁻¹

**Reactor Configuration:**
- **CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration):** Modern units (e.g., UOP Platforming, Axens Aromizing) use a moving-bed reactor stack (3–4 reactors in series) with catalyst continuously withdrawn, regenerated in a separate regenerator (burning off coke), and returned.
- **Semi-Regenerative (SR):** Older, fixed-bed, requires periodic shutdown for regeneration.

**Products:**
| Stream | Yield (wt %) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Reformate (C5+) | ~ 82–86 | High-octane gasoline blendstock |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | ~ 1.5–3.0 | HDS, HC feed |
| LPG (C3–C4) | ~ 6–8 | Petrochem / fuel |
| Fuel gas (C1–C2) | ~ 5 | Refinery fuel gas |

**Significance:**
- Reformate is the **highest-octane component** in the gasoline pool; an octane boost from 40 (feed) to 95+ allows refineries to meet stringent RON specs (BS-VI requires 95 RON).
- **Byproduct H₂** is *extremely valuable*; it offsets refinery hydrogen demand.
- The aromatic-rich reformate is also a source of **BTX (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene)** for the petrochemical industry.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q.7(b) – Modified]** — Petroleum Refinery Products.

**(a)** Describe **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** with a neat flow diagram. List the major products and their downstream uses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** process. Discuss its chemistry, operating parameters, and importance in producing BS-VI diesel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC):**

**Objective:** Convert heavy hydrocarbon feedstocks (Vacuum Gas Oil, deasphalted oil, atmospheric residue) into **high-octane gasoline**, **LPG**, and **distillate** by catalytically breaking long-chain molecules.

**Feed:** VGO (boiling range 380–550 °C), deasphalted oil (DAO), or hydrotreated residues.

**Chemistry:** **Heterogeneous catalytic cracking** of C–C bonds via **Carbenium ion** intermediates on acidic zeolite catalysts (Y-zeolite, ZSM-5). The dominant reactions:
1. **Primary cracking:** VGO → gasoline + middle distillate + LPG.
2. **Secondary cracking:** gasoline → LPG + dry gas (overcracking).
3. **Coke formation:** Heavy molecules → solid carbonaceous coke (deposits on catalyst).

**Catalyst:** **Y-Zeolite (Faujasite) crystalline aluminosilicate** in a matrix of clay/binder with a binder. Modern FCC catalysts include **ZSM-5 additive** (5–25 % of catalyst inventory) which selectively cracks C5–C8 olefins → boosts LPG yield and gasoline octane.

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Reactor (Riser) Outlet Temperature:** 500–540 °C
- **Reactor Pressure:** 1.5–3.0 bar (gauge)
- **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (CTO):** 5–8
- **Residence time (riser):** 2–4 seconds — very short to avoid overcracking.
- **Catalyst regeneration temperature (Regenerator):** 680–750 °C

**Process Flow:**

1. **Feed injection:** Pre-heated VGO is injected at the base of the **riser reactor** through multiple feed nozzles, contacting **hot regenerated catalyst** from the regenerator. The catalyst and oil flow *upward* together in a fluidized state (hence "fluid catalytic cracking").
2. **Riser reactor:** Cracking reactions occur in 2–4 seconds. Cracked vapours and spent catalyst are separated at the top of the riser in the **reactor vessel** via cyclones.
3. **Spent catalyst stripping:** Steam strips residual hydrocarbons from the spent catalyst.
4. **Regenerator:** Spent catalyst (with 0.5–1.0 wt % coke) flows down the **spent catalyst standpipe** to the regenerator, where **air** is blown through the catalyst bed. The coke is burned off in **complete combustion**:
$$\text{Coke} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \text{ (mostly)}$$
$$\text{Coke} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \text{ (minor)}$$
The regenerated catalyst (with < 0.05 wt % coke) is returned to the riser via the **regenerated catalyst standpipe**.
5. **Main fractionator:** Cracked vapours enter the **main column (slurry settler)**, which separates products: cracked gas (C1–C4), cracked naphtha (C5–C12), LCO (Light Cycle Oil), and bottom slurry.

**FCC Products and Their Uses:**

| Product | Yield (vol %) | Carbon No. | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dry Gas (C1–C2) | 4–6 | C1–C2 | Refinery fuel gas |
| LPG (C3–C4) | 12–20 | C3–C4 | Auto LPG, alkylation feed |
| Cracked Naphtha | 45–55 | C5–C12 | High-RON gasoline blending |
| Light Cycle Oil (LCO) | 15–25 | C12–C20 | Diesel blending (low cetane) or FCC feed |
| Slurry / Decant Oil | 5–10 | C20+ | Heavy fuel oil, carbon black feedstock, or Coker feed |
| Coke (on catalyst) | 4–6 wt % | Solid | Burned in regenerator for process heat |

**Significance:** FCC is the *gasoline engine* of the refinery. It alone converts ~ 30 % of a barrel of crude into gasoline-grade stock, and its high cracked-naphtha octane (RON 88–95) is the modern refinery's primary source of gasoline octane-barrels.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):**

**Objective:** Remove sulfur (and to a lesser extent nitrogen, oxygen, and metals) from petroleum fractions to (i) prevent catalyst poisoning in downstream processes, (ii) reduce atmospheric emissions (SO₂, particulate matter), and (iii) improve product stability and color.

**Feed:** Naphtha, kerosene, diesel, VGO — almost every refinery stream that requires sulfur removal.

**Chemistry:**
$$R-SH + H_2 \rightarrow R-H + H_2S \text{ (mercaptan)}$$
$$R-S-R' + 2H_2 \rightarrow R-H + R'-H + H_2S \text{ (sulfide)}$$
$$\text{Thiophene} + 4H_2 \rightarrow \text{Butane} + H_2S \text{ (cyclic sulfide)}$$
$$\text{Dibenzothiophene} + 2H_2 \rightarrow \text{Biphenyl} + H_2S \text{ (refractory DBT)}$$

**Hydrogen Consumption:** Increases with feed boiling point and required desulfurization depth. For diesel HDS: ~ 100–300 SCF H₂/bbl; for VGO HDS: ~ 400–800 SCF H₂/bbl.

**Catalyst:** **Co-Mo / Al₂O₃** (cobalt-promoted molybdenum disulfide) for high-pressure, high-sulfur services. **Ni-Mo / Al₂O₃** is preferred for distillate HDS and where denitrogenation is also required. **Ni-W** for severe hydrocracking conditions.

**Catalyst Chemistry:** The active phase is **MoS₂ (or WS₂)** crystallites; cobalt or nickel acts as a *promoter*, increasing the number of active edge sites (the "Co-Mo-S" rim model).

**Operating Conditions:**
| Stream | T (°C) | P (bar) | LHSV (h⁻¹) | H₂/HC ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Naphtha HDS | 280–340 | 10–30 | 4–8 | 50–100 |
| Kerosene HDS | 300–360 | 20–40 | 3–5 | 100–200 |
| Diesel HDS | 320–380 | 30–80 | 1–3 | 200–500 |
| VGO HDS | 340–400 | 60–150 | 0.5–1.5 | 300–800 |

**Reactor:** **Fixed-bed downflow trickle-bed reactor** — gas and liquid flow co-currently downward over catalyst pellets.

**Byproduct Gas Treatment:** The effluent is cooled, the recycle gas is separated, and the **H₂S-rich gas** is sent to a **Claus Plant** to recover elemental sulfur:
$$2H_2S + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_2 + 2H_2O \text{ (Furnace)}$$
$$4H_2S + 2SO_2 \rightarrow 6S + 4H_2O \text{ (Catalytic converter — Claus reaction)}$$

**Importance in Producing BS-VI Diesel:**
- BS-VI norms (equivalent to Euro-VI) mandate diesel sulfur content of **< 10 ppm** (from 50 ppm under BS-IV).
- Achieving 10 ppm requires **deep HDS**, often in **two-stage** configuration with a stripping column and re-heating.
- Refractory sulfur compounds like **4,6-dimethyldibenzothiophene** are the hardest to crack; their removal requires *high pressure, high temperature, and noble-metal-promoted catalysts*.
- Beyond sulfur, modern HDS also **reduces aromatics and increases cetane number** — improving the *cetane index* by 5–15 points.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers (from KTU Valuation Key patterns):**
> 1. **Conflating Octane and Cetane Numbers** — A common error is to state that *diesel* has octane number. Diesel has *cetane number*. These are mutually exclusive.
> 2. **Confusing FCC and Hydrocracker products** — FCC is a *carbon-rejecting* process (no hydrogen added); Hydrocracker is a *hydrogen-adding* process. The latter yields *cleaner diesel* with lower sulfur and higher cetane.
> 3. **Wrong boiling range for kerosene/diesel** — Kerosene is **150–250 °C**; diesel is **250–350 °C**. Reversing these loses marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "significance" or "engineering context"** — KTU examiners allocate ~ 1–2 marks for the "why this matters" part. Always conclude with the **practical implication**.
> 5. **Forgetting the HDS equation in the HDS question** — Students often describe the *unit* but miss the *stoichiometry* (R–S + H₂ → R–H + H₂S).
> 6. **Writing "RON" instead of giving both RON and MON** — ASTM octane spec demands both. The posted pump number is *Anti-Knock Index* (AKI = (RON+MON)/2).
> 7. **Ignoring the byproduct hydrogen from reforming** — A complete answer must state the **H₂ byproduct** because the H₂ is what makes reforming economically and environmentally critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU ESE.**

- [ ] **Crude oil** is fractionated by **boiling point** in ADC; residues go to VDC.
- [ ] **LPG** = C3–C4; **Naphtha** = C5–C11; **Gasoline** = C5–C12; **Kerosene/ATF** = C10–C16; **Diesel** = C12–C18; **Fuel Oil** = C18+.
- [ ] **Octane Number** is for **gasoline (SI engines)**, measured on the **iso-octane / n-heptane** scale.
- [ ] **Cetane Number** is for **diesel (CI engines)**, measured on the **n-cetane / α-methylnaphthalene** scale.
- [ ] **API Gravity** = 141.5/SG – 131.5; higher °API = lighter crude.
- [ ] **Watson K-factor** > 12 = paraffinic; < 11 = naphthenic.
- [ ] **RVP** is vapour pressure at 37.8 °C with V/L = 4:1; TVP = 1.25 × RVP.
- [ ] **Reforming** raises octane (40 → 95) and produces **H₂**; uses **Pt-Re / Al₂O₃** catalyst.
- [ ] **FCC** uses **Y-zeolite** catalyst; regenerator burns off coke at 700 °C.
- [ ] **Hydrocracker** = hydrogen + acid catalyst; yields *clean* diesel.
- [ ] **HDS** = R–S + H₂ → R–H + H₂S; uses **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo** on Al₂O₃.
- [ ] **BS-VI** mandates < 10 ppm sulfur in diesel and gasoline.
- [ ] **Lubricating oils** are graded by **Viscosity Index (VI)**; high VI = stable across temperatures.
- [ ] **Bitumen** is graded by **Penetration (1/10 mm at 25 °C)** and **Softening Point (°C, R&B)**.
- [ ] **Smoke Point (mm)** is the key test for **ATF / kerosene** quality (Jet A-1: ≥ 25 mm).
- [ ] **Aniline Point** indicates aromaticity — *lower aniline point = more aromatic*.
- [ ] **Reid Vapour Pressure** must be controlled to avoid **vapour lock** in gasoline.
- [ ] **Cetane Index (ASTM D4737)** can be calculated from distillation data without an engine test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Writing Rule (KTU):** Always support any long answer with a **labeled flow diagram** (worth ~ 2 marks). When a question asks for *process significance*, do not just list products — state the **economic and environmental** impact (e.g., "BS-VI compliance", "export market", "petrochemical feed integration").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atmospheric Distillation Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atmospheric Distillation Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU)**, often termed the **Crude Distillation Unit (CDU)** or **Topping Unit**, is the **primary and most voluminous separation process unit** in any petroleum refinery. It is a continuous, large-scale fractional distillation operation carried out at or slightly above atmospheric pressure (typically **101.3 kPa to 120 kPa** at the top), wherein desalted crude oil is thermally separated into distinct hydrocarbon fractions (naphtha, kerosene, diesel, gas oil, atmospheric residue) based on their boiling point ranges.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Box**  
> The ADU is the **first processing unit** that crude oil enters after desalting. It is NOT a chemical reactor — it is purely a **physical separation** process that exploits volatility differences. The "atmospheric" qualifier is essential because the residue from this column is further processed in a **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** operating at **3.3 kPa to 6.6 kPa** absolute pressure to avoid thermal cracking at high temperatures (above 400°C).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large multi-story apartment building** (the distillation tower) where residents (hydrocarbon molecules) are sorted by their **"boiling impatience"**:

- **Tenants on the top floor** are the lightest, most volatile molecules (gases, naphtha) — they have low boiling points and evaporate easily, escaping to the top.
- **Tenants on the middle floors** are medium-weight residents (kerosene, diesel, gas oil) — they condense on their respective floors and exit through side doors (side draws).
- **Tenants in the basement** are the heaviest, most stubborn residents (atmospheric residue) — they refuse to vaporize and are drained from the bottom.

Each floor has a **thermostat (temperature gradient)** that ensures only the right residents step out at that level. The "boiling impatient" ones move up; the "patient" heavy ones stay down. **A heat source (reboiler/furnace) keeps the building warm**, and a **coolant at the top (overhead condenser)** keeps the upper floors cool. This is precisely how an ADU functions — molecules are sorted by volatility in a vertical temperature gradient tower.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why "Atmospheric"?**  
> Performing the distillation at atmospheric pressure instead of vacuum is preferred for the lighter fractions because **lower pressure drastically lowers boiling points**. At atmospheric pressure, the heaviest crude component we want to vaporize is **atmospheric residue** (initial boiling point around **350°C**). Beyond this temperature, hydrocarbons begin to undergo **thermal cracking** — a permanent, undesirable chemical decomposition. Thus, the ADU's thermal ceiling is the "cracking limit," and vacuum distillation handles the leftover residue below this ceiling.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Feed Throughput (Capacity):** Modern Indian refineries process **5 to 27 MMTPA** (Million Metric Tonnes Per Annum). A 1 MMTPA unit processes ≈ **3,000 m³/day** of crude.
- **Crude Oil API Gravity:** Light crudes (35°–45° API), Medium crudes (26°–35° API), Heavy crudes (10°–25° API).
- **Top Tower Temperature:** **120°C – 150°C**
- **Bottom Tower Temperature:** **340°C – 360°C**
- **Furnace Outlet Temperature (HOT oil):** **360°C – 380°C**
- **Reflux Ratio (R/D):** Typical range **2.0 to 4.0**
- **Number of Trays:** **35 to 50** (sieve/glasses/valve trays)
- **Heat Integration Efficiency:** Modern ADUs achieve **150% to 180%** of furnace duty recovery through feed preheat train.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The ADU operates through a carefully orchestrated sequence of physical operations:

**Step 1: Desalting (Pre-treatment)**
- Crude oil entering the refinery contains water, dissolved salts (primarily **NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂**), and entrained solids.
- Salts must be removed because they cause:
  - **Corrosion** of overhead condensers (forms HCl)
  - **Fouling** of furnace tubes and heat exchangers
  - **Catalyst poisoning** in downstream catalytic units
- **Two-Stage Desalter Operation:** Crude is mixed with **3–5% wash water** and demulsifier, then subjected to high-voltage **electrostatic field (15–35 kV AC)** to coalesce water droplets, which settle out by gravity.

**Step 2: Preheat Train (Heat Recovery Network)**
- Cold desalted crude (≈ **120°C** after preheating from ambient) flows through a series of heat exchangers.
- It is progressively heated by **hot product streams** leaving the ADU (pump-around returns, side draw products, overhead vapors).
- This **process-to-process heat exchange** drastically reduces external furnace fuel consumption.
- Typical preheat train: **Desalted crude → Crude preheat exchangers → Desalter → Final heater → Furnace**.

**Step 3: Furnace Heating (Primary Energy Input)**
- The preheated crude enters a **direct-fired tubular furnace** (process gas or fuel oil fired).
- Furnace raises the crude to its **partial vaporization temperature** (typically **360°C – 380°C**), enough to vaporize 50–65% of the feed.
- **Heater Pass Design:** Two-pass or four-pass convection-radiant designs ensure controlled heat flux and avoid tube coking.

**Step 4: Vapor-Liquid Separation in the Main Tower**
- The two-phase mixture enters the **flash zone** (typically between **Tray 5 and Tray 10** from the bottom).
- Vapors rise through **tray stacks** (or structured packing) — each tray acts as a small equilibrium stage.
- A controlled **temperature gradient** is established top-to-bottom.
- **Internal Reflux:** Liquid condenses on cooler trays above, providing the descending liquid that contacts rising vapor — this is the "rectification" mechanism.

**Step 5: Side Draws (Side Stream Withdrawal)**
- At specific tray locations, liquid side streams are withdrawn to obtain specific products.
- Each side draw is **steam-stripped** in a side-stripper (small 4–6 tray column) using **open steam (1.0–1.5 MPa)** to remove light ends and tighten the product's initial boiling point (IBP).
- Common side draws (top to bottom): **Heavy Naphtha → Kerosene/ATF → Diesel → Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO)**.

**Step 6: Overhead System (Condensation and Reflux)**
- Overhead vapors (≈ **120°C – 150°C**) pass through a **tray-type or fin-fan overhead condenser**.
- Condensed liquid splits: a portion returns as **reflux** (R), the rest as **overhead product (D)** — typically unstabilized naphtha.
- **Reflux Ratio (R/D)** controls separation sharpness. Higher R/D → sharper separation, more energy.

**Step 7: Bottom Product (Atmospheric Residue)**
- Non-vaporized heavy bottoms (≈ **340°C**, called **Long Residue** or **Atmospheric Residue**) leave the column bottom.
- It is pumped through heat exchangers (preheating the incoming crude) before storage.
- This residue goes to the **VDU**, **Solvent Deasphalting**, or **Residual Fluid Catalytic Cracker (RFCC)**.

**Step 8: Pump-Around Cooling (Internal Heat Removal)**
- **Pump-around (PA)** is a controlled withdrawal of hot liquid from a tray, cooled externally by heat exchangers, and returned to the column a few trays above.
- PAs serve three purposes:
  1. **Heat removal** from the column (replaces internal condensation)
  2. **Heat recovery** for feed preheating
  3. **Internal reflux control** for tray hydraulics

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Why" of Each Step**  
> Each step addresses a fundamental separation constraint: desalting protects equipment; preheating saves fuel; furnace provides vaporization energy; the tower provides equilibrium stages; side strippers tighten product specifications; reflux increases separation efficiency; PAs manage internal heat balance. Removing any one step degrades product quality, energy efficiency, or equipment life.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reflux Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | $L$ = Liquid reflux flow (kmol/h), $D$ = Distillate flow (kmol/h) |
| 2 | **Minimum Reflux Ratio (Underwood)** | $R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \left[ \dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,F}} - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{x_{HK,D}}{x_{HK,F}} \right]$ | $\alpha$ = relative volatility, $LK/HK$ = light/heavy key |
| 3 | **Fenske Equation (Min Stages)** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( x_{LK}/x_{HK} \right)_D \cdot \left( x_{HK}/x_{LK} \right)_B \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Theoretical minimum number of stages |
| 4 | **Gilliland Correlation (Actual Stages)** | $Y = 1 - \exp\left[\dfrac{(1+54.4X)}{11+117.2X} \cdot \ln X \right]$ | $X = (R - R_{min})/(R+1)$, $Y = (N - N_{min})/(N+1)$ |
| 5 | **Erbar-Maddox Correlation** | $Y = 0.75 \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R+1}\right)^{0.567} \right]$ | More accurate at high reflux ratios |
| 6 | **Material Balance (Overall)** | $F = D + B$ | $F$ = Feed, $D$ = Distillate, $B$ = Bottom (kmol/h) |
| 7 | **Component Balance (for i)** | $F \cdot z_i = D \cdot x_{i,D} + B \cdot x_{i,B}$ | Component $i$ mole fraction balance |
| 8 | **q-line (Feed Thermal Condition)** | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} \cdot x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | $q$ = fraction of feed that is liquid |
| 9 | **Antoine Equation (Vapor Pressure)** | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ | $P^{sat}$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C, A, B, C are component-specific |
| 10 | **Relative Volatility** | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$ | (Raoult's Law assumption; ideal system) |
| 11 | **K-value (Equilibrium Ratio)** | $K_i = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i} = \dfrac{\gamma_i P_i^{sat}}{\phi_i P_{total}}$ | Generalized; for ADU at ≈1 atm, $\phi_i \approx 1$ |
| 12 | **Tray Efficiency (Overall)** | $E_o = \dfrac{N_{ideal}}{N_{actual}}$ | Typically 0.5–0.7 for valve trays in ADUs |
| 13 | **API Gravity Conversion** | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ F}} - 131.5$ | $SG$ = Specific gravity relative to water at 60°F |
| 14 | **Energy Efficiency (Preheat)** | $\eta_{preheat} = \dfrac{T_{crude,heater\ inlet} - T_{crude,desalter\ outlet}}{T_{furnace\ outlet} - T_{desalter\ outlet}} \times 100\%$ | Modern ADU target: **>60%** |
| 15 | **Crude Boiling Range Conversion (TBP → ASTM D86)** | ASTM D86 gap ≈ TBP – 14°C (kerosene range) | Empirical gap depends on fraction type |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Exam Tip — Formula Selection**  
> For a typical KTU 14-mark problem, the examiner expects you to: (1) state the Fenske and Underwood equations with meaning of each variable, (2) apply the Gilliland correlation graphically or via the simplified Eduljee equation, and (3) calculate actual stages. Be prepared to draw the **McCabe-Thiele diagram** for a binary system simplification of the ADU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fenske Equation (Minimum Number of Theoretical Stages)

The Fenske equation is fundamental for ADU design. Below is the rigorous derivation.

**Starting Point:** At total reflux ($D = 0$, $L = V$), the operating lines in the McCabe-Thiele diagram coincide with the diagonal. Under total reflux, the operating condition is mathematically equivalent to requiring the ratio of light to heavy key in the vapor leaving any tray equal to the ratio in the liquid entering that tray:

$$
K_i^{(n)} = \dfrac{y_i^{(n)}}{x_i^{(n)}}
$$

**Step 1:** Define the relative volatility with respect to a reference component (the heavy key, HK):

$$
\alpha_{i,HK} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_{HK}} = \dfrac{y_i \cdot x_{HK}}{x_i \cdot y_{HK}}
$$

**Step 2:** Consider the liquid and vapor leaving tray $n$. By mass balance on component $i$ over the entire column at total reflux, after rigorous induction over $N$ stages, the following relationship holds:

$$
\left( \dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_D = \alpha_{LK,HK}^N \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_B
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $N$ (the number of theoretical stages at total reflux = $N_{min}$):

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{HK,D}} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{LK,B}} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Physical Meaning of $N_{min}$**  
> $N_{min}$ is the absolute minimum number of equilibrium stages required to achieve a specified separation when operating at **infinite reflux** (zero product withdrawal). It is a thermodynamic lower bound — the real column needs MORE stages because real reflux is finite.

### 3.2 Derivation: Underwood Equation (Minimum Reflux Ratio)

The Underwood equation identifies the minimum reflux for a given separation.

**Feed Thermal Condition:** The parameter $q$ represents the mole fraction of feed entering as saturated liquid. For a sub-cooled liquid feed, $q > 1$; for a saturated liquid, $q = 1$; for a two-phase feed, $0 < q < 1$; for a saturated vapor, $q = 0$; and for a superheated vapor, $q < 0$.

**The Underwood Equation:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{q \cdot z_i}{V - q \cdot K_i} = 1
$$

where $V$ is the vapor flow rate under minimum reflux conditions, $z_i$ is the feed composition, and $K_i$ is the equilibrium ratio.

**Step 1:** Solve the equation iteratively for $V$ (the Underwood root). Choose $V$ such that $\alpha_{LK} > V/K_{LK} > \alpha_{HK}$ for proper convergence.

**Step 2:** Calculate minimum reflux using:

$$
R_{min} + 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{\alpha_i \cdot z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta}
$$

where $\theta = V/F$ (dimensionless Underwood root).

**Step 3:** For binary distillation, the simpler form applies:

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{z_F} - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_D}{1 - z_F} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why Two Roots? Why Iteration?**  
> The Underwood equation is transcendental in $V$. Multiple roots can satisfy it, but only the root in the interval $K_{HK} < V/F < K_{LK}$ gives a physically meaningful answer (satisfying the **pinch condition** in the rectifying section).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A refinery plans to separate a desalted crude feed in an ADU. The feed contains 30 mol% n-pentane (LK) and 70 mol% n-decane (HK). The average relative volatility $\alpha = 6.0$. Required top product purity: $x_{D,LK} = 0.98$. Required bottom purity: $x_{B,HK} = 0.99$. The feed enters as a saturated liquid ($q = 1$). Calculate $N_{min}$ and $R_{min}$.

**Step 1: Calculate Component Concentrations**

From the specifications:
- $x_{D,LK} = 0.98 \Rightarrow x_{D,HK} = 0.02$
- $x_{B,HK} = 0.99 \Rightarrow x_{B,LK} = 0.01$

**Step 2: Apply Fenske Equation**

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.98}{0.02} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.99}{0.01} \right) \right]}{\ln 6.0}
$$

Computing the numerator term-by-term:

$$
\dfrac{0.98}{0.02} = 49.0
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.99}{0.01} = 99.0
$$

$$
49.0 \times 99.0 = 4851
$$

$$
\ln(4851) = 8.487
$$

$$
\ln(6.0) = 1.7918
$$

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{8.487}{1.7918} \approx 4.74 \text{ stages}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Underwood (Binary Form)**

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{z_F} - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_D}{1 - z_F} \right]
$$

Substituting values:

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(6.0 - 1)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{0.98}{0.30} - 6.0 \cdot \dfrac{0.02}{0.70} \right]
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{5.0} \cdot \left[ 3.267 - 0.171 \right]
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{3.095}{5.0} = 0.619
$$

**Step 4: Select Operating Reflux**

Typical operating reflux is 1.2 to 1.5 times $R_{min}$. Choosing $R = 1.5 \times R_{min}$:

$$
R = 1.5 \times 0.619 = 0.929
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Actual Stages via Gilliland**

Calculate the Gilliland parameters:

$$
X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \dfrac{0.929 - 0.619}{0.929 + 1} = \dfrac{0.310}{1.929} = 0.1607
$$

Using the **Eduljee Approximation** (commonly used in Indian engineering curricula):

$$
Y = 0.75 \cdot \left[ 1 - X^{0.567} \right]
$$

$$
Y = 0.75 \cdot \left[ 1 - (0.1607)^{0.567} \right]
$$

Computing $(0.1607)^{0.567}$:

$$
\ln(0.1607) = -1.827
$$

$$
-1.827 \times 0.567 = -1.036
$$

$$
e^{-1.036} = 0.355
$$

$$
Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.355) = 0.75 \times 0.645 = 0.484
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $N$ (Actual Theoretical Stages)**

Rearranging the Gilliland correlation:

$$
Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{N_{min} + Y}{1 - Y} = \dfrac{4.74 + 0.484}{1 - 0.484} = \dfrac{5.224}{0.516}
$$

$$
N \approx 10.13 \text{ theoretical stages}
$$

**Step 7: Apply Tray Efficiency**

For an ADU with valve trays, assume $E_o = 0.65$:

$$
N_{actual} = \dfrac{N_{ideal}}{E_o} = \dfrac{10.13}{0.65} \approx 15.6 \approx 16 \text{ actual trays}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Insight**  
> A real ADU has 35–50 trays because it has MULTIPLE side draws (each side draw is essentially a "sub-column" within the main column). The single Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland calculation above only handles a binary separation. For a multi-product ADU, the column is designed as a **sequence of pseudo-binary columns** stacked together, and the calculation is repeated for each adjacent pair of side draws.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ADU Product Specifications vs. Side Draw Location

| Side Draw (Top to Bottom) | Approximate Tray No. (of 40) | TBP Cut Range (°C) | Primary Use | Critical Quality Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Overhead Naphtha | Tray 1 (top) | C5 – 150°C | Gasoline pool / Petrochem feed | Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) |
| Heavy Naphtha | Tray 6 | 90 – 190°C | reformer feed | Paraffin+Naphthene % |
| Kerosene / ATF | Tray 14 | 150 – 250°C | Aviation turbine fuel | Smoke point, Freezing pt |
| Diesel | Tray 22 | 250 – 360°C | Diesel engine fuel | Cetane number, Sulfur % |
| AGO | Tray 30 | 340 – 380°C | FCC/Lube oil feedstock | Aniline point, Viscosity |
| Atmospheric Residue | Reboiler | > 360°C | VDU/RFCC feedstock | Conradson Carbon (CCR) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: ADU Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Raw Crude Oil Storage] --> node2[Crude Charge Pump]
    node2 --> node3[Two-Stage Desalter with Wash Water]
    node3 --> node4[1st Preheat Train Crude Side]
    node4 --> node5[2nd Preheat Train Side Draws]
    node5 --> node6[Crude Furnace Direct Fired]
    node6 --> node7[Atmospheric Distillation Tower Flash Zone]
    node7 --> node8[Rectifying Section with Side Draws]
    node8 --> node9[Overhead Vapor Line to Condenser]
    node9 --> node10[Reflux Drum and Pump]
    node10 --> node11[Overhead Naphtha Product]
    node10 --> node12[Reflux Return to Tower Top]
    node8 --> node13[Kerosene Side Stripper]
    node8 --> node14[Diesel Side Stripper]
    node8 --> node15[AGO Side Stripper]
    node13 --> node16[Kerosene Product to Storage]
    node14 --> node17[Diesel Product to Storage]
    node15 --> node18[AGO Product to Storage]
    node7 --> node19[Atmospheric Residue Pump Around]
    node19 --> node20[Atmospheric Residue to VDU or Storage]
    node8 --> node21[Pump Around Coolers]
    node21 --> node4
    node11 --> node22[Sour Water Stripper and Naphtha Stabilizer]
    node22 --> node23[Stabilized Naphtha to Pool]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagram**  
> The flowchart above maps every major unit operation in a typical refinery ADU. The **closed loop from Pump Around back to Preheat Train** represents the energy integration that gives modern ADUs their high thermal efficiency (recovery of 60–80% of the furnace's input energy as feed preheat).

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: ADU Internal Tray Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Tower Top Tray 40: Overhead Vapor Exit] --> nodeB[Tray 38-39: Naphtha Reflux Section]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Tray 30-37: Heavy Naphtha Side Draw Region]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Tray 25-29: Pump Around 1 PA Cooling Loop]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Tray 18-24: Kerosene ATF Side Draw Region]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Tray 14-17: Pump Around 2 PA Cooling Loop]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Tray 8-13: Diesel Side Draw Region]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Tray 4-7: AGO Atmospheric Gas Oil Side Draw]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Tray 1-3: Stripping Section with Open Steam]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Flash Zone Entry 360C Hot Crude Feed]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Column Bottom Residue Withdrawal]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Process Flow Description (Text Form)

**Top of Tower (Tray 40 → Down):**
- Vapors leave the top, go to **Overhead Condenser** (air-cooled fin-fan or water-cooled shell-and-tube).
- Condensate collected in **Reflux Drum**.
- Split: ~70% returned as reflux via **Reflux Pump**; ~30% withdrawn as **Overhead Naphtha** product.
- Two-phase flow: **Vapors** (lighter than C5) leave uncondensed from the reflux drum as **Refinery Off-Gas (ROG)** — sent to gas treating.

**Side Draw Regions (Top-Down):**
- **Heavy Naphtha** at tray ~33 → **Naphtha Side Stripper** → stabilized naphtha product.
- **Kerosene/ATF** at tray ~22 → **Kerosene Stripper** (uses 0.5 MPa open steam) → aviation turbine fuel.
- **Diesel** at tray ~14 → **Diesel Stripper** → diesel product.
- **AGO** at tray ~7 → **AGO Stripper** → gas oil to FCC feed tank.

**Bottom of Tower:**
- **Atmospheric Residue** at ~340°C → **Residue Pump** → preheats incoming crude → cooled to ~150°C → VDU feed storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Steam Stripping in Side Strippers**  
> Open steam (saturated steam at ~1.0 MPa, 180°C) is injected at the bottom of each side stripper. It lowers the partial pressure of hydrocarbons (steam dilution effect), causing more light ends to vaporize and exit the top of the stripper, where they return to the main column. This tightens the product's initial boiling point without reboiling the stripper — saving energy and capital.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Differentiate clearly between the Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU) and the Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU) in terms of operating pressure, feed condition, and the primary reason for choosing the operating condition.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ADU | VDU |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure (top) | 101.3 – 120 kPa (atmospheric) | 3.3 – 6.6 kPa absolute (vacuum) |
| Feed | Desalted crude oil | Bottoms from ADU (atmospheric residue) |
| Top Temperature | 120–150°C | 70–90°C |
| Bottom Temperature | 340–360°C | 380–420°C |
| Primary Reason for Condition | Avoids excessive vaporization load at top; keeps lightest fractions (naphtha) liquid at top for reflux | Prevents **thermal cracking** of residue at high temperatures; lowers boiling point of heavy molecules by ~200–250°C, allowing vaporization below the cracking threshold (~400°C) |

The ADU separates crude into lighter commercial products. The residue from ADU has an initial boiling point above 350°C. Heating it to vaporize at atmospheric pressure would require >400°C, which **thermally cracks** hydrocarbons into undesirable light gases and coke. VDU reduces pressure, lowering boiling points by 200+°C, enabling separation without cracking.

*(3 marks: 1 for pressure difference, 1 for feed, 1 for the cracking explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Explain the function of a pump-around (PA) circuit in an ADU. List any two of its main purposes.**

**Model Answer:**

A pump-around (PA) is a controlled heat-removal loop in a distillation column where hot liquid is withdrawn from a tray, pumped through an external heat exchanger (where it transfers heat to the cold incoming crude feed), and returned to the column a few trays above the withdrawal point. It is NOT the same as reflux, because the returned liquid does not flow downward to the bottom — it joins the internal liquid traffic.

**Two main purposes:**

1. **Heat Removal:** The energy entering the column from the furnace must be rejected. The PA removes 30–60% of this heat externally, complementing the overhead condenser duty.

2. **Energy Recovery / Feed Preheating:** The heat removed by the PA is productively used to preheat the desalted crude entering the furnace, drastically reducing external fuel consumption and improving the refinery's overall thermal efficiency.

3. (Bonus) **Internal Reflux Control:** PA influences the internal liquid/vapor traffic (L/V) and tray hydraulics, helping maintain stable operation across the column.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two purposes)*

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Question A:**  
**(a)** Describe with a neat flowchart the process flow of a typical **Atmospheric Distillation Unit** in a petroleum refinery, clearly identifying the **furnace, distillation tower, side strippers, overhead system, and preheat train**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A desalted crude feed (F = 1000 kmol/h) with composition $z_{LK} = 0.40$ (light key) and $z_{HK} = 0.60$ (heavy key) is to be separated. The relative volatility $\alpha = 4.0$ is constant. Required top product: $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$. Required bottom: $x_{B,HK} = 0.98$. The feed is a saturated liquid. Using the Fenske and Underwood equations, calculate: (i) the minimum number of theoretical stages $N_{min}$, and (ii) the minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$. Take operating reflux ratio as 1.4 times $R_{min}$ and use the Eduljee form of the Gilliland correlation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer for (a) — Process Flow Description (7 marks):**

The process flow consists of the following major sections:

1. **Desalting Section:** Crude oil is charged from storage via a **crude charge pump**, mixed with **3–5% wash water** and a **demulsifier** chemical, and passed through a **two-stage electrostatic desalter** operating at 15–35 kV AC. Salt content is reduced from 200–500 PTB (pounds per thousand barrels) to <5 PTB.

2. **Preheat Train:** Desalted crude flows through a series of shell-and-tube heat exchangers, picking up heat from hot products: **overhead vapor (partial), pump-around returns, diesel, kerosene, AGO, and finally atmospheric residue**. Crude temperature rises from ambient to ~220–250°C.

3. **Furnace:** The preheated crude enters a **direct-fired, vertical cylindrical tubular furnace** with radiant (high-flux) and convection (low-flux) sections. Furnace outlet temperature reaches **360–380°C**, achieving 50–65% vaporization.

4. **Main Distillation Tower:** The two-phase stream enters the **flash zone** of the ADU column. The tower has 35–50 trays and operates at ~101.3 kPa top, ~120 kPa bottom. Vapor rises, liquid descends, and side draws occur at prescribed trays. Pump-arounds remove excess heat at multiple levels.

5. **Side Strippers:** Each side draw is fed to a small **4–6 tray steam-stripper column** operating at 1.0–1.5 MPa open steam, removing light ends. Stripped product goes to storage; stripped vapors return to the main column.

6. **Overhead System:** Top vapors condense in **air-fin coolers / shell-and-tube condensers**. Condensate collects in the **reflux drum**, where vapor-liquid separation occurs. Liquid is split — reflux returns to the column, while naphtha product goes to the **sour water stripper** and then the **naphtha stabilizer (de-ethanizer)**.

7. **Bottom Product:** Atmospheric residue at ~340°C is pumped out, exchanges heat with incoming crude, and goes to **VDU feed storage**.

*(Marks: 1 mark per major section, 1 mark for overall integration, 1 mark for process logic.)*

**Model Answer for (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $F = 1000$ kmol/h, $z_{LK} = 0.40$, $z_{HK} = 0.60$, $\alpha = 4.0$, $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$, $x_{B,HK} = 0.98$, $q = 1$.

**Step 1: Identify key compositions**

- $x_{D,LK} = 0.95 \Rightarrow x_{D,HK} = 0.05$
- $x_{B,HK} = 0.98 \Rightarrow x_{B,LK} = 0.02$

**Step 2: Apply Fenske Equation**

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{D,HK}} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{B,HK}}{x_{B,LK}} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha}
$$

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.98}{0.02} \right) \right]}{\ln 4.0}
$$

Computing the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.98}{0.02} = 49.0
$$

$$
19.0 \times 49.0 = 931
$$

$$
\ln(931) = 6.836
$$

$$
\ln(4.0) = 1.3863
$$

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{6.836}{1.3863} \approx 4.93 \text{ theoretical stages}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Underwood Equation (Binary form, q = 1)**

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{x_{D,LK}}{z_{LK}} - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_{D,LK}}{1 - z_{LK}} \right]
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(4.0 - 1)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{0.95}{0.40} - 4.0 \cdot \dfrac{0.05}{0.60} \right]
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{3.0} \cdot \left[ 2.375 - 0.333 \right]
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{2.042}{3.0} \approx 0.681
$$

**Step 4: Operating Reflux Ratio**

$$
R = 1.4 \times R_{min} = 1.4 \times 0.681 = 0.953
$$

**Step 5: Gilliland Correlation (Eduljee form)**

$$
X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \dfrac{0.953 - 0.681}{0.953 + 1} = \dfrac{0.272}{1.953} \approx 0.1393
$$

$$
Y = 0.75 \cdot \left[ 1 - (X)^{0.567} \right] = 0.75 \cdot \left[ 1 - (0.1393)^{0.567} \right]
$$

Computing $(0.1393)^{0.567}$:

$$
\ln(0.1393) = -1.971
$$

$$
-1.971 \times 0.567 = -1.118
$$

$$
e^{-1.118} = 0.327
$$

$$
Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.327) = 0.75 \times 0.673 = 0.505
$$

**Step 6: Actual Theoretical Stages**

$$
N = \dfrac{N_{min} + Y}{1 - Y} = \dfrac{4.93 + 0.505}{1 - 0.505} = \dfrac{5.435}{0.495} \approx 10.98 \approx 11 \text{ stages}
$$

**Final Answer:** $N_{min} \approx 5$ stages, $R_{min} \approx 0.68$, $N_{actual} \approx 11$ theoretical stages.

*(Marks: 1 for Fenske setup, 1 for calculation, 1 for Underwood, 1 for R_min, 1 for Gilliland X, 1 for Y, 1 for final N.)*

---

**Question B (Alternative Choice):**  
**(a)** Explain in detail the role of the **two-stage desalter** in an ADU. Discuss the consequences of operating the desalter with poor performance. (7 marks)

**(b)** An ADU processes **5 MMTPA of crude oil** with API gravity of 32°. The atmospheric residue yield is 38% by volume. Calculate: (i) the volumetric flow rate of residue in m³/day, (ii) the residue flow rate in kmol/h assuming an average molecular weight of 580 g/mol, and (iii) the heat duty of the crude furnace if the residue leaves at 340°C and the crude enters the furnace at 230°C. Use an average crude Cp = 2.6 kJ/(kg·K). Take 1 barrel = 0.159 m³; crude density at 15°C ≈ 870 kg/m³ for 32° API. (7 marks)

**Model Answer for (a) — Desalter Operation (7 marks):**

The **two-stage desalter** removes water-soluble salts (NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂), entrained solids, and water from crude oil before it enters the furnace. The crude is mixed with **3–5% fresh wash water** and a **demulsifier chemical** (typically a polymeric surfactant). The mixture flows through a high-voltage **electrostatic field (15–35 kV AC)** that polarizes water droplets, causing them to coalesce. Coalesced droplets settle by gravity to the bottom of the vessel and are removed. Two stages in series reduce salt content progressively.

**Consequences of poor desalter performance:**

1. **Furnace tube fouling:** Salts (especially NaCl) deposit on hot furnace tubes, causing localized overheating and tube failure.
2. **Overhead corrosion:** Hydrolysis of CaCl₂ and MgCl₂ releases HCl gas at high temperatures, which condenses with water in the overhead system, causing severe corrosion of condensers and reflux drums.
3. **Catalyst poisoning:** Downstream catalytic units (FCC, hydrocracker) experience permanent deactivation from sodium and other metals.
4. **Reduced heat transfer:** Deposits in heat exchangers reduce preheat train efficiency, increasing furnace fuel consumption by 5–15%.
5. **Product quality:** Salt carryover into atmospheric residue poisons downstream processes and increases maintenance costs.

*(Marks: 2 for process description, 5 for consequences — distribute as 1 each.)*

**Model Answer for (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** Capacity = 5 MMTPA, API = 32°, Residue yield = 38 vol%, $M_{residue} = 580$ g/mol, $T_{out} = 340°C$, $T_{in} = 230°C$, $C_p = 2.6$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 1: Convert 5 MMTPA to m³/day (1 year = 365 days)**

$$
\text{Mass flow} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}}{365 \times 24 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^9}{8760} \approx 57078 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Convert to volume using $\rho = 870$ kg/m³:

$$
\text{Volume flow} = \dfrac{57078}{870} \approx 65.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

Convert to m³/day:

$$
65.6 \times 24 = 1574.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2: Residue Volumetric Flow**

$$
\dot{V}_{residue} = 0.38 \times 1574.4 = 598.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3: Residue Molar Flow (assuming same density for residue, ρ ≈ 920 kg/m³ typical)**

$$
\dot{m}_{residue} = 598.3 \times 920 = 550436 \text{ kg/day} = 22935 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{residue} = \dfrac{22935 \text{ kg/h}}{580 \text{ kg/kmol}} \approx 39.5 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4: Furnace Heat Duty (based on entire crude mass)**

For sensible heating of the total crude from 230°C to 340°C (ΔT = 110°C):

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{crude} \times C_p \times \Delta T
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 57078 \text{ kg/h} \times 2.6 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times 110 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 57078 \times 286 = 16.32 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

Converting to MW:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{16.32 \times 10^6}{3600} \approx 4534 \text{ kW} \approx 4.53 \text{ MW}
$$

**Final Answer:** Residue flow = **598 m³/day**, Molar flow = **39.5 kmol/h**, Furnace duty = **4.53 MW**.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for unit conversions, 2 for residue volumetric, 1 for molar, 2 for heat duty.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**  
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units in the Fenske/Underwood equation** — Always check whether the problem gives mole fractions or mass fractions. Fenske requires mole fractions.  
> 2. **Using the wrong form of the Gilliland correlation** — There are three versions: original, Molokanov, and Eduljee. State which one you are using; examiners will not award full credit for an ambiguous answer.  
> 3. **Confusing pump-around with reflux** — A PA returns the liquid ABOVE the withdrawal point; reflux returns to the TOP. This is a classic 1-mark trap question.  
> 4. **Drawing a P&ID when asked for a Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** — The PFD shows unit operations and material flows; the P&ID shows instrumentation and pipe sizes. KTU ADU questions typically want a PFD.  
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Always write the unit (kmol/h, MW, m³/day) next to the numerical value. Missing units = lose 0.5 marks per question.  
> 6. **Forgetting to mention thermal cracking** as the reason for VDU — this is the most important engineering rationale in this entire module.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ADU = Crude Distillation Unit = Topping Unit** — the largest, first processing unit in any refinery.
- Operates at **101.3 kPa – 120 kPa** top pressure, bottom at **340–360°C**.
- Furnace outlet temperature is the **cracking ceiling** (~380°C) for atmospheric operation.
- **Pre-treatment is mandatory:** Two-stage desalter with **3–5% wash water, 15–35 kV AC electrostatic field**, salt content reduced from 200–500 PTB to <5 PTB.
- **Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland methodology** is the standard shortcut method for multi-component distillation column design. Always compute $N_{min}$, $R_{min}$, then iterate to actual stages.
- **Reflux Ratio R = L/D;** operating R is typically 1.2–1.5 times $R_{min}$ for economical design.
- **Side draws + steam strippers** produce tight-boiling products. Stripping uses **open steam** (1.0–1.5 MPa) to lower hydrocarbon partial pressure.
- **Pump-arounds (PA)** remove 30–60% of column heat externally and preheat the cold crude — the **economic heart** of the ADU's energy efficiency.
- **Atmospheric residue** is the bottom product; it is sent to the **VDU** for further separation under vacuum to prevent thermal cracking.
- **Tray efficiency** for valve trays in ADUs: typically **0.55–0.70**.
- **Top product** (overhead naphtha) requires further stabilization (de-ethanization) to remove C1–C4 gases before blending to gasoline.
- **Modern ADU thermal efficiency:** preheat train recovers **60–80%** of the furnace's heat input.
- **Total trays in a real ADU: 35–50**, distributed into multiple side-draw regions with pump-arounds in between.
- **Crude oil properties** affecting ADU design: API gravity, TBP curve, sulfur content, salt content, pour point, viscosity, and CCR (Conradson Carbon Residue).
- **Energy integration principle:** the ADU's preheat train is a textbook example of **process-to-process heat exchange** in Pinch Analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
