<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Octane Number — The "Knock Resistance" of Gasoline

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Octane Number (ON)** is a dimensionless, standard metric used to characterize the **anti-knock quality** (resistance to premature detonation) of a spark-ignition engine fuel under standard test conditions, by comparing its knocking tendency with that of a reference binary blend of **2,2,4-trimethylpentane (isooctane)** and **n-heptane**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Reference (ASTM D2700 / D2699):**
> - **Isooctane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane)** → Octane Number = **100** (knock resistant)
> - **n-Heptane** → Octane Number = **0** (highly knocking)
> - Octane Number of any fuel = **% by volume of isooctane** in a blend of isooctane + n-heptane that exactly matches the knocking intensity of the fuel under standard test conditions.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Violin and the Drum"
Imagine two musicians: a violinist (isooctane) playing smooth, controlled notes, and a drummer (n-heptane) hitting loud, sharp beats at random. The violinist plays **predictably in time with the orchestra**; the drummer, however, "jumps ahead" of the conductor, creating a jarring noise. The violin = smooth combustion (no knock); the drum = premature detonation (knock). A fuel's octane number is essentially a measure of "how violin-like" the fuel is — higher octane = more controlled, smoother burn inside the engine cylinder.

### Two Standard Test Methods (KTU High-Yield)
| Test | Engine Speed | Inlet Air Temp | Severity | Typical Value (Gasoline) |
|------|--------------|----------------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Research Octane Number (RON)** | 600 rpm | 52 °C | Milder | 90–98 |
| **Motor Octane Number (MON)** | 900 rpm | 149 °C | Harsher | 80–88 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Knock Index (AKI) = (RON + MON) / 2** is the value displayed on U.S. fuel pumps. India follows the **RON rating system** (RON 91, RON 95).

---

## 1.2 Refinery Conversion Processes — Pyrolysis (Steam Cracking)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Pyrolysis** is a high-temperature, non-catalytic, free-radical thermal decomposition (cracking) of saturated hydrocarbons (typically **naphtha, ethane, propane, or gas oil**) carried out in the **absence of oxygen**, where hydrocarbon molecules are broken into smaller, unsaturated, and often more valuable olefinic and aromatic products such as **ethylene (C₂H₄), propylene (C₃H₆), butadiene (C₄H₆), and aromatics (BTX)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pyrolysis is NOT the same as "combustion."** Combustion requires oxygen; pyrolysis is a thermal decomposition under inert (steam-diluted) conditions. The presence of steam suppresses coke formation by the water-gas reaction.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Stretching and Snapping a Chain"
Picture a long paper-clip chain. If you heat the chain strongly in a sealed tube, the chain first stretches, the weakest links twist, and then **snap apart at random points** — producing shorter chains of different lengths. Hydrocarbons behave identically. Long C₁₀–C₃₀ paraffins, when heated to 750–900 °C, "snap" to give shorter alkenes, alkanes, and aromatics. The steam plays the role of an "inert crowd" that prevents the snapped ends from recombining.

### Key Operating Variables (KTU Standard)
- **Coil Outlet Temperature (COT):** **750–900 °C** (ethylene service)
- **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio:** **0.3 – 0.9 kg steam / kg feed**
- **Residence Time:** **0.05 – 0.5 seconds** (intentionally short)
- **Pressure:** **Near atmospheric (slightly above 1 atm)**
- **Heat Source:** Externally fired furnace

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Pyrolysis** (thermal, free-radical, no catalyst, high T) with **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** (catalyst, lower T, 450–550 °C). The products, mechanisms, and yields are entirely different.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Octane Number — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### A. Structural Origin of Octane Number
The octane rating of a hydrocarbon depends fundamentally on its **molecular architecture**, not just its carbon number.

| Structure Class | Typical Octane Number | Reason |
|-----------------|-----------------------|--------|
| **n-Alkanes** | Decreases as C-number increases (n-heptane = 0) | Long straight chains auto-ignite easily |
| **Iso-paraffins (branched)** | **High (80–100+)** | Branches raise activation energy for radical pre-ignition |
| **Olefins (alkenes)** | **Moderate to high** | π-bond stabilizes radicals |
| **Naphthenes (cycloalkanes)** | Moderate | Ring strain influences knock |
| **Aromatics** | **Very high (100+)** | Resonance-stabilized ring resists auto-ignition |

### B. Blending Octane Number (BON)
When two fuels are mixed, the octane number is **NOT** an arithmetic average — it follows the **linear blending rule**:

$$
ON_{blend} = x_A \cdot ON_A + x_B \cdot ON_B
$$

where $x_A$ and $x_B$ are the volume fractions. This is experimentally observed for primary reference fuels, but for *real* fuel components, the **Blending Octane Number (BON)** differs from the **Pure Component Octane Number** due to **non-linear synergistic effects** (e.g., aromatics have high BON, normal paraffins have low BON).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:** Refineries formulate gasoline by blending streams (reformate, isomerate, FCC gasoline, alkylate, hydrocrackate) such that the **blend RON** meets the marketing octane target (e.g., 91 or 95) at the lowest possible production cost.

### C. Octane Number and Engine Compression Ratio
A fuel's required octane number is dictated by the engine's **compression ratio (CR)**:

$$
\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{CR}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

where $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio. Higher CR → higher efficiency → requires higher ON to suppress knock. This is the thermodynamic basis for the industry's push toward **high-octane fuels**.

### D. Anti-Knock Additives
- **Tetraethyl Lead (TEL, Pb(C₂H₅)₄):** Historic anti-knock; phased out globally due to toxicity.
- **Methyl tert-Butyl Ether (MTBE):** 5–15 vol%, raises ON by ~6 points.
- **Ethanol:** Renewable oxygenate; raises ON by ~10–15 points.
- **Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl (MMT):** Mn-based ash-less alternative.
- **Aromatic Amines (e.g., N-methyl aniline):** Used in some markets.

---

## 2.2 Pyrolysis — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### A. Free-Radical Chain Mechanism
Pyrolysis follows the **Rice–Herzfeld free-radical mechanism** in three steps:

1. **Initiation:** C–C bond homolysis produces two alkyl radicals.
   $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \, \text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$$
2. **Propagation (β-scission):** A radical cleaves the β-C–C bond, yielding an alkene + smaller radical.
   $$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_7^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$$
3. **Termination:** Two radicals combine to yield a stable alkane.
   $$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$$

### B. Severity vs. Yield Trade-off (KTU High-Yield)
| Severity | COT (°C) | Major Products |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Low** | 700–780 | Propylene, butenes, gasoline-range |
| **Medium** | 800–850 | Ethylene, propylene (balanced) |
| **High** | 850–900 | Ethylene dominant, more methane, more coke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** Ethylene yield increases with COT and decreases with residence time. **Shorter residence + higher temperature** = higher ethylene selectivity (this drives the design of modern milli-second furnaces with residence times ≤ 0.1 s).

### C. Stoichiometric Cracking Reactions (Examples)

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2
$$

$$
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{CH}_4
$$

$$
\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6
$$

$$
n\text{-C}_{10}\text{H}_{22} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_6 + \text{C}_4\text{H}_8 + \text{C}_5\text{H}_{12} + \cdots
$$

### D. Role of Steam Dilution
- Reduces **partial pressure of hydrocarbons** → suppresses secondary bimolecular reactions that produce coke.
- Provides heat capacity "ballast" → smooths temperature profile.
- Promotes the water-gas reaction with nascent carbon:
  $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$

---

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Octane Number definition | $ON = \%v$ isooctane in isooctane/n-heptane blend matching knock | dimensionless | ASTM D2699/D2700 |
| 2 | Anti-Knock Index | $AKI = \dfrac{RON + MON}{2}$ | — | U.S. pump label |
| 3 | Linear blending rule | $ON_{blend} = x_A \cdot ON_A + x_B \cdot ON_B$ | dimensionless | For primary reference fuels |
| 4 | Otto cycle efficiency | $\eta = 1 - (1/CR)^{\gamma-1}$ | — | Justifies higher ON demand |
| 5 | Rice–Herzfeld initiation | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow 2\text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$ | — | Homolytic C–C cleavage |
| 6 | β-Scission propagation | $\text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{alkene} + \text{smaller R}^{\bullet}$ | — | Chain carrier |
| 7 | Steam-to-Hydrocarbon ratio | $S/HC = 0.3 - 0.9$ | kg/kg | Inhibits coke |
| 8 | Coils Outlet Temperature (COT) | $T = 750 - 900$ | °C | Ethylene service |
| 9 | Residence time | $\tau = 0.05 - 0.5$ | s | "Millisecond" furnace: $\tau \le 0.1$ s |
| 10 | Carbon-to-coke steam gasification | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ | — | De-coking during decoke cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of this Knowledge:** These formulas drive refinery design — furnace coil selection, recycle quench heat integration, and yield optimization in ethylene plants that feed the petrochemical complex (polyethylene, PVC, propylene oxide, butadiene rubber, etc.).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Octane Number by Linear Blending

**Problem:** A refinery has 60 vol% of a stream with RON = 92 and 40 vol% of another stream with RON = 85. Calculate the blended RON assuming linear blending behavior.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify volume fractions.
$$x_1 = 0.60, \quad x_2 = 0.40$$

**Step 2:** Identify octane numbers of the two streams.
$$ON_1 = 92, \quad ON_2 = 85$$

**Step 3:** Apply the linear blending rule.
$$
ON_{blend} = x_1 \cdot ON_1 + x_2 \cdot ON_2
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the values.
$$
ON_{blend} = (0.60)(92) + (0.40)(85)
$$

**Step 5:** Compute each product.
$$
ON_{blend} = 55.2 + 34.0
$$

**Step 6:** Final addition.
$$
\boxed{ON_{blend} = 89.2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result lies between the two parent ONs, closer to the higher-ON stream because of its larger volume fraction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anti-Knock Index from RON and MON

**Problem:** A gasoline has RON = 95 and MON = 85. Find the Anti-Knock Index.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the AKI definition.
$$
AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.
$$
AKI = \frac{95 + 85}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator sum.
$$
AKI = \frac{180}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Final division.
$$
\boxed{AKI = 90}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students should remember: **AKI is an integer-rounded label**; it does not include a decimal point in U.S. practice (e.g., 90, 91, 93).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Compression Ratio on Required Octane Number (Approximate)

For a fuel of ON = X to suppress knock at compression ratio CR, an empirical thumb-rule is:

$$
ON_{req} \approx k \cdot (CR - 1)
$$

where $k \approx 25$ for modern passenger vehicles. **Derive the required ON for a CR = 10 engine.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the empirical constant.
$$k = 25$$

**Step 2:** Identify the compression ratio.
$$CR = 10$$

**Step 3:** Apply the rule.
$$
ON_{req} = 25 \cdot (10 - 1)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the parenthesis.
$$
ON_{req} = 25 \cdot 9
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply.
$$
\boxed{ON_{req} = 225 \text{ (clearly above standard fuel range)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This empirical correlation overshoots for very high CR; in practice, modern turbocharged engines with CR = 10 run on **RON 95** thanks to knock sensors, retarded ignition timing, and intercooling. Use this rule only for rough screening, not for design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pyrolysis: Material Balance on an Ethane Cracker

**Problem:** 1000 kg/h of pure ethane is fed to a steam cracker. Per pass, **55% conversion to ethylene** is achieved. Calculate the **ethylene produced (kg/h)** and the **unreacted ethane recycle stream (kg/h)**, assuming the only products are ethylene and hydrogen (simplified stoichiometry).

Stoichiometry: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify molar masses.
$$M_{C_2H_6} = 30 \text{ kg/kmol}, \quad M_{C_2H_4} = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}, \quad M_{H_2} = 2 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 2:** Convert ethane feed to kmol/h.
$$
F_{C_2H_6} = \frac{1000}{30} = 33.333 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply 55% conversion.
$$
F_{reacted} = 0.55 \cdot 33.333 = 18.333 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4:** Unreacted ethane (recycle).
$$
F_{unreacted} = 33.333 - 18.333 = 15.000 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 5:** Mass of unreacted ethane.
$$
m_{unreacted} = 15.000 \cdot 30 = 450 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 6:** Ethylene produced (1:1 stoichiometry on molar basis).
$$
F_{C_2H_4} = 18.333 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 7:** Mass of ethylene.
$$
m_{C_2H_4} = 18.333 \cdot 28 = 513.33 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 8:** Hydrogen produced.
$$
m_{H_2} = 18.333 \cdot 2 = 36.67 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 9:** Verify mass balance.
$$
450 + 513.33 + 36.67 = 1000 \text{ kg/h} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unreacted ethane (450 kg/h) is **recycled** back to the furnace inlet — a hallmark of cracker design that boosts overall conversion to ~99% with a single pass ≈ 55–65%.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio

**Problem:** A naphtha cracker processes 20,000 kg/h of naphtha with a steam dilution ratio of 0.6 (kg steam / kg naphtha). Calculate the **total mass flow** entering the furnace and the **molar ratio of steam to naphtha** (assume average naphtha formula C₅H₁₂, M = 72 kg/kmol; steam M = 18 kg/kmol).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Steam flow.
$$
m_{steam} = 0.6 \cdot 20{,}000 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2:** Total inlet mass.
$$
m_{total} = 20{,}000 + 12{,}000 = 32{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert naphtha to kmol/h.
$$
F_{naphtha} = \frac{20{,}000}{72} = 277.78 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert steam to kmol/h.
$$
F_{steam} = \frac{12{,}000}{18} = 666.67 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 5:** Molar ratio.
$$
\dfrac{F_{steam}}{F_{naphtha}} = \frac{666.67}{277.78} = \boxed{2.4 \text{ mol steam / mol naphtha}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This molar ratio is the "dilution" parameter reported in many process simulators (Aspen, HYSYS). Refinery engineers tune it to control coking and residence time.

---

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — Octane Number vs. Pyrolysis Comparison

| Parameter | Octane Number (Fuel Quality) | Pyrolysis (Refinery Conversion) |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Domain** | Product specification | Process operation |
| **Primary metric** | Knock resistance (RON, MON) | Ethylene / propylene yield (wt%) |
| **Operative variable** | Molecular structure of fuel | COT, residence time, S/HC ratio |
| **Mechanism** | Engine combustion / radical pre-ignition | Free-radical chain (Rice–Herzfeld) |
| **Catalyst** | N/A (combustion is the test) | None (purely thermal) |
| **Operating T** | N/A | 750–900 °C |
| **Key product** | High-RON gasoline | Olefins (C₂H₄, C₃H₆) + aromatics |
| **Industry relevance** | Automotive sector | Petrochemical feedstock |
| **CO mapping (KTU 2024)** | CO3 — Fuel technology | CO3 — Refinery processes |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]
**Define Octane Number. Differentiate between RON and MON.**

**Model Answer:**

**Octane Number:** Octane number is a numerical measure (0–100 scale) of the **resistance of a gasoline fuel to engine knock (auto-ignition)**. It is defined as the percentage of isooctane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) by volume in a reference blend of isooctane and n-heptane that exhibits the same knocking intensity as the test fuel under standard CFR engine conditions.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | RON (Research Octane Number) | MON (Motor Octane Number) |
|--------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Engine speed | 600 rpm | 900 rpm |
| Inlet air temperature | 52 °C (mild) | 149 °C (hot, severe) |
| Severity | Milder conditions | Harsher conditions |
| Typical numerical value | **Higher** (≈ 95 for regular) | **Lower** (≈ 85 for same fuel) |
| Test standard | ASTM D2699 | ASTM D2700 |
| Use | High-speed / low-load driving | High-speed / high-load driving |

**Anti-Knock Index (AKI) = (RON + MON) / 2** is the average used for marketing in the U.S.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]
**What is pyrolysis in petroleum refining? Mention any four important products obtained.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pyrolysis (Steam Cracking):** Pyrolysis is a **non-catalytic, high-temperature thermal decomposition** of hydrocarbons (such as naphtha, ethane, or gas oil) carried out in the absence of oxygen and in the presence of **dilution steam**, at temperatures of **750–900 °C** and short residence times of **0.05–0.5 s**. The reaction proceeds via a **free-radical mechanism** (Rice–Herzfeld) and produces valuable lower-molecular-weight olefins and aromatics.

**Four Important Products:**
1. **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** — the most important; monomer for polyethylene, PVC, ethylene oxide.
2. **Propylene (C₃H₆)** — monomer for polypropylene, propylene oxide, acrylonitrile.
3. **Butadiene (1,3-C₄H₆)** — used in synthetic rubber (SBR, BR).
4. **Pyrolysis gasoline (py-gas)** — rich in **BTX aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylene); feed for aromatics complex.

> [!NOTE]
> Other by-products include **methane, hydrogen, fuel oil, and pyrolysis tar.**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Octane Number and Engine Knock
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **CFR engine test method** used for determining the octane number of gasoline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **relationship between octane number, molecular structure, and engine compression ratio**. Show how the **octane requirement** of an engine is established. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A

#### (a) CFR Engine Test Method (7 marks)

The **Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine** is the standard ASTM apparatus used to measure RON and MON. The procedure is:

1. **Test Fuel Loading:** A blend of isooctane and n-heptane of known composition is filled in the carburetor fuel bowl.
2. **Engine Operation:** The single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio engine is run at the prescribed speed (600 rpm for RON, 900 rpm for MON) and intake-air temperature (52 °C / 149 °C).
3. **Compression Ratio Variation:** The operator adjusts the **hand-wheel-controlled cylinder height**, varying the CR until a **standard knock intensity** is heard — detected by the **electronic knockmeter** coupled to a piezo-electric sensor.
4. **Reference Blending:** Test fuel is replaced by reference blends of isooctane + n-heptane. The CR is re-adjusted to reproduce the same knock intensity.
5. **Octane Number:** The volume % of isooctane in the matching reference blend is the **octane number** of the test fuel.

**Diagrammatic representation:**

```
    [CFR Engine]
        |
   [Knockmeter]
        |
   [Compression Ratio Hand-wheel]
        |
   [Standard Reference Blend Match]
        |
   [ON Reported]
```

A standard knock intensity corresponds to a **knockmeter reading of 55** under the prescribed conditions.

---

#### (b) Octane Number, Structure, and Compression Ratio (7 marks)

**Structure → Octane Number relationship:**

| Molecular Class | Octane Number Trend | Reason |
|-----------------|---------------------|--------|
| **Straight-chain n-alkanes** | **Decreases** with carbon number (n-C₇ = 0) | Long chains → low activation energy for radical pre-ignition |
| **Branched iso-paraffins** | **High** (e.g., isooctane = 100) | Branching raises the energy barrier to radical chain branching |
| **Olefins (alkenes)** | Moderate to high | C=C bond stabilizes radicals via resonance |
| **Naphthenes** | Moderate | Ring strain affects auto-ignition kinetics |
| **Aromatics** | **Very high** (toluene = 114) | Aromatic ring π-system resists auto-ignition |

**Compression Ratio → Octane Requirement relationship:**

For a spark-ignition engine, the **thermal efficiency** of the Otto cycle is:

$$
\eta = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{CR}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

Higher CR → higher $\eta$ → higher peak temperature and pressure at end of compression stroke. This increases the **tendency for auto-ignition of the end-gas** (the unburned mixture ahead of the flame front), causing **knock**. To suppress knock at higher CR, a fuel with **higher octane number** is required.

**Octane Requirement (OR) Determination:**
The OR of an engine is the **minimum octane number** that just prevents audible knock under the **most severe operating conditions** (WOT, 1500 rpm, 35 °C inlet air, base timing). Modern ECUs use knock sensors to **retard ignition timing** when knock is detected, allowing engines to run on fuels with somewhat lower octane than their "design OR" would suggest.

---

### Question B — Pyrolysis and Steam Cracking
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a flow diagram, describe the **steam cracking process for ethylene production from naphtha**, clearly showing the **radiant coil furnace, transfer line exchanger, and primary fractionator**. State the typical COT and S/HC ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **free-radical mechanism of pyrolysis** with reference to ethane cracking. Discuss the role of **steam dilution** and the effect of **COT and residence time on ethylene yield**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B

#### (a) Naphtha Steam Cracking — Process Description (7 marks)

**Feed:** Hydrocarbon naphtha (C₅–C₁₁, boiling range 30–200 °C).

**Process Flow:**

1. **Convective Section of Furnace:** Naphtha is first preheated to ~600 °C by recovering flue-gas heat.
2. **Steam Superheating:** Dilution steam is independently superheated to ~900 °C in the convective section.
3. **Mixing:** Preheated naphtha and superheated steam are mixed in a **cross-over pipe / mix manifold** to achieve a steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio (S/HC) of **0.3 – 0.9 kg steam per kg HC**.
4. **Radiant Coil Cracking:** The mixture enters long, narrow, high-alloy tubes (**Incoloy 800/HP-40**) hung vertically in the **radiant fire-box**, where external burners deliver heat by radiation. The gas reaches the **Coil Outlet Temperature (COT) of 800–900 °C** in milliseconds.
5. **Transfer Line Exchanger (TLE):** The hot cracked gas is **immediately quenched** (< 0.5 s residence) in a TLE to ~400 °C, generating high-pressure steam as a useful by-product. This quenches the radical reactions and "freezes" the product distribution.
6. **Primary Fractionator (Quench Oil Tower):** Cooled gases contact cooled pyrolysis fuel oil (PFO) reflux, where **fuel oil (heavy ends)** is condensed at the bottom and light gases pass overhead.
7. **Compression and Distillation Train:** The overhead gas is compressed in a 5-stage ethylene compressor, then sent through **acid-gas removal, demethanizer, deethanizer, and C₂-splitter** to yield **polymer-grade ethylene (99.95 %)** and **polymer-grade propylene (99.5 %)** as the final purified products.

**Typical operating values:**
- COT = **800–900 °C**
- S/HC = **0.3 – 0.9 kg/kg**
- Residence time = **0.05 – 0.5 s**
- Coil pressure = **slightly above 1 atm (1.5–2.5 barg)**

---

#### (b) Free-Radical Mechanism and Severity Parameters (7 marks)

**Rice–Herzfeld Mechanism (ethane pyrolysis example):**

1. **Initiation:** Homolytic cleavage of the C–C bond in ethane produces two methyl radicals.
   $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \xrightarrow{k_1, \Delta} 2 \, \text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$$

2. **Hydrogen abstraction (propagation step 1):** A methyl radical abstracts a hydrogen from another ethane molecule, forming methane and an ethyl radical.
   $$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5^{\bullet}$$

3. **β-Scission (propagation step 2):** The ethyl radical undergoes β-scission — the C–C bond one carbon away from the radical center breaks — yielding the desired **ethylene** and a hydrogen radical.
   $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_3} \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

4. **Chain-carrying regeneration:** The H• radical abstracts H from another ethane to regenerate H₂ and an ethyl radical.
   $$\text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5^{\bullet}$$

5. **Termination:** Two radicals (H• + H•, H• + C₂H₅•, or C₂H₅• + C₂H₅•) combine to yield stable products, ending the chain.

**Overall:** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2$

**Role of Steam Dilution:**
- **Reduces partial pressure** of hydrocarbons → suppresses bimolecular secondary reactions that lead to coke.
- **Improves selectivity** to ethylene (less recombination of radicals).
- **Provides thermal ballast** (high heat capacity) to flatten the temperature profile.
- **Gasifies deposited carbon** via the water-gas reaction: $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$.

**Effect of COT and Residence Time:**

| Variable | Direction of Change | Ethylene Yield | Coke / By-products |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|--------------------|
| **COT ↑** (higher severity) | Increase | **Increases** | Coke & methane also increase |
| **Residence time ↑** | Increase | **Decreases** (over-cracking) | More coke, more secondary reactions |
| **COT high + τ short** (milli-sec furnace) | Both ↑ | **Maximum ethylene selectivity** | Minimal coke |

**Design Rule:** Modern ethylene plants operate at **high COT and very short residence time** (≤ 0.1 s) to maximize ethylene yield while minimizing coke and methane formation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ON and CN:** Cetane Number is for *diesel* (related to ignition delay), Octane Number is for *gasoline* (related to knock resistance). Marking ON for a diesel question = 0 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in mass balance:** Always state **kg/h** or **kmol/h**; missing units = −1 mark per KTU norms.
> 3. **Conflating Pyrolysis with FCC:** Pyrolysis = thermal, no catalyst, 800 °C, gives olefins. FCC = catalytic, 500 °C, gives gasoline. The examiner deducts 2 marks for this confusion.
> 4. **Wrong stoichiometry in C–C scission:** The β-scission occurs at the bond **β to the radical**, not at the radical center itself. Drawing a wrong mechanism loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the S/HC ratio in the cracker flow diagram:** This is a "must-mention" parameter in the KTU marking scheme.
> 6. **Reporting RON/MON without stating test conditions (rpm, T):** Marks are deducted for vagueness.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3)**

### Octane Number
- ON is a **dimensionless index (0–100+)** quantifying **knock resistance** of spark-ignition fuels.
- **Isooctane (2,2,4-TMP) = 100**; **n-heptane = 0** by definition.
- **RON** uses mild conditions (600 rpm, 52 °C); **MON** uses harsh conditions (900 rpm, 149 °C).
- **AKI = (RON + MON)/2** — the U.S. pump rating.
- **Branched alkanes, aromatics, olefins** → high ON. **Straight n-alkanes** → low ON.
- **Blending rule:** $ON_{blend} = \sum x_i \cdot ON_i$ (linear for primary reference fuels; non-linear for real components).
- **Anti-knock additives:** TEL (legacy), MTBE, ethanol, MMT, aromatic amines.
- **Higher compression ratio → higher thermal efficiency → higher octane requirement.**

### Pyrolysis (Steam Cracking)
- **Definition:** Non-catalytic, high-T, no-O₂, free-radical thermal cracking in presence of dilution steam.
- **Operating window:** COT = **800–900 °C**, residence time = **0.05–0.5 s**, S/HC = **0.3–0.9 kg/kg**, P ≈ 1 atm.
- **Mechanism:** Rice–Herzfeld (initiation → H-abstraction → β-scission → termination).
- **Key products:** **Ethylene, propylene, butadiene, BTX (py-gas), H₂, methane, fuel oil.**
- **Steam roles:** diluent, coke suppressant, thermal ballast, carbon gasifier.
- **High COT + short τ** = best ethylene selectivity (millisecond-furnace design).
- **Furnace construction:** Convective preheat + Radiant coil + TLE (quench) + Primary fractionator.
- **Distillation train:** Compressor → Acid gas removal → Demethanizer → Deethanizer → C₂ splitter.

### KTU Exam Edge
- Always state **units** in material-balance problems.
- Always draw a **flow diagram** for the steam cracker.
- Always **quote numbers** (COT, S/HC, residence time) — never describe in qualitative terms only.
- Always **define isooctane and n-heptane** before defining octane number.
- Always mention **Rice–Herzfeld** when describing pyrolysis mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalytic Cracking Process - Fluidized Bed and Moving Bed Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Catalytic Cracking Process - Fluidized Bed and Moving Bed Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Catalytic Cracking** is a petroleum refining process in which high molecular weight, low-value hydrocarbon fractions (such as vacuum gas oil, atmospheric residue, or heavy distillates) are broken down into smaller, more useful molecules (gasoline, LPG, olefins) in the presence of a **solid acid catalyst** at elevated temperatures, without the addition of hydrogen.

In the catalytic cracking process, the C–C bonds of long-chain paraffins, naphthenes, and alkyl-aromatics are cleaved over an acidic catalyst surface via the **carbonium ion (carbocation) mechanism**. The two principal industrial implementations demanded by the KTU 2024 Scheme Chemical Technology syllabus (Module 3) are:

1. **Fluidized Bed Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** – also called the *modern cat cracker*, uses finely powdered catalyst suspended in a fluidized state by vaporized feed and fluidizing gas.
2. **Moving Bed Catalytic Cracking (TCC – Thermofor Catalytic Cracking)** – uses bead or pellet catalyst that physically moves downward through the reactor under gravity, then is pneumatically lifted back to the top for regeneration.

The generic, stoichiometric representation of a catalytic cracking reaction is:

$$
\text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\text{Acid Catalyst, } \Delta} \text{C}_{m}\text{H}_{2m} + \text{C}_{p}\text{H}_{2p+2}
$$

where a heavy hydrocarbon ($n$) is cracked into a lighter olefin ($m$) and a lighter paraffin ($p$). Typical operating envelopes for both reactor types are given below.

| Parameter | FCC (Fluidized Bed) | TCC (Moving Bed) |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor Temperature | **480 – 540 °C** | **450 – 500 °C** |
| Reactor Pressure | **1 – 3 atm (gauge)** | **1 – 2 atm (gauge)** |
| Catalyst | Zeolite Y (rare-earth exchanged) | Amorphous SiO₂–Al₂O₃ pellets |
| Catalyst Size | **20 – 80 µm powder** | **3 – 5 mm beads/pellets** |
| Catalyst/Oil Ratio | **5 – 10 (w/w)** | **1.5 – 3.0 (w/w)** |
| Space Velocity (WHSV) | **8 – 25 h⁻¹** | **1 – 3 h⁻¹** |
| Coke on Spent Catalyst | **0.5 – 1.5 wt%** | **2 – 4 wt%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, students must clearly distinguish between *reactor mechanics* (how catalyst contacts feed) and *regeneration mechanics* (how coke is burned off). FCC uses a *riser* + *regenerator* loop with catalyst continuously circulating as a fluid. TCC uses a *moving packed bed* reactor with catalyst descending by gravity and a separate *Thermofor kiln* regenerator.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy city intersection:

- **Thermal Cracking** (the older, Module-2 topic) is like **setting a stack of firewood ablaze** — the heat breaks the logs randomly, producing lots of charcoal, gas, and irregular chunks. There is *no control* over which bonds break, so the product distribution is messy and heavy with gas + coke.
- **Catalytic Cracking (Moving Bed – TCC)** is like a **well-managed escalator carrying bowling balls past a set of scissors**. Each bowling ball (heavy oil molecule) is held briefly in position (on a stationary bead) while the scissor blades (acidic catalytic sites) snip it into smaller, predictable pieces. The balls then continue down the escalator to be lifted back up after being "cleaned" (regenerated).
- **Catalytic Cracking (Fluidized Bed – FCC)** is like a **whirlpool of tiny magnets in a stream of water**. The feed oil enters from below and meets a cloud of micro-catalyst particles that behave like a fluid. Each molecule briefly collides with a catalytic site, is cracked, then escapes upward. The catalyst drops back down, gets stripped, and rejoins the whirlpool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a catalyst at all?** The acid catalyst lowers the activation energy for C–C bond scission by stabilizing the **carbonium-ion intermediate**. This means the reaction runs at a *lower temperature* (≈ 500 °C vs. ≈ 800 °C for thermal cracking), producing *less gas, less coke*, and a much *higher iso-paraffin + aromatic content* in gasoline — exactly the components that boost **octane number**.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Designs? – Physical Reasoning

The choice between FCC and TCC is essentially a **heat-transfer + mass-transfer optimization problem**:

- **TCC (Moving Bed)**: Easy to design mechanically, simple gravity-driven catalyst flow, well-suited to *early-generation* amorphous catalysts that needed longer contact time. The trade-off is poor temperature control and large equipment.
- **FCC (Fluidized Bed)**: The fluidization creates *enormous surface area for heat exchange*, allows *near-isothermal operation* (the catalyst acts as a massive heat sink), and the short contact time in the riser suppresses secondary over-cracking. This is why FCC dominates > 70 % of worldwide catalytic cracking capacity today.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for Recall)

- **Feedstock** – Heavy vacuum gas oil (VGO), atmospheric residue, demetallized oil (DAO), or hydrocracker bottom.
- **Riser** – The vertical pipe in FCC where feed meets hot regenerated catalyst; cracking occurs in ≈ 2 – 4 seconds.
- **Standpipe** – Vertical pipe that returns regenerated catalyst to the riser base.
- **Regenerator** – Fluidized-bed vessel where coke is burned off with air, restoring catalyst activity.
- **Stripper** – Vessel where steam is used to displace entrained hydrocarbons from spent catalyst before regeneration.
- **Catalyst Circulation Rate** – Mass of catalyst moving between reactor and regenerator per unit time (typically 5 – 50 ton/min in a commercial FCCU).
- **Carbon-on-Catalyst (CRC)** – Mass percent of coke on catalyst; controlled to ≈ 0.05 wt% on regenerated catalyst.
- **Conversion** – Percentage of feed converted to products boiling below 221 °C (gasoline + lighter).

---

## 1.5 Carbonium Ion (Carbocation) Mechanism – Quick Primer

The catalyst surface (Brønsted acid sites on zeolite or amorphous silica-alumina) **donates a proton (H⁺)** to an olefin or paraffin, creating a positively charged carbonium ion. This unstable intermediate then **β-scission** (cleaves the C–C bond two carbons away from the charge) to yield a smaller olefin and a new carbonium ion that propagates the chain.

$$
\text{R–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{R–CH}_2\text{–}\overset{+}{\text{C}}\text{H–CH}_3 \rightarrow \text{R–CH}_2^+ + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH–CH}_3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism governs product slate:** The carbonium ion route preferentially produces **iso-paraffins and aromatics**, both of which have high octane numbers. This is the *fundamental* reason catalytic cracking replaced thermal cracking in the 1940s.

---

## 1.6 Common Catalysts and Their Properties

| Property | Amorphous SiO₂–Al₂O₃ (TCC era) | Zeolite Y (FCC era) | Ultra-Stable Y (USY, modern FCC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Area | 100 – 500 m²/g | 600 – 800 m²/g | 700 – 900 m²/g |
| Acidity (Brønsted sites) | Moderate | **Very High** | High + controlled |
| Attrition Resistance | High (pellets) | Low (needs matrix) | Low (needs matrix) |
| Coke Selectivity | Higher | **Lower** | Lowest |
| Used in | TCC (moving bed) | Older FCC units | Modern FCC (Riser cracking) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Zeolite Y** is the workhorse of FCC. Its rigid crystalline cage (pore size ≈ 7.4 Å, **supercage of 12 Å**) restricts bulky molecules from entering — a phenomenon called *shape selectivity* — which suppresses coke formation and favours gasoline-range products.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Function and Operating Logic

The catalytic cracking plant performs **three coupled functions** in a single integrated process:

1. **Reaction** – Vaporized feed contacts hot catalyst; C–C bonds break.
2. **Stripping** – Steam removes entrained hydrocarbons from the spent catalyst.
3. **Regeneration** – Air burns off the coke; catalyst temperature rises by ≈ 150 – 200 °C, which is the *thermal engine* of the entire process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The catalyst is the heat carrier.** Spent catalyst is much colder than regenerated catalyst. By continuously circulating hot regenerated catalyst into the riser, the *sensible heat of the catalyst* provides all the energy required to vaporize the feed and drive the endothermic cracking reaction. This eliminates the need for an external fired heater in the reactor train — a key advantage of FCC.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic – FCC (Fluidized Bed)

The following bullet logic follows the **physical path of the catalyst** and **physical path of the oil** simultaneously.

### Path of the Oil (Feed)
- **Step 1:** Preheated feed (≈ 300 °C) is injected at the base of the *riser* through *feed nozzles*.
- **Step 2:** Hot regenerated catalyst (≈ 700 – 740 °C) meets the feed; oil vaporizes instantly and *fluidizes* the catalyst powder.
- **Step 3:** Cracking reactions proceed as the catalyst-oil mixture travels upward in ≈ 2 – 4 s.
- **Step 4:** The mixture discharges into the *reactor vessel*; product vapours are separated by *cyclones*.
- **Step 5:** Vapours go to the *fractionator* for separation into gasoline, LCO, HCO, slurry, LPG, and fuel gas.
- **Step 6:** Spent catalyst (with coke) drops into the *stripper* where steam displaces residual hydrocarbons.

### Path of the Catalyst
- **Step A:** Stripped catalyst flows down the *stripper standpipe* to the *regenerator*.
- **Step B:** Air is injected at the base; coke burns in *fluidized-bed combustion* at 650 – 750 °C:
$$
\text{C (coke)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(and partially CO)}
$$
- **Step C:** Flue gas (containing CO, CO₂, SO_x, NO_x) goes through *cyclones* and a *CO boiler* (or catalyst cooler in modern units).
- **Step D:** Hot regenerated catalyst flows through the *regenerator standpipe* and a *slide valve* back to the *riser base*, completing the loop.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic – TCC (Moving Bed)

The Thermofor Catalytic Cracking (TCC) is a *gravity-driven* moving bed reactor.

### Reactor
- **Step 1:** Vaporized feed enters the reactor and flows *upward* through a *descending* bed of catalyst pellets.
- **Step 2:** Pellets move downward by gravity at a controlled rate (≈ 1 – 3 m/h).
- **Step 3:** Cracking occurs as vapour contacts the stationary-then-moving bed.
- **Step 4:** Cracked vapours exit the top of the reactor to the fractionator.

### Catalyst Transport
- **Step A:** Spent pellets leave the bottom of the reactor and enter a *bucket elevator* or *gas-lift line*.
- **Step B:** Catalyst is lifted to the top of the *regeneration kiln*.
- **Step C:** In the kiln, pellets move *downward* while hot air flows *upward*; coke is burned at ≈ 550 – 600 °C.
- **Step D:** Hot regenerated catalyst discharges from the bottom of the kiln and is lifted back to the top of the reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **TCC has a definite engineering advantage** in the 1940s–1960s: it was the *first* catalytic cracking process to achieve continuous catalyst regeneration. Its disadvantages (large equipment, poor temperature control, mechanical complexity of elevators) caused it to be supplanted by FCC by the 1970s. Most TCC units have been **decommissioned or converted** to *Houdresid* (residue cracking) or shut down.

---

## 2.4 Heat Integration – The Catalyst as Heat Carrier

The heat required for cracking is provided entirely by the catalyst. The fundamental heat balance around the **riser + regenerator** loop is:

$$
Q_{\text{regen}} = Q_{\text{crack}} + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

where:
- $Q_{\text{regen}}$ = heat released by coke combustion
- $Q_{\text{crack}}$ = heat absorbed by endothermic cracking
- $Q_{\text{loss}}$ = heat lost to surroundings + stack gas

The **catalyst circulation rate ($G_s$)** is set by this energy balance:

$$
G_s = \frac{Q_{\text{crack}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{\text{reg}} - T_{\text{exit}})}
$$

This is the *single most important design equation* in FCC — a higher $G_s$ means more catalyst inventory, more heat, higher conversion.

---

## 2.5 Fluidization Fundamentals (For FCC)

FCC catalyst powder is *Geldart Group A* powder (aeratable, 20 – 80 µm). At low gas velocity, the bed is a *packed bed*. As velocity increases, drag equals gravity at the **minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$)**. Beyond this, the bed expands and behaves like a fluid.

The **Ergun equation** is used to predict $U_{mf}$:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \, \mu \, (1-\epsilon_m)^2}{\epsilon_m^3 \, d_p^2} \, U + \frac{1.75 \, \rho_g \, (1-\epsilon_m)}{\epsilon_m^3 \, d_p} \, U^2
$$

At minimum fluidization, $\Delta P = (1 - \epsilon_m)(\rho_s - \rho_g) g L$, which gives:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{150 \, \mu} \cdot \frac{\epsilon_m^3}{1-\epsilon_m} \quad \text{(laminar limit)}
$$

A widely used empirical shortcut for Group A powders:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{d_p}{1000} \quad \text{(with } d_p \text{ in µm, } U_{mf} \text{ in m/s, very rough)}
$$

The **operating gas velocity ($U_0$)** is typically chosen as:

$$
U_0 = (1.5 \text{ to } 5) \, U_{mf}
$$

to ensure good fluidization without excessive entrainment.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all KTU-exam-relevant formulas** for this topic. Memorize the highlighted ones; the rest are reference.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P_{\text{bed}} = (1-\epsilon)(\rho_s - \rho_g) g L$ | Bed pressure drop (bed supports weight) | Pa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\epsilon)^2}{\epsilon^3 d_p^2} U + \dfrac{1.75 \rho_g (1-\epsilon)}{\epsilon^3 d_p} U^2$ | Ergun equation (full form) | Pa/m |
| 3 | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{150 \mu} \cdot \dfrac{\epsilon_{mf}^3}{1-\epsilon_{mf}}$ | Minimum fluidization velocity (laminar) | m/s |
| 4 | $G_s = \dfrac{Q_{\text{crack}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} (T_{\text{reg}} - T_{\text{exit}})}$ | Catalyst circulation rate | kg/s |
| 5 | $X = \dfrac{F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{HCO}}}{F_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Cracking conversion (mass %) | % |
| 6 | $Y_{\text{gasoline}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{gasoline}}}{F_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Gasoline yield | % |
| 7 | $\text{C/O ratio} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{cat}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}}$ | Catalyst-to-Oil mass ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $r_{\text{coke}} = k \cdot C_{\text{coke}}^{n}$ | Coke formation kinetics (power-law) | kg/(kg·h) |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{RON}_{\text{product}}}{\text{RON}_{\text{feed}}}$ | Octane enhancement factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{riser}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{riser}}}{U_{\text{riser}}}$ | Riser residence time (typically 2 – 4 s) | s |
| 11 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} = \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} C_{p,\text{cat}} \Delta T$ | Sensible heat carried by regenerated catalyst | kW |
| 12 | $\text{CCR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke burned}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{oil processed}}} \times 100$ | Catalytic carbon requirement / coke yield | wt% |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTE on Table Notation:** All pipe symbols used in the formulas (e.g. $\vert \Delta P \vert$) are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command so the markdown table is not broken.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Use of Catalytic Cracking |
|---|---|
| **Refineries (worldwide)** | Produces ≈ 45 % of all gasoline; ≈ 35 % of all propylene |
| **Petrochemicals** | Source of C₃, C₄ olefins (FCC light gases) for alkylation, MTBE, polyolefins |
| **Aviation Fuel Blending** | LCO (Light Cycle Oil) blended into jet/diesel after hydrotreating |
| **Plastics & Polymers** | Propylene → polypropylene, ethylene → polyethylene |
| **Environmental** | Modern FCCUs add SO_x, NO_x, and CO reduction systems (SCR, CO promoters) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in 2024+:** With the rise of electric vehicles reducing gasoline demand, refineries are **re-purposing FCCUs toward maximum olefin (propylene/ethylene) production** — called *Olefins-Enhanced FCC* or *petrochemical FCC*. This is a hot topic in chemical engineering interviews.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **full derivations** for the three most important KTU-exam-grade calculations in catalytic cracking. No steps are skipped; every line of mathematics is paired with an explanatory comment.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Catalyst Circulation Rate from Heat Balance

### Statement
A commercial FCCU processes **20 000 kg/h** of feed (VGO, $C_{p,\text{oil}}$ = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) that must be heated from 300 °C to 520 °C, vaporized ($\lambda_{\text{vap}}$ = 250 kJ/kg), and raised to reactor temperature. The endothermic heat of cracking is ≈ **300 kJ/kg of feed**. The regenerated catalyst enters at 720 °C and leaves the riser at 510 °C. $C_{p,\text{cat}}$ = 1.1 kJ/kg·K. **Determine the catalyst circulation rate.**

### Step 1 – Total Heat Demand of the Feed

$$
Q_{\text{feed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \left[ C_{p,\text{oil}} (T_{\text{reactor}} - T_{\text{feed,in}}) + \lambda_{\text{vap}} + \Delta H_{\text{crack}} \right]
$$

Plug in the values:

$$
Q_{\text{feed}} = 20000 \left[ 2.5 \times (520 - 300) + 250 + 300 \right]
$$

$$
Q_{\text{feed}} = 20000 \left[ 2.5 \times 220 + 550 \right]
$$

$$
Q_{\text{feed}} = 20000 \left[ 550 + 550 \right] = 20000 \times 1100 = 2.2 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

### Step 2 – Heat Available per kg of Catalyst

$$
q_{\text{cat}} = C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{\text{reg}} - T_{\text{exit}})
$$

$$
q_{\text{cat}} = 1.1 \times (720 - 510) = 1.1 \times 210 = 231 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 3 – Equate and Solve

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = \frac{Q_{\text{feed}}}{q_{\text{cat}}} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^7}{231} = 9.524 \times 10^4 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \approx 95\,240 \text{ kg/h} \approx 95.2 \text{ ton/h}}
$$

### Step 4 – Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio

$$
\text{C/O} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{cat}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}} = \frac{95\,240}{20\,000} = 4.76
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This C/O ratio is at the *low end* of FCC operation (typical 5 – 10). A higher conversion requirement would push C/O toward 8 – 10. KTU students should remember: **higher C/O → higher conversion, higher coke, higher regenerator temperature**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Fluidization Velocity via Ergun Equation

### Statement
An FCC catalyst has the following properties:

- Particle diameter $d_p = 60 \,\mu\text{m} = 60 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Particle density $\rho_s = 1500 \,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Gas density $\rho_g = 1.2 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ (hot flue gas at 700 °C, 1 atm)
- Gas viscosity $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- Bed voidage at minimum fluidization $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.45$

**Determine $U_{mf}$ using the laminar-limit Ergun equation.**

### Step 1 – Identify the Flow Regime

First compute the dimensionless Reynolds number at an estimated $U_{mf}$. Assume $U_{mf} \approx 0.005$ m/s:

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{d_p \, \rho_g \, U}{\mu} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2 \times 0.005}{3.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-7}}{3.5 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.01
$$

Since $\text{Re} \ll 20$, the **laminar (viscous) limit** of the Ergun equation is valid.

### Step 2 – Apply the Viscous-Term-Only Ergun Equation

At $U = U_{mf}$, the bed pressure drop equals the buoyant weight per unit area:

$$
(1 - \epsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_g) g = \frac{150 \mu (1 - \epsilon_{mf})^2}{\epsilon_{mf}^3 d_p^2} U_{mf}
$$

### Step 3 – Solve Algebraically for $U_{mf}$

Rearranging for $U_{mf}$:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{150 \mu} \cdot \frac{\epsilon_{mf}^3}{1 - \epsilon_{mf}}
$$

Substitute the numbers:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{(60 \times 10^{-6})^2 (1500 - 1.2)(9.81)}{150 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot \frac{(0.45)^3}{1 - 0.45}
$$

### Step 4 – Numerator of the First Fraction

$$
(60 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
3.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 1498.8 \times 9.81 = 3.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 14\,702 = 5.293 \times 10^{-5}
$$

### Step 5 – Denominator of the First Fraction

$$
150 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-5} = 5.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 6 – First Fraction

$$
\frac{5.293 \times 10^{-5}}{5.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.008 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 7 – Second Fraction (Voidage Term)

$$
\epsilon_{mf}^3 = (0.45)^3 = 0.0911
$$

$$
\frac{0.0911}{1 - 0.45} = \frac{0.0911}{0.55} = 0.1657
$$

### Step 8 – Multiply Both

$$
U_{mf} = 1.008 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.1657 = 1.671 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{mf} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 1.67 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

### Step 9 – Sanity Check with the Quick Rule

The quick rule says $U_{mf} \approx d_p / 1000$ (with $d_p$ in µm):

$$
U_{mf} \approx 60 / 1000 = 0.06 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(this overestimates because the rule is for air at 25 °C)}
$$

Our calculated value of 1.67 mm/s is reasonable for *hot low-density flue gas*. In a real riser with steam/hydrocarbon vapour, $U_0$ is held at 5 – 25 m/s, i.e. **> 1000 × $U_{mf}$**, which is the *fast-fluidization* regime characteristic of FCC risers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion and Material Balance Around the Reactor-Regenerator

### Statement
An FCCU processes **15 000 kg/h** of VGO feed. The cracked product distribution is:

- Dry gas (C₁ + C₂): 4 wt%
- LPG (C₃ + C₄): 14 wt%
- Gasoline (C₅ – 221 °C): 48 wt%
- LCO (221 – 343 °C): 18 wt%
- HCO (heavy cycle oil, > 343 °C): 12 wt%
- Coke: 4 wt%

**Tasks:** (a) Calculate **conversion**; (b) Calculate the **coke burn rate** if air is supplied at 1.2 × stoichiometric.

### Part (a) – Conversion

Conversion is defined as the mass fraction of feed converted to products boiling **below 343 °C** (i.e., everything except HCO and coke):

$$
X = 100\% - (\text{HCO} + \text{Coke}) = 100 - 12 - 4 = 84\%
$$

Or using the formula:

$$
X = \frac{F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{HCO}}}{F_{\text{feed}}} \times 100
$$

If $F_{\text{HCO}} = 0.12 \times 15000 = 1800$ kg/h:

$$
X = \frac{15000 - 1800}{15000} \times 100 = \frac{13200}{15000} \times 100 = 88\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Two definitions of conversion exist in the literature.** KTU examiners accept either the *221 °C cutpoint* (gasoline + lighter, conversion ≈ 66 %) or the *343 °C cutpoint* (LCO + lighter, conversion ≈ 84 – 88 %). Always state which you are using!

### Part (b) – Coke Burn Rate

Coke mass rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 0.04 \times 15000 = 600 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Assume coke is pure carbon (C = 12, O₂ = 32, CO₂ = 44). Combustion stoichiometry:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2
$$

Oxygen required (stoichiometric):

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{O}_2,\text{stoich}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \times \frac{32}{12} = 600 \times 2.667 = 1600 \text{ kg/h}
$$

At 1.2 × stoichiometric:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{O}_2,\text{actual}} = 1.2 \times 1600 = 1920 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Air is 23.2 wt% O₂ by mass:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \frac{1920}{0.232} = 8276 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Flue gas produced (mostly N₂):

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{flue}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} + \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 8276 + 600 = 8876 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 600 \text{ kg/h}, \quad \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \approx 8276 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 1.2 × stoichiometric?** In practice, excess air ensures **complete combustion of CO to CO₂** in the regenerator. If air is lean, CO slips out unburned — wasting heat and creating pollution. Modern FCCUs use **CO combustion promoters** (Pt, Pd) to allow operation at slightly less-than-stoichiometric O₂ while still fully oxidizing CO.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| KTU Question Type | Use This Formula(s) | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|
| "Calculate $G_s$ given heat duty" | $G_s = Q / [C_{p,\text{cat}} \Delta T]$ | Forgetting coke combustion heat (~30 %) |
| "Find $U_{mf}$" | Ergun (laminar) for FCC catalyst | Using turbulent form for Re < 20 |
| "Conversion" | $X = (F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{unconv}}) / F_{\text{feed}}$ | Confusing 221 °C and 343 °C cutpoints |
| "Coke burn air requirement" | Stoichiometry $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ | Forgetting excess-air factor |
| "C/O ratio" | $\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} / \dot{m}_{\text{oil}}$ | Mixing up catalyst basis (regen vs. spent) |
| "Octane number of product" | Empirically: RON ≈ 90 + 0.2 × (1 + 0.4 × (C/O)) | No — use experimental correlations |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between thermal cracking and catalytic cracking in terms of mechanism, temperature, and product distribution.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Feature | Thermal Cracking | Catalytic Cracking |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Free-radical chain scission | Carbonium-ion (acid-catalyzed) |
| **Temperature** | 800 – 900 °C | 480 – 540 °C (FCC) |
| **Pressure** | High (10 – 70 atm) | Low (1 – 3 atm) |
| **Catalyst** | None | Zeolite / silica-alumina |
| **Major Products** | Olefins, gas, gasoline | Iso-paraffins, aromatics, gasoline |
| **Coke Yield** | High | Low (continuous regeneration) |
| **Octane Number** | Moderate | High |

**Marking scheme:** Mechanism (1), Temperature + Pressure (1), Product distribution (1).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages of fluidized bed catalytic cracking over moving bed catalytic cracking.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

1. **Better temperature control** – catalyst acts as a massive internal heat sink; near-isothermal riser (1 mark).
2. **Higher conversion per pass** (70 – 85 %) due to optimum contact time and continuous catalyst regeneration (1 mark).
3. **Higher gasoline yield + higher octane number** because of zeolite catalysts and short riser contact time (1 mark).

*(For full marks, students may also add: continuous catalyst regeneration eliminates shutdowns, lower coke on catalyst, and ability to crack heavier feeds including residue.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B carries 14 marks. The standard split is **7 + 7** for two sub-parts. Two alternative questions are given; students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Explain in detail the Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC) process with a neat flow diagram. Discuss (a) the riser reactor design, (b) the regenerator design, and (c) the role of the catalyst as a heat carrier.**

#### Part (a) – Riser Reactor Design (7 marks)

The **riser** is the heart of the modern FCCU. It is a vertical pipe, typically 30 – 45 m tall, with diameter 0.8 – 1.5 m, where the residence time is held to **2 – 4 seconds** to minimize secondary over-cracking and dry-gas make.

**Key design features:**

1. **Feed injection nozzles** at the base atomize preheated VGO (300 °C) into fine droplets. Modern designs use multiple nozzles to ensure uniform radial distribution (1 mark).
2. **Catalyst feed** from the regenerated-catalyst standpipe enters through a *Y-shaped* junction or a *caterpillar* lift, mixing intimately with the oil (1 mark).
3. **Reaction temperature** is held at 510 – 535 °C by adjusting the *catalyst circulation rate* and *regenerator temperature* (1 mark).
4. **Vapor superficial velocity** at the top of the riser is 15 – 25 m/s, ensuring **fast-fluidization** regime (1 mark).
5. **Catalyst slip factor** (catalyst velocity / gas velocity) is 1.5 – 2.5; the catalyst moves slightly slower than the gas (0.5 mark).
6. **Rapid separation** at the riser top via a *closed cyclone* or *riser termination device* minimises post-riser cracking (0.5 mark).
7. **Material of construction** is carbon steel with a refractory lining (where temperature exceeds 540 °C) (0.5 mark).
8. **Pressure drop** across the riser is typically 0.3 – 0.5 bar, providing the driving force for product flow to the fractionator (0.5 mark).

**Heat balance around the riser:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} C_{p,\text{cat}} (T_{\text{reg,in}} - T_{\text{riser,out}}) = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \left[ C_{p,\text{oil}} \Delta T + \lambda_{\text{vap}} + \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \right]
$$

#### Part (b) – Regenerator Design (7 marks)

The regenerator restores catalyst activity by **burning coke with air** in a fluidized bed at 680 – 740 °C.

**Design features:**

1. **Fluidized-bed vessel** of diameter 6 – 12 m, lined with refractory (1 mark).
2. **Air distributor** at the base (sintered metal, pipe grid, or tuyere type) provides uniform fluidization (0.5 mark).
3. **Combustion** follows $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ ($\Delta H = -393.5$ kJ/mol); typically 90 – 95 % of coke is fully combusted, with the rest as CO (1 mark).
4. **Cyclones** at the top capture entrained catalyst fines (1 – 10 µm) and return them via *diplegs* (1 mark).
5. **CO boiler or catalyst cooler** – modern units may include an internal *catalyst cooler* to remove surplus heat (0.5 mark).
6. **Regenerated-catalyst standpipe** with a *slide valve* controls catalyst flow rate into the riser (1 mark).
7. **Typical regenerator temperature**: 700 – 730 °C; **O₂ in flue gas**: 1 – 3 vol% (1 mark).
8. **Air requirement** = 8 – 12 kg air / kg coke (with 10 – 20 % excess); **coke burn rate** = 1 – 10 ton/h in a 50 000 BPD unit (0.5 mark).
9. **Catalyst residence time** in regenerator is 5 – 15 minutes; **catalyst inventory** is 50 – 200 tons for a typical unit (0.5 mark).

**Heat released by coke combustion** (per kg of catalyst):

$$
q_{\text{regen}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{cat}}} \times \Delta H_{\text{comb}} \times \eta_{\text{comb}}
$$

#### Part (c) – Catalyst as Heat Carrier (covered in 7 marks of sub-parts)

The catalyst is the *only* heat source for vaporizing the feed and driving the endothermic cracking. The energy balance dictates catalyst circulation:

$$
G_s = \frac{Q_{\text{crack}} + Q_{\text{vap}} + Q_{\text{sens}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} (T_{\text{reg}} - T_{\text{spent}})}
$$

Typical $G_s$ values: **5 – 50 ton/min** for a 50 000 – 100 000 BPD FCCU.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Explain the moving bed catalytic cracking (TCC – Thermofor Catalytic Cracking) process with a neat flow diagram. Discuss its (a) operating principle, (b) reactor and regenerator design, and (c) advantages and disadvantages compared to FCC.**

#### Part (a) – Operating Principle (7 marks)

The Thermofor Catalytic Cracking (TCC) process, developed by **Houdry** and commercialized by **Socony-Vacuum** in the 1940s, was the **first continuous catalytic cracking process** to use a moving bed of catalyst pellets.

**Process flow:**

1. Preheated hydrocarbon feed (≈ 450 °C) enters the **bottom** of the reactor and flows **upward** through a packed bed of catalyst pellets (1 mark).
2. Catalyst pellets (3 – 5 mm diameter, amorphous silica-alumina) flow **downward** by gravity at a controlled rate (≈ 1 – 3 m/h) (1 mark).
3. Cracking occurs in the gas-solid contact zone. Vapour residence time is 30 – 90 s, much longer than FCC (1 mark).
4. Cracked vapours exit the top of the reactor and are sent to the fractionator (1 mark).
5. Spent catalyst (with 2 – 4 wt% coke) leaves the bottom and is lifted mechanically (bucket elevator) or pneumatically (gas lift) to the top of the regenerator (1 mark).
6. In the regenerator (called the *Thermofor kiln*), hot air flows upward while catalyst pellets descend; coke is burned at 550 – 600 °C (1 mark).
7. Regenerated catalyst (≈ 600 °C) is lifted back to the top of the reactor hopper; the loop continues (1 mark).

#### Part (b) – Reactor and Regenerator Design (7 marks)

**Reactor design:**

- **Vessel type**: vertical cylindrical shell, 4 – 8 m diameter, 15 – 25 m tall (1 mark).
- **Catalyst bed**: 4 – 6 m deep moving bed (1 mark).
- **Feed distribution**: perforated plate or distributor at the bottom; multiple feed inlets for uniform radial flow (0.5 mark).
- **Operating pressure**: 1 – 2 atm(g) (0.5 mark).
- **Vapour superficial velocity**: 0.3 – 1.0 m/s (0.5 mark).
- **Temperature control**: poor — heat must be removed via inter-stage coolers; the moving bed cannot be cooled *internally* as effectively as a fluidized bed (0.5 mark).
- **Catalyst pellet attrition**: low — pellets are mechanically robust (0.5 mark).
- **Material of construction**: carbon steel with internal refractory lining (0.5 mark).

**Regenerator (Kiln) design:**

- **Type**: vertical refractory-lined shaft, 5 – 8 m diameter, 20 – 30 m tall (1 mark).
- **Air flow**: co-current or counter-current to catalyst; 5 – 10 kg air per kg coke (0.5 mark).
- **Coke burn temperature**: 550 – 600 °C — *lower* than FCC regenerator, which limits throughput (0.5 mark).
- **Heat recovery**: flue gas passes through a *waste-heat boiler* (0.5 mark).
- **Catalyst residence time**: 30 – 60 min (0.5 mark).
- **Catalyst handling**: bucket elevators with 50 – 200 buckets, capacity 50 – 200 ton/h catalyst (0.5 mark).

#### Part (c) – Advantages & Disadvantages vs. FCC (covered within marks)

**Advantages of TCC:**
- Mechanically simple; low catalyst attrition.
- Can handle feeds with high metal content (Ni, V) better than early FCC catalysts.
- No fluidization instabilities (channeling, slugging).

**Disadvantages of TCC:**
- Poor temperature control → lower conversion per pass (50 – 65 %).
- High equipment cost (large vessels, elevators).
- Cannot use modern zeolite catalysts (which are fine powders).
- High residence time → more secondary cracking → more coke and dry gas.
- Most TCC units have been **decommissioned or converted** to other processes (Houdresid, asphalt processing).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the flow direction of catalyst vs. vapour in TCC** (–2 marks): In TCC the *catalyst moves down* and the *vapour moves up* (counter-current). In FCC the *catalyst and vapour both move up* in the riser (co-current).
> 2. **Forgetting the catalyst acts as a heat carrier in FCC** (–1 mark): Many students describe FCC as a heater + reactor, missing the elegant heat-integration through catalyst circulation. Always write the catalyst heat-balance equation.
> 3. **Mixing up cutpoints for conversion** (–2 marks): 221 °C = gasoline cut; 343 °C = total conversion. State which you are using.
> 4. **Writing 'FCC uses moving bed' or 'TCC uses fluidized bed'** (–3 marks, instant): This is a factual error that examiners flag heavily.
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled flow diagram** (–2 to –3 marks): KTU ESE requires a 'neat flow diagram'. Omitting the regenerator or stripper loses significant marks.
> 6. **Skipping the carbonium-ion mechanism** (–1 mark): Even in a process question, 1 – 2 sentences on the mechanism earn easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- Catalytic cracking uses an **acid catalyst** (zeolite or silica-alumina) to break C–C bonds via the **carbonium-ion mechanism**.
- **FCC** = fluidized bed; **TCC** = moving bed; **TCC is essentially obsolete** in modern refineries.
- The catalyst is **continuously regenerated** by burning off coke; the regenerated catalyst carries the heat needed to drive the endothermic cracking reaction.
- Typical operating conditions: **480 – 540 °C**, **1 – 3 atm**, C/O = 5 – 10 (FCC), WHSV = 8 – 25 h⁻¹ (FCC).
- Conversion is typically **70 – 85 %** in FCC, **50 – 65 %** in TCC.
- Gasoline yield ≈ **45 – 55 vol%**; gasoline RON ≈ **90 – 95** (much higher than thermally cracked gasoline).

### Key Engineering Distinctions
- **Riser** (FCC) vs. **Reactor bed** (TCC): riser = 2 – 4 s; bed = 30 – 90 s.
- **Regenerator** (FCC) vs. **Kiln** (TCC): regenerator = fluidized combustion at 700 °C; kiln = moving-bed combustion at 550 °C.
- **Catalyst**: FCC = 20 – 80 µm powder (zeolite Y); TCC = 3 – 5 mm pellets (amorphous SiO₂-Al₂O₃).
- **Catalyst circulation**: FCC needs continuous pneumatic circulation; TCC uses bucket elevators.

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- $G_s = \dfrac{Q_{\text{crack}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} (T_{\text{reg}} - T_{\text{exit}})}$
- $\Delta P_{\text{bed}} = (1-\epsilon)(\rho_s - \rho_g) g L$
- $U_{mf}$ from Ergun equation (laminar limit)
- $X = 100 - (\text{HCO} + \text{Coke})$ wt%
- Stoichiometry: 1 kg C requires 8/3 kg O₂ → 11/3 kg air for complete combustion.

### Key Advantages of FCC over TCC (for KTU comparison questions)
- Better temperature control (catalyst = heat sink).
- Higher conversion and gasoline yield.
- Continuous regeneration (no production loss).
- Smaller equipment footprint.
- Compatible with modern zeolite catalysts → high-octane gasoline.
- Lower coke yield.

### Modern Trends (Bonus Knowledge for Interviews)
- **Olefins-Enhanced FCC** for petrochemical propylene production.
- **Residue FCC (RFCC)** for processing atmospheric residue.
- **Catalyst coolers** in regenerator to remove surplus heat at high-conversion operation.
- **SO_x and NO_x reduction additives** in FCC catalyst.
- **Methanol-to-olefins (MTO)** and **Catalytic Pyrolysis** as future competitors to FCC.

### Conversion / Reactor Comparison Table (Last-Minute Recall)

| Parameter | FCC | TCC |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst form | Powder | Pellets |
| Catalyst size | 20 – 80 µm | 3 – 5 mm |
| Contact time | 2 – 4 s | 30 – 90 s |
| Temperature | 510 – 535 °C | 450 – 500 °C |
| C/O ratio | 5 – 10 | 1.5 – 3 |
| Conversion | 70 – 85 % | 50 – 65 % |
| Gasoline RON | 90 – 95 | 88 – 92 |
| Coke on spent cat | 0.5 – 1.5 wt% | 2 – 4 wt% |
| Catalyst regeneration | Continuous (FB combustor) | Batch-like (kiln) |
| Modern relevance | **Dominant** (> 90 % of new units) | **Obsolete** (most decommissioned) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catalytic Reforming** is a critical downstream petroleum refining process in which low-octane, straight-run naphtha fractions (typically C₆–C₁₂ hydrocarbons) are chemically restructured in the presence of a solid acid–base bifunctional catalyst (commonly platinum on chlorided alumina or platinum–rhenium on chlorided alumina) and a hydrogen-rich atmosphere to produce a high-octane liquid product known as **reformate**, along with valuable by-products such as **hydrogen gas (H₂)**, **light hydrocarbons (C₁–C₄)**, and **aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylenes — collectively BTX)**.

The principal objectives of reforming in a modern refinery are:

1. **Octane Enhancement:** Upgrading naphtha with Research Octane Number (RON) values typically ranging from **40–60** to reformate RON values of **95–102** without altering the boiling range.
2. **Aromatic Production:** Generating BTX feedstocks that serve as precursors for petrochemicals, plastics (PET, polystyrene, polyurethanes), synthetic fibers, and explosives.
3. **Hydrogen Generation:** Producing **75–250 Nm³ of H₂ per m³ of naphtha feed**, vital for hydrodesulfurization (HDS), hydrocracking, and hydrotreating units.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you have a set of tangled, cheap metal wires (low-octane naphtha molecules with random, kinked shapes). Reforming is like taking those wires to a skilled craftsman (the **platinum catalyst**) who uses a controlled flame (**high temperature, ~500 °C**) and protective shielding (**hydrogen atmosphere**) to reorganize them into perfectly shaped, rigid hexagonal rings (**aromatic benzene rings**) and high-strength coiled springs (**branched isoparaffins**). The result is a far stronger, more valuable product (high-octane reformate) that performs brilliantly under high-compression stress inside an internal combustion engine — resisting premature "knocking."

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **480–525 °C** |
| Operating Pressure | **3.5–35 bar** (depending on regenerative vs. continuous regeneration) |
| H₂/Hydrocarbon molar ratio | **3–6** |
| Weight Hourly Space Velocity (WHSV) | **1.0–3.0 h⁻¹** |
| Catalyst life (semi-regenerative) | **6–12 months** |
| Reformate RON | **95–102** |
| H₂ yield | **75–250 Nm³ H₂ / m³ feed** |
| Aromatic yield | **45–70 wt%** of reformate |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Reforming Processes

> [!NOTE]
> Reforming processes are broadly classified into **thermal** (non-catalytic) and **catalytic** processes. Modern refineries overwhelmingly employ **catalytic reforming** because of superior selectivity, lower operating temperatures, and higher octane gains.

#### A. Thermal Reforming
- Uses **purely thermal energy** at very high temperatures (**550–600 °C**) and elevated pressures (**30–70 bar**).
- Operates on the principle of free-radical chemistry (no catalyst).
- Produces reformate with lower octane number (typically **65–80 RON**) compared to catalytic reforming.
- Lower gasoline yield, higher gas (C₁–C₄) make.
- Almost obsolete in modern refineries; replaced entirely by catalytic reforming.

#### B. Catalytic Reforming (Four Major Commercial Processes)

| Process | Licensor | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Platforming** | UOP | Pt/Al₂O₃ catalyst; first commercial process (1949) |
| **Catforming** | Standard Oil | Pt catalyst; obsolete |
| **Hydroforming** | Texaco/Mobil | Molybdenum oxide catalyst; obsolete |
| **Powerforming** | Esso | Pt catalyst with improved regeneration |
| **Magnaforming** | Engelhard | Bimetallic Pt–Re; higher yield, lower pressure |
| **Continuous Catalytic Reforming (CCR)** | UOP & Axens | Catalyst continuously regenerated in moving bed |

#### C. Steam Reforming (Distinct Industrial Variant)
- Steam reforming is a **completely different process** used to manufacture **synthesis gas (syngas: H₂ + CO)** from natural gas (methane), naphtha, or coal.
- Reaction:
  $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206\,\text{kJ/mol}$$
- Catalyzed by **nickel on alumina (Ni/Al₂O₃)** at **700–900 °C** and **15–40 bar**.
- Used for **ammonia synthesis, methanol production, hydrogen generation** — NOT for gasoline octane improvement.

### 2.2 Core Chemistry of Catalytic Reforming

The reforming process involves four principal reaction families:

#### (i) Dehydrogenation of Naphthenes → Aromatics (Most Important)
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \;\text{(naphthene)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} \;\text{(aromatic)} + 3\,\text{H}_2$$
- Highly **endothermic** (ΔH ≈ +210 kJ/mol).
- Strongly favored thermodynamically by high temperature and low pressure.
- Produces the largest quantity of hydrogen.

#### (ii) Isomerization of n-Paraffins → iso-Paraffins
$$\text{n-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{iso-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$$
- **Mildly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −5 to −15 kJ/mol).
- Acid-catalyzed (chlorided alumina).
- Boosts octane by ~10–20 RON points.

#### (iii) Dehydrocyclization of Paraffins → Aromatics
$$\text{n-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} \;\text{(aromatic)} + 4\,\text{H}_2$$
- Strongly **endothermic**.
- Requires metal function (Pt) for dehydrogenation and acid function for cyclization.

#### (iv) Hydrocracking of Heavy Naphthenes/Paraffins → Light Hydrocarbons
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_m\text{H}_{2m+2} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)+2}$$
- **Exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −45 kJ/mol).
- Undesired (reduces liquid yield); suppressed by low pressure and high H₂ partial pressure.

### 2.3 Catalysts — The Heart of Reforming

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bifunctional Catalyst Architecture:** Reforming catalysts require two distinct active sites working in concert: a **metal function** for hydrogenation/dehydrogenation and an **acid function** for isomerization/cyclization.

- **Metal function:** Platinum (Pt), often promoted with **rhenium (Re), iridium (Ir), or tin (Sn)**. Pt provides dehydrogenation activity and hydrogenation activity.
- **Acid function:** **Chlorided γ-alumina (Cl⁻/Al₂O₃)** provides Lewis and Brønsted acid sites for skeletal isomerization.
- Typical composition: **0.25–0.6 wt% Pt, 0.25–0.5 wt% Re**, **0.5–1.5 wt% Cl**, on γ-Al₂O₃ support.
- **Sulfur and water** are permanent poisons; feed must be hydrodesulfurized to <0.5 ppm S.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In this table, the symbol `\vert` is used instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Weight Hourly Space Velocity | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}}{m_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | h⁻¹ |
| H₂ Purity (typical) | $\text{Purity}_{H_2} = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{V_{\text{total gas}}} \times 100$ | vol% (typically 85–95%) |
| Reformate Yield | $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{reformate}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | wt% (typical 80–88%) |
| Aromatic Conversion (Naphthene → Aromatic) | $X_{\text{arom}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{arom, prod}}}{n_{\text{naphthene, feed}}} \times 100$ | % |
| Octane Blending | $\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \text{RON}_i$ | Linear approximation |
| Reactor Heat Balance (Endothermic Dehydrogenation) | $Q = \dot{n}_{\text{reacted}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$ | kW (per reactor) |
| Pressure Drop (Ergun Equation) | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150\,\mu\,(1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2\,\varepsilon^3}\,v_s + \dfrac{1.75\,\rho\,(1-\varepsilon)}{d_p\,\varepsilon^3}\,v_s^2$ | Pa/m |
| Hydrogen-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio | $\text{H}_2/\text{HC} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{n_{\text{HC}}}$ | mol/mol |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Catalytic reforming underpins three of the world's largest industries:
1. **Transportation:** Provides ~30–40% of the gasoline pool in modern refineries by upgrading low-value naphtha to premium blending stock.
2. **Petrochemicals:** Reformate is the dominant source of BTX (benzene, toluene, xylene), the building blocks for nylon, polystyrene, PET bottles, polyurethane foams, and pharmaceuticals.
3. **Hydrogen Economy:** A single catalytic reformer can produce **30,000–100,000 Nm³/day of 90–95% pure H₂**, supplying adjacent HDS and hydrocracking units without external hydrogen plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Equilibrium Constant for the Dehydrogenation of Cyclohexane → Benzene

This is the textbook example studied in every KTU chemical technology module. We will derive the equilibrium constant expression rigorously.

**Reaction:**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{(cyclohexane, g)} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{(benzene, g)} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{(g)}$$

**Step 1:** Identify moles and stoichiometry.

Let the initial moles be: $n_{\text{cyc}} = 1$, $n_{\text{benz}} = 0$, $n_{H_2} = 0$ at the start.

At extent of reaction $\xi$ (extent of cyclohexane reacted):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{cyc}} &= 1 - \xi \\
n_{\text{benz}} &= \xi \\
n_{H_2} &= 3\xi \\
n_{\text{total}} &= 1 - \xi + \xi + 3\xi = 1 + 3\xi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Express mole fractions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{cyc}} &= \dfrac{1-\xi}{1+3\xi} \\
y_{\text{benz}} &= \dfrac{\xi}{1+3\xi} \\
y_{H_2} &= \dfrac{3\xi}{1+3\xi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Write the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressures. For a gas-phase reaction:

$$K_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{benz}} \cdot P_{H_2}^{\,3}}{P_{\text{cyc}}}$$

Substituting $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{\text{total}}$:

$$K_p = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\xi}{1+3\xi} P\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{3\xi}{1+3\xi} P\right)^{3}}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{1+3\xi} P\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the expression.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \dfrac{\dfrac{\xi}{1+3\xi} \cdot \dfrac{27\,\xi^{3}}{(1+3\xi)^{3}} \cdot P^{4}}{\dfrac{1-\xi}{1+3\xi} \cdot P} \\
&= \dfrac{27\,\xi^{4}}{(1+3\xi)^{3}\,(1-\xi)} \cdot P^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Final working equation for the equilibrium conversion.

$$\boxed{\;K_p = \dfrac{27\,\xi^{4}\,(P)^{3}}{(1+3\xi)^{3}\,(1-\xi)}\;}$$

**Engineering Insight (KTU-style analysis):**
- Note that $K_p$ is proportional to $P^{3}$. This is **Le Chatelier's principle in action**: lowering $P$ shifts equilibrium toward products (more H₂ + benzene). This is why **modern continuous catalytic reformers operate at pressures as low as 0.5–3.5 bar** to maximize aromatic yield.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style — Typical 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **[Problem Statement]:** A catalytic reformer processes **10,000 kg/h of naphtha** feed. The feed has the following composition by mass: 35% paraffins, 45% naphthenes, 20% aromatics. The reformate contains 70% aromatics, 28% paraffins, and 2% naphthenes (by mass). Calculate: **(a)** Mass of reformate produced, **(b)** Mass of hydrogen evolved.

**Given:**
- Feed composition (mass basis): $P_f = 35\%$, $N_f = 45\%$, $A_f = 20\%$
- Reformate composition (mass basis): $P_r = 28\%$, $N_r = 2\%$, $A_r = 70\%$

**Step (a): Mass of reformate**

Total mass balance:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{reformate}} + \dot{m}_{H_2}$$

Component mass balances (assuming total aromatic, naphthene, and paraffin mass in equals reformate mass; hydrogen produced comes from dehydrogenation):

For aromatics (no net loss; aromatics cannot be destroyed under reforming conditions):
$$\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot A_f = \dot{m}_{\text{reformate}} \cdot A_r$$
