$$10{,}000 \times 0.20 = \dot{m}_{\text{reformate}} \times 0.70$$

Solving:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{reformate}} &= \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 0.20}{0.70} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}000}{0.70} \\
&= 2857.14\;\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Mass of hydrogen evolved**

From overall mass balance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} &= \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} - \dot{m}_{\text{reformate}} \\
&= 10{,}000 - 2857.14 \\
&= 7142.86\;\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A 10,000 kg/h naphtha feed yielding ~7143 kg/h H₂ is unrealistic (molar mass limit). The proper interpretation: the 7143 kg/h is total **light hydrocarbons + H₂** in the net gas make, not pure H₂. This is a common KTU board exam trap — students often quote 7143 kg/h of pure H₂. The **pure hydrogen** mass is typically **150–350 kg/h** for this feed rate, with the rest being C₁–C₄ gases.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Conditions vs. Yield

| Operating Parameter | Low Severity | High Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 480 °C | 525 °C |
| Pressure | 35 bar (semi-regen) | 3.5 bar (CCR) |
| WHSV | 3.0 h⁻¹ | 1.0 h⁻¹ |
| H₂/HC Ratio | 6 | 3 |
| Reformate RON | 85–90 | 100–102 |
| Aromatic Yield | 40 wt% | 65–70 wt% |
| Liquid Yield (C₅⁺) | 88 wt% | 80 wt% |
| H₂ Purity | 90 vol% | 75 vol% |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Catalytic Reformer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the interaction of major process units in a **Continuous Catalytic Reforming (CCR) plant**, with moving-bed catalyst regeneration. Node IDs are alphanumeric per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["NAPHTHA FEED"] --> A2["FEED HYDRODESULFURIZATION UNIT"]
    A2 --> A3["CHLORIDE ADJUSTMENT DRUM"]
    A3 --> A4["REACTOR 1 HEATER FURNACE 500C"]
    A4 --> R1["REACTOR 1 DEHYDROGENATION"]
    R1 --> A5["INTER REACTOR HEATER 510C"]
    A5 --> R2["REACTOR 2 ISOMERIZATION CYCLIZATION"]
    R2 --> A6["INTER REACTOR HEATER 520C"]
    A6 --> R3["REACTOR 3 DEHYDROCYCLIZATION"]
    R3 --> A7["FINAL COOLER HEAT EXCHANGE"]
    A7 --> S1["HIGH PRESSURE SEPARATOR"]
    S1 --> C1["RECYCLE GAS COMPRESSOR H2 RICH"]
    C1 --> A4
    C1 --> A5
    C1 --> A6
    S1 --> S2["STABILIZER COLUMN"]
    S2 --> P1["REFORMATE PRODUCT TANK C5 PLUS"]
    S2 --> P2["LIGHT ENDS C1 TO C4 OFF GAS"]
    S1 --> P3["NET HYDROGEN GAS HEADER"]
    R3 --> REG1["CATALYST REGENERATION TOWER CCR"]
    REG1 --> R1
    REG1 --> R2
    REG1 --> R3
    REG1 --> A8["REGENERATION OXYGEN CHLORIDE CONTROL"]
    A8 --> REG1
```

### 4.2 Reactor Staging — Process Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["FRESH FEED + RECYCLE H2"] --> B2["HEATER 1"]
    B2 --> C1["R1 NAPHTHENE DEHYDROGENATION 70 PERCENT OF REACTION"]
    C1 --> B3["HEATER 2"]
    B3 --> C2["R2 NAPHTHENE DEHYDROGENATION ISOMERIZATION 20 PERCENT"]
    C2 --> B4["HEATER 3"]
    B4 --> C3["R3 DEHYDROCYCLIZATION 10 PERCENT MOST ENDOTHERMIC"]
    C3 --> B5["PRODUCT COOLER"]
    B5 --> B6["HP SEPARATOR"]
    B6 --> B7["STABILIZER"]
    B7 --> B8["REFORMATE PRODUCT"]
    B7 --> B9["FUEL GAS"]
    B6 --> B10["RECYCLE H2 COMPRESSOR"]
    B10 --> B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading of the Schematic:**
> - The **first reactor** (R1) is largest because naphthene dehydrogenation is rapid and accounts for ~70% of total heat duty.
> - **Inter-stage heaters** are mandatory because endothermic reactions drop reactor temperatures by **30–60 °C** per pass.
> - **Last reactor** is smallest; it handles the kinetically slowest, most endothermic dehydrocyclization of paraffins.

### 4.3 Bifunctional Catalyst Mechanism (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["PARAFFIN OR NAPHTHENE MOLECULE"] --> D2["PLATINUM METAL SITE DEHYDROGENATION"]
    D2 --> D3["OLEFINIC INTERMEDIATE"]
    D3 --> D4["CHLORIDED ALUMINA ACID SITE ISOMERIZATION CYCLIZATION"]
    D4 --> D5["AROMATIC OR ISOPARAFFIN PRODUCT"]
    D5 --> D6["RETURN TO PLATINUM SITE FINAL H2 RELEASE"]
    D6 --> D7["HIGH OCTANE REFORMATE PLUS H2"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1.** Define catalytic reforming. List the four main reactions occurring in the catalytic reformer.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Catalytic reforming is a refining process that converts low-octane straight-run naphtha (C₆–C₁₂) into high-octane reformate (RON 95–102) by catalytic rearrangement of molecular structures over platinum-based catalysts in a hydrogen atmosphere.
> - **Four Reactions (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
>   1. Dehydrogenation of naphthenes → aromatics
>   2. Isomerization of n-paraffins → iso-paraffins
>   3. Dehydrocyclization of paraffins → aromatics
>   4. Hydrocracking of heavy hydrocarbons → light gases

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the role of rhenium in platinum-rhenium reforming catalysts?
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Rhenium (Re) acts as a **structural promoter** that significantly improves catalyst performance in three ways:
> 1. **(1 mark)** Increases catalyst dispersion of Pt crystallites, preventing sintering at high temperatures (480–525 °C).
> 2. **(1 mark)** Suppresses coke formation on acid sites by moderating acidity, extending cycle length from 6 months (Pt-only) to 12+ months (Pt–Re).
> 3. **(1 mark)** Allows operation at **lower pressures (3.5–7 bar)** while maintaining yields, since Re reduces hydrogenolysis activity and coke precursors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2–4 marks on reforming questions by **(a)** confusing thermal and catalytic reforming, **(b)** omitting reaction stoichiometry/H₂ yield, **(c)** failing to draw proper process flow with **inter-stage heaters**, and **(d)** forgetting to mention **feed hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** before the reformer. Always include: **HDS pretreatment → chloride adjustment → staged reactors with interheaters → separation → stabilizer.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **Continuous Catalytic Reforming (CCR) unit**. Highlight the role of inter-stage heaters and catalyst regeneration loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- A CCR unit consists of **3 or 4 stacked adiabatic reactors** in series.
- Each reactor is a **radial-flow moving-bed reactor** where catalyst flows downward by gravity while feed flows radially inward through the catalyst annulus.
- Catalyst is lifted by **hydrogen or nitrogen gas lift** to the top, creating a continuous loop.
- A **regeneration tower** (separate vessel) burns off coke with controlled O₂/Cl₂/N₂ mixture at ~500 °C, then re-disperses and re-chlorides the catalyst before returning it to reactor 1.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- Pre-treated naphtha (S < 0.5 ppm) mixes with **recycle hydrogen** (H₂/HC ≈ 2–3).
- The mixture is heated to **500–525 °C** in a fired heater before entering Reactor 1.
- Reactor 1: predominantly **naphthene dehydrogenation** (70% of reaction).
- Inter-stage heater reheats to **510 °C** to compensate endothermic temperature drop.
- Reactor 2: remaining **naphthene conversion + isomerization**.
- Reactor 3: **dehydrocyclization of paraffins** (most endothermic, hence highest temperature).
- Effluent cooled, sent to **high-pressure separator**: liquid → stabilizer → reformate; vapor → recycle H₂ compressor.
- Spent catalyst continuously withdrawn from Reactor 3 bottom, lifted to regenerator, cleaned, and returned to Reactor 1 top.

**(b)** A catalytic reformer receives **5000 kmol/h** of naphtha containing 60 mol% naphthenes. Conversion per pass is 75%. Calculate the **kmol/h of hydrogen produced** if the only reaction is dehydrogenation of naphthenes (assumed as cyclohexane → benzene stoichiometry). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reaction:**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

**Given:**
- Molar feed rate: $\dot{n}_{\text{feed}} = 5000$ kmol/h
- Mole fraction naphthenes: $y_N = 0.60$
- Conversion: $X = 0.75$

**Step 1:** Moles of naphthenes fed.
$$\dot{n}_N = 5000 \times 0.60 = 3000\;\text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 2:** Moles of naphthenes reacted.
$$\dot{n}_{N,\text{reacted}} = 3000 \times 0.75 = 2250\;\text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 3:** From stoichiometry (1 mol cyclohexane → 3 mol H₂):
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = 3 \times 2250 = 6750\;\text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to standard volumetric flow at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm).
At NTP, 1 kmol of ideal gas = 22.414 Nm³.
$$V_{H_2} = 6750 \times 22.414 = 151{,}294.5\;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer:** **6,750 kmol/h** or **~151,295 Nm³/h** of hydrogen is produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Board Exams:** "**One mole of naphthene gives three moles of H₂**" — keep this ratio handy for any cyclohexane/methylcyclopentane dehydrogenation numerical.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**(a)** Compare **thermal reforming** and **catalytic reforming** with respect to operating conditions, products, octane number, and modern relevance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Comparison Table — to be drawn in exam):**

| Aspect | Thermal Reforming | Catalytic Reforming |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | None | Pt, Pt–Re on chlorided Al₂O₃ |
| **Temperature** | 550–600 °C | 480–525 °C |
| **Pressure** | 30–70 bar | 3.5–35 bar |
| **Mechanism** | Free-radical chain reactions | Surface catalysis (bifunctional) |
| **Octane Number** | 65–80 RON | 95–102 RON |
| **Yield of Reformate** | Lower (~75 wt%) | Higher (~85 wt%) |
| **Gas Make** | Higher | Lower (controlled) |
| **Hydrogen Purity** | Lower, mixed with olefins | Higher (85–95 vol%) |
| **Modern Relevance** | Obsolete; almost all units shut down | Backbone of modern refinery |
| **Cost** | Lower capex; higher energy | Higher capex; higher efficiency |

**Written Explanation (3.5 marks):** Catalytic reforming is preferred because:
- Lower temperature reduces side reactions and energy cost.
- Bifunctional catalyst delivers higher selectivity toward aromatics (vs. random cracking in thermal).
- Continuous regeneration in CCR units gives 3+ years of catalyst life vs. frequent shutdowns in thermal units.
- Higher-purity H₂ byproduct supports downstream HDS/hydrocracker units.

**(b)** Discuss the **role of catalyst** and explain **why continuous catalyst regeneration (CCR) is preferred** over semi-regenerative reforming. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Catalyst Role (3.5 marks):**
- The Pt metal site performs **dehydrogenation** (C–H bond breaking), supplying hydrogen atoms and olefinic intermediates.
- The acid (Cl⁻/Al₂O₃) site performs **skeletal rearrangement** — isomerization of n-paraffins and cyclization of olefins to form aromatics.
- **Platinum** alone gives good activity but **sinters rapidly** above 500 °C and loses dispersion, causing rapid deactivation.
- **Rhenium addition** (bimetallic Pt–Re) reduces sintering by up to 10×, allowing lower pressure operation.
- Sulfur, nitrogen, and water are **reversible poisons**; arsenic and lead are **permanent poisons** — hence mandatory feed HDS.

**Why CCR is Preferred (3.5 marks):**
1. **Higher Yield & Severity:** Operates at **0.5–3.5 bar**, very low pressure, maximizing equilibrium conversion to aromatics (per Le Chatelier).
2. **Constant Activity:** Catalyst is continuously regenerated (coke burned off, chloride readjusted), so reactor operates at peak performance 24/7.
3. **Longer Onstream:** **3–5 years between shutdowns** vs. 6–12 months for semi-regenerative units.
4. **Higher Octane:** Steady-state RON of **100–102** vs. declining RON in semi-regen as catalyst cokes.
5. **Lower H₂/HC ratio (~2)** saves compression cost.
6. **Better Economics:** Although capex is 20–30% higher, the higher yield and lower operating cost give payback in **2–3 years**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reforming (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Catalytic reforming upgrades low-RON (40–60) naphtha to high-RON (95–102) reformate** without changing boiling range.
- ✅ **Four core reactions:** (1) Naphthene → Aromatic + 3H₂, (2) n-Paraffin → iso-Paraffin, (3) Paraffin → Aromatic + 4H₂, (4) Hydrocracking (undesired).
- ✅ **Catalyst is bifunctional:** **Pt (metal)** + **Cl⁻/Al₂O₃ (acid)**. Pt–Re bimetallics are industry standard.
- ✅ **Operating window:** **480–525 °C**, **3.5–35 bar**, **H₂/HC = 3–6**, **WHSV = 1–3 h⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Pressure paradox:** Lower pressure → higher aromatic yield (Le Chatelier) → but more coke → solved by **CCR** technology.
- ✅ **CCR (UOP, Axens)** is the modern benchmark; semi-regenerative units are being phased out.
- ✅ **Feed must be hydrodesulfurized** to <0.5 ppm S before reformer to prevent catalyst poisoning.
- ✅ **H₂ yield is 75–250 Nm³/m³ feed** — a critical economic driver.
- ✅ **By-products:** BTX aromatics (petrochemical feedstock) and fuel gas (C₁–C₄).
- ✅ **Steam reforming ≠ catalytic reforming:** steam reforming makes syngas/H₂ from CH₄ + H₂O using **Ni catalyst** at 700–900 °C.
- ✅ **Thermal reforming is obsolete** — mention only for contrast in exam answers.
- ✅ **Reformer effluent must be stabilized** to remove dissolved light gases from reformate.
- ✅ **Inter-stage heaters are mandatory** between reactors because each endothermic stage drops temperature by 30–60 °C.
- ✅ **Reforming is endothermic overall** (~210 kJ/mol for cyclohexane → benzene); reformer furnace duty is a major design parameter.
- ✅ **Catalyst regeneration cycle:** coke burn-off with O₂/N₂ → redispersion with Cl₂ → oxychlorination → reduction with H₂.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** *"Catalytic reforming is a high-temperature, hydrogen-rich, Pt-catalyzed molecular rearrangement that converts low-octane naphtha into high-octane reformate plus valuable H₂ and BTX aromatics."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petrochemicals: Manufacture of Acetylene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Petrochemicals: Manufacture of Acetylene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acetylene (C₂H₂ / Ethyne)** is the simplest and most unsaturated of the aliphatic hydrocarbons, characterized by a carbon–carbon triple bond ($\text{C}{\equiv}\text{C}$). It is a colorless, highly flammable gas with a sweet, ether-like odor (commercial grade contains impurities such as H₂S, PH₃, NH₃ which impart a garlic-like smell). Acetylene is the **highest flame-temperature hydrocarbon fuel** and an indispensable **C₂-building block** in the petrochemical industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> "Acetylene is a C₂-unsaturated hydrocarbon (ethyne, C₂H₂) possessing a triple bond. Industrially, it is the foundational petrochemical building block for vinyl chloride, vinyl acetate, acrylonitrile, neoprene, and acetaldehyde — its manufacture routes from petrochemical feedstocks (natural gas / naphtha / methane) form a critical segment of C₂-chemistry."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Twin Spark Plug of Industry"**
> Think of acetylene as the *chemical equivalent of a high-performance spark plug*. Just as a spark plug releases a tiny but extremely hot and energetic spark that ignites fuel in an engine, the triple bond in C₂H₂ stores enormous chemical energy (about **48 MJ/kg** heat of combustion). When this bond breaks, it releases tremendous energy, making acetylene:
> - The hottest commercially used flame (oxygen-acetylene: ~3,480 K)
> - The most reactive C₂ unit for synthesizing complex chemicals
>
> In petrochemical plants, acetylene is essentially a "high-energy, low-mass messenger" — it carries carbon and hydrogen from simple feedstocks (like methane or calcium carbide) to complex products (PVC, acrylic fibers, synthetic rubber).

### 1.3 Key Physical & Thermodynamic Constants

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | C₂H₂ |
| IUPAC Name | Ethyne |
| Molecular Weight | 26.04 g/mol |
| Boiling Point (1 atm) | -84 °C (**189.15 K**) |
| Triple Point | -80.55 °C |
| Critical Temperature | 35.18 °C |
| Critical Pressure | 6.14 MPa |
| Heat of Combustion | **1,300 kJ/mol** |
| Flame Temperature (O₂-C₂H₂) | ~3,480 K |
| Bond Energy (C≡C) | **965 kJ/mol** |
| Bond Energy (C–H) | 411 kJ/mol |
| Density (gas, STP) | 1.097 kg/m³ |
| Autoignition Temperature | 305 °C |
| Explosive Limits in Air | 2.5% – 82% (extremely wide!) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Critical Note for KTU Exam:** Acetylene is **thermodynamically unstable** above **200 kPa (≈ 2 atm)**. It undergoes explosive decomposition into C(s) + H₂(g) releasing **226 kJ/mol**. Therefore, it is **NEVER** transported as a compressed gas — it is dissolved in acetone under pressure in cylinders packed with porous material (e.g., calcium silicate). This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Importance of Acetylene in Petrochemical Industry

Acetylene is the **gateway to C₂-chemistry**. It acts as a precursor for approximately **20–25%** of all petrochemical products historically (though ethylene has now overtaken it for bulk chemicals, acetylene retains strategic value for high-value derivatives).

**Major Derivatives of Acetylene:**

1. **Vinyl Chloride (CH₂=CHCl)** → PVC
2. **Vinyl Acetate (CH₂=CHOCOCH₃)** → PVA, adhesives
3. **Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN)** → acrylic fibers, ABS
4. **Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** → acetic acid, ethanol
5. **Neoprene (Polychloroprene)** → synthetic rubber
6. **Trichloroethylene / Perchloroethylene** → solvents
7. **1,4-Butanediol (BDO)** → THF, polyurethanes
8. **Propargyl alcohol & Butynediol** (Reppe chemistry)

### 2.2 Classification of Manufacturing Routes

The industrial manufacture of acetylene is classified into **two principal families**:

| Family | Feedstock | Category |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Calcium Carbide Route** | CaO + C (electric arc) | Chemical (non-petrochemical) |
| **B. Hydrocarbon Pyrolysis Routes** | CH₄, C₂H₆, LPG, Naphtha | **Petrochemical** (KTU focus) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The petrochemical manufacture routes (B) — specifically **partial oxidation of methane**, **electric arc cracking**, **Wulff regenerative furnace**, and **submerged flame process** are the high-weightage topics for Part B questions.

### 2.3 Detailed Process Analysis

#### **Route 1: Calcium Carbide Process (Classical — For Background Only)**

$$\text{CaO} + 3\text{C} \xrightarrow{2000\text{–}2500\,°\text{C, electric arc}} \text{CaC}_2 + \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +464 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{CaC}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \quad \Delta H = -130 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Limitations:** Very high energy consumption (~10 MWh/ton C₂H₂), obsolete in modern petrochemical hubs, still used in China and India for oxy-acetylene welding.

#### **Route 2: Partial Oxidation of Methane (BASF / Sachsse Process)** ⭐ Most Important for KTU

This is the **dominant petrochemical route** for acetylene production from natural gas.

**Overall Reaction:**

$$4\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2\text{CO} + 7\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +170 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step-wise Mechanism:**
1. Partial combustion of methane supplies process heat:
$$2\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 4\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -71 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
2. The hot combustion gases then crack the remaining methane:
$$2\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +376 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Operating Conditions:**
- Temperature: **1,400 – 1,500 °C** (very brief residence time)
- Residence time: **0.001 – 0.01 seconds** (to prevent acetylene decomposition)
- Pressure: **Slightly above atmospheric** (50–200 kPa gauge)
- O₂:CH₄ molar ratio: **0.55 – 0.65** (sub-stoichiometric)
- Quenching: Rapid quench to < 200 °C within milliseconds (using oil or water spray)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why such short residence time?**
> Thermodynamics: Below 1,500 °C, acetylene is the *kinetically* favored product. Above 1,500 °C, **carbon (soot) and hydrogen become more stable** than C₂H₂. Hence the trick is *high T + ultra-short τ* (the "Sachse trick"). This is a recurring 7-mark question in KTU.

#### **Route 3: Wulff Process (Regenerative Furnace)**

A cyclic process using a **refractory checker-brick furnace** (similar to a Cowper stove in steel industry) for cracking light hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, or butane).

**Cycle (4 phases):**
1. **Heating phase:** Combustion of fuel gas heats the refractory checker bricks to ~1,300 °C.
2. **Cracking phase:** Hydrocarbon feed (C₂H₆ or LPG) is passed through the hot checker — cracked to C₂H₂.
3. **Regeneration / Steam phase:** Steam is passed to sweep out remaining hydrocarbons.
4. **Cooling phase:** Combustion gases reheat the other checker.

**Yield:** ~50% on C₂ basis; the Wulff process can economically use ethane, propane, butane, and naphtha.

#### **Route 4: Electric Arc (Plasma) Cracking Process**

- Natural gas is passed through an **electric arc (~1,600 – 2,000 °C)** sustained between two carbon electrodes.
- Conversion per pass: ~50%; selectivity to acetylene: ~70%.
- Energy consumption: ~10–13 kWh/kg C₂H₂ (high — hence declining commercial use).
- Hüls (Germany) operated this process for decades before switching to partial oxidation.

#### **Route 5: Submerged Flame Process (Hoechst / BASF Modification)**

- A flame of preheated methane/oxygen is **submerged in oil** (e.g., heat transfer oil).
- The oil provides instant quenching, raising acetylene yield to ~35% per pass.
- Steam is injected to suppress soot formation.

#### **Route 6: Plasma Arc / Gliding Arc Process**

Modern variant using **non-equilibrium plasma** at ~10,000 K. Higher selectivity but limited industrial deployment.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Selectivity to C₂H₂ | $S = \dfrac{n_{C_2H_2,\,produced}}{n_{CH_4,\,converted}} \times 100$ | % | Reactor performance |
| 2 | Single-pass yield | $Y_{sp} = \dfrac{n_{C_2H_2,\,out}}{n_{CH_4,\,in}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency |
| 3 | Energy of C≡C bond | $E_{C \equiv C}$ | **965 kJ/mol** | Reaction engineering |
| 4 | Heat of combustion of C₂H₂ | $\Delta H_c$ | **−1,300 kJ/mol** | Flame temperature |
| 5 | Adiabatic flame temp. (O₂–C₂H₂) | $T_{ad}$ | **~3,480 K** | Welding metallurgy |
| 6 | Kinetics of decomposition | $-\dfrac{d[C_2H_2]}{dt} = k\,[C_2H_2]^n$ | mol/L·s | Reactor safety |
| 7 | Optimum O₂:CH₄ ratio | $r = 0.55\text{–}0.65$ | — | BASF / Sachsse |
| 8 | Decomposition limit pressure | $P_{max}$ | **200 kPa** | Cylinder design |
| 9 | Conversion of CaC₂ | $X_{CaC_2}$ | % | Carbide route |
| 10 | Equilibrium constant (Saha-type) | $K_p = \dfrac{p_{C_2H_2}\cdot p_{H_2}^3}{p_{CH_4}^2}$ | bar⁴ | Thermodynamic feasibility |
| 11 | Quench rate | $\dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | K/s | Process control |
| 12 | Acetylene solubility in acetone | $x = k \cdot P^{1/n}$ | — | Cylinder filling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy Tip:** Memorize the 4-marker constants (1,300 kJ/mol, 965 kJ/mol, 200 kPa, 0.55–0.65) — these appear in 80% of KTU 2024 part (a) questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oxy-acetylene welding/cutting** in shipyards and steel plants (historically the largest market).
- **Raw material** for Reppe chemistry (now NMP-based processes for BDO).
- **Reducing agent** in metallurgy (Cu, Zn, W ore reduction).
- **Rocket propellant additive** for bipropellant thrusters.
- **Specialty chemical intermediate** for vitamins (Vitamin A, E), pharmaceuticals, and fragrances.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Flame Temperature of Oxy-Acetylene Flame

The adiabatic flame temperature (AFT) is a high-weightage derivation in KTU. We derive it below for the stoichiometric reaction:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \dfrac{5}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$

**Given Data (Standard Enthalpies of Formation, 298 K):**

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_2) = +226.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 1: Enthalpy of Reaction at 298 K**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = \left[2 \times (-393.5) + 1 \times (-241.8)\right] - \left[1 \times (+226.7) + \dfrac{5}{2} \times 0\right]$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = -787.0 - 241.8 - 226.7 = -1255.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2: Energy Balance for Adiabatic Flame**

For adiabatic flame, the entire heat of reaction (negative, since exothermic) goes into heating the products:

$$\sum_{\text{products}} \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} n_i \cdot C_{p,i}(T)\, dT = -\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298)$$

Using mean molar heat capacities (constant approximation, valid for first estimate):

$$\overline{C_p}(\text{CO}_2) \approx 54 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\overline{C_p}(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) \approx 44 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3: Solve for T_ad**

$$2(54)(T_{ad} - 298) + 1(44)(T_{ad} - 298) = 1,255,500 \text{ J}$$

$$(108 + 44)(T_{ad} - 298) = 1,255,500$$

$$152(T_{ad} - 298) = 1,255,500$$

$$T_{ad} - 298 = 8260 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{ad} = 8,558 \text{ K (theoretical maximum)}$$

**Step 4: Apply Dissociation Correction**

At such extreme temperatures, CO₂ and H₂O dissociate, absorbing energy. The empirical corrected value:

$$T_{ad,\,corrected} \approx 3,480 \text{ K} \quad (\text{after dissociation losses} \approx 60\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer Key Expectation:** Mention that the theoretical AFT (~8,558 K) is never achieved due to:
> 1. Thermal dissociation of CO₂ ⇌ CO + ½O₂
> 2. Thermal dissociation of H₂O ⇌ H₂ + ½O₂
> 3. Radiation losses from flame
> 4. Incomplete mixing in real burners
> Actual value **≈ 3,480 K** = highest of all combustion flames.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Process Evaluation

The following analytical matrix is a **guaranteed KTU 14-marker** structure (Table-style comparison asked in past papers).

| Parameter | Calcium Carbide | Partial Oxidation (BASF) | Wulff Process | Electric Arc |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | CaO + Coke | CH₄ (natural gas) | C₂H₆, LPG, naphtha | CH₄ |
| **Reaction Type** | Chemical / Thermal | Partial Combustion | Regenerative Cracking | Plasma Pyrolysis |
| **Temperature** | 2,000 – 2,500 °C | 1,400 – 1,500 °C | 1,200 – 1,300 °C | 1,600 – 2,000 °C |
| **Residence Time** | Hours | **0.001 – 0.01 s** | 0.1 – 0.5 s | 0.01 – 0.05 s |
| **C₂H₂ Yield (per pass)** | ~85% (from CaC₂) | 25 – 35% | 40 – 50% | 50 – 70% |
| **Selectivity** | >95% | 60 – 70% | 70 – 80% | 60 – 70% |
| **Energy Consumption** | 10 MWh/ton | 6 – 8 MWh/ton | 5 – 6 MWh/ton | 10 – 13 MWh/ton |
| **By-products** | Ca(OH)₂, CO | CO, H₂, soot | CO, H₂, CH₄ | C (soot), H₂ |
| **CO + H₂ co-product use** | None | **Syngas for methanol** | Similar | Limited |
| **Capital Cost** | High (electric arc) | Moderate | Moderate | Very High |
| **Operating Cost** | Very high | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Scale of Operation** | Small/medium | **Large (global standard)** | Medium | Declining |
| **Environmental Impact** | High (Ca(OH)₂ waste, dust) | Moderate (CO₂ from burner) | Low | Moderate |
| **Industrial Use Today** | Welding, niche | **Major petrochemical** | Specialty | Rare |

### 3.3 Derivation: Material Balance for Partial Oxidation of Methane

A feed of 100 mol/h of methane and 60 mol/h of oxygen enters the partial oxidation reactor. The conversion is 30% and selectivity to C₂H₂ is 65% (remainder to CO + H₂). Determine the off-gas composition.

**Step 1: Define molar flows**
- $F_{CH_4,in} = 100$ mol/h
- $F_{O_2,in} = 60$ mol/h

**Step 2: Methane converted**

$$F_{CH_4,\,converted} = 0.30 \times 100 = 30 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 3: C₂H₂ produced (selectivity basis)**

$$F_{C_2H_2} = 0.65 \times 30 = 19.5 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 4: By-products from non-selective path**

By carbon balance, the remaining 30 − 2(19.5) = 30 − 39 → negative! 

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** Selectivity definitions vary. Using *carbon-atom selectivity* $S = \frac{2 \cdot n_{C_2H_2}}{n_{CH_4,\,converted}}$: then C₂H₂ produced = 0.65 × 30 / 2 = 9.75 mol/h. The other 30 − 2(9.75) = 10.5 mol/h of CH₄-C goes to CO. So $F_{CO} = 10.5$ mol/h.

**Step 5: Hydrogen balance**
H atoms: CH₄ provides 4 × 30 = 120 atoms. 
- C₂H₂ uses 2 × 9.75 = 19.5 atoms.
- Remaining 120 − 19.5 = 100.5 H atoms → go to H₂ → $F_{H_2} = 50.25$ mol/h.

**Step 6: Oxygen balance**
O₂ in: 60 mol. Burner reactions use some O₂ to give CO and H₂O. Assume 60 mol O₂ is fully consumed. Oxygen leaves as CO and H₂O. 

- O in CO: 10.5 mol
- Let $F_{H_2O} = x$, O in H₂O = x. So x = 60 − 10.5 = 49.5 mol/h H₂O.

**Final off-gas composition (mol/h):**

| Component | Flow (mol/h) | Mole % |
|---|---|---|
| CH₄ (unreacted) | 70.0 | 70/201.5 ≈ **34.7%** |
| C₂H₂ | 9.75 | **4.8%** |
| CO | 10.5 | 5.2% |
| H₂ | 50.25 | 24.9% |
| H₂O | 49.5 | 24.6% |
| N₂ (if air used) | — | — |
| **Total** | **201.5** | **100%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Many students confuse *yield* with *selectivity*. **Selectivity = (desired product formed) / (feed converted)**, while **Yield = (desired product) / (feed in)**. Yield = Conversion × Selectivity. Always clarify which one is being asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Petrochemical Manufacture of Acetylene (Partial Oxidation Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[Natural Gas Feed CH4] --> compA[Feed Compressor]
    feedB[Oxygen Feed O2 from ASU] --> compB[O2 Compressor]
    compA --> mix[Mixing Tee O2:CH4 ratio 0.55 to 0.65]
    compB --> mix
    mix --> preheater[Feed Preheater 600 C]
    preheater --> burner[Burner Reactor 1400 to 1500 C Residence 0.005 s]
    burner --> quench[Quench Tower Oil Spray T less than 200 C]
    quench --> soot[Soot Scrubber Water Wash]
    soot --> compressor[Off gas Compressor]
    compressor --> absorber1[Absorber 1 DMF or NMP Selective C2H2 Capture]
    absorber1 --> lean[Lean Solvent Regenerator]
    lean --> absorber1
    absorber1 --> rich[Rich Solvent C2H2 Loaded]
    rich --> flash[Flash Drum]
    flash --> absorber2[Absorber 2 Final C2H2 Purification]
    absorber2 --> product[Pure C2H2 Product 99.5 percent]
    absorber2 --> tail[Tail Gas CO plus H2 Syngas to Methanol]
    absorber1 --> tail
    product --> cylinder[Cylinder Filling Acetone Dissolved]
    cylinder --> market[Market PVC PVA Acrylic]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Process Architecture — All Acetylene Manufacturing Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph src[Feedstock Family]
        direction TB
        A1[Calcium Carbide Route]
        A2[Petrochemical Route]
    end

    subgraph chemcarb[Calcium Carbide Path]
        direction TB
        B1[CaO plus 3C Electric Arc 2500 C] --> B2[CaC2 Intermediate]
        B2 --> B3[CaC2 plus 2H2O Hydrolysis] --> B4[C2H2 plus CaOH2]
    end

    subgraph petro[Petrochemical Paths]
        direction TB
        C1[Partial Oxidation BASF Sachsse]
        C2[Wulff Regenerative]
        C3[Electric Arc Plasma]
        C4[Submerged Flame]
    end

    A1 --> chemcarb
    A2 --> petro

    C1 --> P1[C2H2 25 to 35 percent yield]
    C2 --> P2[C2H2 40 to 50 percent yield]
    C3 --> P3[C2H2 50 to 70 percent yield]
    C4 --> P4[C2H2 30 to 35 percent yield]

    P1 --> final[Acetylene C2H2]
    P2 --> final
    P3 --> final
    P4 --> final
    B4 --> final

    final --> app1[Vinyl Chloride]
    final --> app2[Vinyl Acetate]
    final --> app3[Acrylonitrile]
    final --> app4[Oxy Acetylene Welding]
    final --> app5[BDO via Reppe]
```

### 4.3 Reactor Configuration Schematic — Partial Oxidation Burner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preheated CH4 and O2 Mix] --> B[Conical Burner Nozzle]
    B --> C[Combustion Zone Zone1 Exothermic 1400 C]
    C --> D[Cracking Zone Zone2 Endothermic 1500 C]
    D --> E[Quench Zone Zone3 Oil Spray Less than 200 C]
    E --> F[Crude Acetylene Stream]
    F --> G[Soot Removal Electrostatic Precipitator]
    G --> H[Compression and Solvent Absorption]
    H --> I[Pure C2H2]
    H --> J[Syngas Co Product to Methanol]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The burner is essentially a **staged adiabatic reactor** — Zone 1 supplies heat (combustion), Zone 2 uses that heat (endothermic cracking). Quench Zone 3 is critical — it freezes the acetylene before it can decompose to soot. The 3-zone design is the heart of the BASF process and appears in KTU Part B questions as a labeled diagram.

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Acetylene Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    upstream[Upstream Feedstock Natural Gas Naphtha] --> midstream[Midstream Acetylene Manufacture]
    midstream --> downstream1[Downstream C2 Derivatives]
    downstream1 --> enduse1[PVC Pipe Cables]
    downstream1 --> enduse2[PVA Adhesives Paints]
    downstream1 --> enduse3[Acrylic Fiber Clothing Carpets]
    downstream1 --> enduse4[Neoprene Rubber Hoses Belts]
    downstream1 --> enduse5[Welding and Metal Cutting]
    midstream --> byproduct[By Product Syngas CO plus H2]
    byproduct --> methanol[Methanol Synthesis]
    methanol --> downstream1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is acetylene never stored as a compressed gas above 200 kPa? What safety measure is adopted in commercial cylinders?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Acetylene is thermodynamically unstable above 200 kPa. It undergoes explosive decomposition:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{C}(s) + \text{H}_2(g) \quad \Delta H = -226 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This decomposition can be initiated by heat, shock, or spark, releasing large energy. To prevent this, commercial cylinders store acetylene **dissolved in acetone** absorbed in a porous mass (calcium silicate diatomite). The acetone acts as a solvent stabilizing the gas, and the porous mass prevents localized decomposition. Cylinders are pre-filled with the porous mass, evacuated, then filled with acetone (~40% by volume) and pressurized with C₂H₂ up to ~1.5 MPa (within safe decomposition limits due to acetone stabilization). **[3 marks]**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the term "selectivity" in the context of partial oxidation of methane to acetylene. What is the typical selectivity achieved in the BASF process?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Selectivity refers to the fraction of converted feed that goes to the **desired product (acetylene)** rather than to by-products (CO₂, soot, H₂O).

$$S_{C_2H_2} = \frac{\text{moles of CH}_4 \text{ converted to C}_2\text{H}_2}{\text{total moles of CH}_4 \text{ converted}} \times 100\%$$

In the BASF partial oxidation process, the typical selectivity to acetylene is **60 – 70%** on a carbon-atom basis, with the balance going to CO, H₂, CO₂, H₂O, and soot. Selectivity is critically dependent on residence time (≤ 0.01 s) and rapid quenching. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam (Dec 2023 Pattern)**

**Q3(a) [7 marks]** *Describe with a neat flow diagram the manufacture of acetylene by partial oxidation of methane (BASF process). Discuss the role of temperature, residence time, and O₂:CH₄ ratio.*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Acetylene is manufactured by partial oxidation of methane (natural gas) using the BASF/Sachsse process. The reaction is endothermic overall, and partial combustion of methane supplies the necessary heat for the cracking of the remaining methane.

**Overall Reaction (1 mark):**
$$4\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2\text{CO} + 7\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +170 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Process Description (3 marks):**
1. **Feed Preparation:** Natural gas (≥95% CH₄) is filtered, desulfurized, and preheated to 600 °C. Oxygen (99.5%) from an air separation unit is mixed with the methane in a mole ratio of **0.55–0.65** (O₂:CH₄).
2. **Burner/Reactor:** The mixture is fed into a refractory-lined burner. Combustion of part of the methane supplies the heat for endothermic cracking of the rest. The reaction is carried out at **1,400 – 1,500 °C** for a residence time of only **0.001 – 0.01 s**.
3. **Quenching:** The hot gases are quenched immediately with cold oil or water spray to below 200 °C to prevent decomposition of acetylene into soot and hydrogen.
4. **Soot Removal:** The crude gas passes through an electrostatic precipitator or water scrubber to remove soot (carbon black by-product).
5. **Compression and Absorption:** The clean gas is compressed to ~1 MPa and passed through an absorber where C₂H₂ is selectively absorbed in **N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP)** or **dimethylformamide (DMF)**. CO + H₂ (syngas) passes through and is sent to a methanol plant.
6. **Stripping and Purification:** The rich solvent is heated and stripped to release pure C₂H₂ (>99.5%).

**Critical Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Temperature (1,400–1,500 °C):** Below this, conversion is too low; above 1,500 °C, C₂H₂ decomposes to soot and H₂.
- **Residence time (≤ 0.01 s):** Critical to "freeze" the kinetics before thermodynamic equilibrium (which favors C + H₂) is reached.
- **O₂:CH₄ ratio (0.55–0.65):** If too low, insufficient heat; if too high, complete combustion to CO₂ + H₂O dominates and yield drops.

**Diagram (Bonus for full marks):** Refer to the Block Flow Diagram in Section 4.1. **[7 marks]**

**Q3(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the Wulff process for acetylene manufacture. How does it differ from the partial oxidation process?*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The Wulff process is a **regenerative cyclic process** in which hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, or butane — not methane) are cracked in a furnace whose checker-brick refractory is alternately heated and used for cracking — analogous to a Cowper stove in a steel blast furnace.

**Process Cycle (3 marks):**
The Wulff furnace has **two chambers** of refractory checker-bricks, operated in **4-step cycles**:
1. **Heating (10–15 min):** Fuel gas (natural gas or product tail gas) is burned in one chamber, raising checker temperature to ~1,300 °C.
2. **Cracking (1–3 min):** Hydrocarbon feed (ethane/propane/butane) is passed through the hot checker; it cracks to acetylene + H₂ + CH₄ + CO.
3. **Steam purge (1 min):** Steam is passed to sweep out residual hydrocarbon and prevent carbon deposition.
4. **Valve reversal:** The roles of the two chambers are interchanged; the freshly heated chamber enters cracking service while the previous cracking chamber is reheated.

**Operating Conditions:**
- Temperature: 1,200 – 1,300 °C
- Pressure: ~50 kPa (slight vacuum to favor C₂H₂)
- Hydrocarbon residence time: 0.1 – 0.5 s

**Comparison with Partial Oxidation (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Wulff Process | BASF Partial Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | C₂H₆, C₃H₈, C₄H₁₀ (LPG) | CH₄ (natural gas) |
| Heat Source | Regenerated refractory (no flame) | In-situ partial combustion |
| O₂ Requirement | None (during cracking) | Yes (O₂:CH₄ ≈ 0.6) |
| Yield (per pass) | 40 – 50% | 25 – 35% |
| Cycle | Cyclic (4-step) | Continuous |
| CO + H₂ co-product | Yes (syngas) | Yes (syngas) |
| Soot formation | Low | Moderate (needs scrubber) |

**Advantages of Wulff:** Can use cheaper LPG feedstocks; no O₂ plant required; flexible feedstock.
**Disadvantages of Wulff:** Cyclic operation; large furnace; high capital cost; not as scalable as BASF.

**[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam (June 2024 Pattern, Alternative Choice)**

**Q4(a) [7 marks]** *With the help of a flow diagram, describe the manufacture of acetylene from calcium carbide. Why is this route not preferred in modern petrochemical industry?*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Manufacture of Calcium Carbide (2 marks)**
In an **electric arc furnace**, a mixture of **quicklime (CaO) and coke (C)** is heated to **2,000 – 2,500 °C**:

$$\text{CaO} + 3\text{C} \xrightarrow{2000\text{–}2500\,°\text{C}} \text{CaC}_2 + \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +464 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Liquid CaC₂ is tapped periodically and cooled. The energy consumption is very high (~10 MWh per ton of C₂H₂ produced).

**Step 2: Generation of Acetylene (3 marks)**
Calcium carbide is fed into a **generator** where it reacts with water (controlled rate) at room temperature:

$$\text{CaC}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \quad \Delta H = -130 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The acetylene gas is purified by passing through:
- **Water scrubber** (removes NH₃, PH₃)
- **Acid scrubber** (H₂SO₄ removes basic impurities)
- **Alkaline scrubber** (NaOH removes H₂S, CO₂)
- **Drier** (CaCl₂ removes moisture)

Pure acetylene (>99.5%) is collected in gasholders and compressed into cylinders (dissolved in acetone).

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):**

$$\text{Limestone} \rightarrow \text{CaO} \rightarrow \text{Electric Arc Furnace} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2 \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{Purification} \rightarrow \text{Cylinders}$$

**Why Not Preferred in Modern Petrochemical Industry? (1 mark)**
- **Very high energy consumption** (electric arc + coke) — economically unviable where natural gas is cheap.
- **Large CO₂ emissions** from the electric arc and from Ca(OH)₂ waste.
- **Ca(OH)₂ by-product** is a low-value waste (only 0.5 tons per ton of C₂H₂, but accumulates).
- **Cannot compete** with petrochemical routes using cheap natural gas/LPG.
- **Limited scale**: Carbide plants are typically < 50,000 tons/year C₂H₂.

**[7 marks]**

**Q4(b) [7 marks]** *Compare the partial oxidation (BASF) and electric arc processes for acetylene manufacture. Under what conditions is each preferred?*

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | BASF (Partial Oxidation) | Electric Arc (Hüls) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Natural gas (CH₄) | Natural gas (CH₄) |
| Heat source | In-situ combustion of CH₄ | External electric arc (plasma) |
| Temperature | 1,400 – 1,500 °C | 1,600 – 2,000 °C |
| Conversion per pass | 30 – 40% | 50 – 60% |
| Acetylene yield | 25 – 35% | 50 – 70% |
| Energy efficiency | High (heat of combustion used) | Low (electricity expensive) |
| Energy consumption | 6 – 8 MWh/ton | 10 – 13 MWh/ton |
| Co-product | Syngas (CO + H₂) → methanol | Mostly soot (low value) |
| O₂ requirement | Yes (ASU needed) | No |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High (electrode maintenance) |
| Operating cost | Low (cheap NG + syngas revenue) | High (electricity cost) |
| Environmental impact | Moderate CO₂ from burner | Low CO₂ but high soot waste |

**Process Description BASF (1.5 marks):** Already covered in Q3(a).

**Process Description Electric Arc (1.5 marks):**
- **Hüls (Germany) process** — methane is passed through a **direct-current electric arc** sustained between two carbon electrodes.
- The arc generates temperatures of 1,600 – 2,000 °C.
- Methane dissociates: $2\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$
- The gases are rapidly quenched with water/oil.
- Soot is the major by-product (collected as carbon black — partially marketable).
- The arc operates at voltages of 7–8 kV and currents of 1,000–1,200 A.

**Preference Conditions (1 mark):**
- **BASF is preferred** when natural gas is cheap, electricity is expensive, and a methanol (or syngas) plant can absorb the CO + H₂ by-product. This is the modern petrochemical standard globally.
- **Electric arc is preferred** when electricity is exceptionally cheap (e.g., hydroelectric power, captive power plants) and there is no downstream syngas utilization. Hüls used this in Germany during 1940–1990s before switching to BASF.
- **Modern trend:** BASF dominates (~80% global capacity), with electric arc plants being phased out.

**[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing yield with selectivity (–1 to –2 marks):** Always define and use the *correct* formula. Selectivity = product / (reactant converted); Yield = product / (reactant fed in). Many students write "yield" when the question demands "selectivity".
>
> 2. **Forgetting safety notes (–1 to –2 marks):** Any question on acetylene manufacture *must* mention the explosive decomposition risk and the acetone-dissolved cylinder storage. Examiners explicitly look for "200 kPa", "acetone", and "porous mass" in any Part A answer.
>
> 3. **Missing the thermodynamic argument for short residence time (–2 marks in 14-mark Q):** The whole point of the BASF process is that *kinetically* acetylene forms faster than soot in the temperature range 1,400–1,500 °C, but *thermodynamically* C + H₂ is favored at high T. Therefore, the trick is to *quench* before equilibrium. Forgetting this logic is the single biggest mark loss.
>
> 4. **Confusing acetylene with ethylene (–1 mark):** Acetylene = C₂H₂ (triple bond); Ethylene = C₂H₄ (double bond). KTU examiners are strict on this. Always write the structure once.
>
> 5. **Not writing O₂:CH₄ ratio (–1 mark):** This is a 1-line addition that scores a free mark. Always include it.
>
> 6. **Writing "explosion" without mechanism (–1 mark):** Saying "acetylene explodes" is incomplete. Write: "Acetylene decomposes exothermically (ΔH = –226 kJ/mol) into C + H₂ above 200 kPa, leading to detonation."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before exam):**

- **Acetylene formula:** C₂H₂, IUPAC = Ethyne, with C≡C triple bond. **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Heat of combustion:** 1,300 kJ/mol (highest among hydrocarbons). **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Adiabatic flame temperature (O₂):** ~3,480 K (theoretical: 8,558 K; corrected for dissociation). **[Free 1 mark]**
- **C≡C bond energy:** 965 kJ/mol. **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Decomposition threshold:** 200 kPa — NEVER store as compressed gas. Use acetone + porous mass in cylinders. **[Free 2 marks]**
- **BASF/Sachsse process conditions:** 1,400–1,500 °C, 0.001–0.01 s residence time, O₂:CH₄ = 0.55–0.65, atmospheric pressure. **[Free 3 marks]**
- **Overall reaction (BASF):** $4\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2\text{CO} + 7\text{H}_2$, ΔH = +170 kJ/mol. **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Carbide route:** $\text{CaO} + 3\text{C} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2 + \text{CO}$, then $\text{CaC}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2$. **[Free 2 marks]**
- **Wulff process:** Regenerative, cyclic, uses C₂H₆/LPG, ~1,300 °C, 4-step cycle (heat → crack → steam → reverse). **[Free 3 marks]**
- **Electric arc (Hüls):** 1,600–2,000 °C, 7–8 kV arc, 10–13 MWh/ton energy. Declining. **[Free 2 marks]**
- **Syngas co-product (BASF & Wulff):** CO + H₂ is sent to methanol synthesis — a key economic point. **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Solvent for absorption:** NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) is the modern standard; DMF is older. **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Derivatives to remember:** Vinyl chloride → PVC; Vinyl acetate → PVA; Acrylonitrile → acrylic fiber; Acetaldehyde → acetic acid; BDO → THF; Neoprene → synthetic rubber. **[Free 2 marks]**
- **Yield vs Selectivity:** Yield = Conversion × Selectivity. Always define both. **[Free 1 mark]**
- **Thermodynamic instability above 1,500 °C:** C + H₂ becomes more stable than C₂H₂ — the "Sachse trick" (high T, short τ) overcomes this. **[Free 2 marks]**
- **Industrial reality:** BASF/Sachsse is the **global standard** for petrochemical acetylene (~80% market share). Calcium carbide is now limited to welding/niche applications. **[Free 1 mark]**

> **Total potential "free marks" achievable from this recap alone: ~22 marks in a 14-mark question set.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Butadiene and Phenol-Cumene Process, Polymerization Industries - Polyethylene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Butadiene and Phenol-Cumene Process, Polymerization Industries - Polyethylene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 1,3-Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
1,3-Butadiene (chemical formula **C₄H₆**, molar mass **54.09 g/mol**, boiling point **−4.4 °C**) is a conjugated diene hydrocarbon that exists as a colorless, flammable gas at room temperature. It is the simplest and most industrially significant conjugated diene, serving as the foundational monomer for synthetic rubbers (SBR, NBR, BR, ABS) and a key petrochemical intermediate in the C4 hydrocarbon family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Industrial manufacturing routes of 1,3-butadiene (a) from ethyl alcohol (Lebedev process) and (b) from n-butenes (Houdry / Catadiene dehydrogenation). The student must be able to draw the flow sheet, list the catalysts, and write all key reactions.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Tinker-Toy" Monomer):**
Think of 1,3-butadiene as a *two-claw plastic connector*. The two C=C double bonds act like snapping clips that can interlock with thousands of identical clips, building a long chain called *polybutadiene*. Because it has two reactive ends (a "di-" functional monomer), it crosslinks efficiently with other monomers like styrene to make *Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR)* — the very rubber used in your car tyres. The conjugation (alternating double–single–double bonds) makes it highly reactive, which is exactly why it polymerizes so readily.

---

### 1.2 Phenol–Cumene Process (Isopropylbenzene / Hock Process)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
The Cumene Process (also called the **Hock Process**) is the dominant industrial route for the co-production of **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** and **acetone (CH₃COCH₃)** from benzene and propylene through a three-stage sequence: (1) Friedel–Crafts alkylation of benzene with propylene to form cumene, (2) liquid-phase auto-oxidation of cumene to cumene hydroperoxide (CHP), and (3) acid-catalyzed cleavage of CHP to phenol and acetone. The overall yield is approximately **85–90%** based on cumene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Manufacture of phenol from cumene is a *high-weightage* topic. Students are routinely asked to write reactions, list operating conditions, draw the flow diagram, and discuss the by-product acetone recovery. The reaction is exothermic (~58 kcal/mol), and temperature control is critical to prevent CHP decomposition explosions.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Side-Swap" Trick):**
Imagine you have a benzene ring wearing a "backpack" (the isopropyl group, –CH(CH₃)₂). In step 1, you put the backpack on. In step 2, you carefully *blow oxygen* onto the backpack's "shoulder" (the tertiary C–H), turning it into a peroxide (–OOH). In step 3, you add dilute sulfuric acid, which acts like a pair of molecular scissors that snips the O–O bond and *swaps* an oxygen into the ring, kicking out a small acetone molecule as a bonus. The benzene ring is now *phenol* — with an –OH group instead of a –H.

---

### 1.3 Polyethylene (PE) – Polymerization Industry

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Polyethylene is a semicrystalline thermoplastic polymer (–CH₂–CH₂–)ₙ produced by the addition polymerization of ethylene (CH₂=CH₂). Industrial grades are classified as **LDPE** (low density, 0.910–0.925 g/cm³, high-pressure free-radical process), **HDPE** (high density, 0.941–0.965 g/cm³, low-pressure Ziegler–Natta or Phillips), **LLDPE** (linear low density, made by copolymerization with α-olefins), and **UHMWPE** (ultra-high molecular weight, Mw > 10⁶ g/mol). Global production exceeds **120 million tonnes/year**, making PE the largest-volume synthetic polymer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Manufacturing processes of LDPE (ICI autoclave/tubular reactor, high pressure free-radical) and HDPE (Ziegler–Natta coordination polymerization, Phillips chromium oxide on silica). Students must know catalysts, pressures (1000–3000 atm for LDPE; 1–100 atm for HDPE), temperatures, and product properties.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Bead-Necklace" Polymer):**
Picture ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) as a *single magnetic bead*. If you string **10,000** of them in a row with no branches, you get **HDPE** — a stiff, dense, crystalline rope (used in pipes and milk jugs). If your stringing process is *chaotic and high-energy*, lots of side-branches sprout off the main chain, preventing the chains from packing closely together; the resulting floppy, less-dense material is **LDPE** (used in plastic bags and squeeze bottles). The same monomer, the same chemistry — only the *polymerization conditions* determine the final product!

---

### 1.4 Master Summary of the Three Sub-Topics

| Sub-Topic | Core Feedstock | Primary Product | Dominant Industrial Process | Global Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1,3-Butadiene | n-Butene / n-Butane / Ethanol | Synthetic rubber monomer | Catalytic dehydrogenation (Catadiene) | ~15 Mt/yr |
| Phenol–Cumene | Benzene + Propylene | Phenol (+ Acetone) | Hock process (CHP route) | ~12 Mt/yr phenol |
| Polyethylene | Ethylene (from naphtha cracking) | LDPE / HDPE / LLDPE | High-pressure radical / Ziegler–Natta | ~120 Mt/yr |

> [!NOTE]
> The unifying thread in Module 4 is the **petrochemical value chain**: crude oil → naphtha → ethylene/propylene/butenes → monomers (1,3-butadiene, phenol, ethylene) → polymers (synthetic rubbers, phenolic resins, polyethylene). Understanding each process step is essential for KTU board questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 1,3-Butadiene — Manufacturing Routes

#### 2.1.1 Lebedev Process (Ethanol → 1,3-Butadiene)

**Reaction (one-step, single reactor):**
$$2 \, C_2H_5OH \xrightarrow{\text{MgO–SiO}_2,\,\text{ZnO–Al}_2O_3} CH_2=CH\text{–}CH=CH_2 + 2\,H_2O + H_2$$

- **Catalyst:** Mixed oxide (MgO/SiO₂ or ZnO/Al₂O₃) at **400–450 °C**
- **Yield:** ~70% (older process, used in India/Russia when ethanol is cheap)
- **Mechanism:** Dehydrogenation of ethanol → acetaldehyde → aldol condensation → crotonaldehyde → reduction → butadiene

> [!NOTE]
> Modern Lebedev plants are nearly obsolete for *bulk* butadiene but still appear in Indian B.Tech syllabi. KTU examiners test this as a "name-the-process" question worth 3 marks.

#### 2.1.2 Houdry / Catadiene Process (n-Butene → 1,3-Butadiene)

**Primary reaction (oxidative dehydrogenation):**
$$CH_3\text{–}CH_2\text{–}CH=CH_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} CH_2=CH\text{–}CH=CH_2 + H_2O$$

**Catalyst:** Calcium-nickel phosphate / chromium oxide at **600–650 °C**
**Conditions:** Superheated steam dilutes the C4 to prevent coke; air/O₂ added to drive equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why steam?** The reaction is endothermic. Steam supplies heat *and* prevents carbon deposition. This is a classic *equilibrium-shifting* trick — Le Chatelier's principle in action.

#### 2.1.3 Two-Step Dehydrogenation (n-Butane → n-Butene → 1,3-Butadiene)

Step 1 (Dehydrogenation of butane):
$$C_4H_{10} \xrightarrow{\text{Cr}_2O_3/Al_2O_3,\,550\text{ °C}} C_4H_8 + H_2$$

Step 2 (Oxidative dehydrogenation):
$$C_4H_8 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} C_4H_6 + H_2O$$

---

### 2.2 Phenol–Cumene Process (Hock Process)

#### Stage 1: Alkylation of Benzene with Propylene → Cumene

$$C_6H_6 + CH_3\text{–}CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2SO_4 \text{ or } \text{AlCl}_3} C_6H_5\text{–}CH(CH_3)_2$$

- **Catalyst:** H₃PO₄/SiO₂ (modern) or AlCl₃ (older)
- **Temperature:** 35–50 °C (liquid phase)
- **Pressure:** 1–5 atm
- **Selectivity:** ~96% to cumene

#### Stage 2: Liquid-Phase Air Oxidation of Cumene → Cumene Hydroperoxide (CHP)

$$C_6H_5\text{–}CH(CH_3)_2 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{0.5–3\% initiator,\,100–120 °C}} C_6H_5\text{–}C(CH_3)_2\text{–}OOH$$

- **Mechanism:** Free-radical chain (initiation, propagation, termination)
- **Conversion per pass:** ~25–30% (to prevent CHP buildup → explosion risk)
- **Selectivity:** ~90–95% to CHP
- **Critical safety limit:** CHP concentration in reactor **must stay below 65%** to avoid runaway decomposition

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that cumene hydroperoxide is the *key intermediate* — they jump directly to phenol. Examiners specifically test whether you know the three-stage sequence: cumene → CHP → phenol + acetone.

#### Stage 3: Acid-Catalyzed Cleavage of CHP → Phenol + Acetone

$$C_6H_5\text{–}C(CH_3)_2\text{–}OOH \xrightarrow{\text{dil. }H_2SO_4,\,60\text{ °C}} C_6H_5OH + (CH_3)_2CO$$

- **Mechanism:** 1,2-aryl migration (Hock rearrangement) — concerted, stereo-specific
- **Exothermicity:** ΔH ≈ −58 kcal/mol (releases a *lot* of heat)
- **Yield:** ~85–90% based on cumene

> [!NOTE]
> **Co-product economics:** The cumene process is profitable largely because *both* phenol and acetone are saleable. Phenol : acetone mass ratio is **0.94 : 1**, so ~equal tonnages. This makes the process resilient to acetone market fluctuations.

---

### 2.3 Polyethylene — Polymerization Mechanisms

#### 2.3.1 LDPE — High-Pressure Free-Radical Polymerization

**Initiation:**
$$Initiator \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,R^{\bullet} \quad ; \quad R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \rightarrow R\text{–}CH_2\text{–}CH_2^{\bullet}$$

**Propagation (head-to-tail):**
$$R\text{–}(CH_2\text{–}CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \rightarrow R\text{–}(CH_2\text{–}CH_2)_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

**Termination (combination or disproportionation):**
$$R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow R\text{–}R \quad \text{(combination)}$$

- **Pressure:** **1000–3000 atm** (forces ethylene into supercritical fluid)
- **Temperature:** **80–300 °C**
- **Initiator:** Di-*tert*-butyl peroxide or oxygen
- **Reactor types:** ICI *autoclave* (stirred, ~1000 atm) or *tubular* (long coiled pipe, ~3000 atm)
- **Chain transfer to polymer** → creates long-chain branches → low density

#### 2.3.2 HDPE — Low-Pressure Coordination Polymerization

**Ziegler–Natta catalysis:**
$$n\,CH_2=CH_2 \xrightarrow{TiCl_4/Al(C_2H_5)_3,\,50–100\text{ °C},\,1\text{–}10\text{ atm}} (CH_2\text{–}CH_2)_n$$

- **Catalyst:** TiCl₄ + Al(Et)₃ (heterogeneous, multi-site)
- **Phillips catalysis:** CrO₃ on SiO₂ / Al₂O₃ (single-site, supported)
- **Mechanism:** Cossee–Arlman — monomer inserts into the M–C bond on the catalyst surface
- **Result:** Linear chains, minimal branching → high crystallinity → high density

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question:** "Compare LDPE and HDPE processes on the basis of pressure, catalyst, mechanism, density, and crystallinity." This is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-part. Memorize the table below.

#### 2.3.3 Comparison Table — LDPE vs HDPE

| Parameter | LDPE | HDPE |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure (atm) | 1000–3000 | 1–100 |
| Temperature (°C) | 80–300 | 50–100 |
| Catalyst | Peroxide (radical) | TiCl₄/AlEt₃ or CrO₃/SiO₂ |
| Mechanism | Free radical | Coordination (Ziegler–Natta / Phillips) |
| Branching | Long & short chain | Essentially linear |
| Crystallinity | ~50% | ~80–90% |
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.910–0.925 | 0.941–0.965 |
| Melting point (°C) | 105–115 | 125–135 |
| Tensile strength | Low | High |
| Major uses | Films, bags | Pipes, containers |

---

> Use `\vert` for absolute-value bars in tables (NOT a single `|` character) so the markdown table is not corrupted.

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Units / Conditions |

| 1 | Conversion of CHP per pass | $X_{CHP} = \frac{[CHP]_{in} - [CHP]_{out}}{[CHP]_{in}}$ | dimensionless |

| 13 | Phenol-acetone mass ratio | $r = 94 / 58 = 1.62$ | mass basis |
| 14 | CHP explosion limit | $[CHP]_{max} = 65\,\text{wt}\%$ | safety constraint |
| 15 | Ethylene critical constants | $T_c = 9.2\,°C$, $P_c = 50.5\,\text{atm}$ | defines supercritical regime |

In a KTU 14-marker, you can earn full marks by quoting the right equation **and** explaining its application.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Number-Average Degree of Polymerization (DPₙ) for Radical Polymerization

**Starting point:** A free-radical polymerization of ethylene initiated by peroxide decomposition.

**Step 1 — Write the rate of initiation.**
A peroxide initiator (I) decomposes with rate constant $k_d$ to give two radicals. Only a fraction $f$ (efficiency, $0 < f < 1$) successfully initiates a chain. The rate of *effective* radical generation is:

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state approximation on radicals.**
At steady state, the rate of radical generation equals the rate of radical termination (by combination, $k_t$):

$$R_i = R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

Solving for the radical concentration:

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$$

**Step 3 — Write the rate of propagation.**
Propagation consumes monomer at a rate $R_p$:

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$$

Substituting $[M^{\bullet}]$ from Step 2:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the kinetic chain length $\nu$.**
The kinetic chain length is the average number of monomer molecules consumed *per active radical* before termination:

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p\,[M]}{2 \sqrt{f\,k_d\,k_t\,[I]}}$$

**Step 5 — Relate kinetic chain length to number-average DP.**
If termination occurs by *combination* (most common in ethylene), the dead polymer chain contains *two* radicals joined together, so each dead polymer contains $2\nu$ monomer units. If termination is by *disproportionation*, the number-average DP equals the kinetic chain length:

$$\overline{DP}_n = \begin{cases} 2\nu & \text{combination} \\ \nu & \text{disproportionation} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Express the number-average molecular weight.**

$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \times M_0$$

For polyethylene, $M_0 = 28\,\text{g/mol}$ (–CH₂–CH₂–). For LDPE, typical $\overline{M}_n$ is **20,000–50,000 g/mol**, which corresponds to $\overline{DP}_n \approx 700–1800$.

**Step 7 — Engineer the molecular weight.**
From the formula for $\nu$, we see that:

$$\overline{DP}_n \propto \frac{[M]}{\sqrt{[I]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** To make *higher* molecular weight PE, **decrease** the initiator concentration. To *decrease* DP, **raise** the temperature (which increases $k_d$ more than $k_p$). This single relationship is asked in nearly every KTU board exam under "control of molecular weight."

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Cumene-to-Phenol Material Balance

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A phenol plant processes **1000 kg/h** of cumene (isopropylbenzene, MW = 120 g/mol). The conversion per pass in the oxidation reactor is **30%**, selectivity to CHP is **92%**, and the cleavage step gives **90% yield** of phenol (MW = 94 g/mol) and **90% yield** of acetone (MW = 58 g/mol). Calculate:
(a) Mass of phenol produced per hour.
(b) Mass of acetone produced per hour.
(c) Unreacted cumene recycle (if any).

**Step 1 — Moles of cumene fed per hour.**

$$n_{cumene} = \frac{1000\,\text{kg/h}}{120\,\text{kg/kmol}} = 8.333\,\text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of cumene converted to CHP in the oxidizer.**
Apply the *combined* conversion-selectivity factor:

$$n_{CHP\,formed} = n_{cumene} \times X_{oxidation} \times S_{CHP}$$

$$n_{CHP\,formed} = 8.333 \times 0.30 \times 0.92 = 2.300\,\text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of phenol produced in the cleavage reactor.**
One mole of CHP gives one mole of phenol (and one mole of acetone). Apply the cleavage yield:

$$n_{phenol} = n_{CHP} \times Y_{cleavage} = 2.300 \times 0.90 = 2.070\,\text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass flow of phenol.**

$$\dot{m}_{phenol} = 2.070 \times 94 = 194.6\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of acetone produced.**

$$n_{acetone} = 2.070\,\text{kmol/h} \quad \text{(1:1 stoichiometry)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{acetone} = 2.070 \times 58 = 120.1\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Unreacted cumene available for recycle.**

$$n_{unreacted} = 8.333 - 2.300 = 6.033\,\text{kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{unreacted\,cumene} = 6.033 \times 120 = 723.9\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the mass ratio (sanity check).**

$$\frac{m_{phenol}}{m_{acetone}} = \frac{194.6}{120.1} = 1.62 \quad \checkmark \text{(matches } 94/58 = 1.62\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Phenol = **194.6 kg/h**; Acetone = **120.1 kg/h**; Cumene recycle = **723.9 kg/h**. Overall phenol yield on cumene = 19.46 wt%.

---

| Process | Catalyst | Temperature (°C) | Pressure (atm) | Selectivity (%) | Major By-product |

| Lebedev (EtOH → BD) | MgO–SiO₂ | 400–450 | 1 | ~70 | H₂O, H₂, ethyl ether |
| Houdry (1-butene → BD) | Ca-Ni phosphate | 600–650 | 0.5–1 | ~85 | CO, CO₂, coke |
| Oxidative deH₂ (2-butene → BD) | Bi-Mo / Sb-Sn oxide | 400–500 | 1 | ~90 | H₂O |
| Cumene → CHP (oxidation) | None (radical) | 100–120 | 5–10 | 90–95 | Acetophenone, dicumyl peroxide |
| CHP → Phenol + Acetone | H₂SO₄ (1–3%) | 60 | 1 | 90–95 | α-Methylstyrene |
| LDPE (radical) | Di-*t*-butyl peroxide | 80–300 | 1000–3000 | 95 | PE waxes |
| HDPE (Ziegler–Natta) | TiCl₄ / AlEt₃ | 50–100 | 1–10 | 97 | Atactic PP impurity |

### 3.4 Numerical on Polymerization Kinetics

**Problem:** LDPE is polymerized at 130 °C with initiator concentration $[I]_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol/L}$ and monomer concentration $[M] = 18\,\text{mol/L}$ (supercritical ethylene). Given $k_p = 4.7 \times 10^{3}\,\text{L/(mol·s)}$, $k_d = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $k_t = 7.2 \times 10^{7}\,\text{L/(mol·s)}$, and $f = 0.6$, compute:
(a) Rate of polymerization $R_p$
(b) Kinetic chain length $\nu$
(c) Number-average DP (combination termination)

**Step 1 — Compute $R_p$ directly.**

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$$

$$R_p = 4700 \times 18 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.2 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$R_p = 84{,}600 \times \sqrt{\frac{3.6 \times 10^{-8}}{7.2 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$R_p = 84{,}600 \times \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-16}} = 84{,}600 \times 2.236 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{R_p = 1.89 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/(L·s)}}$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic chain length $\nu$.**

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{R_p}{2 f k_d [I]} = \frac{1.89 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\nu = \frac{1.89 \times 10^{-3}}{7.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 26{,}250$$

**Step 3 — Number-average DP (combination termination).**

$$\overline{DP}_n = 2\nu = 52{,}500$$

**Step 4 — Number-average molecular weight.**

$$\overline{M}_n = 52{,}500 \times 28 = 1.47 \times 10^{6}\,\text{g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\overline{M}_n$ of this magnitude is characteristic of *UHMWPE*, not standard LDPE. This indicates our assumed $[I]$ is low. Increase initiator to ~$5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/L}$ to get commercial LDPE ($\overline{M}_n \sim 50{,}000$). This illustrates the **engineering lever** of initiator concentration.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Phenol–Cumene Process (Hock Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Benzene C6H6] --> B[Friedel-Crafts Alkylation Reactor]
    A2[Fresh Propylene CH3-CH=CH2] --> B
    B -- H3PO4 on SiO2 catalyst, 50 C --> C[Cumene C6H5-CH-CH3-CH3]
    C --> D[Oxidation Reactor]
    D2[Air O2 feed] --> D
    D -- 100 to 120 C, radical initiator --> E[Cumene Hydroperoxide CHP]
    E --> F[Cleavage Reactor]
    F2[Dilute H2SO4, 60 C] --> F
    F --> G[Phenol C6H5OH stream]
    F --> H[Acetone CH3-CO-CH3 stream]
    C -- unreacted recycle --> D
    E -- unreacted recycle --> F

    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Node **B** = alkylation (Friedel–Crafts); output is cumene.
- Node **D** = air oxidation; output is the key intermediate **CHP** (highlighted in red — explosion hazard).
- Node **F** = acid cleavage (highlighted in teal); two product streams exit.
- Recycle loops show unconverted cumene and unconverted CHP being returned to their reactors to maximize overall yield.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — LDPE High-Pressure Tubular Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ethylene Feed Compressor<br/>1 to 1000 atm] --> M1[Primary Compressor]
    M1 --> M2[Hyper Compressor<br/>up to 3000 atm]
    M2 --> R1[Tubular Reactor<br/>length 1 km, 80 to 300 C]
    I1[Peroxide Initiator Injection<br/>multiple points] --> R1
    R1 --> S1[High-Pressure Separator<br/>300 atm]
    S1 --> S2[Low-Pressure Separator<br/>1 atm]
    S2 --> E1[Extruder and Pelletizer]
    S1 -- unreacted ethylene recycle --> M1
    S2 -- unreacted ethylene recycle --> M1
    E1 --> P2[LDPE Pellets<br/>0.910 to 0.925 g per cm3]

    style R1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style M2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **hyper-compressor** is the heart of the LDPE plant — it compresses ethylene from 1000 atm to ~3000 atm.
- The **tubular reactor** is a long, jacketed pipe where initiator is injected at multiple points (to control exotherm).
- The **recycle loop** is critical: only 10–20% of the ethylene is converted per pass; the rest is recompressed.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — HDPE Ziegler–Natta Slurry Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E1[Ethylene Feed CH2=CH2<br/>1 to 10 atm] --> R1[Loop Reactor<br/>or Stirred Autoclave]
    M1[Catalyst TiCl4] --> R1
    M2[Co-catalyst Al-C2H5-3] --> R1
    D1[Diluent n-hexane] --> R1
    R1 -- 50 to 100 C, slurry --> S1[Centrifuge]
    S1 --> S2[Dryer]
    S2 --> E2[Extruder and Pelletizer]
    S1 -- diluent recycle --> R1
    E2 --> P2[HDPE Pellets<br/>0.941 to 0.965 g per cm3]
    M1 -.->|active site| R1

    style R1 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A **slurry (suspension) process** in n-hexane diluent; the catalyst is heterogeneous and is filtered out after the reaction.
- The dashed arrow (`M1 -.-> R1`) indicates the *active site* on the catalyst surface where monomer insertion occurs (Cossee–Arlman mechanism).

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison — Butadiene Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ethanol C2H5OH] -- Lebedev Process, 400 C --> B[1,3-Butadiene]
    C[n-Butene C4H8] -- Oxidative Dehydrogenation, 600 C --> B
    D[n-Butane C4H10] -- Two-step Dehydrogenation --> C
    B --> P1[Synthetic Rubber SBR, BR, NBR]
    B --> P2[ABS Plastics]
    B --> P3[Adiponitrile Nylon 66]

    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> All three feedstocks converge to 1,3-butadiene. The dominant commercial route globally is the **Catadiene / oxidative dehydrogenation of n-butenes** obtained from steam crackers. The Lebedev route survives only in regions with cheap ethanol (e.g., India, Brazil).
