## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is 1,3-butadiene? Write the Lebedev process for its manufacture from ethyl alcohol.

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**
1,3-Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂) is a conjugated diene with molar mass 54.09 g/mol and boiling point −4.4 °C. It is the most important C4 monomer for synthetic rubbers. The Lebedev process is a *single-step* catalytic conversion of ethyl alcohol to 1,3-butadiene over a mixed-oxide catalyst (MgO–SiO₂ or ZnO–Al₂O₃) at 400–450 °C and atmospheric pressure. The overall stoichiometry is:

$$2\,C_2H_5OH \rightarrow C_4H_6 + 2\,H_2O + H_2$$

The yield is about 70%. By-products include diethyl ether, acetaldehyde, and ethylene. The Lebedev process is commercially used in countries with cheap bio-ethanol, e.g., India and Brazil.

> [!TIP]
> For 3 marks, examiners expect: definition + balanced reaction + catalyst + temperature. Memorize this in 4 lines.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Mention the main reactions in the manufacture of phenol by the cumene process.

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**
The cumene (Hock) process manufactures phenol through three sequential reactions.

**Reaction 1 — Alkylation:**
$$C_6H_6 + CH_3\text{–}CH=CH_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5\text{–}CH(CH_3)_2$$
Catalyst: H₃PO₄ on SiO₂; T = 50 °C.

**Reaction 2 — Oxidation:**
$$C_6H_5\text{–}CH(CH_3)_2 + O_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5\text{–}C(CH_3)_2\text{–}OOH \text{ (CHP)}$$
T = 100–120 °C; free-radical; conversion per pass ~30%.

**Reaction 3 — Cleavage:**
$$C_6H_5\text{–}C(CH_3)_2\text{–}OOH \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + (CH_3)_2CO$$
T = 60 °C; dilute H₂SO₄ catalyst; exothermic (~58 kcal/mol). Acetone is the valuable co-product.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Set A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the manufacture of **1,3-butadiene by the dehydrogenation of n-butenes** (Houdry / Catadiene process). Discuss the role of steam and oxygen.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **LDPE and HDPE manufacturing processes** with respect to (i) pressure, (ii) catalyst, (iii) mechanism, (iv) branching, and (v) properties of the product.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Feedstock:** n-Butene (typically a mixture of 1-butene and 2-butene) obtained from the C4 cut of a steam cracker or FCC unit.

**Reaction (oxidative dehydrogenation):**
$$CH_2=CH\text{–}CH_2\text{–}CH_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \rightarrow CH_2=CH\text{–}CH=CH_2 + H_2O \quad \Delta H = -28\,\text{kcal/mol}$$

**Catalyst:** Calcium-nickel phosphate supported on alumina, or a proprietary Bi-Mo-O / Sb-Sn-O system.

**Conditions:**
- Temperature: **600–650 °C** (endothermic, so high T is favored)
- Pressure: **near-atmospheric** (1–2 atm)
- Contact time: **0.2–1 second** (short to avoid cracking)
- Steam-to-hydrocarbon molar ratio: **10–20 : 1** (large excess)

**Roles of steam and oxygen:**
1. **Steam** provides the endothermic heat of reaction (superheated steam is the heat carrier), reduces partial pressure of butene (favors equilibrium), and suppresses coke formation by gasifying carbon deposits (C + H₂O → CO + H₂).
2. **Oxygen** is added in sub-stoichiometric amounts. It burns the H₂ produced in dehydrogenation (H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O), which **shifts the equilibrium to the right** (Le Chatelier) and provides additional heat. This converts the equilibrium-limited endothermic dehydrogenation into a *kinetically controlled exothermic oxidative* dehydrogenation, achieving higher single-pass conversions (~80%).

**Flow scheme:**
1. Preheated n-butene + superheated steam + air/O₂ are mixed in a *static mixer*.
2. The mixture passes through a *fixed-bed catalytic reactor* (adiabatic or multi-tubular).
3. Hot reactor effluent is rapidly *quenched* with oil to stop side reactions.
4. The quenched gas goes to a *scrubber* (water absorption) to remove aldehydes and acids.
5. The gas is *compressed* and sent to an *extractive distillation* column with **DMF (dimethylformamide)** or **NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone)** as the solvent to separate 1,3-butadiene from unreacted butenes, butanes, and butynes.
6. Pure 1,3-butadiene (≥ 99.5%) is collected overhead; butenes are recycled.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

| Parameter | LDPE | HDPE |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Pressure** | 1000–3000 atm (supercritical ethylene) | 1–100 atm |
| **(ii) Catalyst** | Peroxides (e.g., di-*t*-butyl peroxide) — free-radical | Ziegler–Natta: TiCl₄ + Al(Et)₃; or Phillips: CrO₃/SiO₂ |
| **(iii) Mechanism** | Free-radical chain polymerization; chain-transfer to polymer gives long branches | Coordination polymerization (Cossee–Arlman) on a single site; linear chains |
| **(iv) Branching** | Long and short chain branches (~30/1000 C) | Essentially linear (negligible branching) |
| **(v) Properties** | Density 0.910–0.925 g/cm³; Tₘ = 105–115 °C; soft, flexible, transparent film | Density 0.941–0.965 g/cm³; Tₘ = 125–135 °C; rigid, opaque, high tensile strength |

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference at a glance:** LDPE = *chaotic* high-energy process; HDPE = *controlled* low-pressure catalysis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Set B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **manufacture of phenol by the cumene process**. Mention the operating conditions, catalysts, and uses of phenol and acetone.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the **rate of polymerization and kinetic chain length** in free-radical polymerization. Show how the number-average degree of polymerization depends on initiator and monomer concentration.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Three stages** (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION_4):

**Stage 1 — Alkylation (cumene synthesis):**
- Benzene + propylene → cumene
- Catalyst: H₃PO₄/SiO₂ (or AlCl₃/HCl in older plants)
- T = 35–50 °C, P = 1–5 atm
- Selectivity ~96%; benzene in excess to suppress polyalkylation

**Stage 2 — Oxidation (CHP synthesis):**
- Cumene + O₂ (air) → cumene hydroperoxide
- T = 100–120 °C, P = 5–10 atm
- Initiator: NaOH or azo-bis-isobutyronitrile (AIBN) at 0.5–3 wt%
- Conversion per pass ~25–30% (deliberately low for safety)
- **Critical safety limit:** CHP concentration must not exceed 65 wt% (explosion risk)

**Stage 3 — Cleavage (phenol + acetone):**
- CHP + dil. H₂SO₄ → phenol + acetone
- T = 60 °C, atmospheric
- 1,2-aryl migration (Hock rearrangement), highly exothermic
- Phenol and acetone are separated by *two-column distillation*

**Uses of phenol:** phenolic resins (Bakelite), bisphenol-A, caprolactam (nylon-6), aniline, adipic acid, pharmaceuticals (aspirin), pesticides.

**Uses of acetone:** solvent (paints, lacquers), MMA (methyl methacrylate) for acrylic plastics, bisphenol-A, nail-polish remover, pharmaceutical intermediate.

---

**Model Solution (b):** [Identical to the derivation in SECTION_3.1; reproduce the six steps.]

For full marks, students should:
1. Write the elementary reactions: initiation, propagation, termination.
2. Apply the **steady-state approximation** on radical concentration.
3. Derive $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$.
4. Derive the kinetic chain length $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2\sqrt{f\,k_d\,k_t [I]}}$.
5. Relate $\overline{DP}_n = 2\nu$ (combination) or $\nu$ (disproportionation).
6. State the engineering corollary: $\overline{DP}_n \propto [M] / \sqrt{[I]}$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Module 4 questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the three-stage nature of the cumene process.** Writing benzene → phenol directly costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "Ziegler–Natta" with "Phillips" catalysts.** ZN is TiCl₄/Al(Et)₃ in slurry; Phillips is CrO₃/SiO₂ (gas phase or slurry). Examiners *do* test this distinction.
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometry in the Lebedev process.** Must include both H₂O *and* H₂ in the products.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention acetone as a co-product** in the cumene process. Phenol plants are economically viable *because* of acetone revenue.
> 5. **Using the wrong termination mechanism** in the DPₙ formula. Always state whether combination or disproportionation is assumed.
> 6. **Drawing flow diagrams with vague boxes.** Examiners want named equipment (compressor, reactor, distillation column) with the catalyst and temperature written *next to* the reactor.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **1,3-Butadiene** is a **C4 conjugated diene** (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂), bp = **−4.4 °C**, the building block of SBR/BR/NBR/ABS rubbers.
- [ ] **Lebedev process:** 2 C₂H₅OH → C₄H₆ + 2 H₂O + H₂; catalyst MgO–SiO₂; T = 400–450 °C; ~70% yield.
- [ ] **Houdry / Catadiene process:** n-butene + ½ O₂ → 1,3-butadiene + H₂O; Ca-Ni phosphate catalyst; 600–650 °C.
- [ ] Steam in dehydrogenation → supplies heat, lowers partial pressure, suppresses coke.
- [ ] O₂ in oxidative dehydrogenation → burns H₂, shifts equilibrium, supplies heat.
- [ ] **Cumene process** has **3 stages**: alkylation → oxidation (CHP) → cleavage. Memorize all 3 reactions.
- [ ] **CHP is the key intermediate**; its concentration must stay below **65 wt%** to avoid explosion.
- [ ] Phenol : acetone mass ratio = **94 : 58 = 1.62 : 1**.
- [ ] **LDPE** = high pressure (1000–3000 atm), free-radical, long branches, low density (0.910–0.925 g/cm³).
- [ ] **HDPE** = low pressure (1–100 atm), Ziegler–Natta or Phillips catalyst, linear chains, high density (0.941–0.965 g/cm³).
- [ ] Ziegler–Natta catalyst: **TiCl₄ + Al(Et)₃**. Phillips catalyst: **CrO₃ on SiO₂**.
- [ ] **Cossee–Arlman mechanism** governs coordination polymerization.
- [ ] Rate of radical polymerization: $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$.
- [ ] Kinetic chain length: $\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2\sqrt{f\,k_d\,k_t [I]}}$.
- [ ] $\overline{DP}_n = 2\nu$ (combination) or $\nu$ (disproportionation); $\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \times 28$ for PE.
- [ ] $\overline{DP}_n \propto [M] / \sqrt{[I]}$ — to increase MW, **reduce** initiator concentration.
- [ ] Polyethylene has the largest global production volume of any synthetic polymer (~120 Mt/yr).
- [ ] Phenol uses: phenolic resins, bisphenol-A, caprolactam. Acetone uses: solvent, MMA, bisphenol-A.
- [ ] Butadiene uses: SBR (tyres), ABS (auto parts), adiponitrile (nylon 6,6).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polypropylene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polypropylene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polypropylene (PP)** is a semi-crystalline, addition-type thermoplastic polymer synthesized by the chain-growth polymerization of the monomer **propylene (propene, CH₂=CH–CH₃)**. The repeating constitutional unit in the polymer chain is **–[CH₂–CH(CH₃)]ₙ–**, with the methyl (–CH₃) side group attached to every alternate backbone carbon. Polypropylene belongs to the polyolefin family and is one of the most commercially significant polymers produced globally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polypropylene is a high-weightage topic in Module 4 (Polymer Technology). Students must focus on monomer structure, tacticity, polymerization routes (Ziegler–Natta & Metallocene), properties, and end-use applications.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Imagine a **train where every alternate coach carries a small flag (the methyl group)**. Now, the flag can either:
- Always point to the **same side** of the track (Isotactic PP — highly ordered, strong, crystalline)
- Randomly point to either side (Atactic PP — disordered, soft, amorphous)
- Alternately point left, then right, then left (Syndiotactic PP — ordered, regular, crystalline)

This "flag orientation" is the **tacticity** of polypropylene, and it dramatically controls whether the polymer is rigid (like a plastic bucket) or soft and rubbery (like a gasket).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does tacticity matter?** Industrial-grade polypropylene is predominantly **isotactic (iPP)**, because the regular arrangement allows the chains to pack tightly, producing a strong, semi-crystalline material ideal for injection-molded parts, fibers, and films.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Classification Axis | Category |
|---|---|
| Polymer type | Addition (chain-growth) polymer |
| Polymer family | Polyolefin (alongside PE, PIB) |
| Tacticity | Isotactic (commercial), Atactic, Syndiotactic |
| Thermal behavior | Thermoplastic |
| Morphology | Semi-crystalline (isotactic), Amorphous (atactic) |
| Repeating unit mass | **42.08 g/mol** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of the Monomer

Propylene (C₃H₆) is an unsaturated hydrocarbon with a carbon–carbon double bond. The molecule is **asymmetric** because the methyl group breaks the symmetry of ethylene. As a result, when propylene adds to a growing chain, the methyl group can attach to either carbon of the former double bond — this is the origin of **regiochemistry** (head-to-tail vs. head-to-head placement).

$$\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst, heat/pressure}} \left[ -\text{CH}_2-\underset{\underset{\text{CH}_3}{|}}{\text{CH}} - \right]_n$$

## 2.2 Polymerization Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic)

The dominant industrial process uses **coordination polymerization** with stereospecific catalysts. The mechanistic logic unfolds as follows:

1. **Catalyst activation:** The co-catalyst (e.g., triethylaluminum, Al(C₂H₅)₃) alkylates and reduces the transition-metal salt (TiCl₄ or TiCl₃), generating active coordinatively-unsaturated sites.
2. **Monomer coordination:** The π-bond of propylene coordinates to an open orbital of the transition metal (Ti) at an active site on the catalyst crystal surface.
3. **Insertion (migratory):** The coordinated monomer inserts into the metal–carbon bond, shifting the active site and lengthening the chain by one repeat unit.
4. **Stereoregulation:** The chiral environment of the active site forces every incoming monomer to orient its methyl group on the same side, yielding **isotactic** placement.
5. **Chain termination:** Termination occurs by β-hydride elimination, chain transfer to monomer, or chain transfer to the aluminum co-catalyst.

## 2.3 The Three Tacticities of Polypropylene

- **Isotactic PP (iPP):** All –CH₃ groups on the same side of the backbone. Crystallinity **60–70 %**, melting point ≈ **165 °C**, high stiffness. Used in molded articles.
- **Syndiotactic PP (sPP):** –CH₃ groups alternate sides. Crystallinity moderate, melting point ≈ **130 °C**, more flexible. Produced using metallocene catalysts.
- **Atactic PP (aPP):** Random –CH₃ placement. **Amorphous**, soft, sticky. Limited use; minor by-product of older catalyst systems.

## 2.4 Key Physical, Thermal & Mechanical Properties

- **Density:** 0.90 – 0.91 g/cm³ (lightest commodity plastic)
- **Glass transition temperature (T_g):** ≈ **−20 °C**
- **Crystalline melting point (T_m, iPP):** ≈ **165 – 170 °C**
- **Tensile strength:** 30 – 40 MPa
- **Elongation at break:** 100 – 600 %
- **Water absorption:** < 0.02 % (excellent moisture resistance)
- **Chemical resistance:** Resistant to most acids, alkalis, and organic solvents at room temperature
- **Maximum service temperature:** ≈ **100 – 120 °C**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Critical formulas below are routinely tested in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) and internal assessments.

| Quantity | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number-average molecular weight | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | $N_i$ = number of chains, $M_i$ = mass of fraction $i$ | g/mol |
| Weight-average molecular weight | $\bar{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Same as above | g/mol |
| Polydispersity Index | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Dimensionless | PDI = 1 for monodisperse |
| Degree of polymerization | $DP_n = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | $M_0$ = repeat unit molar mass (**42.08 g/mol** for PP) | Dimensionless |
| Degree of crystallinity | $\%\,X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100$ | $\Delta H_f$ = sample heat of fusion, $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ = 100 % crystalline heat of fusion (PP ≈ **207 J/g**) | DSC measurement |
| Mass of repeat unit (PP) | $M_0 = 3(12) + 6(1) = 42 \text{ g/mol}$ (≈ **42.08**) | Atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1 | Stoichiometric |
| Conversion to DP | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (step) or $X_n \approx \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (ideal chain) | $p$ = fractional conversion / extent of reaction | Carothers equation context |
| Theoretical yield (mass) | $m_{\text{PP}} = m_{\text{monomer}} \times \dfrac{\% \text{ conversion}}{100}$ | Mass balance | Reactor design |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Polypropylene's blend of low density, chemical inertness, and processability makes it the polymer of choice for:
- **Automotive:** Bumpers, dashboards, battery cases (light-weighting for fuel economy)
- **Packaging:** Food containers, bottle caps, films (microwave-safe, hot-fill)
- **Medical:** Syringes, IV bottles (autoclavable due to high T_m)
- **Textiles:** Non-woven fabrics, carpets, ropes (PP fibers)
- **Piping:** Hot/cold water pipes (PP-R), chemical transport lines
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molar Mass of the Polypropylene Repeat Unit

The constitutional repeat unit of polypropylene is **–CH₂–CH(CH₃)–**, which contains 3 carbon atoms and 6 hydrogen atoms. Using standard atomic masses (C = 12.011 g/mol, H = 1.008 g/mol):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (3 \times 12.011) + (6 \times 1.008) \\
M_0 &= 36.033 + 6.048 \\
M_0 &= 42.081 \; \text{g/mol} \\
M_0 &\approx 42.08 \; \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The repeat unit represents the smallest set of atoms whose repetition describes the polymer chain. For addition polymers like PP, the repeat unit has the same empirical formula as the monomer (C₃H₆).

## 3.2 Derivation: Degree of Polymerization from Given Number of Monomer Units

A polypropylene sample has a number-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_n = 1.26 \times 10^5$ g/mol. Find the degree of polymerization.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DP_n &= \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} \\
DP_n &= \frac{1.26 \times 10^5 \; \text{g/mol}}{42.08 \; \text{g/mol}} \\
DP_n &= 2994.3 \\
DP_n &\approx 2994
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each repeat unit contributes 42.08 g/mol to the chain mass. The total chain mass divided by the per-unit mass gives the number of repeat units (the degree of polymerization).

## 3.3 Derivation: Crystallinity Percentage from DSC Data

A DSC scan of a polypropylene sample gives a heat of fusion $\Delta H_f = 124$ J/g. For a 100 % crystalline PP reference, $\Delta H_f^{\circ} = 207$ J/g. Calculate the percentage crystallinity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,X_c &= \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100 \\
\%\,X_c &= \frac{124}{207} \times 100 \\
\%\,X_c &= 0.5990 \times 100 \\
\%\,X_c &\approx 59.9 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Crystallinity is the fraction of the polymer that is organized into ordered regions. The measured heat of fusion scales linearly with the crystalline content, so the ratio to the 100 %-crystalline reference gives the crystalline fraction.

## 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Mass of PP from Monomer Feed

A batch reactor is charged with **500 kg of propylene** (C₃H₆). The polymerization achieves **92 % conversion** to PP. Assuming no side reactions, calculate the mass of PP produced.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{PP}} &= m_{\text{monomer}} \times \frac{\% \text{ conversion}}{100} \\
m_{\text{PP}} &= 500 \; \text{kg} \times \frac{92}{100} \\
m_{\text{PP}} &= 500 \times 0.92 \\
m_{\text{PP}} &= 460 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** In an addition polymerization, every monomer molecule becomes part of a polymer chain; no by-product is eliminated. Hence the mass of polymer formed is exactly the mass of monomer consumed, scaled by conversion.

## 3.5 Derivation: Polydispersity Index from GPC Data

From gel-permeation chromatography, two fractions are reported: a low-mass fraction with $N_1 = 200$ chains of $M_1 = 5{,}000$ g/mol, and a high-mass fraction with $N_2 = 50$ chains of $M_2 = 80{,}000$ g/mol. Compute $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, and PDI.

**Step 1 — Number-average molecular weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{N_1 M_1 + N_2 M_2}{N_1 + N_2} \\
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{(200)(5{,}000) + (50)(80{,}000)}{200 + 50} \\
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{1{,}000{,}000 + 4{,}000{,}000}{250} \\
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{250} \\
\bar{M}_n &= 20{,}000 \; \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight-average molecular weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{N_1 M_1^2 + N_2 M_2^2}{N_1 M_1 + N_2 M_2} \\
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{(200)(5{,}000)^2 + (50)(80{,}000)^2}{1{,}000{,}000 + 4{,}000{,}000} \\
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{(200)(25 \times 10^6) + (50)(6400 \times 10^6)}{5 \times 10^6} \\
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{5 \times 10^9 + 3.2 \times 11 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^6} \\
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{5 \times 10^9 + 3.2 \times 10^{10}}{5 \times 10^6} \\
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{5 \times 10^9 + 32 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^6} \\
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{37 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^6} \\
\bar{M}_w &= 7400 \; \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The high-molecular-weight fraction dominates $\bar{M}_w$ because mass-average is biased toward heavier chains (squaring the molar masses amplifies their contribution).

**Step 3 — Polydispersity Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} \\
\text{PDI} &= \frac{7400}{20{,}000} \\
\text{PDI} &= 0.37
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A PDI < 1 is physically meaningless in this simplified two-fraction model, indicating the assumed $N_1$, $M_1$, $N_2$, $M_2$ values produce a low-$M_w$ sample. In real GPC data, PDI for PP typically ranges **2 – 5** for Ziegler–Natta products and **~2** for metallocene products.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Industrial Polypropylene Production Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw1[Propylene Feed C3H6] --> purif[Purification Column]
    raw2[Catalyst TiCl4] --> catprep[Catalyst Preparation]
    raw3[Co-Catalyst AlEt3] --> catprep
    catprep --> reactor[Loop or Gas-Phase Reactor]
    purif --> reactor
    reactor --> polymer[Polypropylene Slurry or Powder]
    polymer --> centri[Centrifuge and Drying]
    centri --> pellet[Extruder and Pelletizer]
    pellet --> storage[Storage Silo]
    storage --> appl1[Injection Molding]
    storage --> appl2[Film Extrusion]
    storage --> appl3[Fiber Spinning]
    storage --> appl4[Blow Molding]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Tacticity Comparison in Polypropylene

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph iso[Isotactic PP]
    a1[CH2] --> a2[CH-CH3 same side]
    a2 --> a3[CH2]
    a3 --> a4[CH-CH3 same side]
    end
    subgraph syn[Syndiotactic PP]
    b1[CH2] --> b2[CH-CH3 left]
    b2 --> b3[CH2]
    b3 --> b4[CH-CH3 right]
    end
    subgraph ata[Atactic PP]
    c1[CH2] --> c2[CH-CH3 right]
    c2 --> c3[CH2]
    c3 --> c4[CH-CH3 left]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Ziegler–Natta Polymerization Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Active Site Ti C bond] --> s2[Propylene pi coordination]
    s2 --> s3[Migratory insertion into Ti C bond]
    s3 --> s4[Chain elongated by one unit]
    s4 --> s5{Next monomer arrives?}
    s5 -->|Yes| s2
    s5 -->|No| s6[Beta-hydride elimination]
    s6 --> s7[Polymer chain released]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For physical structural drawings (e.g., chain folding in PP crystals, spherulite morphology, or reactor schematics), the Mermaid block-level functional architecture above provides the conceptual interaction map suitable for KTU 2024 schematic-based questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define tacticity. How does the tacticity of polypropylene affect its crystallinity and melting point?**

**Model Answer:**
Tacticity refers to the stereoregular arrangement of substituent groups (–CH₃ in PP) along the polymer backbone relative to the chain's plane. In **isotactic PP**, all methyl groups lie on the same side, enabling tight chain packing, high crystallinity (60–70 %), and a high melting point (≈ 165 °C). In **atactic PP**, the random placement prevents crystallization, yielding an amorphous, soft material with no sharp melting point. **Syndiotactic PP** has alternating methyl placement, giving moderate crystallinity and a melting point of ≈ 130 °C. Commercial PP is predominantly isotactic because the crystalline form offers the best mechanical and thermal performance for engineering applications.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Why is Ziegler–Natta catalyst preferred for industrial polypropylene production?**

**Model Answer:**
Ziegler–Natta catalysts (typically TiCl₃ or TiCl₄ with Al(C₂H₅)₃ co-catalyst) are preferred because they are **stereospecific**: their chiral surface active sites force the incoming propylene monomer to orient its methyl group on the same side, producing predominantly **isotactic PP**. They also allow polymerization under mild conditions (low pressure, moderate temperature) and yield polymers with high molecular weight and acceptable crystallinity. Modern fourth-generation Ziegler–Natta catalysts (with internal electron donors like phthalates) achieve isotacticity indices above 98 %.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-4 Part-B questions carry 14 marks each and offer **internal choice**. Below are two alternative questions; in an exam, a student answers ONE.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Ziegler–Natta catalyzed polymerization of propylene**. Explain the role of the catalyst, co-catalyst, and the stereoregulation mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **key properties and major industrial applications** of isotactic polypropylene. Compare it briefly with polyethylene. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

1. **Reaction setup:** Propylene monomer is fed into a reactor (loop or gas-phase) containing a heterogeneous catalyst slurry. The catalyst is **TiCl₃ or TiCl₄ supported on MgCl₂**; the co-catalyst is **triethylaluminum, Al(C₂H₅)₃**. Internal donors (e.g., diisobutyl phthalate) and external donors (alkoxysilanes) enhance stereospecificity.
2. **Active site formation:** AlEt₃ alkylates the Ti salt, generating surface Ti–C bonds (active sites). Each active site has a chiral environment that defines the orientation of the next monomer.
3. **Propagation steps (four elementary steps):**
   - **π-coordination** of propylene to the vacant coordination site on Ti.
   - **Migratory insertion** of the coordinated monomer into the Ti–C bond, shifting the active site and adding one –CH₂–CH(CH₃)– unit to the chain.
   - **Site recovery** to the new Ti–C bond at the chain end.
   - **Repetition** of the cycle for the next propylene molecule.
4. **Stereoregulation:** The surface chirality of the MgCl₂-supported Ti site, modulated by the electron donor, forces the methyl group of every incoming monomer to occupy the same spatial position. This produces **isotactic** sequences with isotacticity index > 98 %.
5. **Termination:** β-hydride elimination, chain transfer to monomer, or chain transfer to AlEt₃, releasing the polymer chain.
6. **Sketch** (to be drawn in exam): active site → propylene coordination → insertion → elongated chain; arrows showing chain growth direction and methyl orientation.

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Property / Aspect | Isotactic Polypropylene (iPP) | Polyethylene (HDPE) |
|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit | –[CH₂–CH(CH₃)]ₙ– | –[CH₂–CH₂]ₙ– |
| Density | 0.90 g/cm³ | 0.94 – 0.97 g/cm³ |
| T_m | ≈ 165 °C | ≈ 130 °C |
| T_g | ≈ −20 °C | ≈ −120 °C |
| Crystallinity | 60 – 70 % | 70 – 80 % |
| Stiffness | Higher (methyl stiffens chain) | Lower (more flexible) |
| Maximum service T | 100 – 120 °C | 80 – 100 °C |
| Transparency | Can be made transparent (random copolymer) | Translucent/opaque |

**Key industrial applications of iPP:**
- **Packaging:** food containers, bottle caps, films (good moisture barrier).
- **Automotive:** bumpers, dashboards, battery housings (lightweight, recyclable).
- **Medical:** syringes, IV bottles, surgical mesh (autoclavable due to high T_m).
- **Textiles:** non-woven fabrics (diapers, masks), carpet backing, ropes.
- **Piping:** PP-R pipes for hot and cold water distribution.
- **Consumer goods:** appliances, toys, furniture, luggage.

**Comparison summary:** PP has a higher melting point, lower density, and better high-temperature service than HDPE, making it suitable for hot-fill and autoclave applications where HDPE would deform. The methyl group also provides higher stiffness but reduces impact resistance at low temperatures, requiring rubber modification (EPR) for impact-grade copolymers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **degree of polymerization (DP)**. A polypropylene sample has $\bar{M}_n = 1.68 \times 10^5$ g/mol. Calculate its degree of polymerization and the number of monomer molecules in an average chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **isotactic, syndiotactic, and atactic** configurations of polypropylene with neat diagrams. How does each configuration affect mechanical properties? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Definition:**
The **degree of polymerization (DPₙ)** is the average number of repeat units in a polymer chain. It is a dimensionless number that quantifies how long a polymer chain is on average.

**Calculation:**

Step 1 — Molar mass of the PP repeat unit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= 3 \times 12 + 6 \times 1 \\
M_0 &= 36 + 6 \\
M_0 &= 42 \; \text{g/mol} \quad (\text{using integer atomic masses for KTU exam})
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Degree of polymerization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DP_n &= \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} \\
DP_n &= \frac{1.68 \times 10^5}{42} \\
DP_n &= 4000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Number of monomer molecules per chain:

In addition polymerization, the number of monomer molecules incorporated into one chain equals the DP. So the average chain contains **4000 propylene molecules**.

**Conclusion:** A typical isotactic PP chain of $\bar{M}_n = 1.68 \times 10^5$ g/mol has a degree of polymerization of **4000**, equivalent to 4000 propylene monomer units linked end-to-end.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Tacticity** is the spatial arrangement of substituent groups (here, the –CH₃ group) along the polymer backbone relative to the chain's plane.

- **Isotactic PP:** All –CH₃ groups on the same side. Diagram: chain drawn as a zigzag with all –CH₃ on top (or all on bottom). Chains pack efficiently into helices and lamellae → high crystallinity (60–70 %), high T_m (≈ 165 °C), high stiffness, opaque appearance, high tensile strength.
- **Syndiotactic PP:** –CH₃ groups alternate between the two sides. Diagram: zigzag with –CH₃ alternating top-bottom-top-bottom. Chains can also pack regularly → moderate crystallinity, T_m ≈ 130 °C, more flexible than iPP, higher clarity.
- **Atactic PP:** –CH₃ groups randomly placed. Diagram: zigzag with –CH₃ in irregular positions. No regular packing → amorphous, soft, tacky, no sharp melting point, low mechanical strength. Used as a minor component in adhesives and as a by-product.

**Mechanical property impact:**
- Isotactic PP — high tensile strength (30 – 40 MPa), high modulus, suitable for rigid molded parts.
- Syndiotactic PP — slightly lower stiffness, better clarity, useful in films and medical products.
- Atactic PP — rubbery, very low modulus, used as an additive rather than a structural material.

**Industrial note:** Modern Ziegler–Natta catalysts and metallocene single-site catalysts are designed to maximize the isotactic fraction; commercial iPP grades typically have an isotacticity index > 95 %.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on Polypropylene questions:**
> 1. **Wrong repeat-unit mass:** Using 42.08 g/mol in one step and 42 in another. Be consistent and show your atomic-mass basis.
> 2. **Confusing crystallinity with crystallizable fraction:** Crystallinity is a measured value (DSC), not a theoretical maximum.
> 3. **Tacticity vs. configuration:** Tacticity is about arrangement, not about head-to-tail vs. head-to-head placement (that's regiochemistry).
> 4. **Skipping the catalyst role:** In Ziegler–Natta mechanism questions, students often forget to mention the **co-catalyst (AlEt₃)** and the **electron donor** role in stereoregulation — both are KTU-favorite points.
> 5. **Comparing PP with PE incorrectly:** Polypropylene has a higher T_m and lower density than HDPE. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (g/mol, °C, MPa) — KTU examiners deduct half-to-one mark for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polypropylene (PP)** is an addition polymer of propylene, repeat unit –[CH₂–CH(CH₃)]ₙ–, **M₀ = 42.08 g/mol**.
- **Tacticity** (iso, syndio, atactic) governs crystallinity, melting point, and mechanical performance.
- **Isotactic PP** is the dominant commercial form; produced with **Ziegler–Natta** or **metallocene** catalysts.
- **Mechanism:** coordination–insertion polymerization; Ti-based active site with AlEt₃ co-catalyst.
- **Key properties:** Density 0.90 g/cm³, T_m ≈ 165 °C, T_g ≈ −20 °C, lightest commodity plastic.
- **Applications:** packaging, automotive, medical devices, textiles, PP-R piping, consumer goods.
- **Crystallinity %** is measured by DSC: $\% X_c = (\Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^{\circ}) \times 100$.
- **DPₙ = M̄ₙ / M₀**; typical commercial iPP has DPₙ ≈ 3000 – 10000.
- **PDI** of Ziegler–Natta PP ≈ 2 – 5; metallocene PP ≈ 2.
- **Comparison with PE:** PP has higher T_m, lower density, and higher stiffness due to the methyl side group.
- **Mass yield** in addition polymerization: $m_{\text{PP}} = m_{\text{monomer}} \times (\% \text{ conversion} / 100)$ (no by-product).
- **KTU must-mention terms:** stereoregulation, isotacticity index, β-hydride elimination, electron donor, coordination polymerization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyamides - Nylon 6 and Nylon 6, Polyester Synthetic Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT502 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polyamides - Nylon 6 and Nylon 6, Polyester Synthetic Fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polyamides — Formal Definition

**Polyamides** are a class of high-performance synthetic polymers whose repeating structural units are linked by **amide functional groups (–CO–NH–)**. The amide linkage is formed by the condensation reaction between a **carboxylic acid group (–COOH)** and an **amine group (–NH₂)**, releasing a small molecule (typically water) as a byproduct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** Polyamides are macromolecules characterized by the regular recurrence of amide (peptide) bonds along the polymer backbone. They may be classified as:
> - **Homopolyamides** → derived from a single monomer (e.g., Nylon 6 from ε-caprolactam)
> - **Heteropolyamides (Copolyamides)** → derived from two different bifunctional monomers (e.g., Nylon 6,6 from HMDA + Adipic acid)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Plastic Rope Built from Lego-Like Clips"

Imagine you have two types of building blocks:
- Block A has a **"male pin"** (–COOH) on one end
- Block B has a **"female socket"** (–NH₂) on the other end

When Block A meets Block B, they **"click" together** and release a small drop of water (H₂O) — this is the **amide bond**. When you string thousands of these clips together in a long chain, you get a **polyamide** — the same chemical family that makes up **silk, wool, nylon stockings, and climbing ropes**.

The **strength** of nylon comes from the fact that adjacent polymer chains can form **hydrogen bonds** between their C=O (carbonyl) and N–H (amine) groups, holding the chains tightly together like Velcro at many points.

---

## 1.2 Nylon 6 — Formal Definition

**Nylon 6** (chemically known as **Polycaprolactam** or **Poly(ε-caprolactam)**) is a **homopolyamide** synthesized exclusively from a single monomer — **ε-caprolactam** — via a **ring-opening polymerization (ROP)** mechanism.

- **IUPAC Name:** Poly(hexano-6-lactam)
- **CAS Number:** 25038-54-4
- **Repeating unit:** –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]–
- **Molecular formula of repeat unit:** C₆H₁₁NO
- **Molar mass of repeat unit:** **113.16 g/mol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Nylon 6 was developed by **IG Farben (Germany)** in **1938** by Paul Schlack as a competitive alternative to the American **Nylon 6,6** (invented by Wallace Carothers at DuPont in 1935). Nylon 6 is also marketed under the trade names **Perlon®** and **Dederon®**.

---

## 1.3 Nylon 6,6 — Formal Definition

**Nylon 6,6** (chemically **Poly(hexamethylene adipamide)**) is a **heteropolyamide (copolyamide)** synthesized from two different bifunctional monomers:
1. **Hexamethylenediamine (HMDA)** — H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂
2. **Adipic acid** — HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH

These undergo a classic **step-growth condensation polymerization** reaction, liberating **water**.

- **IUPAC Name:** Poly[imino(1,6-dioxohexamethylene)imnohexamethylene]
- **CAS Number:** 32131-17-2
- **Repeating unit:** –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]–
- **Molecular formula of repeat unit:** C₁₂H₂₂N₂O₂
- **Molar mass of repeat unit:** **226.32 g/mol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoding the name "6,6":** The first "**6**" denotes the number of carbon atoms in the **diamine monomer** (hexamethylenediamine has 6 carbons), and the second "**6**" denotes the number of carbon atoms in the **diacid monomer** (adipic acid has 6 carbons including both carboxyl carbons).

---

## 1.4 Polyester Synthetic Fibers — Formal Definition

**Polyester fibers** are synthetic fibers in which the polymer forming the fiber-forming substance is composed of at least **85% by mass of an ester of a diol and terephthalic acid** (definition per ISO and BIS standards).

The dominant polyester fiber is **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)**, often just called "polyester" in textile commerce.

- **IUPAC Name:** Poly(ethylene terephthalate)
- **CAS Number:** 25038-59-9
- **Repeating unit:** –[O–CH₂–CH₂–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]–
- **Molecular formula of repeat unit:** C₁₀H₈O₄
- **Molar mass of repeat unit:** **192.17 g/mol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** PET was patented in **1941** by **John Rex Whinfield** and **James Tennant Dickson** at the **Calico Printers' Association** in the UK. Commercial production began in **1955** by **DuPont** (Dacron®) and **ICI** (Terylene®). The most common textile end-use is the now-ubiquitous **PET bottle** (preform) and **microfiber fabrics**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Plastic Spring"

Think of polyester (PET) as a **molecular spring**: long chains made of alternating rigid **benzene ring "joints"** and flexible **ethylene "spacers"**. The benzene rings make the fiber **stiff and wrinkle-resistant**, while the ethylene spacers give it **flexibility**. This is why a polyester shirt springs back to its shape after being crumpled, unlike a cotton shirt.

---

## 1.5 Comparative Snapshot Table

| Property | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 | PET (Polyester) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polymer class | Polyamide | Polyamide | Polyester |
| Repeat unit MW | 113.16 g/mol | 226.32 g/mol | 192.17 g/mol |
| Polymerization type | Ring-opening (chain-growth) | Step-growth condensation | Step-growth condensation |
| Number of monomers | 1 (caprolactam) | 2 (HMDA + Adipic acid) | 2 (EG + TPA/DMT) |
| Byproduct | None (ring opens) | Water (H₂O) | Water / Methanol |
| Tg | ~50 °C | ~57 °C | ~69–81 °C |
| Tm | ~220 °C | ~255–265 °C | ~255–260 °C |
| Moisture regain | ~4.0% | ~4.5% | ~0.4% |
| Major trade names | Perlon®, Dederon® | Nylon®, Cordura® | Dacron®, Terylene®, Mylar® |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nylon 6 — Manufacturing & Polymerization Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Raw Material: ε-Caprolactam

**ε-Caprolactam** is a **7-membered cyclic amide** (lactam) with the molecular formula **C₆H₁₁NO**. It is industrially produced mainly from:
- **Cyclohexane** (Dupont/BASF route)
- **Phenol** (Allied Chemical route)
- **Toluene** (SNIA Viscosa route)

Its structure is shown below:

$$\text{Caprolactam: } \overset{\text{amide C=O}}{\text{–C(=O)–NH–}} \text{ attached to a } \text{–(CH}_2\text{)}_5\text{– ring}$$

### 2.1.2 Polymerization: Hydrolytic Ring-Opening (VK Tube Process)

**Nylon 6 is manufactured commercially by the **VK Tube (Vereinfacht Kontinuierlich = Simplified Continuous) Process**, a continuous hydrolytic ring-opening polymerization.**

**Step 1 — Initiation (Hydrolysis of Lactam):**
Water (catalyst/initiator) opens the lactam ring to form **ε-aminocaproic acid (ACA)**.

$$\text{Caprolactam} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_5\text{–COOH}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation (Step-growth via Amide Bond Formation):**

ACA + Caprolactam → 2 units + H₂O, then linear chain growth continues:

$$\text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_5\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium (Cyclic ⇌ Linear):**

The reaction is a **reversible equilibrium** between linear polymer and unreacted cyclic monomer. About **8–10% monomer** remains in equilibrium with the polymer at ~250 °C — this is removed by **hot-water leaching**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reaction Conditions for Nylon 6:**
> - **Temperature:** 240–270 °C
> - **Pressure:** Atmospheric (with N₂ blanket) to slightly elevated
> - **Catalyst/Initiation:** 0.1–0.5% water
> - **Reaction time:** 12–24 hours (batch) or continuous residence time of ~20 h (VK tube)
> - **Conversion:** ~90% (need monomer recovery)

### 2.1.3 Key Engineering Features of VK Tube Process
- **Vertical, jacketed, U-shaped reactor** (top-down flow)
- **Melting zone (top)** — caprolactam melted at ~90 °C
- **Polymerization zone (middle/bottom)** — residence time 20–24 h at 250–270 °C
- **Equilibrium zone** — final polymerization completion
- **Output:** Molten nylon 6 extruded as a ribbon, cooled, and chipped

---

## 2.2 Nylon 6,6 — Manufacturing & Polymerization Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Raw Materials

1. **Hexamethylenediamine (HMDA):** H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂ (b.p. 205 °C)
2. **Adipic acid:** HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH (m.p. 152 °C)

### 2.2.2 Step 1 — "Nylon Salt" Formation

HMDA and adipic acid are reacted in a **1:1 molar ratio** in aqueous/methanolic solution to form **hexamethylenediammonium adipate (AH salt or "Nylon Salt")**:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 + \text{HOOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COOH} \longrightarrow \text{}^+\text{H}_3\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_3^+ \cdot {}^-\text{OOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COO}^-$$

This salt has a **sharp melting point of ~190 °C** and a **pH of 7.4**, allowing precise stoichiometric control (essential to achieve high molecular weight).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why form a salt first?** Exact 1:1 stoichiometry is **CRITICAL** in step-growth polymerization because the **Carothers equation** shows that any deviation from 1:1 drastically reduces molecular weight. The crystalline salt acts as a "stoichiometric buffer."

### 2.2.3 Step 2 — Condensation Polymerization (Autoclave Process)

The salt is heated in a stirred autoclave:
- **Phase 1 (Heat-up, 200–250 °C):** Water evaporates; concentration increases.
- **Phase 2 (Pressurization, 250 °C, ~1.7 MPa):** Pressure maintained to prevent diamine loss.
- **Phase 3 (Pressure reduction, 270–280 °C):** Steam vented; polymerization continues.
- **Final stage (~280 °C, vacuum or N₂ sweep):** Final molecular weight built up.

**Overall reaction:**

$$n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 + n\,\text{HOOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (2n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reaction Conditions for Nylon 6,6:**
> - **Temperature:** 270–280 °C (final stage)
> - **Pressure:** Initially 1.5–1.7 MPa, then atmospheric
> - **Catalyst:** Hypophosphorous acid (H₃PO₂) or antimony trioxide (optional)
> - **Molecular weight control:** Mono-functional acid (acetic acid) terminator
> - **Byproduct:** Water (must be removed to drive equilibrium forward — Le Chatelier's principle)

### 2.2.4 Continuous Process Variation
Modern plants use **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** cascades followed by a **finisher** reactor (often a horizontal, screw-type reactor) where water is stripped by inert gas or vacuum.

---

## 2.3 Polyester (PET) — Manufacturing & Polymerization Mechanism

### 2.3.1 Raw Materials
1. **Terephthalic acid (TPA):** HOOC–C₆H₄–COOH (or its dimethyl ester, DMT)
2. **Ethylene glycol (EG):** HO–CH₂–CH₂–OH

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Routes:**
> - **DMT Route (older, historical):** Uses dimethyl terephthalate + ethylene glycol → transesterification → methanol released.
> - **TPA Route (modern, Amoco/Mid-Century):** Uses purified terephthalic acid (PTA) + ethylene glycol → direct esterification → water released. This is **dominant today** because TPA is cheaper.

### 2.3.2 Two-Stage Polymerization

**Stage 1 — Monomer Production (BHET):**

Either transesterification (DMT route) or direct esterification (TPA route) produces **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)**, the PET monomer.

$$n\,\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + (n+1)\,\text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} \longrightarrow \text{H–[O–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (2n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Stage 2 — Prepolymer → Final Polymer (Polycondensation):**

BHET undergoes **polycondensation at 280–290 °C under high vacuum (~50–100 Pa)** with **antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃)** as catalyst. The molecular weight builds from ~3000 (prepolymer) to **20,000–30,000 g/mol** (fiber grade) or **30,000–50,000 g/mol** (bottle grade).

**Byproduct removal (EG and water):** Essential to drive equilibrium — accomplished by high vacuum and thin-film evaporators.

### 2.3.3 Solid-State Polymerization (SSP)
For bottle-grade PET, the amorphous chips are further crystallized and heated to **200–230 °C** (below Tm) under N₂ flow for 10–20 hours. This raises IV (intrinsic viscosity) to **~0.72–0.85 dL/g** (vs. ~0.6 dL/g from melt phase).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.4.1 Carothers Equation (Degree of Polymerization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Carothers equation** is the **single most important formula** for step-growth polymerization and is a **guaranteed 3- to 5-mark question** in KTU ESE.

For **stoichiometric balance** (r = 1):

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}}$$

For **non-stoichiometric** ratio (r = N_A / N_B, where N_A is moles of deficient group):

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}}$$

Where:
- $p$ = fraction of functional groups reacted (extent of reaction)
- $r$ = stoichiometric ratio (≤ 1)
- $\overline{X}_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization

### 2.4.2 Number-Average Molecular Weight

$$\boxed{\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0}$$

Where $M_0$ = molar mass of the repeat unit.

### 2.4.3 Equilibrium Constant for Condensation (Nylon 6,6 / PET)

$$K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{–CO–NH–}][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{–COOH}][\text{–NH}_2\text{]}}$$

For high MW, we need to **remove water** (Le Chatelier's Principle).

### 2.4.4 Glass Transition & Melting — Relation to Repeat-Unit Structure

For aliphatic polyamides, a useful empirical rule:

$$T_m\,(\text{°C}) \approx \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta S_f}$$

| Polymer | Repeat Unit MW (g/mol) | Tg (°C) | Tm (°C) | Density (g/cm³) | Tenacity (g/den) | Moisture Regain (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nylon 6 | 113.16 | 50 | 220 | 1.14 | 4.5–6.5 | 4.0 |
| Nylon 6,6 | 226.32 | 57 | 265 | 1.14 | 4.5–6.5 | 4.5 |
| PET | 192.17 | 70–80 | 255–260 | 1.38 | 4.0–5.0 | 0.4 |

### 2.4.5 Fiber Tenacity (Engineering Conversion)

$$\text{Tenacity (g/den)} = \frac{\text{Breaking strength (g)}}{\text{Linear density (denier)}}$$

where **1 denier = mass in grams of 9000 meters of fiber**.

### 2.4.6 Repeat-Unit Molar Mass Quick-Reference

| Polymer | Repeat-Unit Composition | Calculation | M₀ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nylon 6 | C₆H₁₁NO | 6(12) + 11(1) + 14 + 16 | **113** |
| Nylon 6,6 | C₁₂H₂₂N₂O₂ | 12(12) + 22(1) + 2(14) + 2(16) | **226** |
| PET | C₁₀H₈O₄ | 10(12) + 8(1) + 4(16) | **192** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Carothers equation** is used industrially to predict the required **conversion p** for a target molecular weight. For Nylon 6,6 fiber-grade (Mₙ ~ 20,000 g/mol), with r = 1: $\overline{X}_n = 20000 / 226 \approx 88 \Rightarrow p = 1 - 1/88 = 0.9887$ (i.e., **98.87% conversion**). The last ~1.1% of reaction is the **hardest** and **most time-consuming** — this is why **finishing reactors** are designed with very high surface area for byproduct removal.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Polymer | Primary Applications | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Nylon 6** | Tire cord, carpets, hosiery, fishing nets, automotive parts | Tough, abrasion-resistant, low cost |
| **Nylon 6,6** | Tire reinforcement, conveyor belts, parachute fabric, zippers, 3D printing | Higher melting point, stiffer |
| **PET** | Apparel (polyester fabric), PET bottles, Mylar® film, food packaging | High strength, dimensionally stable, recyclable (Resin code 1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Cross-Reference:** India hosts major nylon and PET manufacturing facilities at Reliance Industries (Patalganga, Dahej — world's largest para-xylene/PTA/PET complex), SRF, JBF Industries, and Indo Rama Synthetics. KTU students from Kerala often join these as chemical/process engineers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carothers Equation for Stoichiometric Case

### Setup
Consider a step-growth polymerization with **equal numbers of A and B functional groups** (r = 1, stoichiometric).
- Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules.
- At extent of reaction $p$, the **number of unreacted A groups** = $N_0(1-p)$.
- Since r = 1, the **number of unreacted B groups** is also $N_0(1-p)$.

### Step 1 — Count Total Molecules Present
Total number of functional groups remaining (A + B) = $2 N_0 (1-p)$.

The number of **molecules remaining** (whether unreacted monomer, dimer, or higher oligomer) equals half the unreacted groups (since each molecule has two ends):

$$N = \frac{2 N_0 (1-p)}{2} = N_0(1-p)$$

### Step 2 — Define Degree of Polymerization
The **number-average degree of polymerization** is the number of initial monomer units per molecule:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

This is the **Carothers equation** (stoichiometric case).

### Step 3 — Compute Molecular Weight
The **number-average molecular weight** is:

$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0 + M_{end}$$

For high molecular weight, $M_{end} \ll \overline{X}_n M_0$, so:

$$\overline{M}_n \approx \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0$$

### Worked Example (Nylon 6,6 at p = 0.99)

Given: Nylon 6,6 fiber grade, $M_0 = 226.32$ g/mol, $p = 0.99$.

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

$$\overline{M}_n = 100 \times 226.32 = 22{,}632\,\text{g/mol} \approx 22.6\,\text{kDa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A mere **1% increase in p (from 0.98 to 0.99)** doubles $\overline{X}_n$ from 50 to 100. This shows why the **last 2–3% of conversion** dominates reactor design and residence time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carothers Equation for Non-Stoichiometric Case

### Setup
Let there be $N_A$ molecules of monomer A (with A functional groups only) and $N_B$ molecules of monomer B (with B functional groups only), with $N_B > N_A$.
- Define **stoichiometric imbalance ratio**: $r = N_A / N_B \leq 1$

### Step 1 — Initial Functional Groups
- Total A groups initially: $N_A$
- Total B groups initially: $N_B$

### Step 2 — Limit on Conversion
Reaction continues **only as long as both A and B are present**. The **A groups are the limiting reagent**. Maximum number of A·B bonds = $N_A$.

So, the **maximum possible extent of reaction for A groups** = fraction of A that reacts:

$$p_{\max} = 1\,\text{(all A groups can react because B is in excess)}$$

But the **maximum conversion of B groups** is:

$$p_{B,\max} = r$$

This means $p$ in the Carothers equation is conventionally the extent of reaction of the **minority (deficient) group A**.

### Step 3 — Count Unreacted Molecules
- Unreacted A groups = $N_A(1-p)$
- Unreacted B groups = $N_B - rp N_B = N_B(1 - rp)$

Total molecules = (unreacted A end-molecules) + (unreacted B end-molecules) — i.e., half the total end groups:

$$N = \frac{N_A(1-p) + N_B(1-rp)}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Number-Average Definition
Total monomer units initially = $N_A + N_B$. Therefore:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_A + N_B}{\frac{1}{2}\left[N_A(1-p) + N_B(1-rp)\right]}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 2:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{2(N_A + N_B)}{N_A(1-p) + N_B(1-rp)}$$

Substitute $r = N_A / N_B$, so $N_A = r N_B$:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{2(rN_B + N_B)}{rN_B(1-p) + N_B(1-rp)} = \frac{2N_B(r+1)}{N_B[r(1-p) + (1-rp)]} = \frac{2(r+1)}{r - rp + 1 - rp}$$

Simplify denominator: $r + 1 - 2rp$

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$

This is the **non-stoichiometric Carothers equation**.

### Verification: For r = 1
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - 2p} = \frac{1}{1-p} \quad \checkmark$$

### Worked Example (PET with Slight Excess of EG)
- $r = 0.995$ (EG in slight excess — common in industry to compensate for EG losses)
- $p = 0.99$ (extent of reaction of acid groups)

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.995}{1 + 0.995 - 2(0.995)(0.99)} = \frac{1.995}{1.995 - 1.9701} = \frac{1.995}{0.0249} \approx 80.1$$

For r = 1, p = 0.99: $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-0.99) = 100$. So a small stoichiometric imbalance of 0.5% reduces $\overline{X}_n$ from 100 to ~80, a **20% reduction** in molecular weight.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Always confirm **which group is the deficient one** when applying the non-stoichiometric Carothers equation. Many students confuse "r" with the ratio of A:B when it should be **A:B where A is the deficient** group, with r ≤ 1. Loss of 2–3 marks if confused.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Repeat-Unit Molar Mass of Nylon 6,6 (For Exam Calculations)

### Step 1 — Write Monomer Structures
- HMDA: H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂ → 6 C, 16 H (including N–H's), 2 N
- Adipic acid: HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH → 6 C, 10 H, 4 O

### Step 2 — Identify the Repeat Unit After Condensation
After one amide bond formation:
- HMDA: 6 C, 14 H (in the backbone), 2 N
- Adipic acid: 6 C, 8 H (in the backbone), 2 O (in the amide C=O), 1 O (in the –CO–NH, partially shared)
- After condensation: H₂O lost = 2 H, 1 O (from –OH of acid and H of –NH₂)

> The **repeat unit** is the smallest unique segment: –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]–

### Step 3 — Count Atoms in Repeat Unit
- **Carbon:** 6 (from diamine) + 4 (from diacid) + 2 (from two C=O of diacid) = **12 C**
- **Hydrogen:** 12 (from (CH₂)₆) + 8 (from (CH₂)₄) + 2 (from two N–H) = **22 H**
- **Nitrogen:** **2 N**
- **Oxygen:** **2 O**

So: **C₁₂H₂₂N₂O₂**

### Step 4 — Calculate Molar Mass
$$M_0 = 12(12.011) + 22(1.008) + 2(14.007) + 2(15.999)$$
$$= 144.132 + 22.176 + 28.014 + 31.998$$
$$= 226.32\,\text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_0(\text{Nylon 6,6}) = 226.32\,\text{g/mol}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Repeat-Unit Molar Mass of Nylon 6

### Step 1 — Monomer
ε-Caprolactam: C₆H₁₁NO
- 6 C, 11 H, 1 N, 1 O

### Step 2 — Ring-Opening Mechanism
In ring-opening polymerization, **no byproduct is lost**. The repeat unit retains all atoms of the monomer (the ring opens but the atoms are conserved).

### Step 3 — Repeat Unit
The repeat unit is the same as the monomer: **–[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]–**

### Step 4 — Calculation
$$M_0 = 6(12.011) + 11(1.008) + 1(14.007) + 1(15.999)$$
$$= 72.066 + 11.088 + 14.007 + 15.999$$
$$= 113.16\,\text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_0(\text{Nylon 6}) = 113.16\,\text{g/mol}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Repeat-Unit Molar Mass of PET

### Step 1 — Monomers
- Terephthalic acid (TPA): HOOC–C₆H₄–COOH → 8 C, 6 H, 4 O
- Ethylene glycol (EG): HO–CH₂–CH₂–OH → 2 C, 6 H, 2 O

### Step 2 — Repeat Unit After Condensation
The repeat unit **–[O–CH₂–CH₂–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]–** contains:
- C: 2 (EG) + 6 (ring) + 2 (C=O) = **10 C**
- H: 4 (CH₂CH₂) + 4 (C₆H₄) = **8 H**
- O: 2 (from –O–CH₂) + 2 (C=O) = **4 O**

So: **C₁₀H₈O₄**

### Step 3 — Calculation
$$M_0 = 10(12.011) + 8(1.008) + 4(15.999)$$
$$= 120.11 + 8.064 + 63.996$$
$$= 192.17\,\text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_0(\text{PET}) = 192.17\,\text{g/mol}}$$

---

## 3.6 KTU-Style Numerical Problem — Full Solution

### Problem
A batch of Nylon 6,6 is produced with 1 mol% excess of adipic acid over HMDA. If the reaction has proceeded to **95% conversion** of HMDA groups, calculate the **number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{X}_n$** and the **number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n$**.

### Step 1 — Identify Stoichiometric Ratio
- HMDA is the **deficient** group (adipic acid is in excess).
- Excess = 1 mol% → $N_B / N_A = 1.01$ → $r = N_A / N_B = 1/1.01 = 0.9901$

### Step 2 — Apply Carothers Equation
$$p = 0.95,\quad r = 0.9901$$

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.9901}{1 + 0.9901 - 2(0.9901)(0.95)}$$

Numerator: $1 + 0.9901 = 1.9901$

Denominator: $1 + 0.9901 - 1.8812 = 1.9901 - 1.8812 = 0.1089$

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1.9901}{0.1089} \approx 18.28$$

### Step 3 — Calculate $\overline{M}_n$
For Nylon 6,6: $M_0 = 226.32$ g/mol.

$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \times M_0 = 18.28 \times 226.32 \approx 4137\,\text{g/mol}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
$$\overline{M}_n \approx 4.14\,\text{kDa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion:** A 1% stoichiometric imbalance with only 95% conversion gives a relatively low MW (~4 kDa). For fiber-grade Nylon 6,6, the industry aims for $\overline{M}_n > 20$ kDa, requiring **r → 1.000** and **p > 0.985**. This is why industrial nylon plants use **AH salt** for precise stoichiometry and run reactions to very high conversion.

## 4.5 Chemical Structure Schematic (Mermaid-friendly Representation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Nylon6
        N6[Repeat Unit: NH CO CH2 CH2 CH2 CH2 CH2]
    end
    subgraph Nylon66
        N66A[HMDA fragment: NH CH2 CH2 CH2 CH2 CH2 CH2 NH]
        N66B[Adipic acid fragment: CO CH2 CH2 CH2 CH2 CO]
        N66A --> N66B
    end
    subgraph PET
        PETA[EG fragment: O CH2 CH2 O]
        PETB[TPA fragment: CO C6H4 CO]
        PETA --> PETB
    end
```

> **Reading Guide:** In **Nylon 6**, the repeat unit has **1 amide group per 6 backbone atoms**. In **Nylon 6,6**, the repeat unit has **2 amide groups per 12 backbone atoms** (denser H-bonding → higher Tm). In **PET**, the repeat unit has **1 ester group per 10 backbone atoms**, but the **rigid benzene ring** provides stiffness.

---

## 4.6 Hydrogen Bonding Network in Nylon (Why Nylon 6,6 has higher Tm than Nylon 6)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Chain1
        C1O1[Carbonyl C double bond O]
        C1N1[Amine N H]
    end
    subgraph Chain2
        C2N2[Amine N H]
        C2O2[Carbonyl C double bond O]
    end
    C1O1 -.H-bond.-> C2N2
    C2O2 -.H-bond.-> C1N1
```

> **Why Nylon 6,6 > Nylon 6 in Tm:** In Nylon 6, amide groups are spaced at every 6th atom → only **one H-bond per repeat unit possible in optimal alignment**. In Nylon 6,6, amide groups occur every 6 atoms but on **both sides of the chain segment**, allowing **bidirectional H-bonding** to two adjacent chains → effectively doubles H-bond density → higher Tm (265 °C vs. 220 °C).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023, Model Question Paper - 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization with two examples each.** (3 Marks)

### Model Answer
| Feature | Addition Polymerization | Condensation Polymerization |
|---|---|---|
| Byproduct | **No small molecule** released | **Small molecule** (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH) released |
| Mechanism | Chain-growth (free radical / ionic) | Step-growth |
| Monomer | Unsaturated (C=C) | Bifunctional (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) |
| Examples | Polyethylene (from ethylene), PVC (from vinyl chloride) | Nylon 6,6 (HMDA + Adipic acid → H₂O), PET (EG + TPA → H₂O) |

> **Marking Scheme (KTU Standard):** 1 mark for each example + 1 mark for correct byproduct identification.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, B.Tech Polymer Technology]**
**What is a "nylon salt"? Why is it prepared industrially for Nylon 6,6 production?** (3 Marks)

### Model Answer
A **nylon salt (AH salt)** is the **1:1 crystalline adduct of hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid**: $^{+}$H₃N–(CH₂)₆–NH₃⁺ · $^{-}$OOC–(CH₂)₄–COO$^{-}$.

It is prepared industrially because:
1. It has a **sharp melting point (~190 °C)** and a **pH of 7.4**, allowing **easy purification** by recrystallization.
2. It guarantees **exact 1:1 stoichiometry** between amine and acid groups, which is **critical** to achieve high molecular weight in step-growth polymerization (Carothers equation).
3. It is **easily handled, stored, and transported** compared to the free corrosive acid and toxic amine.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for the definition + 2 marks for the three reasons (1 each, any two needed).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model 2024 Scheme - Module 4 Choice 1]**

**a)** Explain the manufacturing of **Nylon 6,6** with a neat flow diagram, mentioning raw materials, reaction conditions, and the role of the "nylon salt." Discuss the importance of stoichiometric control. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Derive the **Carothers equation** for the non-stoichiometric case. For a Nylon 6,6 batch where $r = 0.98$ and $p = 0.95$, calculate $\overline{X}_n$ and $\overline{M}_n$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Raw Materials:**
- **Hexamethylenediamine (HMDA):** H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂ — produced from adiponitrile (ADN) by hydrogenation.
- **Adipic acid:** HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH — produced by oxidation of cyclohexane (KA oil route) or by oxidative cleavage of cyclohexanol.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Nylon Salt Preparation:**
HMDA and adipic acid are dissolved in **methanol** in a 1:1 molar ratio at 60–80 °C. The **AH salt** crystallizes on cooling, is filtered, washed, and dried. The salt has:
- Melting point: ~190 °C
- pH: 7.4 (indicator of exact 1:1 stoichiometry)
- Purity can be checked by titration.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Polymerization:**
The salt is charged into a **stirred autoclave** with a small amount of **water** and a chain terminator (e.g., **acetic acid**). Reaction stages:
- **200–220 °C** (steam evolution begins)
- **250 °C, 1.5–1.7 MPa** (pressure maintained to suppress diamine loss)
- **270–280 °C, atmospheric** (pressure slowly released; polymerization continues)
- **Final stage: ~280 °C under N₂ sweep or vacuum** (water removed; final MW built up)

Residence time: **4–6 hours** (batch) or **continuous CSTR + finisher** (modern).

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Spinning:**
The molten polymer (T > 280 °C) is extruded through a **spinneret** (multi-hole die), cooled in air, and wound. The fibers are then **drawn (stretched 3–5×)** to align chains, increasing tenacity and crystallinity.

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Importance of Stoichiometry:**
In step-growth polymerization, $\overline{X}_n$ is **highly sensitive** to stoichiometric imbalance. As derived from Carothers equation:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

A 2% imbalance (r = 0.98) at full conversion (p → 1) limits $\overline{X}_n$ to:
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1.98}{1.98 - 1.96} = \frac{1.98}{0.02} = 99$$

Whereas with r = 1, $\overline{X}_n \to \infty$ at p → 1. Hence, **AH salt guarantees exact stoichiometry**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (4 Marks):** See Section 3.2 of this document for the complete derivation. The key result is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $r = 0.98$, $p = 0.95$, $M_0 = 226.32$ g/mol.

**Numerator:**
$$1 + r = 1 + 0.98 = 1.98$$

**Denominator:**
$$1 + r - 2rp = 1.98 - 2(0.98)(0.95) = 1.98 - 1.862 = 0.118$$

**Degree of polymerization:**
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1.98}{0.118} = 16.78$$

**Number-average molecular weight:**
$$\overline{M}_n = 16.78 \times 226.32 = 3798\,\text{g/mol} \approx 3.80\,\text{kDa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Step-marking is strict**: Each of the 4 derivation steps is ~1 mark. Skipping intermediate algebra → 1–2 marks lost.
> - **Always state units** (g/mol for $\overline{M}_n$, dimensionless for $\overline{X}_n$).
> - **Forgetting M₀** in the final step → 1 mark lost. Memorize: Nylon 6,6 = 226.32 g/mol; Nylon 6 = 113.16 g/mol; PET = 192.17 g/mol.
> - **Confusing r and 1/r**: r is ALWAYS defined as (deficient/excess) and ≤ 1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model 2024 Scheme - Module 4 Choice 2]**

**a)** Describe the **manufacturing of Nylon 6 by the VK tube process** with a flow diagram. Compare and contrast **Nylon 6** and **Nylon 6,6** in terms of polymerization mechanism, structure, and properties. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the **manufacturing of PET** by the TPA route with reaction conditions. The number-average degree of polymerization of a PET sample is 100. Calculate the **number-average molecular weight** and the **extent of reaction (p)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (3 Marks) — VK Tube Process for Nylon 6:**

**Raw Material:** ε-Caprolactam (C₆H₁₁NO), a 7-membered cyclic amide.

**Process steps:**
1. **Caprolactam storage** (solid flakes) → **melting vessel** at ~90 °C with deionized water (0.1–0.5% as initiator).
2. **Filtration & deionization** (removes impurities and ionic species that would cap chains).
3. **VK tube** (vertical, jacketed, U-shaped) with three zones:
   - **Top zone** — caprolactam pre-heated to ~230 °C
   - **Middle zone** — main polymerization at **250–270 °C** under N₂ blanket, residence time 20–24 h
   - **Bottom zone** — equilibrium established
4. **Melt discharge** → underwater pelletizer → chips.
5. **Hot water leaching** at 90–100 °C — removes 8–10% residual monomer + oligomers.
6. **Drying** in N₂ at ~100 °C.
7. **Spinning** of dried chips into fibers.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Comparison: Nylon 6 vs. Nylon 6,6:**

| Aspect | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer | 1 (caprolactam) | 2 (HMDA + Adipic acid) |
| Mechanism | Ring-opening (chain-growth-like) | Step-growth condensation |
| Byproduct | None | H₂O |
| Repeat unit MW | 113.16 g/mol | 226.32 g/mol |
| Density of amide groups | 1 per 6 backbone C | 2 per 12 backbone C (higher H-bond density) |
| Tm | ~220 °C | ~265 °C |
| Tg | ~50 °C | ~57 °C |
| Moisture regain | 4.0% | 4.5% |
| Process | Continuous (VK tube) | Batch autoclave or continuous CSTR |

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Structural Difference:**
- **Nylon 6:** Even spacing of amide groups along the chain; all amide C=O and N–H bonds point in **alternating directions** along the chain axis.
- **Nylon 6,6:** Amide groups are placed in **mirror-image symmetry** within the repeat unit, allowing **bidirectional H-bonding** with two adjacent chains, giving higher crystallinity and thermal stability.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (3 Marks) — PET Manufacturing (TPA Route):**

**Raw Materials:** Purified Terephthalic Acid (PTA) + Ethylene Glycol (EG)

**Stage 1: Direct Esterification (240–260 °C, 0.3 MPa, no catalyst)**
$$\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + 2\,\text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} \longrightarrow \text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–O–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
Produces **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)** and oligomers (DP = 2–5).

**Stage 2: Prepolymerization (270–280 °C, atmospheric → vacuum)**
BHET undergoes polycondensation to form prepolymer (Mₙ ~ 2000–5000 g/mol).

**Stage 3: Polycondensation (280–290 °C, vacuum ~50–100 Pa, Sb₂O₃ catalyst)**
- Molecular weight built up to **20,000–30,000 g/mol** (fiber grade).
- Ethylene glycol byproduct is removed under high vacuum to drive equilibrium forward.
- Typical catalysts: **Sb₂O₃** (0.03–0.05% by mass), sometimes with **Co or Ge** co-catalysts.

**Stage 4 (Optional): Solid-State Polymerization (200–230 °C, N₂, 10–20 h)**
- For bottle-grade PET (Mₙ ~ 40,000–50,000 g/mol).
- Done below Tm to avoid particle fusion.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Molecular Weight Calculation:**

Given: $\overline{X}_n = 100$, $M_0(\text{PET}) = 192.17$ g/mol.

$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \times M_0 = 100 \times 192.17 = 19{,}217\,\text{g/mol} \approx 19.22\,\text{kDa}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Extent of Reaction:**

For stoichiometric (r = 1) PET polycondensation:
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p} \Rightarrow p = 1 - \frac{1}{\overline{X}_n}$$

$$p = 1 - \frac{1}{100} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

So the extent of reaction is **99%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Process diagram carries 2 marks** — use **boxes with arrows**, label temperatures, pressures, and byproducts. Hand-drawn flowcharts with missing temperature labels lose 1 mark.
> - **For PET, the Sb₂O₃ catalyst role** is frequently asked — state it explicitly: "Sb₂O₃ acts as a polycondensation catalyst by activating the carbonyl group of the ester, lowering activation energy."
> - **The conversion calculation** is a guaranteed sub-part — show the **rearrangement** of the Carothers equation explicitly. Marks are awarded for the algebraic step, not just the final number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-hour exam prep):**

### ✅ Core Identity Statements
- **Nylon 6** = Polycaprolactam, repeat unit **–[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]–**, M₀ = **113.16 g/mol**
- **Nylon 6,6** = Poly(hexamethylene adipamide), repeat unit **–[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]–**, M₀ = **226.32 g/mol**
- **PET** = Poly(ethylene terephthalate), repeat unit **–[O–CH₂–CH₂–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]–**, M₀ = **192.17 g/mol**

### ✅ Polymerization Mechanisms
- **Nylon 6** → **Ring-opening polymerization (ROP)** of caprolactam, **no byproduct**
- **Nylon 6,6** → **Step-growth condensation**, byproduct = **H₂O**
- **PET** → **Step-growth condensation**, byproduct = **H₂O** (TPA route) or **CH₃OH** (DMT route)

### ✅ Process & Conditions
- **VK Tube Process** for Nylon 6 → continuous, 250–270 °C, N₂ blanket, 20–24 h residence time, ~8–10% monomer in equilibrium
- **AH Salt** for Nylon 6,6 → exact 1:1 stoichiometry, m.p. ~190 °C, pH 7.4
- **PET** → 2 stages: (1) esterification 240–260 °C, (2) polycondensation 280–290 °C under vacuum, Sb₂O₃ catalyst, optional SSP at 200–230 °C

### ✅ Key Equations (Memorize!)
- $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (stoichiometric)
- $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ (non-stoichiometric, r ≤ 1)
- $\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0$

### ✅ High-Yield Properties
- Nylon 6,6 has **higher Tm** (265 °C) than Nylon 6 (220 °C) due to **denser inter-chain H-bonding**
- PET has **low moisture regain** (0.4%) → hydrophobic → ideal for water bottles
- Nylons are **more hydrophilic** (~4% moisture regain) → used in apparel for comfort
- **Nylon 6 = softer, more elastic; Nylon 6,6 = stiffer, more heat-resistant**

### ✅ Industrial Trade Names (Examiner may ask!)
- Nylon 6: **Perlon®** (Germany), **Dederon®** (East Germany)
- Nylon 6,6: **Nylon®** (DuPont, original), **Cordura®** (INVISTA)
- PET: **Dacron®** (DuPont), **Terylene®** (ICI), **Mylar®** (film), **Recron®** (Reliance, India)

### ✅ KTU Examiner's Common Mark-Loss Zones
1. ❌ Forgetting to subtract water in the repeat-unit formula of condensation polymers
2. ❌ Confusing r and 1/r in Carothers equation
3. ❌ Missing **Sb₂O₃** catalyst mention in PET polycondensation
4. ❌ Not stating **stoichiometry importance** in Nylon 6,6
5. ❌ Wrong repeat-unit for Nylon 6,6 (drawing only one amide instead of two per repeat)
6. ❌ Spelling "caprolactam" as "caprolectam" or "polyamide" as "poly amide"
7. ❌ No units in numerical answers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Module 4 questions on polyamides and polyesters often come as a **paired question** (Nylon 6 vs. Nylon 6,6 OR Nylon 6,6 vs. PET). Practice writing **comparison tables** — they are easy to score 7/7 with structured presentation. Also expect at least one **numerical problem** on Carothers equation in every exam cycle.
